Tunisian Laws — Comprehensive Legal Reference

A navigable compendium of the Republic of Tunisia's constitutions, codes, statutes, and regulations

Version 4.289 — July 14, 2026 (Run 289)  |  Sources: WIPO Lex, NYU GlobaLex, JurisiteTunisie, ConstitutionNet, ILO NATLEX, JORT (via 9anoun.tn), Assemblée des Représentants du Peuple, ICNL, CPJ, RSF, HRW, Amnesty International, FIDH, Carnegie Endowment, TIMEP, Business News, La Presse de Tunisie, African Manager, Kapitalis, Managers.tn, Le Temps News, Nawaat, LPA Legal, allAfrica, InforMEA, Office des Terres Domaniales, IACE, L'Economiste Maghrébin, EuroMed Rights, MERIP, Arab Reform Initiative, JURIST, Webmanagercenter, Tustex, Business News Tunisia, legislation-securite.tn (DCAF), FAOLEX, Tunisie Numérique, WebManagerCenter, RTCI, Le Monde, BCT payment-systems & monetary bulletins, europarl.europa.eu, Mondafrique, webdo.tn, urbanisme.fr, TAP (Agence Tunis Afrique Presse), Inkyfada, ICIJ, printempsarabe.info, OECT.org.tn, Autonomia.org, Espace Manager, Tunisie Tribune, CKF.com.tn, Jeune Afrique, IFJ, CFJustice.org, Gnet News, INS.tn (Institut National de la Statistique), Leaders.com.tn, tunisienumerique.com, Tuniscope, Tax Administration (JIBAYA circulars), Africanews, Entreprises Magazine, Echos Plus, directinfo.webmanagercenter.com, AOMF (Association des Ombudsmans et Médiateurs de la Francophonie), IAPL Monitoring Committee on Attacks on Lawyers, CRLDHT, Mosaïque FM, Réalités Magazine, The New Arab, Arab News, ICJ (International Commission of Jurists), Le Economiste Maghrébin, Al-Estiklal, The Arab Weekly

Legal Family
Tunisia's legal system is predominantly civil law-based, derived from the French legal tradition with elements of Islamic law (particularly in personal status). The unified secular judiciary was established in 1956 upon abolition of Shari'a courts. The current constitution is the 2022 Constitution (adopted July 25, 2022 by referendum; published August 18, 2022), which supersedes the 1959 and 2014 constitutions. Official legal texts are published in the Journal Officiel de la République Tunisienne (JORT).
v4.0 update (June 30, 2026): Added the Finance Law 2026, the pending Foreign Exchange Code reform (draft 115/2025), the December 2023 local council elections that seated the National Council of Regions and Districts, the 2025 draft Personal Data Protection Organic Law, and developments on civil-society restrictions (LTDH/ATFD/FTDES suspensions) and Decree-Law 2022-54 prosecutions. Refreshed status notes on the Constitutional Court, INLUCC/anti-corruption institutions, and the investment code reform bill. Five new entries added (187 total); several existing entries revised for currency.
v4.1 update (June 30, 2026, Run 2): Cross-checked v4.0 against fresh research; it already captured nearly all major 2023–2026 developments tracked this run (Labor Law reform, FY2026 Finance Law, Foreign Exchange Code bill, CNRD seating, NGO reform bill, Personal Data Protection bill). Four genuine gaps were found and closed: (1) the 2024 Commercial Code bad-cheque reform (Law No. 2024-41), entirely absent; (2) the September 27, 2024 Electoral Law amendment transferring electoral-dispute jurisdiction from the Administrative Court to the Court of Appeal of Tunis, not previously noted; (3) the 1969 agricultural-land foreign-ownership restriction (Law No. 69-56) and the 2025–2026 pending state ("domanial") land regularization bills, an uncovered area; (4) updated the whistleblower-protection and electoral-law entries' descriptions in place. Two new standalone entries added (189 total); two existing entries revised for currency.
v4.2 update (June 30, 2026, Run 3): Cross-checked v4.1 against a broad parallel research sweep (Finance Laws, Penal/Labor/Central Bank/Health/Social Security legislation, post-2021 decree-laws, and pending-bill trackers). v4.1 already captured nearly all major 2023–2026 developments. Nine genuine gaps were closed: (1) the Finance Law 2023 (Decree-Law No. 2022-79), entirely absent, plus corrected placeholder law numbers on the Finance Law 2024 and 2025 entries (now Law No. 2023-13 and Law No. 2024-48, confirmed against JORT-mirroring sources); (2) the February 2024 Central Bank financing-authorization amendment (Law No. 2024-10), not previously noted on the Central Bank Law entry; (3)–(4) the two Saied-era judicial decree-laws underlying the Judicial Statute entry's "changed by presidential decree" reference (Decree-Law No. 2022-11 creating the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council, and Decree-Law No. 2022-35 granting magistrate-dismissal power), now cited with their own entries; (5)–(6) the criminal-reconciliation and anti-speculation decree-laws of March 2022 (Decree-Law Nos. 2022-13 and 2022-14), distinct from the 2017 Administrative Reconciliation Law already present; (7) the July 2025 Penal Code amendment to Articles 96/98 on abuse of function (Law No. 2025-14); (8) Tunisia's February 2024 accession to the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime (Organic Law No. 2024-9), and a correction to the cybersecurity decree-law entry noting the ANSI→ANCS agency rename it enacted; (9) the June 2024 patient-rights/medical-responsibility law (Organic Law No. 2024-32) and the October 2024 social-protection decree-law for female agricultural workers (Decree-Law No. 2024-4), both absent from the Health and Social Security sections respectively. The CNSS entry was also updated with the 2024–2025 contribution-amnesty decrees and the still-undrafted CNSS–CNRPS merger discussion. Nine new standalone entries added (198 total); three existing entries revised for currency.
v4.3 update (July 1, 2026, Run 4): Cross-checked v4.2 against a further research sweep focused on the narrow June 30 – July 1, 2026 window plus a scan of structurally plausible gaps (competition, mining, water, copyright, and public-debt legislation). v4.2 already captured nearly all major developments; three genuinely new standalone items and several refinements were found: (1) the April 30, 2026 wage-increase package (Decrees No. 2026-66, 2026-67 and 2026-68) setting SMIG/SMAG and general salary scales for 2026–2028, entirely absent; (2) the Presidential Decree No. 2026-108 (JORT, June 8, 2026) ratifying a US$500 million Afreximbank financing facility supporting the FY2026 budget, not previously noted; (3) the Decree-Law No. 2025-3 (JORT, October 3, 2025) framework for "sociétés communautaires" (community enterprises), a wholly uncovered area of Kais Saied's "new social project" economic legislation, distinct from the Social and Solidarity Economy Law already implied elsewhere. Six existing entries were refined for currency: the Finance Law 2026 entry now cites the wealth-tax basis (Art. 88) with its exemptions and June 2026 Note Commune n°13 implementing guidance; the Constitutional Court entry now notes pending Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025; the Copyright Law entry now notes pending Bill Proposal No. 2024-47; the Competition Law and Mining Code entries now note preparatory "radical revision" and code-modernization efforts respectively; the Water Code entry now notes a reported (unconfirmed) new "Code de l'eau" in preparation; and the civil-society-suspensions entry now includes the May 5, 2026 suspension of Avocats Sans Frontières Tunisia. Three new standalone entries added (201 total); six existing entries revised for currency.
v4.4 update (July 1, 2026, Run 5): Cross-checked v4.3 against a fresh research sweep, with adversarial verification of every candidate finding before inclusion — one claim (that a "Loi n° 011-2025/ALT" had reformed the Associations Law) was investigated and confirmed to be a hallucinated Burkina Faso statute misattributed to Tunisia; it was discarded, and the existing entry (Decree-Law No. 2011-88 still unamended, reform bills pending since 2023) is confirmed accurate with no change needed. Three genuine, independently verified items were added or refined: (1) a new standalone entry for Proposition de loi n° 42/2024, a pending 49-article ARP bill regulating social-media/online commerce sales (cahier des charges requirement), in committee as of June 2026; (2) the Insurance Code entry now notes a March 29, 2026 bill by 14 ARP deputies to raise road-accident compensation caps, pending; (3) the Road Traffic Code entry now notes the "violence routière" reform package — automatically confirmed as a draft approved by the Council of Ministers on July 28, 2025 (correcting a since-discarded claim that it was already enacted as law on that date) and still awaiting ARP examination as of December 2025. One new standalone entry added (202 total); two existing entries revised for currency.
v4.5 update (July 1, 2026, Run 6): Cross-checked v4.4 against a fresh two-pass research sweep (broad gap scan, then independent verification of every candidate before inclusion). v4.4 already captured essentially all enacted 2023–2026 legislation; the remaining gaps were three genuinely uncovered pending bills, all confirmed across multiple independent sources: (1) a draft "Code du Numérique" (Digital Code) — a ~258-article ministerial text to replace the 2001 Telecommunications Code entirely, deregulating MVNO/ISP/data-operator entry and restricting licensing to scarce resources (spectrum, submarine cables, satellite slots); previously only alluded to in passing on the Cybercrime Law entry ("pre-dates the current Digital Code effort") with no standalone entry — now added; (2) a gambling/betting law reform effort, an entirely uncovered area: the baseline Decree-Law No. 74-20 of October 24, 1974 (games of chance/lotteries) was not previously documented at all; a 23-deputy reform bill (filed January 19, 2026, referred to committee January 29, 2026) to criminalize unlicensed online gambling, plus a parallel Ministry of Youth & Sports sports-betting oversight bill awaiting Council of Ministers review, are now documented — note that specific penalty figures reported by some outlets (prison terms, fine amounts) could not be independently confirmed against a primary text and were deliberately omitted; (3) the Tax Adviser Organic Law (proposition de loi organique n° 2023-013), a deputy-initiated bill to regulate the "conseiller fiscal" profession, under ARP Commission de la Législation Générale review with sessions confirmed for June 3 and June 13, 2026. All three are pending, not-yet-enacted bills, documented as such. No changes were made to the Personal Data Protection or Constitutional Court entries — a candidate refinement (specific bill-proposal numbers) could not be corroborated on a second independent pass and was excluded rather than risk an unverified citation. Three new standalone entries added (205 total); no existing entries altered.
v4.6 update (July 1, 2026, Run 7): Cross-checked v4.5 against a fresh research sweep spanning constitutional, criminal, labor, tax, financial, digital, land, transport, and energy law, followed by independent citation verification before inclusion. v4.5 already captured essentially every enacted and pending 2023–2026 development tracked this run (Finance Law 2026, the Constitutional Court's continued non-establishment, the July 2025 Penal Code corruption amendment, the May 2025 anti-subcontracting labor law, the September 2024 electoral-law amendment, 2026 SMIG/SMAG figures, the pending Code des Changes and Investment Code revisions, the pending Personal Data Protection bill, and the Mining/Hydrocarbons/Road Traffic Code entries) — in several cases with more precise, independently-verified citations than this run's own initial research turned up (e.g., this run's draft finding of a "May 2025" electoral amendment and a "Law No. 2025-16" labor statute were both superseded by v4.5's already-correct September 27, 2024 and Law No. 2025-9 citations, and were discarded rather than introducing a regression). One genuine gap was found and closed: a dedicated Expropriation Law entry (Law No. 2016-53 of July 11, 2016, as amended by Decree-Law No. 2022-65 of October 19, 2022) was entirely absent from the Land & Property section — previously only implied via the Investment Law's Art. 21–25 reference to protection against expropriation. An initial draft citation (based on this run's own research) mis-dated the base law to "August 11, 2016" and the pending Dec. 2024 Hedhili working session to "December 2025"; both were corrected after independent verification against InforMEA and the Office des Terres Domaniales before publication. One new standalone entry added (206 total); no existing entries altered.
v4.7 update (July 1, 2026, Run 8): Cross-checked v4.6 against a fresh research sweep, then independently re-verified each candidate finding via targeted follow-up searches before inclusion. Several candidate findings turned out to already be captured near-identically by v4.5/v4.6 (the Digital Code's 258-article structure, the Personal Data Protection bill's 123-article/6-title structure, the gambling-reform bill's mechanics, the Tax Adviser Organic Law's committee timeline, and the Code des Changes bill's summer-2026 vote target) and were left unchanged rather than duplicated. Three genuine items were confirmed across multiple independent sources and added: (1) the NGO reform entry was refined with the April 2026 organic law proposal's concrete structure (45 articles/8 chapters) and its Article 26 foreign-funding pre-authorization mechanism, plus the ARP Rights and Freedoms Committee's June 24, 2026 report deadline; (2) a new standalone entry for the Unified Electoral Code proposal (29 deputies, May 2026) — a broader codification effort distinct from the narrower September 2024 amendment already documented, aiming to consolidate all electoral and referendum rules into a single text; (3) a new standalone entry for Consumer Credit & Household Over-indebtedness — a wholly uncovered area, documenting the dormant December 2020 Council-of-Ministers bill and the March 2026 IACE policy note reviving calls for its promulgation, with the "113/2020" bill number explicitly flagged as sourced from secondary reporting rather than a primary JORT record. Lower-confidence candidates (a nascent Health Code initiative, a sports-governance organic law, and a National Council of Tunisians Abroad reform) were investigated but excluded for insufficient independent corroboration or missing bill citations, consistent with this project's practice of omitting unverifiable claims rather than risking inaccurate citations. Two new standalone entries added (208 total); one existing entry revised for currency.
v4.8 update (July 1, 2026, Run 9): A targeted follow-up check identified one genuine, previously overlooked gap despite this project's extensive coverage of criminal and constitutional law: Organic Law No. 2018-50 of October 23, 2018 on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination was entirely absent from the artifact, even though it is one of Tunisia's most internationally documented statutes (the first anti-racial-discrimination criminal law in the MENA region). The new entry cites the law's adoption/promulgation dates, Article 2's definition of racial discrimination, the criminal penalties and legal-entity fines it establishes, and the Article 11 National Commission against Racial Discrimination, and adds a 2024–2026 implementation note — drawn from EuroMed Rights, MERIP, and Arab Reform Initiative analysis — that enforcement has been limited, alongside the case of Mnemty president Saadia Mosbah, whose 8-year sentence on separate money-laundering/illicit-enrichment charges was upheld on appeal in June 2026 and which Amnesty International and other rights groups describe as ordinary criminal law being used against a leading anti-racism advocate. One new standalone entry added (209 total); no existing entries altered.
v4.9 update (July 1, 2026, Run 10): Cross-checked v4.8 against a fresh research sweep spanning labor, migration/asylum, and treaty ratifications, then independently re-verified each candidate finding before inclusion. Most candidates duplicated existing entries (the May 2025 subcontracting reform, the September 2024 electoral-jurisdiction amendment, and the Decree-Law 2022-54 press-freedom entry were already documented, in v4.8's case with a more precise citation than this run's own initial research turned up). Three genuine items were confirmed across independent sources and added: (1) Law No. 2024-44 of August 12, 2024 (Maternity and Paternity Leave Reform), entirely absent — expands paternity leave from a pre-existing 1-day (private sector) / 2-day (civil servants) baseline to 7–10 days at full pay and restructures maternity leave into prenatal/postnatal components; (2) the June 2024 UNHCR suspension of asylum registration and status-determination processing in Tunisia, confirmed via HRW's 2025 and 2026 World Report chapters, added to the Entry and Residence of Foreigners entry; (3) Decree No. 23 of 2025 (JORT Issue No. 4, January 11, 2025) ratifying the North Western Sahara Aquifer System tripartite groundwater consultation mechanism with Algeria and Libya, a wholly uncovered treaty. Also fixed a structural bug: the judicial tag class was used on 5 entries but had no corresponding CSS color rule or custom property, rendering those tags uncolored — added --tag-judicial and its .tag.judicial rule. Two new standalone entries added (211 total); two existing entries revised for currency; one CSS bug fixed.
v4.10 update (July 1, 2026, Run 11): Cross-checked v4.9 against a fresh research sweep focused on climate, AI governance, and a re-verification of the labor, tax, criminal, digital, and electoral entries closed in earlier runs (all confirmed already accurate and left unchanged, avoiding duplication). Nearly every 2025–2026 development already appeared in the artifact by this point — the two genuine refinements found were updates to existing entries rather than uncovered gaps: (1) the Paris Agreement / NDC entry was updated with Tunisia's third Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC 3.0), published end of April 2026 and presented at a Gammarth seminar (May 16–17, 2026) to 200+ non-state actors — raising the carbon-intensity reduction target to 62% by 2035 (2010 baseline) and estimating US$55 billion in financing needs for 2026–2035; (2) the Personal Data Protection entry was given a brief, deliberately hedged AI-governance note: Tunisia's 2026–2030 National Development Plan references finalizing and launching a national AI strategy (building on an earlier 2018 strategy), but no decree or law number could be identified for it as of mid-2026, so it is documented as a policy-level initiative rather than as a standalone legal entry — consistent with this project's practice of not asserting a legal citation that cannot be independently confirmed. No new standalone entries added (211 total remains accurate); two existing entries revised for currency.
v4.11 update (July 1, 2026, Run 12): Cross-checked v4.10 against an independent, broad research sweep spanning post-2021 institutional decree-laws, the Finance Laws for 2025/2026, wage decrees, cybersecurity, data protection, foreign exchange reform, and previously-uncovered codes (transport, maritime, aviation, mining, hydrocarbons, PPP, legal professions). Essentially every candidate finding from this sweep was already captured — in most cases with more precise citations than this run's own initial research produced (e.g., this run's draft "Loi n° 62-13 du 24 avril 1962" for the maritime code and "Decree-Law 2011-79" for the bar association were both superseded by the artifact's already-correct Law No. 62-36/March 27, 1962 and Law No. 87-79/December 29, 1987 citations, and were discarded rather than introducing a regression). One genuine, independently corroborated gap was found and closed: the dissolution of the National Authority for Access to Information (INAI) in early August 2025 — confirmed across ARTICLE19, the IDEA Global State of Democracy tracker, and regional press (The New Arab, APAnews) — had not been reflected anywhere in the artifact despite the Access to Information entry otherwise being current; the entry now documents the dissolution and its effect of removing the law's independent enforcement mechanism. The State of Emergency entry was also refined with the specific end-of-January 2026 renewal decree extending the emergency through December 31, 2026 (the tenth consecutive year), replacing a more generic "extended for years" description. No new standalone entries added (211 total remains accurate); two existing entries revised for currency.
v4.12 update (July 1, 2026, Run 13): A broad multi-source research sweep (Finance Laws, local governance, wage decrees, cybercrime enforcement, migration, nationality, pensions) found that nearly every development had already been captured by prior runs, in most cases with equal or better precision (e.g., this run's own research separately re-derived the SMIG/SMAG decrees, the Finance Law 2026 wealth-tax overhaul, the pending Foreign Exchange Code and Personal Data Protection bills, and the 2025 civil-society suspensions — all already present and, on comparison, more precisely sourced than this run's initial drafts). Four genuine gaps were closed, all existing-entry refinements rather than new standalone cards: (1) the Local Government Code entry now notes that Organic Law No. 2025-4 of March 12, 2025 repealed its region/district provisions (and Organic Law 89-11 of 1989), replacing the elected-region model with decree-organized councils — a legislative change with no prior mention in the artifact; (2) the Nationality Code entry now notes a pending February 27, 2026 bill (36 deputies) that would tighten naturalization to 7 years' legal residence while easing rules for stateless persons and creating a skilled-foreigner track; (3) the Decree-Law 2022-54 (press) entry now cites the specific February 3, 2025 Cour de Cassation ruling (Criminal Chamber 29, Sonia Dahmani case) limiting the decree's scope, replacing a vague "2024 court ruling" reference, and notes Dahmani's subsequent April 2026 conviction under a separate case showing inconsistent application; (4) the CNSS entry now cites two specific pending pension bills (Proposition de loi 16/2026 on retirement deferral; Projet de loi 104/2025 on women's early retirement) previously described only in general "merger discussed" terms. No new standalone entries added (211 total remains accurate); four existing entries revised for currency.
v4.13 update (July 1, 2026, Run 14): Two parallel research sweeps (recent 2023–2026 legislation, and entirely uncovered legal-code areas) initially surfaced roughly 30 candidate items across tax, labor, transport, energy, culture, health, and institutional topics — cross-checking against the artifact showed essentially all of them were already present, in most cases with more precise citations than this run's own initial drafts (e.g., this run's draft citations for the Sport Law, Cinema Law, Blood Transfusion Law, and Organ Transplant Law were all superseded by the artifact's already-correct Law No. 1995-11, Law No. 93-40, Law No. 91-72, and Law No. 91-22 entries, and were discarded rather than introducing a regression; a draft claim that Finance Law 2023/2024 taxed telecommunications operators directly was also investigated and found to be inaccurate — that levy applied only to banks, financial establishments, and insurers, per the official Note Commune n°7/2024 — and was not added). Three genuine gaps were closed after independent verification: (1) Décret n° 2025-165 of March 20, 2025 (JORT No. 2025-033) appointing Sarra Zaafrani Zenzri as Head of Government in place of Kamel Madouri — the fourth such change since 2021 — entirely absent from the artifact despite its extensive institutional coverage; (2) the Investment Law entry now cites Décret n° 2024-182 of April 4, 2024, which amended the law's implementing regulation (Décret gouvernemental n° 2017-389 of 2017, not the 2016 law itself) on incentive-claim deadlines and eligible agricultural services; (3) the Finance Law 2026 entry now notes that the bank/insurer conjunctural contribution was reportedly made permanent and extended to telecommunications operators and auto dealers, sourced to two independent secondary outlets (ilBoursa, InFirst Auditors) with an explicit caveat that the specific implementing article could not be confirmed against primary JORT text. Also fixed a version-string bug: the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.11" for two prior runs while the header/footer correctly read "v4.12" — now synchronized. One new standalone entry added (212 total); two existing entries revised for currency; one version-string bug fixed.
v4.14 update (July 1, 2026, Run 15): This run initially started from a stale local checkout of the artifact's v1.0 baseline (87 entries) rather than current main; the mistake was caught before merging by fetching and diffing against origin/main (v4.13, 212 entries), and the branch was reset to match current main rather than risk a large regression — consistent with how prior runs (see the discarded PRs preceding v2.0 and v4.10) have handled the same failure mode. A subsequent, narrower gap-check confirmed that virtually every 2023–2026 development surfaced by this run's initial research (Finance Laws 2024–2026, Law No. 2025-9, Law No. 2024-41, Law No. 2024-10, CNRD establishment, Constitutional Court status, the pending Personal Data Protection and NGO-funding bills) was already present, in most cases with more precise citations. Three genuine gaps were closed: (1) the Higher Education Law entry now notes a pending 27-deputy bill to amend Article 15, replacing elected university presidents with ministry-appointed ones (opposed by FGESRS, under debate as of February 2026); (2) the Judicial Officers (Huissiers) entry now notes a pending modernization bill containing a provision allowing mutual-consent divorce before a huissier-notaire without court involvement, criticized by women's rights groups as weakening Personal Status Code safeguards; (3) a new standalone entry for the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill, examined by all ARP standing committees June 29–30, 2026 but not yet put to a plenary vote — the framework expected to underpin the AI strategy and NDC 3.0 climate plan already noted elsewhere in this document. Also added a missing .tag.pending CSS rule (used by these three entries but previously undefined). One new standalone entry added (213 total); two existing entries revised for currency; one CSS gap fixed.
v4.15 update (July 1, 2026, Run 16): This run also began from a stale local checkout (a v1.0-era baseline, 87 entries) rather than current main; per the same failure mode documented in the v4.14 entry above, this was caught before merging by fetching origin/main (v4.14, 213 entries) and resetting the branch to match it. A subsequent research sweep spanning finance/tax, the Foreign Exchange Code bill, the 2026–2030 Development Plan, the Water Code, renewable energy, e-commerce, and health-insurance (CNAM) reform found no genuine gaps: every lead either (a) duplicated an existing entry with equal or better precision (the Finance Law 2026 wealth-tax and e-invoicing provisions, the May 2025 anti-subcontracting labor law, the IFAD Thirteenth Replenishment ratification, the Code des Changes committee's June 17, 2026 status — commission at ~90% of hearings, weighing a vote without the Central Bank's input — already matched what this run's own research turned up almost verbatim), (b) remained an unconfirmed draft-stage measure not yet traceable to enacted JORT text (preliminary PLF 2026 press reports of additional levies on phone recharges, "cahier des charges" fees, and large-retailer invoices could not be confirmed against the final Law No. 2025-17 text and were not added), or (c) was misattributed foreign content on inspection — a purported "Office des Changes" digital platform launch and an "auto-production d'électricité Law No. 82-21" both resolved to Morocco's Office des Changes and Moroccan legislation respectively, not Tunisia, and were discarded. No new standalone entries added (213 total remains accurate); no existing entries altered. Consistent with the project's stated practice (task item 5), this run confirms the artifact is current as of July 1, 2026; see "Areas for Future Expansion" below for topics to prioritize once genuinely new legislation emerges.
v4.16 update (July 1, 2026, Run 17): This run correctly started from current origin/main (v4.15, 213 entries) rather than a stale checkout. A fresh research sweep targeted the seven items listed in "Areas for Future Expansion" plus a general scan for newly enacted JORT text since the v4.15 confirmation run earlier the same day. No genuinely new legislation, standalone gap, or enacted bill was found — consistent with v4.15's conclusion that the artifact is current — but four existing pending-bill entries were refined with more precise detail confirmed across independent sources: (1) the Foreign Exchange Code entry now cites the bill's 91-article count, notes hearings additionally covering the Ministry of Finance and Customs Directorate, and the economists' estimated 1–1.5 percentage-point GDP growth impact; (2) the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry now cites the plan's specific growth targets (4.2% average annual growth vs. 2.4% previously, 5.1% by 2030), names Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh's June 30, 2026 hearing, and clarifies the remaining procedural steps (synthesis report → ARP Bureau → plenary → Conseil National des Régions et des Districts); (3) the Proposition de loi n° 42/2024 (online commerce/social-media marketing) entry now notes the bill's consumer-protection chapter, including a three-day right of withdrawal (droit de rétractation) and a bar on non-disclosure of a seller's identity and licensing details; (4) the CNAM (Law No. 2004-71) entry now documents the fund's 2026 liquidity crisis (a theoretical ~TND 900 million surplus alongside acute cash shortfalls) and the January 2026 tripartite payment-guarantee agreement with pharmacists, while noting no CNAM reform bill has yet reached the ARP. No new standalone entries added (213 total remains accurate); four existing entries revised for currency.
v4.17 update (July 1, 2026, Run 18): Verified this branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.16, 213 entries, 19 merged runs) before starting, rather than a stale local checkout — the failure mode documented repeatedly in prior run notes above. A fresh research sweep re-checked the seven "Areas for Future Expansion" items (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill, Personal Data Protection & AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Water Code replacement, gambling reform, CNAM reform) and found no enactments or plenary votes — all remain at the same committee stage previously documented, confirming this artifact's existing detail is current. Two genuine standalone gaps were identified and closed, both previously undocumented pending bills: (1) a new Draft Law on Combating Financial Exclusion — a government (Ministry of Finance) bill built on the National Financial Inclusion Strategy, heard by the Finance & Budget Committee on June 26, 2026, addressing Tunisia's low banking/financial-service penetration (~60% of adults hold any bank/postal account; single-digit uptake of mobile finance and voluntary insurance); (2) Proposition de loi n° 83/2025 — a July 2025 parliamentary bill creating a dedicated criminal framework (6 months–10 years' imprisonment depending on the act) for stadium and sports-facility violence, under review by the ARP's Education Commission. Both were independently corroborated across multiple sources before inclusion. Two new standalone entries added (215 total); the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.18 update (July 1, 2026, Run 19): This run's local checkout again began from the stale v1.0 baseline (87 entries) rather than origin/main; per the failure mode documented repeatedly above, this was caught immediately (before any content was drafted) by checking git log against GitHub's PR history, fetching origin/main (v4.17, 215 entries, 20 merged PRs), and resetting the branch to match it. A structural audit found no regressions: all 215 law-card ids unique, all `.tag` classes styled, all internal anchors resolving, balanced HTML tags. A fresh research sweep independently re-verified the seven "Areas for Future Expansion" items via web search and source fetches rather than relying on the prior run's notes alone. Most leads confirmed the existing detail was already accurate or more precise than newly-found sources (e.g., the Personal Data Protection bill's "123 articles" and the nationality bill's "36 co-sponsors, February 27, 2026" both matched independently). Three genuine gaps were closed: (1) a new standalone entry for the Draft Organic Law on Audiovisual Communication (Proposition de loi organique, filed February 25, 2025 by 16 deputies, 99 articles/5 chapters) to repeal Decree-Laws 2011-115/2011-116 and replace HAICA with a new authority — entirely absent from the Press section despite two related HAICA entries already present; (2) the Associations Decree entry's pending-reform description was corrected: the bill is not merely "with the government" as the v4.13-era watchlist stated, but has been under active ARP Rights & Freedoms Commission review since a study day held April 15, 2026, with a June 24, 2026 committee-report deadline set by the parliamentary bureau and a named committee chair's (Thabet El Abed) criticism of its foreign-funding provisions now cited; (3) the Investment Code reform bill entry was refined with its specific approval-in-principle date (Restricted Ministerial Council, December 31, 2025) and current stage (ARP Strategic Planning Commission, article-by-article voting as of late May 2026, chaired by Saber Jelassi), replacing a vague "since 2025" description. One new standalone entry added (216 total); two existing entries revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.19 update (July 1, 2026, Run 20): Verified this branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.18, 216 entries, 21 merged PRs) before starting, rather than a stale local checkout. Because Run 19 had completed only hours earlier the same day, this run's research sweep targeted the ten items in "Areas for Future Expansion" specifically for status changes (committee vote, plenary vote, enactment, or JORT publication) rather than re-running a broad gap scan, plus general searches for any new July 2026 JORT text. No item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and no wholly new legislation or standalone gap was found — consistent with the short interval since the last check. Two existing pending-bill entries were refined with genuinely new, dated detail: (1) the Tax Adviser Organic Law (proposition de loi organique n° 2023-013) entry now reflects that the ARP Commission de la Législation Générale finalized its article-by-article examination around June 3–4, 2026 and the bill is now awaiting plenary scheduling, rather than still being mid-review; (2) the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill entry now notes a second joint ARP audition of Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh held July 1, 2026 (the same day as this run), at which Tunisian media flagged the plan's limited treatment of the media/audiovisual sector, alongside the ongoing synthesis-report process. One unconfirmed lead — a secondary source describing the Personal Data Protection bill as "132 articles" rather than the previously documented 123 — was investigated and deliberately not adopted, since it conflicts with the more precise, multiply-corroborated 123-article/six-title structure already in the entry and could not be independently verified against a primary text. No new standalone entries added (216 total remains accurate); two existing entries revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.20 update (July 1, 2026, Run 21): Confirmed this branch had never been merged (its sole prior commit was the project's original v1.0 baseline, already an ancestor of origin/main) and reset it to current origin/main (v4.19, 216 entries, 22 merged PRs) before starting. A research sweep combining the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist with a broader scan for wholly uncovered legal areas found most leads already accurately documented — including an independent re-check of the Personal Data Protection bill's article count, which again surfaced the same 132-vs-123 conflict Run 20 had identified and correctly left unresolved (businessnews.com.tn reports 132 articles/six chapters; webmanagercenter.com, reporting on the same committee hearings days later, reports 123 articles/six chapters; both citing the same ARP committee sessions, so this is treated as an unresolved primary-source conflict, not corroboration for either figure, and the entry is left unchanged). Two genuine gaps were closed: (1) a new standalone entry for the National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad (Law No. 2016-68 of August 3, 2016, implementing Decree No. 2019-820 of 2019) — an entire diaspora-governance institution with no prior mention anywhere in the artifact — now documented together with its pending reform bill (projet de loi n° 2024/064, under ARP Foreign Affairs/Tunisians Abroad Committee review, working session held June 4, 2026) which would introduce individual/electronic voting for diaspora representatives and reposition the Council as a cross-ministerial coordination body; (2) the Cinema and Cultural Industries entry was refined with the previously undocumented Proposition de loi n° 2024-03, a dedicated cinema-industry statute that reached its article-by-article finalization stage before the ARP's Tourism, Culture, Services and Handicrafts Committee on June 24, 2026. Both were independently corroborated across multiple sources (official ARP agenda republications, TAP, Gnet, RTCI, Le Temps News, La Presse, WMC) before inclusion. One new standalone entry added (217 total); one existing entry revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.21 update (July 1, 2026, Run 22): This branch's prior sole commit (v1.0 baseline) had already been merged into origin/main, so per this project's failure-mode handling documented in earlier run notes above, the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.20, 217 entries, 23 merged PRs) before starting. A targeted research sweep re-checked the ten "Areas for Future Expansion" items for status changes rather than re-running a broad gap scan, given how recently Run 21 completed. No item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and no wholly new standalone legal area was found. Three existing pending-bill entries were refined with genuinely new, independently sourced detail: (1) the Investment Code reform bill entry now notes late-June 2026 reporting describing the bill's shift from prior-authorization to a prior-declaration principle with positive (tacit-approval) administrative silence, plus a proposed "licence premium" fast-track investor status — explicitly flagged as reported reform direction rather than confirmed article text, since it could not be verified against a primary committee document; (2) the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry now notes that deputies and the Finance Committee raised feasibility doubts about the plan's growth and investment targets during the July 1, 2026 joint ministerial audition; (3) the National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad reform bill entry now notes the proposed gender-parity basis for diaspora representative voting. Two items already matched what this run's own research found nearly verbatim (the Foreign Exchange Code's CONECT amendments and the Financial Exclusion bill's four-axis structure and June 26 hearing were already documented by Run 21) and were left unchanged rather than duplicated. One lead — a claimed June 24, 2026 committee-report outcome for the Associations Decree reform bill — could not be independently confirmed this run and is flagged in the watchlist as still needing verification, rather than asserting an unconfirmed status. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); three existing entries revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.22 update (July 1, 2026, Run 23): This branch's prior sole commit (v1.0 baseline, 87 entries) had already been merged into origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in earlier run notes above — so the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.21, 217 entries, 24 merged PRs) before starting. A dedicated research agent re-checked all fourteen items tracked across the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist and the recently-added diaspora and cinema entries, plus a general scan for newly enacted JORT text or wholly new legal areas in the June 28 – July 2, 2026 window. Two leads that initially looked like wholly new gaps — a "Unified Electoral Code" bill and the LTDH suspension/court appeal — were checked against the artifact first and found to already be documented (as the electoral-code-unification-2026 and civil-society-suspensions-2025-26 entries, added in Run 8 and earlier respectively); rather than duplicating them, both were refined with the substantial new detail this run's research turned up. No item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome, and no wholly new standalone legal area was identified, so no new standalone entries were added (217 total remains accurate). Twelve existing entries were refined with genuinely new, independently sourced detail: (1) the Unified Electoral Code proposal now cites its 261-article/three-part structure and the May 19, 2026 committee debate over a 5,000-dinar candidacy deposit and candidate education-level requirements; (2) the civil-society-suspensions entry now notes the Tunis court's June 10, 2026 postponement of LTDH's suspension appeal to July 15, 2026; (3) the Decree-Law 2022-54 (press) entry now documents journalist Khaoula Boukrim's June 2026 four-year sentence and further cumulative sentences against Sonia Dahmani; (4) the 2026–2030 Development Plan entry now cites the SNJT's July 1, 2026 named complaint about the plan's omission of a media strategy, plus the plan's poverty (<15%), project-count (~21,100), and local-council-proposal (34,715 of 40,748) figures; (5) the Investment Code reform bill entry now notes a second independent source (22nd Tunisia Investment Forum, June 25–26, 2026) corroborating — though still not confirming against primary text — the prior-declaration/"licence premium" reform direction; (6) the Financial Exclusion bill entry now notes the Finance Ministry's June 28, 2026 public "temporisation" on the bill's proposed postal bank ("banque postale"), signaling likely delay of that component; (7) the CNAM entry now cites the ~TND 1.95 billion 2025 debt to public hospitals and a further, explicitly provisional July 1, 2026 payment agreement with pharmacies; (8) the Nationality Code reform entry now cites the bill's number (projet de loi n° 17/2026) and its May 7, 2026 committee session, including a stateless-children's-parents residency detail; (9) the Associations Decree reform entry now explicitly flags that no source could be found confirming what happened at or after the June 24, 2026 committee-report deadline, leaving that outcome unverified rather than silently repeating the deadline as if it were an outcome; (10) the Water Code entry now notes a reported seven-chapter structure and a single, uncorroborated report that the draft was submitted to the Prime Ministry, explicitly flagged as unconfirmed; (11) the Proposition de loi n° 42/2024 (online commerce) entry now cites its 88 co-sponsoring deputies, May 2024 filing date, June 2, 2026 finalized committee examination, and the Ministry of Commerce's competing e-commerce strategy through 2030; (12) the Draft Organic Law on Audiovisual Communication entry now notes the inter-committee jurisdictional dispute and Commission vice-president Aymen Ben Salah's announcement of hearings planned for June 2026 (not independently confirmed as having occurred). Four items — the Tax Adviser Organic Law, the stadium-violence bill, the cinema-industry bill, and the diaspora-council reform bill — were independently re-verified this run and found to already match the artifact's existing detail essentially verbatim, so were left unchanged rather than duplicated. A false rumor of a parliamentary "informant reward" bill, publicly and explicitly denied by the ARP Security & Defense Commission president on July 1, 2026, was investigated and correctly excluded rather than added as a real pending bill. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); twelve existing entries revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.23 update (July 1, 2026, Run 24): This branch's prior sole commit (v1.0 baseline, 87 entries) had already been merged into origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in earlier run notes above — so the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.22, 217 entries, 25 merged PRs) before starting. Because Run 23 had completed only about 50 minutes earlier the same day, this run's research agent specifically targeted the items most likely to have moved in that narrow window — the Associations Decree reform's still-unresolved June 24 committee-report deadline, the Audiovisual Communication bill's announced June hearings, the Foreign Exchange Code committee's vote timeline, and the Development Plan's post-audition scheduling — plus a general scan for any JORT publication or breaking Tunisia legal news dated June 28 – July 1, 2026. No item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee outcome: the Associations Decree deadline outcome remains unconfirmed by any source, no independent confirmation was found that the Audiovisual Communication bill's June hearings actually occurred, the Foreign Exchange Code committee's status is unchanged since its June 17–19, 2026 near-verbatim reporting (already fully captured in the existing entry, including the Central Bank's non-response), and the Development Plan's synthesis report has still not been transmitted to the ARP Bureau. A July 1, 2026 SNJT statement on the plan's media-sector omission was already documented by Run 23. The one superficially new item surfaced — the ARP Security & Defense Commission's public denial of a rumored "informant reward" bill — was independently re-confirmed as the same false rumor Run 23 had already investigated and correctly excluded. No genuinely new legislation, standalone gap, or watchlist resolution was identified this run. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision, since Run 23's detail already reflected everything this run's research turned up. This confirms the artifact is current as of July 1, 2026, 20:00 UTC; see "Areas for Future Expansion" below for the unchanged watchlist.
v4.24 update (July 1, 2026, Run 25): This run's local checkout again began from the project's stale v1.0 baseline (87 entries, the branch's original single commit) rather than origin/main; a full draft revision was written on top of it before a routine pre-merge check (fetching and diffing origin/main) caught the discrepancy — the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above. The draft was discarded and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.23, 217 entries, 26 merged PRs) before restarting. A research sweep targeted the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist for status changes since Run 24's 20:00 UTC cutoff, plus a general scan for breaking JORT/legal news: the wealth-tax "seuil relevé à 5 MDT" headline circulating June 30, 2026 (Tustex, citing analyst Ayari) was checked and found to restate — not change — the existing 0.5%/1% bracket structure at the 3 MDT and 5 MDT thresholds already documented on the Finance Law 2026 entry (confirmed against African Manager, Kapitalis, and Century21.tn implementation guides); the Associations Decree reform, Foreign Exchange Code committee, Data Protection bill, and Development Plan bill items showed no new committee action, vote, or JORT publication beyond what Run 24 already captured. No wholly new enacted legislation, standalone gap, or watchlist resolution was identified. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. This confirms the artifact remains current as of July 1, 2026; see "Areas for Future Expansion" below for the unchanged watchlist.
v4.25 update (July 1, 2026, Run 26): This run also began from the stale v1.0 baseline (87 entries) and, before that was caught, drafted and briefly opened a full standalone-artifact PR (#28) against it — a more severe instance of the recurring failure mode than in prior runs, since it reached PR stage before a routine pre-merge diff against origin/main caught the discrepancy. PR #28 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment, the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.24, 217 entries, 27 merged PRs), and a research pass was run specifically against the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist rather than repeating the discarded draft's broad sweep. Findings diverged from prior "no change" runs: several watchlist items had been slightly ahead of the evidence. Thirteen existing entries were refined rather than restated: (1) the Personal Data Protection bill entry now cites 132 articles (confirmed via the ARP's own bill tracker as Proposition n°95/2025, cross-checked against Espace Manager and Nessma) rather than 123 — the "123" figure traced to a conflation with Article 123 of the ARP's internal rules of procedure, the provision under which the bill was referred to committee — and adds the June 25, 2026 session date; (2) the Associations Decree reform entry adds the June 3, 2026 session detail that the commission was working from a newly-received bill text version, suggesting the June 24 deadline was more likely missed than met; (3) the Tax Adviser Organic Law entry is corrected — examination was not finalized by June 3–4 as previously tracked; a June 18, 2026 session continued reviewing disputed articles with no finalization confirmed since; (4) the social-media e-commerce bill (42/2024) entry is corrected likewise — not finalized June 2 as tracked; the committee instead formed a working group, indicating open points remained; (5) the CNAM entry adds a verification hedge: June 28–30, 2026 reporting shows the crisis escalating (pharmacists' union threatening to stop honoring CNAM prescriptions from July 1, a crisis cell formed June 30) immediately before the previously-documented July 1 payment agreement, which is consistent with, but not independently re-confirmed same-day as, that agreement; (6) the cinema-industry bill entry is corrected — the June 24, 2026 session was reported as continued study, not a completed article-by-article finalization; (7)–(13) smaller additions with no change in substance: the Foreign Exchange Code entry notes a July 1 "still urging acceleration" signal and a May 14 Chamber of Commerce hearing; the Development Plan entry adds its formal bill number (n°42/2026), single-article yes/no/abstain structure, rapporteur name, and the prior plan's sub-40% execution rate; the Investment Code reform entry flags a possible identity ambiguity with a separate MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024; the Water Code entry adds the confirmed (and enacted, unlike the rumored draft code) Finance Law 2026 Art. 65 "Fonds de l'Eau"; the Audiovisual Communication bill entry notes the ARP Bureau's June 4, 2026 reaffirmation of existing committee jurisdiction; the Diaspora Council reform entry adds a June 22, 2026 hearing with the Council's President; and the Financial Exclusion bill entry adds its registration number (n°23-2024). Also fixed a version-string bug: the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.23" while the header/footer read "v4.24" (the same class of bug the v4.13 note above once fixed and which recurred) — now synchronized. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate, since these were all refinements to existing cards); thirteen existing entries revised for currency; one version-string bug fixed.
v4.26 update (July 1, 2026, Run 27): This run's local checkout initially had only the project's original v1.0-baseline commit rather than current origin/main; a full draft update was written against it (and briefly opened as PR #30) before a routine pre-merge diff caught the discrepancy — the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above. PR #30 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.25, 217 entries, 28 merged PRs) before restarting. Rather than repeat the discarded draft's broad sweep (which had independently re-derived material already present, confirming this artifact's coverage rather than finding gaps), a research agent ran a narrow, targeted check against every item in the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist plus a general scan for new July 2026 JORT text or decree-laws. No committee vote, plenary vote, or JORT enactment was found for any of the 13 watchlist items — no wholly new legislation was identified either. However, this run's check surfaced that the watchlist bullet list itself (not the underlying standalone entries) had drifted out of sync with two corrections v4.25 had already made to the actual cards: (1) the Tax Adviser Organic Law watchlist bullet still said examination was "finalized around June 3–4, 2026" when the standalone entry (correctly updated in v4.25) already reflects that a June 18, 2026 session found the commission still reviewing disputed articles with no finalization; (2) the Proposition de loi n° 42/2024 watchlist bullet still said examination was "finalized in committee June 2, 2026" when the standalone entry (also correctly updated in v4.25) already reflects that an early-June "decisive" session instead formed a working group to keep harmonizing the text. Both bullets were corrected to match their standalone entries — a reminder that the summary watchlist, not just the cards themselves, needs to be checked for drift each run. One further genuine refinement was made: (3) the CNAM entry's "same-day" verification hedge from v4.25 (the July 1, 2026 pharmacist payment agreement) could still not be independently confirmed by this run's own search (through June 29, 2026); the entry now states plainly that the prior presidentially-mediated agreement expires June 30, 2026, that the pharmacists' union threatened suspension over ~TND 80 million in unpaid 2025 claims, and that the "July 1 agreement" claim remains unconfirmed rather than restating the same unresolved hedge. A secondary finding, logged here rather than acted on: 9anoun.tn's public JORT index appears to lag behind real time (most recently crawled issue dated early April 2026 at the time of this check), meaning "no JORT hit" searches against that specific source are a coverage gap, not proof that no new JORT text exists — future runs relying on that index for a negative result should note this limitation. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); three existing entries (two watchlist-bullet corrections, one CNAM refinement) revised for currency.
v4.27 update (July 2, 2026, Run 28): This run's initial local checkout again held only the project's original v1.0-baseline commit rather than current origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above — and, unlike most prior recurrences, a full standalone draft was written and briefly pushed to a PR (#32) before a routine pre-merge diff against origin/main (v4.26, 217 entries, 29 merged PRs) caught the discrepancy. PR #32 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset to match origin/main before restarting, consistent with how the v4.14, v4.15, v4.18, v4.19, v4.24, and v4.26 runs handled the same mistake. A targeted research pass re-checked every item on the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist for status changes since the July 1, 2026 cutoff, plus a general scan for new JORT text: no item crossed into a committee vote, plenary vote, or enactment (the Code des Changes committee remains at ~90% of hearings per its June 17–19 status; the Development Plan bill's June 30 / July 1 joint ministerial auditions were already documented; the Personal Data Protection bill remains in Rights & Freedoms Commission review; the Audiovisual Communication bill's jurisdictional reassignment discussion continued at a May 20, 2026 commission meeting with no confirmation of the "planned June hearings" having occurred). One genuine refinement was identified and added, addressing the specific "genuine information gap" the v4.26 watchlist flagged for the Associations Decree reform: a May 22, 2026 letter from commission member Fatma Mseddi to the Rights & Freedoms Commission president — surfaced only now via a dedicated search — describes the bill (by then covering at least twelve hearing sessions plus a study day) as effectively "blocked" despite reaching an "advanced" stage, and presses the ARP not to delay it further. This predates, and is consistent with, the already-documented June 24 deadline subsequently slipping without any independently confirmed outcome; it does not resolve the outcome but adds a dated data point closest to it that had not previously been captured. No new standalone entries added (217 total remains accurate); one existing entry revised for currency. This confirms the artifact remains current as of July 2, 2026; see "Areas for Future Expansion" below for the unchanged watchlist.
v4.28 update (July 2, 2026, Run 29): This run's local checkout again held only the branch's original v1.0-baseline commit (105 entries) rather than current origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above — and was reset to match origin/main (v4.27, 217 entries, 30 merged PRs) before any drafting began, avoiding the wasted-work pattern seen in the v4.14/15/18/19/24/26/27 recurrences. A research sweep re-checked all fifteen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items (no committee vote, plenary vote, or enactment found for any of them — the Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Data Protection bill, Associations Decree, Investment Code, and Electoral Code items are all unchanged since v4.27) and independently verified a specific claim surfaced during research — a third-party legal tracker (regulations.ai) asserting the Personal Data Protection bill takes "full effect July 11, 2026" — against the artifact's own hedge; this date is not corroborated by any primary ARP/JORT source and directly conflicts with confirmed reporting that the bill remains in committee, so it was correctly excluded rather than added. A broader scan for wholly uncovered legal areas surfaced one genuine gap: the Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures (approved in principle by the Council of Ministers November 29, 2025; under ARP Education/Sports Commission review since March 5, 2026; would repeal Sport Law No. 1995-11) — entirely absent from the Sport & Youth section despite that section already covering the very law it would replace. It was added as a new standalone entry, cross-verified across kawarji.com (Council of Ministers presentation), Tunisie Tribune (commission examination), and an allAfrica report of a May 15, 2026 expert hearing raising independence concerns. The Renewable Energy Law entry was also refined with a concrete May 13, 2026 application of its concession regime — CNRD approval of five utility-scale photovoltaic plants (TND ~1.6 billion) in Sidi Bouzid, Gafsa, and Gabès — and the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist gained a corresponding bullet for the new sports-structures bill. One new standalone entry added (218 total); one existing entry revised for currency; the watchlist was refreshed accordingly.

v4.29 update (July 2, 2026, Run 30): Two parallel research agents ran this run: one re-checked all fourteen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items for status changes, the other scanned broadly for genuinely uncovered legal areas or newly enacted (not merely pending) legislation. Most watchlist items showed no material change since v4.28 (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Audiovisual Communication, Investment Code, Water Code, Electoral Code, Financial Exclusion, stadium-violence, and diaspora-council bills all remain at their previously documented committee stages); the Associations Decree reform's post-June-24-deadline outcome remains a genuine unresolved information gap despite a dedicated search. Two items were resolved or refined: (1) the CNAM entry's long-running "unconfirmed" hedge on the July 1, 2026 pharmacist payment agreement (carried across Runs 26–29) is now closed — Business News (July 1, 2026) independently confirms CNAM reached a payment commitment with private pharmacies averting the threatened tiers-payant suspension, though pharmacists still call it a stopgap pending structural reform; the still-unresolved medical-analysis-labs threat (from July 13, 2026) is now flagged separately; (2) the Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures entry is refined with confirmation that the ARP commission has moved from expert hearings (having since heard the Tunisian Olympic and Paralympic Committees) into drafting its committee report. A broader gap scan surfaced one genuine, independently corroborated enacted-law gap: Law No. 2026-5 of March 11, 2026 (Regularization of Distressed Agricultural Debts), adopted by the ARP March 10, 2026 and confirmed across Business News, Managers.tn, and the Library of Congress Global Legal Monitor — it mandates bank rescheduling of classified agricultural debts over up to 7 years and automatic removal of the resulting negative credit classification, a narrow banking/agriculture niche that had not previously been captured despite the Agriculture & Fisheries section's otherwise broad coverage. Several other candidates surfaced by the gap-scan agent (a new Code des changes, cryptocurrency/AI regulation, a whistleblower-protection overhaul, animal-welfare and asylum bills) were confirmed still at the pending-bill stage, not enacted, and were correctly excluded rather than added as if current law. One new standalone entry added (219 total); two existing entries revised for currency; the watchlist was refreshed accordingly.

v4.30 update (July 2, 2026, Run 31): This run's local checkout again started from a stale v1.0-baseline branch commit rather than current origin/main — the same recurring failure mode noted in every run above — and was caught before a PR was merged: a PR was opened against the stale base, found to have merge conflicts on submission, closed unmerged, and the branch was reset to match origin/main (v4.29, 219 entries, 31 merged PRs) before redoing this run's actual research. Because this run falls on the same calendar day as Run 30, a research agent re-verified all ten most-pressing "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items plus a brief broad gap scan rather than a full sweep, on the expectation that little would have changed within hours — which was borne out: no committee votes, plenary passages, or enactments were found for the Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform (the June-24-deadline outcome remains an unresolved information gap, now spanning two runs), Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment Code reform, Sports Structures law, Unified Electoral Code, or Nationality Code revision. One genuine update was found and incorporated into the CNAM entry: the private biologists' union voted at a June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly to proceed with suspending tiers-payant payment processing from July 13, 2026 over unpaid claims — previously reported only as "under consideration," now confirmed as a taken decision, though the suspension itself had not yet occurred as of this run and should be watched for confirmation or a last-minute agreement (as happened with pharmacists on July 1). The Part 2 broad scan for newly-enacted (JORT-published) legislation in the June 25 – July 2, 2026 window found none — the only JORT item located was a presidential appointment decree naming members of the existing National Commission for Criminal Reconciliation, an administrative appointment under the pre-existing framework rather than new substantive law, and was correctly excluded. No new standalone entries added (219 total remains accurate); one existing entry (CNAM) revised for currency; the watchlist was refreshed accordingly.

v4.31 update (July 2, 2026, Run 32): This run repeated the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above: its local checkout held only the branch's original v1.0-baseline commit, and a full standalone-artifact draft was written and briefly opened as PR #37 before a pre-merge attempt surfaced conflicts, at which point it was closed unmerged (with an explanatory comment) and the branch reset to current origin/main (v4.30, 219 entries). Rather than repeat the discarded draft's broad rewrite — which had independently re-derived material already present, again confirming this artifact's coverage rather than finding structural gaps (transport, energy, public-finance, agriculture, tourism, and sport sections all already existed under those names) — a targeted re-verification pass was run against a sample of existing entries rather than the watchlist, on the theory that after 31 runs of addition-focused research, factual drift in long-standing entries was more likely than an uncovered legal area. This surfaced two genuine, independently-sourced errors dating back to the original v1.0 baseline that had survived 31 subsequent runs: (1) the Anti-Terrorism Law entry's enactment date was wrong — "Law No. 2015-26 of July 7, 2015" is actually Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015 ("7 août 2015," not "7 juillet"), confirmed against legislation-securite.tn, UNODC, OHCHR, and ecolex primary-text mirrors; the duplicate cross-listed copy of this entry in the Security section carried the same error and was corrected to match; (2) the standalone Anti-Money-Laundering entry cited a "Law No. 2019-23 of March 6, 2019" that does not appear to exist in any primary source checked (JORT, legislation-securite.tn, FATF) — Tunisia's actual AML/CFT statute is the same Organic Law No. 2015-26 (as amended by Organic Law No. 2019-9 of January 23, 2019), which FATF itself names as "Organic Law Number 26 for the year 2015 relating to Countering Terrorism and Stopping Money Laundering"; the entry was rewritten as an explicit correction and cross-reference rather than deleted outright, so readers searching for "money laundering" still land on the right law. No new standalone entries added (219 total remains accurate, since these were corrections to existing cards, not additions); three existing entries (two Anti-Terrorism Law copies plus the Money-Laundering entry) revised for accuracy. This is the seventh documented instance of the stale-checkout failure mode (after Runs 16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, and 31) — future runs should verify git log origin/main shows the current run count before beginning any drafting, not merely before opening a PR.
v4.32 update (July 2, 2026, Run 33): This run again repeated the recurring failure mode documented above: the initial local checkout held only the branch's original v1.0-baseline commit, and a full draft (PR #39) was written and briefly opened before a pre-merge status check surfaced merge conflicts against origin/main (already at v4.31, 219 entries, 32 merged PRs); the PR was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset to match current main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline — the eighth documented instance of this pattern (after Runs 16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, and 32). A subsequent, narrower research pass — cross-checking the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist plus a scan for JORT-published legislation in the July 1–2, 2026 window — found no newly enacted laws, and confirmed most watchlist items unchanged (Investment Code reform still at article-by-article committee voting; Associations Decree reform's June-24 committee-report outcome remains an unresolved information gap, now spanning three runs; Data Protection/AI bill, Audiovisual Communication bill, Sports Structures law, Unified Electoral Code, and Nationality Code revision bill all unchanged since Run 32). One genuine gap was found and closed: a standalone entry for Bill No. 58/2025, a parliamentary proposal to let residents regularize undeclared foreign-currency assets via a one-off "liberatory contribution" (7%–15% depending on asset type) in exchange for amnesty from exchange-control and tax violations — distinct from, but related to, the comprehensive Foreign Exchange Code reform (Draft Law 115/2025) already documented. The bill reached a plenary vote on April 14, 2026 but was sent back to the Finance Committee 54–3–2 rather than adopted or rejected outright, and reporting since December 2025 suggests it is being harmonized with the wider Code des Changes reform rather than advanced separately; both the Foreign Exchange Code entry and the watchlist were cross-referenced accordingly. One new standalone entry added (220 total); two existing entries (Foreign Exchange Code, watchlist) revised for cross-referencing.
v4.33 update (July 2, 2026, Run 34): This run correctly began from current origin/main (v4.32, 220 entries, 33 merged PRs) rather than a stale local checkout, avoiding the recurring failure mode documented in every run note above (nine prior instances: Runs 16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 33). A structural audit found no regressions: all 220 law-card ids unique, all 29 .tag classes styled, all TOC anchors resolving to matching section ids. A dedicated research agent re-checked all seventeen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items plus a broad scan for wholly new or newly-enacted legislation; two candidate leads were independently spot-checked before inclusion. No item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and no genuinely new standalone legal area was identified — but six existing entries were refined with independently verified detail: (1) the Draft Laws on Combating Financial Exclusion entry's bill-number citation is corrected from an unconfirmable "n° 23-2024" to the verified companion bills n° 38/2026 and n° 39/2026 (urgent-procedure requested), confirmed via the June 26, 2026 Finance & Budget Committee hearing roster (chair Maher Ktari, vice-president Dhafer Sghiri, and named deputies); (2) the Finance Law 2026 entry's Art. 56 e-invoicing provision now cites its operative platform (TTN's "El Fatoora"), technical format (TEIF/XML with a TunTrust/ANCE qualified signature), January 1, 2026 effective date, and penalty structure (100–500 TND/invoice, capped at 50,000 TND/year, for paper invoices wrongly issued outside the system; 250–10,000 TND, doubled on repeat, for non-compliant e-invoices) — independently confirmed across five specialist compliance sources; (3) the State of Emergency entry now cites the specific renewal decree, Décret n° 2026-15 of January 30, 2026, extending the state of emergency declared by Décret n° 2025-485 of December 29, 2025; (4) the Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures entry now cites its reported 211-article/7-chapter structure, confirmed across three independent outlets (La Presse, African Manager, Kawarji); (5) the Associations Decree reform entry now confirms — via a July 1, 2026 report — that the bill remains active (not abandoned) under joint Rights & Freedoms Commission review alongside the nationality and data-protection bills, with the commission publicly calling for broad stakeholder consultation before adoption, narrowing the outstanding information gap from "is it still moving" to "what will the eventual report conclude"; (6) the Tax Adviser Organic Law entry now cites the October 23, 2025 committee-level article-by-article vote that preceded the already-documented June 2026 disputed-article sessions. Two leads were checked and correctly excluded: a claim that the Personal Data Protection bill's article count was newly "resolved" at 132 relied on the same secondary sources already weighed in the v4.25/v4.28 back-and-forth, not a primary JORT/ARP text, so the existing "123 vs. 132 unresolved" hedge was left unchanged; and the Unified Electoral Code's existing 261-article/5,000-dinar-deposit citation was independently re-confirmed (not corrected) via a direct search that located the underlying May 19, 2026 businessnews.com.tn reporting. Also fixed a recurring version-string bug: the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.31" for two prior runs while the header/footer read "v4.32" — now synchronized (the same class of bug previously fixed in the v4.13 and v4.25 notes above). No new standalone entries added (220 total remains accurate, since these were all refinements to existing cards); six existing entries revised for currency; one version-string bug fixed; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist was refreshed accordingly.
v4.34 update (July 2, 2026, Run 35): This run's initial local checkout again held only the branch's original v1.0-baseline commit (87 entries) rather than current origin/main — the tenth documented instance of the recurring failure mode noted in every run above (after Runs 16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 33). A full standalone draft was written and briefly opened as PR #42 before a routine pre-merge status check surfaced merge conflicts against origin/main (already at v4.33, 220 entries, 34 merged PRs); the PR was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset to match current main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline. Rather than repeat the discarded draft's broad rewrite — which had independently re-derived a large amount of material already present in near-identical or more precise form (the bad-cheque reform, the September 2024 electoral amendment, Law No. 2025-9's labor reform, the Finance Laws 2025/2026, the Digital Code, the Constitutional Court's continued non-establishment, the mining/hydrocarbons/PPP/tourism-investment/road-traffic/aviation/maritime codes, and the Personal Data Protection bill were all already documented, in most cases with more precise citations than this run's own initial research produced) — the discarded draft's findings were systematically cross-checked against the actual current artifact via targeted grep searches before anything was added, isolating only genuinely absent items rather than re-adding duplicates. Five genuine standalone gaps were identified this way and independently verified (in one case via a dedicated adversarial-verification sub-agent) before inclusion: (1) a Postal Code entry (Law No. 98-38 of June 2, 1998) — entirely absent despite the Digital & ICT section otherwise covering the Telecommunications Code in detail; (2) a Weapons Law entry (Law No. 69-33 of June 12, 1969) — entirely absent from the Security & Counter-terrorism section; (3) a dedicated Extradition & Mutual Legal Assistance entry (Code of Criminal Procedure, Book IV, Ch. VIII, Arts. 308–335) — previously only alluded to in a single clause of the Budapest Convention entry, now given its own card in the Judicial Organization section noting Tunisia has no standalone extradition statute; (4) a Martyrs and Wounded of the Revolution entry (Decree-Law No. 2011-97 of October 24, 2011, amended by Law No. 2012-26 of December 24, 2012) — an entire benefits framework for 2010–2011 revolution victims with no prior mention anywhere in the artifact, added to the Social Security section and cross-referenced against the pre-existing, broader Transitional Justice Law entry to avoid conflation; (5) a Maritime Ports Code entry (Law No. 2009-48 of July 8, 2009) — the Maritime Commercial Code entry already referenced "OMMP," the ports authority, but the ports code itself, which repealed separate 1999 commercial-ports and 2002 fishing-ports laws, had no standalone card. One further item was a correction rather than a pure addition: the existing Consumer Credit & Household Over-indebtedness entry asserted Tunisia has "no credit bureau" — a dedicated verification sub-agent confirmed this is inaccurate and added a new Credit Reporting (Credit Bureau) Decree-Law entry (Decree-Law No. 2022-2 of January 4, 2022), while narrowing the over-indebtedness entry's claim to what remains true (no over-indebtedness observatory or debt-to-income caps) rather than deleting it outright; the new entry also notes that the one firm initially licensed under this framework, MITIGAN CIB, had its BCT agrément withdrawn on March 17, 2026 for failing to launch, so no credit bureau is actually operating despite the framework being in force since 2022. Six new standalone entries added (226 total); one existing entry revised for accuracy; no "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist changes were needed, since all six items are enacted law rather than pending bills.
v4.35 update (July 2, 2026, Run 36): This run correctly began from current origin/main (v4.34, 226 entries, 35 merged PRs) — the local branch checkout again initially held only the stale v1.0-baseline commit, but this was caught immediately via GitHub's PR history (open/closed PR list showed 35 prior merged runs) before any content was drafted, so no discarded-PR cycle was needed this time. A structural audit found no regressions: all 259 element ids unique, all 30 TOC anchors resolve to matching section ids, all 13 .tag classes used are styled (and vice versa), and <div> open/close tags balanced (986/986). Because this run falls only hours after Run 35 on the same calendar day, a dedicated research agent re-checked all nineteen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items plus a broad scan for newly JORT-published legislation in the July 1–2, 2026 window, rather than repeating a full ground-up sweep. The result confirms the artifact is essentially saturated for same-day developments: no watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome, and no wholly new standalone legal area or newly enacted law was found — in nearly every case, the research agent's independently-sourced findings (the Development Plan's July 1 ministerial audition and SNJT criticism, the Investment Code reform's Tunisia Investment Forum corroboration, the biologists' confirmed July 13 tiers-payant suspension, the Tax Adviser Law's disputed-article sessions) were already documented in the artifact, in most cases with more precise detail than the fresh research turned up. One genuine refinement was identified and verified: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) entry previously described the Finance & Budget Committee as "weighing whether to proceed to a committee vote without the Central Bank of Tunisia's opinion" as of mid-June 2026; a June 23, 2026 report (BusinessNews, covering the committee's continued work alongside separate STEG financing-agreement hearings) shows that posture has since softened — committee chair Maher Ktari now calls continued BCT consultation "essential" before final adoption, with the BCT still not having formally responded as of that date.
