The entry was updated to flag the earlier "vote without BCT" framing as superseded rather than the operative plan. One lead was checked and correctly excluded: a claimed pair of bill numbers (n° 38/2026 and n° 39/2026) for the Draft Laws on Combating Financial Exclusion, already cited in the artifact per the v4.33 note, could not be re-confirmed against a primary ARP source this run either — the existing citation is left as is pending stronger sourcing in a future run, rather than being removed or asserted with unwarranted confidence. No new standalone entries added (226 total remains accurate); one existing entry revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist reviewed and found to still accurately reflect all nineteen tracked items' status.
v4.36 update (July 2, 2026, Run 37): This run began from current origin/main (v4.35, 226 entries, 36 merged PRs) — the branch's initial checkout again held only the stale v1.0-baseline commit, caught before drafting via git ls-remote and the merged-PR history. Because this run falls only hours after Run 36 on the same calendar day, a dedicated research agent re-checked all seventeen "Areas for Future Expansion" bullets (covering nineteen tracked items) item-by-item against fresh French-language sources (Business News, African Manager, Tunisie Numérique, La Presse, Webmanagercenter, Le Temps, RTCI, Managers, L'Économiste Maghrébin), plus a broad scan for wholly new JORT-published legislation, Council of Ministers decisions, or treaty ratifications in the July 1–2, 2026 window. Result: no genuine change on any watchlist item — the Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Data Protection/Associations, Audiovisual Communication, Investment Code, Water Code, gambling/betting, Tax Adviser Law, social-media-sales bill, CNAM/tiers-payant, Financial Exclusion bills, stadium-violence bill, nationality code, Diaspora Council, cinema bill, Electoral Code, and Sports Structures items all remain at exactly the committee/hearing stage already documented, with no JORT publication, committee vote, or plenary vote found for any of them. One secondary source (a non-Tunisian aggregator, regulations.ai) claimed the Personal Data Protection/AI bill would see "full enforcement beginning July 11, 2026"; this was checked against primary Tunisian reporting, found uncorroborated and inconsistent with the bill's confirmed still-pending committee status, and correctly excluded rather than added. No wholly new standalone legal area or newly enacted law was found. No new standalone entries added (226 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist reviewed and confirmed still accurate with no items requiring status changes.
v4.37 update (July 2, 2026, Run 38): This run began from current origin/main (v4.36, 226 entries, 37 merged PRs); the local branch was reset to that head before any drafting. Two parallel research agents ran this run: one independently re-verified all fourteen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist bullets (foreign exchange code & amnesty bill, development plan, data protection/associations, audiovisual communication, investment code, water code, gambling/tax-adviser, e-commerce bill, CNAM, financial-exclusion/stadium-violence/nationality bills, diaspora council/cinema bill, electoral code, sports structures) against fresh sources; the other ran a broad sweep for newly enacted JORT legislation and structurally plausible uncovered legal categories (arbitration, competition, cryptocurrency, cybersecurity, animal welfare). Consistent with Runs 36–37, no watchlist item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and nearly every finding from both agents — including the CNAM biologists' confirmed July 13, 2026 tiers-payant suspension, the Investment Code bill's article-by-article voting stage, and the Foreign Exchange Code committee's ~90%-complete hearings — was already captured in the artifact, in most cases with citations as precise or more precise than this run's fresh research. One genuine, independently-verified gap was closed: the April 30, 2026 wage-increase decree package entry previously documented only three of its seven constituent decrees (Nos. 2026-66/67/68, covering SMAG/SMIG/collective-agreement sectors); Decrees No. 2026-63 (state/local-authority/administrative-public-establishment employees), 2026-64 (public-enterprise employees under special statutes), 2026-65 (judiciary, Administrative Court, and Court of Accounts magistrates), and 2026-69 (non-agricultural Labor Code employees outside any specific statute or collective agreement) were confirmed via JORT No. 44 (April 30, 2026) republications (Juridoc AI, L'Économiste Maghrébin, La Presse, Réalités, CCI Centre, Paie-Tunisie) and added, along with the sensitive-public-service-corps salary schedule and the package's Finance Law 2026 Art. 15 legal basis. The Investment Code reform bill entry was refined with a specific 2026 growth figure (3.3% GDP, ~TND 29.9 billion investment target) tying the reform to the Development Plan, sourced to Tustex. No new standalone entries added (226 total remains accurate); two existing entries substantively revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" intro paragraph updated to reflect the run count and flag the July 13, 2026 CNAM deadline as the nearest concrete date to re-check next run.
v4.38 update (July 2, 2026, Run 39): A fresh broad-plus-targeted watchlist scan found no newly enacted legislation since Run 38. Concrete additions, all refinements to existing cards: the civil-society-suspensions entry now cites Amnesty International (May 2026) and Human Rights Watch (June 9, 2026) reporting putting the wider tally at roughly 25 associations administratively suspended since mid-2025, naming Aswat Nissa, OMCT-Tunisia, I Watch, and Nawaat beyond the four organizations already tracked; the cinema-industry bill entry now separately notes Finance Law 2026 Art. 61's approximately TND 100 million/year CNCI film-subsidy funding, enacted and in effect January 1, 2026 independent of the still-pending organizational reform bill; and an internal inconsistency was corrected where the "Areas for Future Expansion" note had overstated the cinema-industry bill's committee status against its own standalone entry. The Foreign Exchange Code, e-commerce bill (42/2024), and Tax Adviser Law entries were also refined with the latest committee-session detail. No new standalone entries added (226 total remains accurate); four existing entries revised for currency. Correction (added Run 40): this run's own commit bumped the header/footer version string to "4.38" but the corresponding note-box paragraph was never written — the same class of documentation-drift bug fixed in the v4.13, v4.25, and v4.33 notes above, this time affecting a missing paragraph rather than a stale one. This paragraph reconstructs it from the merged commit message for continuity of the version history.
v4.39 update (July 2, 2026, Run 40): This run's local branch checkout again held only a stale prior commit rather than current origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in every run note above — with a more severe variant this time: three parallel research agents had already run and a full draft (built on a stale v2.0 baseline, itself descended from the original v1.0) was opened as PR #48 before a merge-conflict error on submission (not a routine pre-merge diff) caught the discrepancy against origin/main (already at v4.38, 226 entries, 39 merged PRs). Rather than discard the research, its findings were systematically cross-checked against the actual current artifact via targeted grep searches (following the v4.34 precedent) before anything was added, and the same branch/PR #48 was updated in place with corrected content rather than opening a new one. This isolated genuine gaps: despite 39 prior runs, the entire "State Security Conspiracy" prosecution saga — the mass 2023–2025 case against opposition politicians and lawyers, its November 2025 appeal, the related Ennahda-leadership "Case 2," and 2026 convictions of prominent rights figures — was completely absent from the artifact (zero matches for "conspiracy," "Ghannouchi," "Bensedrine," "Moussi," or "Hmaidi"), a significant omission given the artifact's otherwise thorough coverage of post-2021 rule-of-law developments. A consolidated new entry was added to the Judicial Organization section (rather than several thin cards, consistent with this artifact's existing preference — see the civil-society-suspensions entry — for grouping thematically related 2025–2026 developments under one card) covering: the Case 1 appeal ruling (Nov 27–28, 2025: 34 of 37 sentences upheld, 3 acquittals, several sentences raised); the Case 2 appeal ruling against Ennahda leadership (Feb 2, 2026: Rached Ghannouchi's sentence raised to 20 years, cumulative 56 years across cases); transitional-justice figure Sihem Bensedrine's 25-year sentence (June 26, 2026); and the Abir Moussi and Anas Hmaidi (AMT president) cases — each independently corroborated across Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, Le Monde, and the African Commission on Human and Peoples' Rights. A second genuine gap — the brand-new Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions Law (Law No. 2026-8 of May 13, 2026), adopted after over a decade of lobbying and notably touching AI-assisted creative-work recognition — was added to the Tourism & Cultural Heritage section alongside the existing cinema-industry entry. Six existing entries were refined with independently-sourced, dated detail not previously captured: (1) the Judicial Statute entry now documents the ONAT lawyers' general strike of June 18, 2026 and its unresolved aftermath, plus judge Anas Hmaidi's prosecution, both cross-referenced from the new consolidated entry; (2) the ISIE Reorganization entry now notes the council members' 4-year terms (Presidential Decree No. 2022-459 of May 9, 2022) expired May 9, 2026 with no successors named, per CRLDHT commentary — flagged as a single-source finding pending stronger corroboration; (3) the Decree-Law 2022-54 (press) entry now adds Mourad Zeghidi and Borhen Bsaies's further January 22, 2026 sentences (3.5 years, layered with money-laundering charges) and RSF's May 2026 World Press Freedom Index ranking Tunisia 137th amid a reported 25-year global low; (4) the bad-cheque reform (Law 2024-41) entry now cites BCT payment-systems data (published June 7, 2026) showing cheque volume falling from 12.25 million (2024) to 4.07 million (2025), with a 35.9%-by-volume shift toward bills of exchange (lettres de change) in Q1 2026; (5) the Central Bank Law entry now notes the Finance Law 2026's separate TND 11 billion interest-free BCT-to-Treasury advance (up from TND 7 billion in 2024), a March 26, 2026 circular restricting bank financing of "non-priority" imports, three policy-rate cuts to 7% between late 2025 and March 2026, and a January 2026 payment-institution license granted to OFT Tunisie. One lead (a claimed draft cybersecurity bill n°2024/036 distinct from the already-documented Decree-Law 2023-17) could not be corroborated this run and was excluded rather than asserted. Two new standalone entries added (228 total); six existing entries revised for currency; the missing v4.38 note-box paragraph was also reconstructed (see above).
v4.40 update (July 2, 2026, Run 41): This run's local checkout again held only the project's original v1.0-baseline commit (112 entries) rather than current origin/main — the same recurring failure mode documented in essentially every run note above. A full draft was written against that stale base by an independent research agent and opened as PR #49; the merge attempt failed with a merge-conflict error (not a routine pre-merge diff), which caught the discrepancy against origin/main (already at v4.39, 228 entries, 40 merged PRs). Rather than discard the underlying research, its ~15 candidate findings were individually cross-checked against the actual current artifact via targeted grep searches (following the v4.34/v4.39 precedent) before anything was added. The overwhelming majority — the entire April–June 2026 civil-society suspension wave, the Finance Law 2026's companion decrees (wage increases, the Afreximbank loan), the "State Security Conspiracy" appeal and judge Anas Hmaidi's prosecution, the Nationality Code reform bill (n° 17/2026), the National Development Plan 2026–2030, and others — were already present, in most cases with materially more precision and corroboration than the stale draft had reconstructed independently. The branch was reset to origin/main and only genuinely absent material was added: three new standalone entries in the International Law and Nationality/Immigration sections. (1) Tunisia's reported March 2026 withdrawal of its Article 34(6) declaration under the Protocol establishing the African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights, which would end direct individual/NGO access to that Court — flagged explicitly as a single-source finding pending stronger corroboration, consistent with this artifact's practice for unverified leads (see the ISIE-terms-expiry precedent in the v4.39 note above). (2) A previously wholly-missing standalone entry for the July 2023 EU–Tunisia Memorandum of Understanding (the "Strategic and Global Partnership"), whose migration, budget-support, and macro-financial-assistance pillars have shaped Tunisian migration enforcement since 2023 but which had never been documented despite the artifact's otherwise thorough post-2021 migration-policy coverage. (3) The EU's February–June 2026 designation of Tunisia as a "safe country of origin" under the EU's Pact on Migration and Asylum, again flagged pending independent corroboration of exact vote tallies against a primary EU source. Three new standalone entries added (231 total); no existing entries required correction, underscoring that after 40 prior runs the remaining gaps are narrow and increasingly EU/international-law-adjacent rather than domestic-legislative.
v4.41 update (July 2, 2026, Run 42): This run's local checkout correctly held current origin/main (v4.40, 231 entries, 41 merged PRs) from the start, avoiding the stale-branch failure mode documented in the notes above. A targeted sweep covering the Foreign Exchange Code's committee-vote status, the CNAM/biologists tiers-payant dispute, and a general scan for anything published in the July 1–2, 2026 window found one genuine, well-corroborated development and no newly enacted legislation. The Tunis Court of Appeal's correctional chamber confirmed judge Anas Hmaidi's one-year prison sentence on July 1, 2026 — in a ruling issued by default (in absentia) that, unlike the April 6, 2026 first-instance judgment, orders immediate execution of the sentence — corroborated independently across Kapitalis, webdo.tn, and Business News (all citing TAP/Diwan FM). This update was applied to both the existing Judicial Statute entry and the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry, which cross-reference each other. Two other candidate leads were checked and found not yet ripe for inclusion: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee vote, expected in the June 20–30, 2026 window per Committee Chair Maher Ktari, has still not been confirmed as completed or transmitted to plenary as of this run; and the ARP Finance Committee's June 28, 2026 approval of two ordinary World Bank/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n° 38/2026 and n° 39/2026, ~US$430 million for STEG's energy-sector modernization) remains routine loan-guarantee legislation awaiting its plenary vote, consistent with the Afreximbank-facility precedent, and was judged not to warrant a new standalone entry unless and until it clears plenary. No existing entries required correction beyond the Hmaidi update; no new standalone entries added (231 total remains accurate).
v4.42 update (July 2, 2026, Run 43): This run's local checkout correctly held current origin/main (v4.41, 231 entries, 42 merged PRs) from the start. A targeted sweep re-checked the three items Run 42 flagged as the nearest concrete watch-points and found all three still open: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee vote remains unconfirmed as of July 2 (Chair Maher Ktari's June 17 "essential BCT consultation" posture is still the last confirmed status); the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n° 38/2026 and n° 39/2026, US$400M + US$30M) remain committee-approved (June 28, 2026) but awaiting plenary vote; and the private biologists' tiers-payant suspension with CNAM, confirmed by a June 30, 2026 union vote, remains on track for July 13, 2026 with no negotiated fix reported. This run also surfaced and corrected a genuine data-quality error introduced in an earlier run: the "combating financial exclusion" bill had been mislabeled with bill numbers "n° 38/2026 & n° 39/2026" — those numbers, independently confirmed via the ARP's own official bill registry (arp.tn/loi/project/4016) and seven-plus French and Arabic press sources, in fact belong to the unrelated STEG loan-guarantee bills. The financial-exclusion bill's correct, registry-confirmed number is Draft Law No. 023/2024, filed March 6, 2024 — restoring an earlier, correct "23-2024" reference that a prior run had erroneously overwritten. Given the numbering conflict was now resolved with primary-source confirmation, the STEG loan-guarantee bills — previously judged in Run 42 as not yet warranting a standalone entry — were promoted to one, since their existence and correct numbering are now independently verified beyond the routine-legislation threshold that held them back last run. Research also identified that the long-discussed "banque postale" (postal bank) proposal is not part of the financial-exclusion bill at all but a separate multi-party member's bill, Proposition de loi n° 82/2025 (21 co-sponsoring deputies), now added as its own standalone entry. Two further existing entries were refined with newly corroborated detail: the CNAM entry now notes the Ordre des Pharmaciens' June 30, 2026 formation of a separate "cellule de crise," indicating the CNAM liquidity crisis is sector-wide rather than isolated to biologists; and the EU–Tunisia Migration MOU entry now cites a July 1, 2026 joint NGO letter (HRW, Amnesty International, ICJ, CPJ, EuroMed Rights, Avocats Sans Frontières) to European Commission President von der Leyen marking the MOU's third anniversary and calling for a human-rights review. The Judicial Statute entry was also refined with ONAT's July 11, 2026 annual general assembly date. Several other candidate leads (a cybersecurity bill n°2024/036, a ~258-article "Code du Numérique," the Personal Data Protection organic bill's 132-article count) were checked against the artifact and found already accurately documented from prior runs, requiring no changes. Two new standalone entries added (233 total); one numbering error corrected across two entries; three existing entries refined for currency.
v4.43 update (July 2, 2026, Run 44): This run's local checkout initially held only a long-diverged, never-merged branch history (an orphaned "v2.0/v2.1" line that had forked from the shared v1.0 baseline and was never merged into origin/main — a variant of the stale-branch failure mode documented in essentially every run note above, except that here a full independent research pass and draft edits had already been completed against the wrong base before the divergence was caught via git log origin/main). The branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.42, 233 entries, 43 merged PRs) before restarting. Rather than discard the underlying research, its roughly ten candidate findings — the July 2021 Article 80 crisis, the Anti-Racial Discrimination Law (2018-50), legal aid, notary law, the Mining and Hydrocarbons Codes, the Sports Structures Law, a 2020 COVID-19 legal framework, and the Maritime Commerce/Civil Aviation Codes — were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact (following the v4.34/v4.39/v4.40 precedent for handling this exact situation) rather than blindly re-added. All but one turned out to already be documented, in most cases with citations this run's own research could verify but which were less precise than initially assumed — cross-checking surfaced four apparent citation errors that had persisted since early runs and could not be corroborated against any primary or secondary source in a fresh, independent search: the Notaries Law entry's "Law No. 67-39 of June 8, 1967" (no such law found; replaced with the verified Law No. 94-60 of May 23, 1994, cross-confirmed via jurisitetunisie.com's full text and its citation in the 2011 bar-reorganization decree-law); the Legal Aid entry's "Law No. 2002-42 of April 3, 2002" (replaced with the verified Law No. 2002-52 of June 3, 2002, confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and jurisitetunisie.com, along with its Law No. 2007-27 amendment — the unverifiable article-by-article "key provisions" list built on the old citation was removed rather than carried over); the Maritime Commercial Code entry's "Law No. 62-36 of March 27, 1962" (replaced with Law No. 62-13 of April 24, 1962, directly confirmed via NATLEX/ILO's own legislative database); and the Sport Law entry's "amended by Law No. 2014-17 of April 14, 2014" (no such amending law found; replaced with multiply-corroborated secondary reporting of 2004 and 2006 amendments, explicitly flagged as needing primary-text confirmation of exact JORT numbers). These are flagged here as corrections rather than asserted with full confidence — each is a "could not verify the old number, could verify a different one" finding rather than a primary-JORT-text confirmation, and a future run with direct JORT/legislation.tn access should re-verify all four. The Presidential Decree No. 117 entry was also corrected: it had conflated the September 2021 decree with the ARP's dissolution, when in fact the decree only froze Parliament — formal dissolution came separately on March 30, 2022, after roughly 120 MPs' defiant online session; the entry now states both events with their correct dates. One genuine standalone gap was closed: a new COVID-19 Enabling Act & Related Decree-Laws entry (Law No. 2020-19 and Decree-Laws 2020-2/2020-8/2020-30) alongside the existing COVID Solidarity Fund entry, documenting the Labor Code suspension, procedural-deadline suspension, and economic relief measures that had not been captured despite the Solidarity Fund entry's presence. One new standalone entry added (234 total); four citation errors corrected; one entry's event sequencing corrected.
v4.44 update (July 2, 2026, Run 45): Re-checked the four items Run 44 flagged as highest priority (Foreign Exchange Code committee status, STEG loan-guarantee bills' plenary vote, the July 13, 2026 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, ONAT's July 11, 2026 general assembly) — all four were confirmed unchanged from Run 43/44's reporting, including confirmation that the biologists' tiers-payant suspension remains on track for July 13 rather than averted; no edits were needed to those entries. A broader sweep for June 30 – July 2, 2026 JORT publications, decree-laws, and ARP votes found no new enacted legislation, and one near-miss was caught and discarded: a search result implying Decree-Law 2022-54's revision committee "began examination July 2, 2026" traced back to source-date confusion — the underlying reporting was from July 2, 2025, not 2026, and was excluded rather than risk a fabricated timeline (consistent with this project's practice of discarding unverifiable or miscorroborated claims; see Run 5's discarded Burkina Faso misattribution for a prior example). Three genuinely new, independently-corroborated pending bills were found and added: (1) a standalone entry for a Penal Code amendment bill (Arts. 261/262/264) sharply raising aggravated-theft and embezzlement penalties, opposed by the Syndicat des Magistrats Tunisiens, confirmed via 4+ independent sources and explicitly distinguished from the already-documented Law No. 2025-14 abuse-of-function amendment; (2) a "Pending reform" note added to the Narcotics Law entry for a 17-article Drug Law amendment bill balancing tougher trafficking enforcement with a therapeutic approach to consumption, filed April 2025 and still in committee; (3) a "Pending reform" note added to the Urban Planning Code entry for a construction-regularization bill updating Law No. 2015-11, approved in amended form by committee vote on June 23, 2026 — its ARP registry number could not be confirmed and was deliberately omitted. One new standalone entry added (235 total); two existing entries revised for currency; no existing entries required correction.
v4.45 update (July 2, 2026, Run 46): This run's local checkout initially held a long-stale, never-merged v2.0 branch line (an early fork off the original v1.0 baseline, last touched hours before the project's first run and superseded ever since); it was caught immediately via git log origin/main before any drafting and reset to current origin/main (v4.44, 235 entries, 45 merged PRs) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in most run notes above. A dedicated research agent re-checked the eight items flagged in the prior "Areas for Future Expansion" note (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Data Protection/Associations Decree, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment Code reform, the theft-penalty/drug-law/construction-regularization bills) plus a broad scan for new JORT/ARP developments in the June 25 – July 2, 2026 window. Six of the eight showed no material change and required no edits. Two genuine, independently-corroborated developments were found and applied as refinements (no new standalone entries, since both extend existing cards rather than describing wholly new legal instruments): (1) the CNAM entry now confirms the private biologists' tiers-payant suspension is proceeding as planned on July 13, 2026 — not averted — with the concrete practical impact specified (family-doctor-scheme patients keep coverage but pay upfront and reclaim later; other insured categories lose direct billing outright), sourced to L'Économiste Maghrébin, Business News, and RTCI (all July 1–2, 2026); (2) the Decree-Law 2022-13 (Criminal Reconciliation) entry now documents the June 27, 2026 swearing-in of a reconstituted National Commission for Criminal Reconciliation under chair Ali Abbès, formalized via Presidential Decree No. 2026-109 (June 8, 2026) and Decree No. 131/2026 (JORT, June 26, 2026). A third finding corrected an initial research misdirection rather than adding new fact: the "ONAT July 11, 2026 general assembly" watch-item, already present in the Judicial Statute entry since Run 43, was independently re-confirmed and refined — a first-pass search misidentified ONAT as the architects' order before correcting to the actual body (Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie, the bar association); Bâtonnier Boubaker Belthabet has now been confirmed as the source describing the assembly's purpose as a strategy review of the Bar's dispute with the government rather than a leadership election (he was already elected for 2025–2028 in September 2025), and a previously undocumented prosecutorial challenge to an earlier lawyers' extraordinary general assembly's quorum (hearing reportedly set for June 10, 2026, outcome unconfirmed) was surfaced and added. The Drug Law reform bill's article count was flagged as inconsistently reported (17 vs. 40 articles) rather than asserted with false precision. No new standalone entries added (235 total remains accurate); three existing entries substantively revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" intro and watchlist bullets updated to reflect current status and the next run's two highest-priority follow-ups (July 13 tiers-payant fallout, July 11 ONAT assembly outcome).
v4.46 update (July 2, 2026, Run 47): This run's local checkout again initially held the same long-stale, never-merged v2.0 branch line flagged in the v4.45/Run 46 note (a fork off the original v1.0 baseline that a prior session had independently rebuilt to 120 entries without ever checking it against origin/main); it was caught via git log origin/main before pushing, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.45, 235 entries, 46 merged PRs) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in nearly every run note above, this time doubly confirmed by first independently re-deriving several items (the Status of the Artist Law 2026-8, the African Court Article 34(6) withdrawal, the civil-society suspensions, the Sonia Dahmani case, the 2026–2030 Development Plan) only to find each already present in origin/main with materially more precision and corroboration. A dedicated research agent then re-checked the highest-priority open items from the Run 46 note (the July 13 tiers-payant suspension and July 11 ONAT assembly, both still pending as those dates had not yet arrived), the Foreign Exchange Code, the Development Plan, and the remaining watchlist bullets, plus a narrow scan for JORT publications in the June 30 – July 2, 2026 window. Result: no JORT-published law, decree-law, or organic law was found for that window, and every finding the research agent turned up — the biologists' June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly vote and its practical impact, ONAT's July 11 assembly being confirmed as a strategy-review rather than an elective session, and the Development Plan's June 30/July 1 joint-committee hearings with their exact project-count and growth-target figures — was already documented essentially verbatim in the CNAM, Judicial Statute, and Development Plan entries respectively. A flagged possible duplicate (a reported Bill n° 2025/058 on regularizing past exchange-control violations) was checked and confirmed to already be correctly captured as its own distinct standalone entry, separate from the broader Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025). No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (235 total remains accurate) — the artifact is confirmed current as of July 2, 2026. A structural audit (unique element ids, balanced <div> tags, TOC-anchor resolution, tag-class/style parity) found no regressions.
v4.47 update (July 2, 2026, Run 48): This run's own initial local checkout again held the same stale, never-merged v1.0/v2.0 branch line documented in nearly every prior run note; caught via git log origin/main before drafting, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.46, 235 entries, 47 merged PRs). A fresh independent research pass — the Penal Code corruption amendment, the civil-society-suspensions wave, the African Court Article 34(6) withdrawal, the Status of the Artist Law, the agricultural-debt regularization law, and the Local Government Code's Organic Law 2025-4 update — was drafted before cross-checking, and every item was found already present in origin/main, in each case with more precise citations (e.g. the agricultural-debt law's confirmed number, Law No. 2026-5 of March 11, 2026, versus this run's initially unconfirmed "Bill No. 60/2025" placeholder). A further targeted sweep re-checked the Foreign Exchange Code, the 2026–2030 Development Plan (independently re-deriving the same 21,100-project/TND 101.835 billion/4.2%-growth/sub-15%-poverty figures already in the standalone entry, including the July 2, 2026 WMC report on regional-disparity framing), and the Constitutional Court entry. One lead was investigated and correctly discarded: a search result describing an ARP Bureau "withdrawal" of the Constitutional Court organic law proposal traced back to an April 2025 episode (the original 10-signatory proposal, abandoned after five signatories withdrew over provisions on presidential removal) that had already been superseded by the current, distinct Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025 (filed July 15, 2025 by 21 deputies) — the entry's existing "under committee review, no vote scheduled" status remains accurate and was not changed. No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (235 total remains accurate) — the artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 2, 2026, consistent with Run 47's finding hours earlier.
v4.48 update (July 2, 2026, Run 49): Branch state was verified against origin/main before drafting (v4.47, 235 entries, 48 merged PRs) rather than working from a stale checkout, avoiding the recurring failure mode documented in prior run notes. A fresh research sweep covered: JORT/ARP activity in the window since Run 48 (none found), the Foreign Exchange Code (91-article count re-confirmed, no committee vote yet), the Personal Data Protection bill (132-article figure re-confirmed against the "123 articles" confusion already flagged in the entry), the Constitutional Court proposal (Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025, still in committee), the theft/robbery Penal Code amendment (Arts. 261/262/264, already fully detailed), the CNAM/tiers-payant and ONAT/Bar-standoff watchlist items (both still pending their respective July 11 and July 13 dates, which remain after this run's cutoff), and the pending construction-regularization bill. That last item yielded one genuine, independently-corroborated enrichment: pre-committee-vote coverage of the bill (businessnews.com.tn, La Presse de Tunisie, African Manager, from its February 27, 2026 committee hearing, predating the June 23 amended-form approval already documented) supplies detail not previously captured — a 12-article structure, a December 31, 2025 eligibility cutoff, sponsor estimates of roughly 800,000–1,000,000 potential beneficiaries across ~1,400 informal neighborhoods, and a demolition-or-treble-value-fine penalty for post-cutoff violations — added to the Urban Planning Code entry with the existing caveat about the still-unconfirmed registry number retained, since these figures come from pre-vote reporting rather than a primary ARP/JORT text. No new standalone entries added (235 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Urban Planning Code) enriched. The artifact is confirmed current as of July 2, 2026 — no JORT-published legislation and no material status change on any other tracked item this run.
v4.49 update (July 2, 2026, Run 50): This run's own initial local checkout again reproduced the exact recurring failure mode this document has now flagged in nearly every prior run note: it began from a long-stale v1.0/v2.0-line branch (112 entries, last touched hours after the project's first run) and drafted a full round of "new" section additions — Transportation, Agriculture, a Cybersecurity Decree-Law entry, the March 2026 agricultural-debt law, an e-commerce/marketing bill entry — entirely from independent research, without first checking origin/main. A merge-conflict error on attempted PR merge surfaced the problem before anything was merged; the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.48, 235 entries, 49 merged PRs), and every one of that independently-drafted round of additions was found already present — in each case with materially more precision, corroboration, and self-correction history (e.g. the Maritime Commercial Code entry already documents and corrects an earlier run's fabricated citation). Only one genuine, independently-verified finding survived cross-checking against the real current state: the Road Traffic Code's enactment date, previously given as an unverifiable "August 23, 1999," is corrected to July 26, 1999 (Law No. 99-71, adopted by the Chamber of Deputies July 6, 1999, published JORT No. 61 of July 30, 1999), confirmed via jurisitetunisie.com's promulgation-law page and cross-checked against an independent web search; the entry was also enriched with its 2001-101 amendment. A further sweep re-confirmed the Foreign Exchange Code, Sports Structures bill, Personal Data Protection bill, Mining Code revision, and e-commerce/marketing bill (n°42/2024) statuses unchanged from Run 49. No new standalone entries added (235 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Road Traffic Code) corrected and enriched. The artifact is confirmed current as of July 2, 2026.
v4.50 update (July 3, 2026, Run 51): This branch (claude/nice-edison-g31ysp) had itself diverged from origin/main at a stale early point (a self-contained v1.0→v2.1 line, 3 commits, never merged) while main independently progressed through 50 separate merged runs to v4.49 — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog, this time affecting the branch's own starting state rather than a single run's draft. Per project practice, the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.49, 235 entries, 50 merged PRs) before starting this run's research, and the three superseded local-only commits were discarded (their content — v1.0 through v2.1 — was independently confirmed to be a subset of, and less precise than, what main already documents). A fresh research sweep covered: a scan for JORT-published legislation in the July 2–3, 2026 window (none found); the two nearest-dated watchlist items, the July 13, 2026 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension and the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly (both re-confirmed via multiple independent sources as still scheduled and unresolved, with no new developments since both dates remain in the future); the Foreign Exchange Code's CONECT amendment detail, the Personal Data Protection bill, the Tax Adviser Organic Law, and the gambling/online-betting reform bills (all re-confirmed unchanged from Run 50's documented status); and a broader gap check on the reported Water Code replacement. That last check surfaced a genuine refinement: the reported seven-chapter draft structure — previously sourced only to an undated, unattributed reference to "early 2025" — is now precisely traced to Agriculture, Water Resources & Fisheries Minister Ezzeddine Ben Cheikh's presentation of the draft to a Conseil ministériel restreint on February 25, 2025 (reported March 11, 2025 by La Presse de Tunisie, Webmanagercenter, L'Economiste Maghrébin, and allAfrica), closing the sourcing gap for that structural detail while leaving the separate, still-uncorroborated "submitted to the Prime Ministry" claim flagged as unconfirmed as before. A version-string synchronization bug was also found and fixed: the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.48" while the header/footer read "v4.49" — the same recurring class of bug documented in the v4.13 and v4.25 notes above. No new standalone entries added (235 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Water Code) enriched with a precise date and named source; one version-string bug fixed. The artifact is confirmed current as of July 3, 2026.
v4.51 update (July 3, 2026, Run 52): This run's own initial local checkout again started from the stale, never-merged v1.0-baseline branch line (3 commits, "v2.2", built independently without checking origin/main first) — the same recurring failure mode flagged in nearly every prior run note. It was caught before drafting, via git fetch origin main and a branch/PR history check, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.50, 235 entries, 51 merged PRs); the three discarded local commits' content (two "new" statutes and several refinements, independently researched) was cross-checked and confirmed to already exist in main — Law No. 2025-9 (labor subcontracting reform) and Law No. 2026-5 (agricultural-debt regularization) were both already present as standalone entries, in each case with materially more detail than the discarded draft. A dedicated research agent then re-checked thirteen of the highest-priority open watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Personal Data Protection bill, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment Code reform, gambling/betting bills, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Sports Structures bill, construction-regularization bill, the ONAT standoff, the CNAM/biologists tiers-payant dispute, the theft/robbery Penal Code amendment, and the Drug Law reform) plus a broad scan for any JORT-published legislation in the July 1–3, 2026 window. Result: no JORT publication was found, and every apparent lead the agent surfaced — a June 17, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee update, the biologists' June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly vote to proceed with the July 13 tiers-payant suspension, and the pharmacists' June 30, 2026 "cellule de crise" — was already documented in the artifact, in each case with equal or greater precision (the Foreign Exchange Code entry's Run 36 update already supersedes the June 17 snapshot with a more recent June 23 one; the CNAM entry's Run 31/43/46 updates already cover both the biologists' vote and the pharmacists' crisis cell in full detail). No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (235 total remains accurate). The artifact is confirmed current as of July 3, 2026 — the tracked watchlist in "Areas for Future Expansion" below is unchanged from Run 51.
v4.52 update (July 3, 2026, Run 53): This run's own initial local checkout reproduced the exact recurring failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note above (most recently Run 52 itself): a full independent research pass was completed, and six candidate entries were drafted and briefly committed, entirely from a stale, never-merged v1.0-baseline branch line ("v2.0" → "v2.1" → a new "v2.2") without first checking origin/main. This was caught before any PR was opened — via git fetch origin main and git log comparison, which showed main already at v4.51 (235 entries, 52 merged PRs) — and the branch was reset to current origin/main before restarting. Per the established practice for this failure mode (see Runs 34/39/40/43/49/50/52), the discarded draft's six candidate items were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep searches rather than blindly re-added: four (a Sport Law entry, a Nationality Code reform bill entry, a Cybersecurity Law entry, and an Exchange Code status update) were confirmed already present, in each case with materially more precision than the discarded draft (e.g., the existing Sport & Youth section already documents Organic Law 95-11 in far greater depth, and the Nationality Code reform is already tracked as the registry-confirmed projet de loi n° 17/2026). Two candidates survived cross-checking as genuinely absent and were independently re-verified via a dedicated verification sub-agent before inclusion, and a third was found during that verification: (1) the Public Enterprises & Privatization Law (Law No. 89-9 of February 1, 1989, with its five confirmed amendments and the CAREPP oversight body), added to Investment & Business Environment and explicitly distinguished from the pre-existing, narrower 2011 Confiscation Law entry; (2) the National Identity Card Law (Organic Law No. 2024-22 of March 11, 2024, amending Law No. 93-27 of March 22, 1993), added to Digital Law & ICT, noting its companion biometric-passport statute (Organic Law 2024-23) and distinguishing it from the separate "e-Houwiya" administrative service; (3) the Debt-Recovery Companies Law — the verification pass caught that the discarded draft's own citation ("Law No. 2001-91") was itself an amendment, not the foundational statute, which is actually Law No. 98-4 of February 2, 1998 (amended by 2001-91 and 2003-42); this was added as its own entry and cross-referenced with Human Rights Watch's June 10, 2024 report "No Way Out: Debt Imprisonment in Tunisia" (496 officially confirmed, ~7,200 business-association-estimated debt-related imprisonments), which was also woven into the existing bad-cheque reform (Law 2024-41) entry as directly relevant context this artifact had not previously captured. Three new standalone entries added (238 total); one existing entry (the bad-cheque reform) enriched with the HRW findings. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 238 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1052/1052 balanced <div> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes used and CSS classes styled.
v4.53 update (July 3, 2026, Run 54): This run's own initial local checkout again reproduced the exact recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog (most recently Runs 51–53): a full independent research pass was completed, and a wide set of candidate entries — a "Regulated Professions" section (lawyers, notaries, bailiffs, chartered accountants, accounting profession), an "Agriculture & Fisheries" section, a "Tourism, Sports & Cultural Industries" section, plus a Criminal Reconciliation Decree-Law entry, a Real Estate Development Law entry, a Commercial Register Law entry, and a COVID-19 Emergency Powers Law entry — were drafted and briefly committed as "v2.2," entirely from the same stale, never-merged v1.0-baseline branch line flagged in every prior run note, without first checking origin/main. This was caught before any PR was opened, via git fetch origin main and git log --graph comparison showing main already at v4.52 (238 entries, 53 merged PRs), and the branch was reset to current origin/main before restarting. Per established practice, the discarded draft's candidate items were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep searches rather than blindly re-added: the overwhelming majority were already present, in every case with materially more precision and better-verified citations than the discarded draft independently produced (e.g., the existing Fisheries Code entry already cites Law No. 94-13 with its 2002/2014 amendments in far more depth; the existing Tourism & Cultural Heritage section already documents the 2012 Code du Tourisme, the 1993 cinema framework, and the 2009 Crafts Law — all with different, better-sourced citations than the discarded draft's 1973-era decree-laws and 1959/1960 statutes, which appear superseded or conflated; the existing Judicial Organization section already documents lawyers (Law No. 87-79), notaries (Law No. 94-60, itself a prior Run 44 citation correction), and bailiffs (Law No. 66-49) in materially greater depth than the discarded draft's citations, which in the lawyers' case conflicted outright — Law No. 87-79 vs. the discarded draft's "Decree-Law No. 2011-79" — and were not re-added given the existing entry's superior sourcing; the Criminal Reconciliation Decree-Law No. 2022-13 and the COVID-19 emergency powers law were also already present, each with a dedicated standalone entry). Three candidates survived cross-checking as genuinely absent and were independently re-verified via a dedicated verification sub-agent (2+ sources each, including oect.org.tn, WIPO Lex, NATLEX, and jurisitetunisie.com) before inclusion: (1) the Chartered Accountants Law (Law No. 88-108 of August 18, 1988, creating OECT), added to Financial & Banking Law; (2) the Accounting Profession Law (Law No. 2002-16 of February 4, 2002, creating the Compagnie des Comptables de Tunisie), added to the same section; (3) the Commercial Register Law (Law No. 95-44 of May 2, 1995, amended by Law No. 2010-15), added to Commercial Law as a standalone entry distinct from the general Commercial Code register provisions. A dedicated news sweep for the July 2–3, 2026 window found no newly enacted legislation (consistent with Runs 51–52's findings for the same period). Three new standalone entries added (241 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 241 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1066/1066 balanced <div> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes used and CSS classes styled.
v4.54 update (July 3, 2026, Run 55): This run's own designated branch was itself a long-stale line (a 3-commit "v1.0 to v2.1" fork last touched hours after the project's first run, never merged) while origin/main had independently progressed through 54 merged runs to v4.53 — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. It was caught immediately via git fetch origin main and git log --graph before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.53, 241 entries, 54 merged PRs) before starting research. A dedicated research agent then re-checked the two nearest-dated watchlist items — the July 13, 2026 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension and the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly — plus all other tracked pending-bill items (Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment Code reform, Water Code replacement, gambling/betting bills, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Proposition n° 42/2024, CNAM structural reform, Financial Exclusion/"banque postale" bills, stadium-violence and nationality-code bills, Tunisians-Abroad Council and cinema-industry bills, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, theft/robbery Penal Code amendment, and Drug Law reform), plus a dedicated scan for JORT-published legislation and a structural gap check across animal-welfare, consumer-leasing, e-signature/trust-services, and urban-transit-authority law. Result: no watchlist item's status changed, no new JORT publication was found for the June 28 – July 3, 2026 window, and every candidate structural gap was either not yet enacted (a July 2024 animal-welfare bill remains pending) or already covered by an existing entry (e-signature/trust services fall under the Electronic Commerce Law, Law No. 2000-83; consumer leasing under Law No. 94-89 of 1994). The biologists' union's June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly vote to proceed with the July 13 suspension — already fully documented in the CNAM entry since Run 31/46 — was independently re-corroborated (Business News, July 1, 2026; L'Economiste Maghrébin, July 2, 2026), but since this adds no new fact beyond existing sourcing, the entry text was left unchanged. No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (241 total remains accurate). The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, consistent with Run 54's finding hours earlier; the two live watch-dates (July 11 ONAT, July 13 tiers-payant) remain the next concrete triggers for a future run.
v4.55 update (July 3, 2026, Run 56): This run's designated branch was again a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork (3 commits, last touched the morning of this project's first day) while origin/main had independently progressed through 55 merged runs to v4.54 — caught via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.54, 241 entries, 55 merged PRs) before starting research. A dedicated research agent re-checked all tracked pending-bill items and ran a fresh JORT sweep for the June 28 – July 3, 2026 window, plus a structural gap scan against a battery of candidate uncovered areas (expropriation, habous/waqf, civil-service statute, political-party financing, public procurement, insurance code, capital-markets/CMF law, local-government code, gender-based-violence law). Result: no new JORT-published legislation was found, no pending bill crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and every structural-gap candidate the agent proposed was already present in the artifact under an existing entry (verified by direct grep against the live document rather than trusting the agent's summary) — including the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill's own draft-law number (n°42/2026), which the agent's independent search surfaced as if new but which the artifact's existing #development-plan-2026-2030 card already documented verbatim since a prior run. The Associations Decree reform and state-of-emergency entries were likewise independently re-confirmed as already carrying their most current detail (the lapsed June 24, 2026 committee-report deadline; Décret n° 2026-15 extending the emergency through December 31, 2026) and were left unchanged. No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (241 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 241 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1067/1067 balanced <div> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes and CSS. The July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) watch-dates remain the next concrete triggers for a future run.
v4.56 update (July 3, 2026, Run 57): This run's designated branch was again a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork (3 commits, last touched the morning of this project's first day) while origin/main had independently progressed through 56 merged runs to v4.55 — caught via git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD before any drafting began (a first draft round, based on the stale fork, had already added four candidate entries — a base Exchange Control Law card, an Agricultural Loan Regularization law, a Photovoltaic Concession Agreements law, and the African Court Article 34(6) withdrawal — all discarded once cross-checked, since each was found already present in origin/main with materially more precision: the agricultural-debt law as the registry-confirmed Law No. 2026-5 of March 11, 2026 vs. the discarded draft's unconfirmed description, and the photovoltaic concessions already documented as a May 13, 2026 CNRD approval of five plants with named capacities, rather than the discarded draft's less-precise April 29, 2026 ARP-vote framing). The branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.55, 241 entries, 56 merged PRs) before restarting research. A dedicated research agent then ran a fresh sweep for JORT-published legislation (none found for the window) and re-checked all twelve highest-priority pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment Code reform, Constitutional Court proposal, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, Mining/Hydrocarbons Code reform, gambling/betting bills) plus a scan for wholly new legal areas (AI regulation, e-invoicing, consumer-protection agency, arbitration reform, maritime labour, telecom spectrum) and independently re-confirmed the ONAT (July 11) and CNAM tiers-payant (July 13) watch-dates as still pending, not yet occurred. Every finding matched the artifact verbatim or with less precision than what is already documented (e.g. the e-invoicing/El Fatoora detail the agent surfaced is already captured in the Finance Law 2026 entry's Art. 56 discussion). No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (241 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 241 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1068/1068 balanced <div> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes and CSS. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the third consecutive run.
v4.57 update (July 3, 2026, Run 58): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork while origin/main had independently progressed through 57 merged runs to v4.56 — caught via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.56, 241 entries, 57 merged PRs) before starting research. A dedicated research agent ran a focused sweep (14 targeted web searches) for developments in the roughly 24 hours since Run 57's cutoff: no new JORT-published laws, decree-laws, or organic laws were found; all fifteen highest-priority pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, Investment Code reform, Water Code replacement, gambling/betting bills, Tax Adviser Organic Law, CNAM/tiers-payant, ONAT standoff, Unified Electoral Code, nationality-code revision bill, construction-regularization bill, Penal Code theft-penalty amendment, Drug Law reform) were re-confirmed unchanged, still in their previously documented committee/pre-vote stages. The private medical laboratories' tiers-payant suspension effective July 13, 2026 — the run's one actively evolving story — was independently re-surfaced but was already fully captured in this document's CNAM watchlist note from Run 57; no revision was needed. One search result claiming a July 8, 2026 court sentencing of Ennahda figures was identified as a date-logic artifact (a future date relative to this run's July 3, 2026 cutoff) and correctly discarded rather than added. No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (241 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 241 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1069/1069 balanced <div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the fourth consecutive run; the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.58 update (July 3, 2026, Run 59): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork while origin/main had independently progressed through 58 merged runs to v4.57 — caught via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.57, 241 entries, 58 merged PRs) before starting research. A dedicated research agent re-checked all 21 tracked pending-bill/watchlist items and ran a fresh news sweep for the June 28 – July 3, 2026 window: no watchlist item crossed into enacted law or a plenary vote, and every development it surfaced — the Tunis Court of Appeal's July 1, 2026 confirmation (with immediate execution ordered) of AMT president Anas Hmaidi's one-year sentence, and President Saied's relaunch of the National Commission for Criminal Reconciliation (Ali Abbès sworn in as chair, Presidential Decree No. 2026-109 and Decree No. 131/2026) — was independently cross-checked directly against this document (rather than trusted on the agent's summary alone) and found already fully captured, in equal or greater precision, in the existing Judicial Statute entry (Run 42/43/46 updates) and the Decree-Law No. 2022-13 entry (Run 46 update) respectively. A social-media rumor of a parliamentary "reward for informers" bill was investigated and confirmed false — publicly denied by ARP Security & Defense Commission president Abdessalem Hamrouni on July 1, 2026 — and is correctly not added as an entry. A targeted gap-scan of five candidate areas (standardization/metrology law, political-party-financing citation accuracy, civil-service statute, ANSI/cybersecurity-agency renaming, dedicated AI legislation) found four already fully and accurately covered (the Political Parties entry already cites the correct Decree-Law No. 2011-87 rather than any erroneous citation; the General Statute of Civil Servants — Law No. 83-112 — already has its own standalone entry; the ICT Security Law entry already documents ANSI's 2023 renaming to ANCS; no dedicated Tunisian AI statute exists, consistent with the still-pending organic bill already tracked). One genuine, independently verified gap was closed: the National Standardization System Law (Law No. 2009-38 of June 30, 2009, repealing Law No. 82-66 of August 6, 1982), confirmed via WIPO Lex, added to Intellectual Property Law as a standalone entry distinct from the existing Industrial Property Law's trademark/patent registration provisions, though both are INNORPI-administered. One new standalone entry added (242 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 242 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, all TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes and CSS. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the fifth consecutive run; the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.59 update (July 3, 2026, Run 60): This run's designated branch was, once again, the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork (this time independently rebuilt to a "v2.2" with 15 additional cards — legal-profession statutes, Fisheries Law, Cinema Industry Code, Sports Structures Law, SNCFT rail statute, tourism/gambling framework, blood-donation law, and an agricultural-investment code, plus a Development Plan 2026-2030 pending-reform card — entirely from independent research against that stale baseline) while origin/main had independently progressed through 59 merged runs to v4.58 (242 entries). The discrepancy was caught via git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD after the stale-branch draft had already been committed and pushed, but before any PR was opened — the branch was reset to current origin/main before restarting. Per established practice, the discarded draft's 15 candidate items were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep searches rather than blindly re-added: all fifteen were found already present, in every case with materially more precision and better-verified citations (e.g. the discarded draft's "Decree-Law No. 2011-79" for lawyers directly conflicts with the existing, better-sourced Law No. 87-79 entry, consistent with the same conflict already flagged and resolved in the Run 54 note above; the discarded draft's 1960 Cinema Industry Code and 1994/1995 Sports/Fisheries citations are likewise superseded by this artifact's existing, differently-sourced entries in the Tourism, Sport, and Agriculture & Fisheries sections; the Development Plan 2026-2030 card was already present nearly verbatim, as flagged as a recurring false-positive in the Run 56 note above). A dedicated research agent then ran a fresh sweep for JORT-published legislation in the June 28 – July 3, 2026 window (none found) and re-checked the two nearest-dated watchlist items (July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM tiers-payant suspension — both still pending, dates not yet reached) plus the remaining pending-bill watchlist, cross-checked directly against this document rather than trusted on the agent's summary alone. Two genuine, independently-corroborated findings survived cross-checking as not already captured: (1) UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers Margaret Satterthwaite's July 2, 2026 public statement that Anas Hmaidi's conviction "should have been overturned," amplified by the AMT's own denunciation of the July 1 ruling — added to the Judicial Statute entry; (2) a related but distinct terrorism-chamber case, opened July 3, 2026, against Ennahda secretary-general Ajmi Lourimi and co-defendant Mossaâb Gharbi (pre-trial release requests rejected June 30, 2026; charge: failure to report a terrorism-related offense under the Anti-Terrorism Law) — added to the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry as a further extension, consistent with this artifact's established preference for consolidating thematically related 2025–2026 judicial-independence developments under existing cards rather than creating thin new ones. A social-media rumor investigated by the discarded draft's research (an ARP "reward for informers" bill) was independently found to be the same debunked rumor already documented in the v4.58/Run 59 note above and was not re-added. No new standalone entries added (242 total remains accurate, since both findings extend existing cards); two existing entries (Judicial Statute, State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) revised for currency. Also fixed a recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged in the v4.13, v4.25, and v4.50 notes above): the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.57" while the header/footer read "v4.58." A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 242 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, all TOC anchors resolving, and full parity between markup .tag classes and CSS. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the sixth consecutive run; the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.60 update (July 3, 2026, Run 61): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork while origin/main had independently progressed through 60 merged runs to v4.59 (242 entries) — caught via git fetch origin/git log origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current origin/main before starting research. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh sweep (33 searches) covering all 22 tracked pending-bill/watchlist items, a JORT scan for the window since Run 60's July 3, 2026 cutoff, and a general scan for any Tunisian legal or judicial news dated after July 3. Result: no source dated later than July 3, 2026 was found — search-engine coverage simply had not yet indexed anything past the run's own cutoff — so no watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or any other status change. Several leads the agent surfaced as if new (the state-of-emergency renewal decree, Décret n° 2026-15; Sonia Dahmani's Cour de Cassation precedent and subsequent convictions; the CONECT 41-amendment detail on the Foreign Exchange Code bill; Sarra Zaafrani Zenzri's appointment as Head of Government; the Sports Structures bill's 211-article/7-chapter structure) were cross-checked directly against this document rather than trusted on the agent's summary alone, and each was found already documented, in every case with equal or greater precision and sourcing (see the State of Emergency, Decree-Law 2022-54, Foreign Exchange Code, Zaafrani Zenzri, and Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures entries respectively). No new standalone entries added and no existing entries required revision (242 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 242 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1076/1076 balanced <div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the seventh consecutive run; the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check once a future run's research cutoff actually reaches them.
v4.61 update (July 3, 2026, Run 62): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork (independently rebuilt on it as a "v2.2," adding 15+ cards entirely from scratch before any cross-check) while origin/main had independently progressed through 61 merged runs to v4.60 (242 entries) — caught via git ls-remote origin/git fetch origin main before opening a PR, and the branch was reset to current origin/main before restarting research. Per established practice, the discarded draft's candidate additions were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep searches rather than blindly re-added: the Hmaidi/UN-Special-Rapporteur update, the CONECT 41-amendment detail on the Foreign Exchange Code, and the Sports Structures bill's article/chapter structure were all found already documented (consistent with the v4.60 note above); a proposed Constitutional Court refinement ("March 27, 2026 relaunch, Bar Association hearing") could not be independently corroborated on a fresh verification search and was correctly discarded rather than asserted, leaving the existing "February 2026 hearings, still in committee" status unchanged. A targeted follow-up sweep on the discarded draft's remaining leads found two genuine, independently-corroborated gaps: (1) the Disability Law entry was missing its own 2016 amendment — Law No. 2016-41 of May 16, 2016 raised the employment quota from 1% to 2% and extended it to private enterprises (100+ employees: 2%; 50–99 employees: minimum one position), confirmed via Autonomia.org and Gnet News — and was further updated with the Finance Law 2026's Article 32, which created a CNSS-managed Fund for the Promotion of Persons with Disabilities financed by a 1% levy on accident-compensation payouts effective January 1, 2026, confirmed independently across La Presse, Espace Manager, Tunisie Tribune, and African Manager; (2) a wholly new standalone entry, Draft Organic Law No. 24/2025 on the General Framework/Status of the Health Sector (96 articles/4 titles, filed by 105 deputies, ARP Health Commission hearing May 5, 2026), addressing health-personnel staffing structure and regional care-continuity gaps — a "nascent Health Code initiative" that Run 8 (v4.7) had explicitly investigated and excluded for insufficient corroboration, now independently confirmed via La Presse de Tunisie. One new standalone entry added (243 total); one existing entry (Disability Law) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 243 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1082/1082 balanced <div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.62 update (July 3, 2026, Run 63): This run's designated branch was, once again, the stale, never-merged v1.0-baseline fork (independently rebuilt as a "v2.2" with an Agriculture/Fisheries/Veterinary section, a Sports law entry, a Tourism regulation entry, a Prisons law entry, and a "Regulatory Gaps" section, entirely from scratch before any cross-check) while origin/main had independently progressed through 62 merged runs to v4.61 (243 entries). The discrepancy was caught via git log --oneline origin/main after a PR (#71) had already been opened against the stale base and returned a "dirty" mergeable_state — the PR's branch was reset to current origin/main before restarting rather than merging a regression. Per established practice, the discarded draft's candidate additions were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep searches rather than blindly re-added: the Agriculture & Fisheries section, the Civil Aviation Code citation (Law No. 99-58 of June 29, 1999 — identical to what this run's own research had independently re-derived), the Sports Organic Law entry, the Prisons Organization Law entry (Law No. 2001-52), and the asylum/refugee-law gap note were all found already present, in every case with materially more precision than the discarded draft (e.g., the existing Fisheries Code entry already cites the 2002/2014 amendments and ICCAT tuna/swordfish commitments; the existing Nationality/Immigration section already explicitly documents the absence of a domestic asylum law across two separate entries). The discarded draft's claimed "no standing public-health-emergency law" gap was independently found to be inaccurate — a dedicated COVID-19 Enabling Law entry already exists — and was correctly not re-added. Two candidates survived cross-checking as genuinely absent and were independently re-verified via a fresh multi-source search (4+ independent Tunisian outlets each) before inclusion: (1) former MP Sahbi Smara's appeal in the "faux complot" case — a prosecution for fabricating a conspiracy to incriminate opponents ahead of the October 2024 presidential election, distinct from the main conspiracy prosecution — reduced from 11 to roughly nine and a half years on July 2, 2026, plus his separate March 2026 6-month sentence for assaulting Abir Moussi in Parliament; added as an extension to the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry; (2) journalist Zied El Heni's one-year sentence under Telecommunications Code Article 86 (arrested April 24, 2026 over a social-media post about journalist Khalifa Guesmi; sentenced May 7; confirmed on appeal June 26, 2026), corroborated across Jeune Afrique, RSF, IFJ, and the Committee for Justice; added as an extension to the Telecommunications Code entry, which had not previously documented Article 86 prosecutions at all despite the artifact's detailed coverage of the parallel Decree-Law 2022-54 press-prosecution pattern. A recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged in the v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, and v4.59 notes above) was found and fixed: the footer had been left at "Version 4.60 (Run 61)" for two full runs while the title/header read "v4.61 (Run 62)." No new standalone entries added (243 total remains accurate, since both findings extend existing cards); two existing entries (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions, Telecommunications Code) revised for currency; one version-string bug fixed. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 243 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1083/1083 balanced <div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.63 update (July 3, 2026, Run 64): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged fork (this time carrying two committed-but-unpushed "v2.0"/"v2.1" updates layered on the original v1.0 baseline) while origin/main had independently progressed through 63 merged runs to v4.62 (243 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log origin/main before any drafting began; no PR had yet been opened against the stale base, so the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh sweep for JORT-published legislation and general Tunisian legal/judicial news in the window since the Run 63 cutoff, and re-checked a set of specifically-named niche legal areas (consumer credit/leasing, insurance code, capital markets/CMF, arbitration, public procurement, telecom spectrum, party financing, civil-service statute, gender-based-violence law, habous/waqf, e-signatures) directly against the file via grep; all were confirmed already present with existing standalone entries, and no source dated later than July 3, 2026 was found (the July 11 ONAT and July 13 CNAM tiers-payant dates remain unindexed, as expected since they are still in the future relative to this run's cutoff). One genuine, independently-corroborated gap survived cross-checking as not already captured: Law No. 2025-18 of December 22, 2025 (JORT No. 2025-153, adopted by the ARP December 16, 2025, 92-4-1) creating an exceptional public-sector/civil-service recruitment mechanism for higher-education graduates in long-term unemployment — an enacted labor/civil-service statute entirely absent from the document despite 63 prior runs, confirmed via 8+ independent Tunisian outlets (Business News, 9anoun.tn, RTCI, Managers.tn, La Presse de Tunisie, L'Economiste Maghrébin, Le Temps News, Réalités Magazine, WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager, Mosaïque FM) including its unresolved implementation status (implementing decrees still unpublished as of July 2026, prompting graduate protests in Siliana on July 1 and a further protest planned in Tunis for July 8). Added as a new standalone entry in the Labor Law section. One new standalone entry added (244 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 244 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.64 update (July 3, 2026, Run 65): This run's designated branch was, yet again, the stale never-merged v1.0-baseline fork — this time independently rebuilt from scratch as "v2.2" with its own Agriculture/Fisheries/Tourism section, Sports Law entry, Cinema Code entry, and press-freedom updates, entirely unaware of origin/main's 64 merged runs to v4.63 (244 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log origin/main before any PR was opened, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Rather than blindly re-adding the discarded draft's candidates, each was individually cross-checked against the real artifact via targeted grep: the Anti-Trafficking-in-Persons Law (2016-61), CNAM (2004-71), Fisheries Law (94-13), Cinema Industry Code (60-19), Sports Law (94-104/95-11), the Zied El Heni and Khaoula Boukrim press cases, the Exchange Control Code (76-18) and its pending reform, the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, and the AI-regulation gap note were all found already present — in most cases with materially more precision and more recent sourcing than the discarded draft (e.g., the existing Decree-Law 2022-54 entry already documents Boukrim's June 2026 sentencing and Dahmani's cumulative cases in more depth). Two genuine findings survived this cross-check: (1) a wholly new standalone entry for the Public Assemblies Law (Law No. 69-4 of January 24, 1969), governing public meetings, processions, and demonstrations — distinct from and predating Decree-Law 2011-88 on associations — added to the Associations & Civil Society section; (2) a citation error in the existing Organic Budget Law entry, which had cited Law No. 2004-42 of 2004 (in fact only a 2004 amendment to the 1967 organic law) as the operative statute — corrected to Law No. 2019-15 of February 13, 2019, the law that actually replaced the 1967/2004 framework and introduced performance-based budgeting, verified against the Finance Ministry's official text. One new standalone entry added (245 total); one existing entry corrected for accuracy. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 245 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.

v4.65 update (July 3, 2026, Run 66): This run's designated branch was, once again, the stale never-merged v1.0-baseline fork — independently rebuilt from scratch as "v2.2" with 18 new candidate entries drawn from a large parallel research fan-out (human trafficking, torture prevention, extradition, HAICA status, NGO crackdown, blood donation, fisheries, referendum, telecom/5G/Starlink), entirely unaware of origin/main's 65 merged runs to v4.64 (245 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main before any PR was opened, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Every discarded-draft candidate was individually cross-checked against the real artifact via targeted grep before re-adding anything: the Anti-Trafficking-in-Persons Law (2016-61), Blood Transfusion Law (91-72), Fisheries Code (94-13), Extradition/MLA framework (CPP Arts. 308–335), the Agricultural Debt Regularization Law (2026-5), the SMIG/SMAG 2026 decrees, the civil-society-suspensions entry (LTDH/ASF), and the Draft Audiovisual Communication/HAICA-replacement bill were all found already present — in every case with materially more precision and sourcing than the discarded draft (e.g., the existing agri-debt entry already cites the BCT class-4 threshold and 15-day implementing-decree deadline the draft lacked).
