No new standalone entries were added (263 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Urban Planning Code) received a re-confirmation note. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 263 unique law-card ids (verified via a scoped match restricted to actual law-card elements, since a naive full-document id scan false-positives on the changelog's own code-quoted id references), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1193/1193 balanced <div> tags, and the header stat (263) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run. v4.97 update (July 5, 2026, Run 97): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-e5rg16) held only a stale, never-merged "v2.4" fork (six commits, 152 entries) with no PR history of its own, while origin/main had independently progressed through 96 merged runs to v4.96 (263 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log, and an empty list_pull_requests result before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. Two dedicated research passes ran: (1) a fresh WebSearch sweep of the eleven highest-priority pending bills (Exchange Code/crypto reform, Development Plan 2026-2030, Personal Data Protection organic law, Constitutional Court organic law, HAICA reorganization, Unified Electoral Code, informal-housing/urban-planning regularization bill, Sports Structures organic law, nationality-code revision, Investment Code reform, Water Code reform) — none had crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or JORT publication since Run 96's cutoff, and no new July 4–5, 2026 JORT text was found; (2) a targeted fact-check of a specific claim, surfaced by the sweep, that a Tunisian data-protection regulation was "signed July 2025" and reaches "full entry into force July 11, 2026" per the aggregator site regulations.ai. This was independently investigated and found to be fabricated aggregator noise with no primary-source support: DLA Piper's actively-maintained data-protection guide and multiple Tunisian outlets (webdo.tn, businessnews.com.tn, africanmanager.com) confirm the draft organic law remained in ARP committee hearings as of May 2026 with no plenary vote, directly contradicting the aggregator's invented JORT-publication date — correctly not added. Two July 3, 2026 court rulings re-surfaced by the sweep (Marouane Mabrouk's 14-year sentence, Taoufik Mekacher's 6-year sentence) were re-confirmed as already captured at equal precision in prior runs' entries and were not re-added. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (263 total remains accurate). A recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged at v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, v4.59, and v4.62) was found and fixed: the footer had been left at "Version 4.95 (Run 95)" for two full runs while the title/header read "v4.96 (Run 96)"; all three version markers (title tag, header meta, footer) are now synchronized at v4.97/Run 97. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 263 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1193/1193 balanced <div> tags, and the header stat (263) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run. v4.98 update (July 5, 2026, Run 98): This run's designated branch held only a stale, never-merged history (a divergent fork last touched at v2.4, five commits) with no PR of its own, while origin/main had independently progressed through 97 merged runs to v4.97 (263 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep of all 25 tracked watchlist items plus a targeted check of the three upcoming trigger dates (July 8 graduate-recruitment-decree protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension) — none has yet occurred as of this run's July 5, 2026 cutoff (all three post-date it), and no watchlist item has crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion since Run 97. The agent surfaced three leads that, on independent cross-check against the actual current artifact, were all found already fully captured rather than genuine gaps: (1) Sihem Ben Sedrine's 25-year June 26, 2026 conviction and (2) Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appeal-confirmation with immediate execution are both already documented in the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry's existing text; (3) a "land regularization" bill (~800,000–1M residents, ~1,400 informal neighborhoods) surfaced as if new is the same construction-regularization bill already tracked in the Urban Planning Code entry since Run 45, down to matching beneficiary figures. The already-known regulations.ai/dpo-consulting.com fabrication (Personal Data Protection bill "in force July 11, 2026") recurred again and was again correctly withheld, consistent with Runs 72, 76, 86, and 97. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (263 total remains accurate). A structural audit (unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, header-stat vs. actual card count, TOC-anchor resolution) found no regressions to fix — the file was already internally consistent at the point this run began. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, which should treat their actual occurrence (rather than pre-event reporting) as the primary signal that genuinely new material may finally be available.
v4.99 update (July 5, 2026, Run 99): This run's designated branch held no PR history of its own and was several runs stale (based on the project's very first commit, never touched again), while origin/main had independently progressed through 98 merged runs to v4.98 (263 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log, and an empty list_pull_requests result before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent re-checked all 25 tracked watchlist items plus the three trigger dates (July 8 graduate-recruitment protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM/biologists' suspension) — none has yet occurred as of this run's July 5, 2026 cutoff, and no watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion since Run 98; the exchange-violations amnesty bill (58/2025) was confirmed remanded to the Finance Committee in April 2026 after amendment overload, and the Investment Code reform was confirmed to have progressed to article-by-article committee voting — both refinements of already-tracked status rather than new developments. The July 3, 2026 Mabrouk/Chahed asset-unfreezing and money-laundering appeal rulings were re-surfaced and re-confirmed already fully captured, at equal precision, in the Countering Terrorism & Money Laundering entry's existing Run 81 update — not re-added. Two known false-positive traps recurred and were correctly withheld again: the regulations.ai/dpo-consulting.com "Personal Data Protection bill in force July 11, 2026" fabrication (Runs 72, 76, 86, 97, 98), and the Algeria/Tunisia "Saïd Sayoud" party-financing mislabeling (Runs 74, 84). This run closed one genuine gap entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: independently verified via direct WebFetch of primary Tunisian reporting (Businessnews, L'Économiste Maghrébin, La Presse de Tunisie, WebManagerCenter, Gnet News, allAfrica, all June 29–30, 2026) and the ILO's own 114th International Labour Conference record (letemps.news, June 28, 2026), a UGTT research-department study revealed that Tunisia's ~60,000 digital-platform ("gig-economy") workers — delivery, pharmacy-delivery, home care, and moto-transport — operate entirely outside any legal framework (98% without social coverage, 60% of delivery workers with no insurance at all), a wholly new standalone Digital Platform Workers — Regulatory Vacuum entry (Labor Law section); the same conference saw Tunisia vote in favor (406–8–36) of newly-adopted ILO Convention No. 193 on decent work in the platform economy, with Employment Minister Issam Lahmar announcing a joint ILO governance-framework study covering a considerably larger estimated 150,000–250,000 platform workers economy-wide — added as a second new standalone entry (International Law & Treaties section), since it is not yet a ratified domestic instrument. Both entries were cross-checked against the entire existing corpus first and confirmed genuinely absent — no prior run had documented gig-economy/platform-work regulation in any form. Two new standalone entries added (265 total); no existing entries required substantive revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 265 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (265) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.100 update (July 5, 2026, Run 100): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-8xd1q4) again held only a stale, never-merged fork — a five-commit "v2.5" draft built on a v2.4 baseline (152 entries) with no PR history, unaware of origin/main's 99 merged runs to v4.99 (265 entries) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. Unusually, a full PR (#114) had already been opened against the stale base; it returned a "dirty" mergeable_state on inspection (627 lines of diff against a 3,462-line current file, vs. a 30-line actual local diff), which caught the discrepancy. Rather than merge a regression, the stale draft's five candidates were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep/direct-quote comparison before anything was re-added: a "2026 Press Crackdown Beyond Decree-Law 54" case entry (Zghidi/Bsaies, Dahmani, Ben Khelifa) was found already captured, in materially more depth and with more precise dates, in the existing Decree-Law 2022-54 entry; the State of Emergency extension (Décret n° 2026-15 of January 30, 2026, to December 31, 2026) was found already quoted near-verbatim in the existing State of Emergency Decree entry; the 2026 Finance Law's e-invoicing expansion (Art. 56) was found already documented in the Finance Law 2026 entry; the Associations Decree (2011-88) foreign-funding amendment was found already tracked, with finer committee-session detail, in the Future Expansion watchlist and referenced from the Associations Decree entry; and the Stadium Violence Bill (Proposition de loi n° 83/2025) was found already present as its own standalone pending-bill entry, added back in Run 18. The branch was reset directly to current origin/main, and a fresh research pass was run instead: a general JORT/ARP sweep for July 4–5, 2026 publications (none found), a re-check of the Foreign Exchange Code's committee-vote timeline (unchanged since its June 17–19, 2026 near-verbatim status), and two candidate leads surfaced by fresh search — a "new" ARP maternity/paternity leave law and a "new" agricultural-drone import authorization — were investigated via direct WebFetch/date-check and found to be, respectively, the already-documented August 2024 Law No. 2024-44 (tracked since Run 10) and a provision already quoted within the existing Finance Law 2026 entry's text, not new legislation. No watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion, and no genuinely new standalone gap was identified this run. No new standalone entries were added (265 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 265 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (265) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.101 update (July 5, 2026, Run 101): This run's designated branch held only a stale fork (v2.4-era baseline) with no PR history, unaware of origin/main's 100 merged runs to v4.100 (265 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began. A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty-five tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Investment Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, HAICA/Audiovisual Communication bill, Water Code, nationality-code revision, Unified Electoral Code, CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension, Constitutional Court organic law, gambling reform, and the rest) against a fresh WebSearch sweep: none has crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, JORT publication, or formal abandonment since Run 100's same-day cutoff, and the July 8 graduate-recruitment protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, and July 13 CNAM/biologists' suspension were all independently re-confirmed as still scheduled (all three still post-date this run's July 5, 2026 cutoff). This run closed one genuine gap that had eluded all 100 prior runs: the "affaire de l'appareil secret" (Ennahda "Secret Apparatus") case, a first-instance verdict rendered June 2, 2026 by the Tunis Court of First Instance's specialized anti-terrorism criminal chamber — wholly distinct from the already-documented Case 1/Case 2 "state security conspiracy" prosecutions (different docket, opened 2022 over the 2013 Belaïd/Brahmi assassinations, charged under the Anti-Terrorism Law rather than Penal Code Arts. 72–85), with markedly heavier sentences (life imprisonment plus 30 years for Rached Ghannouchi; life plus 96 years for alleged mastermind Mustapha Khedher; 42 years for Ali Laarayedh). Independently corroborated across Al Jazeera, Bloomberg, La Presse de Tunisie, businessnews.com.tn, Le Courrier de l'Atlas, Kapitalis, and Middle East Eye/Middle East Monitor before being added as a substantial update to the existing "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry (consistent with this project's established practice of using that card as the umbrella for related-but-distinct Ennahda-leadership prosecution developments) rather than creating a duplicate entry. Two leads were investigated and correctly ruled out: the regulations.ai/dpo-consulting.com "Personal Data Protection bill in force July 11, 2026" fabrication recurred yet again (consistent with Runs 72, 76, 86, 97, 98, 99) and was withheld; and a "July 8" mass-conviction search result naming Ghannouchi (12–35 years) was identified as a stale-dated hit actually referring to the July 8, 2025 Case 2 verdict, not a new 2026 development. No new standalone entries were added (265 total remains accurate, since the finding was folded into an existing card); one existing entry substantially revised. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 265 unique law-card ids, balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (265) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.102 update (July 5, 2026, Run 102): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-9prj33) again carried no PR history and was based on a stale v2.4-era fork (152 entries), unaware of origin/main's 101 merged runs to v4.101 (265 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log, and an empty list_pull_requests/search_pull_requests result before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily remained disabled, HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with Runs 68, 71, 76, 86, 96–101) re-checked the highest-priority watchlist items — the Foreign Exchange Code (confirmed still short of a committee vote, with Ktari's April-package "vote article-by-article June 20–30 or proceed without the BCT" plan superseded, as already noted in the existing entry, by his more cautious June 23 "BCT consultation essential" stance), the Personal Data Protection/AI organic bill (132-article structure and stalled committee status re-confirmed, including the committee's request to widen expert/civil-society consultation, already captured in the existing entry), the 2026–2030 Development Plan (the June 30, 2026 joint-commission audition of Minister Samir Abdelhafidh and deputies' financing-transparency reservations re-confirmed, already captured), and the three trigger dates — the July 8 graduate-recruitment protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, and July 13 CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension (the biologists' union's June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly decision and its scope — chronic-condition and ordinary-illness patients alike must pay upfront and seek reimbursement, with family-doctor-scheme patients unaffected — was independently re-verified and found already fully captured at equal precision) — none has yet occurred as of this run's July 5, 2026 cutoff. A secondary sweep for newly JORT-published legislation and for four small candidate leads (a Tataouine municipal-waste-company unpaid-wages labor dispute; four minor ARP committee referrals for STEG/World Bank loan-guarantee and Tunisia–Oman/Tunisia–Kuwait air-services bilateral agreements) found the former to be an ordinary labor grievance with no distinct legislative text to anchor a standalone entry (consistent with the Run 88/93 precedent for withholding labor unrest without an accompanying bill), and the latter to be routine, not-yet-enacted procedural ratifications below this project's inclusion threshold — both correctly left undocumented rather than added as filler. No watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, JORT publication, or formal abandonment since Run 101's same-day cutoff, and no genuinely new standalone gap was identified this run. No new standalone entries were added (265 total remains accurate); no existing entries required substantive revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 265 unique law-card ids, balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (265) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.103 update (July 5, 2026, Run 103): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-tgdbt4) again held only a stale, never-merged fork — a five-commit "v2.4" line (152 entries) with a PR (#108) that had already been merged long ago, entirely unaware of origin/main's 102 subsequent merged runs to v4.102 (265 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main and git merge-base before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent (WebSearch- and Tavily-based) re-checked the watchlist and searched for July 4–5, 2026 JORT/ARP developments; no watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion, and one search hit — a "Cour constitutionnelle" ruling dated July 4, 2026 — was correctly identified and discarded as a Moroccan (Médias24) story mislabeled by country, a new instance of the cross-country-mislabeling trap flagged in Runs 74/84. The agent's ten suspected coverage-gap candidates (Organic Budget Law, Social & Solidarity Economy Law, Tourism Law, Sports Law, Exchange Control Law, Leasing Law, Crowdfunding Law, a "Code Rural," Vocational Training Law, and the Public Accounting Code) were cross-checked directly against the live artifact via targeted grep/Read before any edit: eight were found already accurately captured (Organic Budget Law 2019-15, Social & Solidarity Economy Law 2020-30 with the correct June 30, 2020 date, the 2012 Tourism Organization Law, the 1995 Sport Law, the 1976 Exchange Control Law, the 1994 Leasing Law, the 1993 Vocational Training Law, and the confirmed non-existence of any unified "Code Rural") — none were re-added or duplicated. One was a genuine citation error: the existing Crowdfunding entry's "Decree-Law No. 2020-170 of November 10, 2020" could not be verified against any primary or secondary source and was corrected to the actual statute, Law No. 2020-37 of August 6, 2020 (adopted by the ARP 127–0–0 on July 21, 2020), with its long-delayed October 19, 2022 implementing decrees (Nos. 2022-765 & 2022-766) added; an unverifiable pair of funding-cap figures in the prior text was dropped rather than carried forward unverified. The Public Accounting Code (Law No. 73-81 of December 31, 1973) was independently confirmed to exist but, on balance, judged not to warrant a new standalone entry this run given the artifact's existing Public Finance coverage of budget law, the Court of Accounts, and public debt — flagged here for a future run to add if a citable 2026 development arises. Beyond the gap list, this run closed one genuine, independently corroborated case entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: a wholly new standalone Affaire Taïeb Rached — "Cassations Sans Renvoi" entry (Judicial Organization section) documenting the October 27–28, 2025 first-instance conviction (30/20/20 years for Rached, Bahouri, and Tlili respectively) and May 23, 2026 appeal ruling (all three reduced to 10 years, ~935 million TND in confirmed civil damages) for a scheme in which the sitting First President of the Court of Cassation created two unauthorized chambers in 2019 to issue cassation-without-remand rulings for two businessmen — the first prosecution of a judge of this rank in Tunisian history, missed by all 102 prior runs. Two existing entries were refined with newer, independently verified detail: the Court of Accounts entry gained the Court's January 9, 2026 32nd annual report (TND 1.07 billion in cumulative losses across eleven public entities, led by Tunisair and the Office of Maritime Commerce & Ports, prompting a presidential demand for prosecutions); and the Entry and Residence of Foreigners entry gained a July 2, 2026 Amnesty International report quantifying, for the first time in this artifact, the June 2024 UNHCR-registration suspension's effect (registered refugees falling from 18,362 to 7,515 by April 2026) and documenting a government "organized voluntary return" migrant program running since June 2025. One new standalone entry added (266 total); three existing entries revised for currency, one of which corrected a fabricated citation. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 266 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (266) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.104 update (July 5, 2026, Run 104): This run's designated branch held only a stale, never-merged fork (a v2.4-era baseline, 152 entries) with a PR (#108) already merged long ago, unaware of origin/main's 103 subsequent merged runs to v4.103 (266 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. Because Run 103 had completed only about fifty minutes earlier the same day, this run's research swept narrowly for JORT/ARP developments in that window and re-checked the highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Constitutional Court organic law, Development Plan) rather than re-running a full broad gap scan. No watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's status is unchanged since its June 17–23, 2026 reporting (a next committee meeting to decide between an article-by-article vote without the Central Bank or a further delay was reported as scheduled for "Monday" — i.e. July 6, 2026, still in the future as of this run); the Constitutional Court proposal (n°100/2025) remains at the same committee stage documented since Run 76. One candidate lead was investigated and correctly withheld as not a new legislative development: national press reported that, since July 1–2, 2026, the National Register of Enterprises (RNE) has made online filing via Mobile ID ("e-Houwiya") or a DigiGo certificate mandatory, ending paper-based commercial-register procedures — independently verified across Managers.tn, La Presse de Tunisie, allAfrica, and African Manager as an operational/administrative milestone with no accompanying law or decree number, so it was added as a refinement to the existing Commercial Register Law entry rather than as a standalone item or new gap. A further check of recent political/judicial news (the December 2025 Abir Moussi sentencing and Ahmed Nejib Chebbi/Chaima Issa/Ayachi Hammami arrests) found these already predate, and are already substantively captured within the precision of, the existing consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions and civil-society entries. No new standalone entries were added (266 total remains accurate); one existing entry revised for currency. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.105 update (July 5, 2026, Run 105): This run's designated branch again held only a stale, never-merged fork (a v2.4-era baseline, 152 entries) unaware of origin/main's 104 subsequent merged runs to v4.104 (266 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. Because Run 104 had completed only shortly before this run on the same day, a dedicated research agent searched narrowly for JORT/ARP developments since that cutoff and re-checked the highest-priority watchlist items rather than re-running a full broad gap scan. No JORT-published legislation was found dated July 4–5, 2026 or later — a Decree No. 136/2026 (July 1, 2026) naming Raja Yassine Bahri to head Beït al-Hikma surfaced but is an appointment decree, not artifact-relevant. No watchlist item crossed into a plenary vote, enactment, or committee-report conclusion: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's "Monday" (July 6, 2026) meeting to decide between an article-by-article vote without the Central Bank or a further delay had, as expected, not yet occurred or been reported as of this run's cutoff — the single highest-priority item to check next run. The Investment Code reform bill, 2026–2030 Development Plan, and CNAM/private-biologists' July 13 tiers-payant suspension were all re-confirmed unchanged from their Run 104 status. A targeted structural gap-scan of nine additional regulatory areas (consumer credit/microfinance, Competition Council, INPDP's own statute, ANSI/cybersecurity, Code des Collectivités Locales, general Consumer Protection Law, e-signature/e-transactions law, Arbitration Code, Insurance Code) found each already accurately covered by an existing dedicated entry, with no uncaptured 2025–2026 amendment for any of them. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (266 total remains accurate) — this run's contribution is confirming the artifact's continued currency rather than closing any gap. The July 6 (Foreign Exchange Code vote), July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT general assembly), and July 13, 2026 (CNAM biologists' suspension) dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.106 update (July 5, 2026, Run 106): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-4991ll) again held only a stale, never-merged fork — a five-commit "v2.4" line (152 entries) with a PR (#108) that had already been merged long ago, entirely unaware of origin/main's 105 subsequent merged runs to v4.105 (266 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A WebSearch-based research sweep re-checked the highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code committee, Development Plan bill, Personal Data Protection/AI organic bill) and found none had moved beyond their Run 105 status as of this run's cutoff — no JORT-published legislation, committee vote, or plenary passage for any of them; the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6, 2026 "Monday" meeting had, as expected, not yet occurred. It closed one genuine gap entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: a wholly new standalone Passports & Travel Documents Law entry (Law No. 75-40 of May 14, 1975, as amended by Organic Laws No. 2015-46 and 2024-23), documenting Articles 38/39/41's border-crossing offenses and their use — per a July 2026 Inter Press Service report cross-verified against Human Rights Watch (May 11, 2026) and BusinessNews Tunisie — to prosecute Tunisian Council for Refugees founder Mustapha Djemali and project manager Abderrazek Krimi (convicted November 24, 2025; upheld on appeal May 14, 2026) for their UNHCR-mandated refugee-assistance work — a case entirely absent from all 105 prior runs despite this project's otherwise thorough documentation of 2024–2026 civil-society and refugee-policy prosecutions — and cross-referenced it in both directions with the general Entry and Residence of Foreigners entry. Other leads surfaced by this run's research (the January 2026 Bssais/Zeghidi money-laundering sentences, the Saadia Mosbah appeal, the 91-article Foreign Exchange Code's committee status, the Personal Data Protection bill's 132-article/six-chapter structure) were each independently checked and found already captured at equal or greater precision by existing entries. One new standalone entry added (267 total); one existing entry (Entry and Residence of Foreigners) refined with a cross-reference. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 267 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (267) matching the true card count. The July 6 (Foreign Exchange Code vote), July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT general assembly), and July 13, 2026 (CNAM biologists' suspension) dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.107 update (July 5, 2026, Run 107): This run began from the correct current origin/main (v4.106, 267 entries, 106 merged runs) rather than a stale local branch, confirmed via git fetch and git log before drafting. A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty-one highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill, Personal Data Protection/AI organic bill, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, Investment Code, Water Code, Hydrocarbons Code, Environment Code, Constitutional Court organic law, nationality-code revision, unemployed-graduates protest, ONAT general assembly, CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension, exchange-amnesty bill, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, Drug Law reform, construction-regularization bill, Renewable Energies Code, and gig-economy/ILO Convention 193) plus a fresh structural scan for any newly JORT-published legislation since Run 106's cutoff (Tavily remained disabled with HTTP 402 "account currently disabled," so this run again relied on WebSearch alone, consistent with Runs 68, 71, 76, 86, and 106). Every lead the research surfaced — including the Tunis Court of Appeal's July 1–2, 2026 confirmation of judge Anas Hmaidi's sentence and the same week's reduction of Sahbi Smara's "faux complot" sentence to nine and a half years, the Investment Code's article-by-article committee voting, the Development Plan's 101–102 billion TND/21,100-project figures, and the private biologists' union's June 30, 2026 vote to proceed with the July 13 tiers-payant suspension — was independently cross-checked directly against this document's existing Judicial Statute, State Security Conspiracy, Investment Code, Development Plan, and CNAM entries and found already captured at equal or greater precision in each case (several by name and exact date). The recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing-boilerplate false positive (flagged since Run 72) was encountered and correctly discarded again. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (267 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. None of the four highest-priority trigger dates (July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension) had yet arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff; they remain the primary signals for the next run to check first, in date order.
v4.108 update (July 5, 2026, Run 108): This run began from current origin/main (v4.107, 267 entries, 107 merged runs) — the branch had no PR history of its own, so it was reset directly rather than continuing a stale fork. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep (24+ searches) re-checking all four highest-priority trigger dates and the full watchlist. None of the four trigger dates had yet occurred as of this run's cutoff: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6 meeting, the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, and the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension all remain scheduled but not yet reported on. The Development Plan bill advanced procedurally — all permanent ARP committees completed review around July 3, 2026 and a synthesis report is now being compiled before plenary scheduling — but this was already captured in the existing Development Plan entry. Every other lead this run's research surfaced (the Investment Code's continuing article-by-article committee hearings and planned Parliamentary Academy study day; the Hmaidi, Smara, Mabrouk/Chahed/Ben Gharbia, Turki, and Mekacher July 1–3, 2026 appellate rulings) was independently cross-checked against the existing State Security Conspiracy, Anti-Money Laundering/Countering Terrorism, and financial-crime-cluster entries and found already captured at equal or greater precision in every case — including the Ben Gharbia acquittal-on-appeal detail and the two distinct Mabrouk dockets (the 6-year asset-unfreezing case and the separately-confirmed 14-year embezzlement/money-laundering case). A single-source claim resurfacing the "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing-boilerplate false positive (flagged since Run 72) was again correctly discarded, as was a stale July 2025 "21 condamnations" state-security verdict that search results had mis-dated as current. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (267 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 267 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1218/1218 balanced <div> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving to a matching id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — the next run should check whether any of them has occurred first before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.109 update (July 5, 2026, Run 109): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-b3qadh) held only a stale, never-merged five-commit "v2.4" fork (152 entries, self-labeled up to a PR "#108" from an entirely different, unrelated numbering sequence) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A first draft (adding a stadium-violence bill and a plastic-bag bill as if new) was committed and pushed on that stale base before the GitHub merge-conflict check surfaced the divergence; per established practice, the branch was then reset directly to current origin/main (v4.108, 267 entries, 108 merged runs) and the draft was re-verified from scratch against the live document rather than assumed correct. That re-check found the stadium-violence bill already fully captured, at equal or greater precision (the 5–10 year weapons-offense tier and stadium-ban durations this run's draft lacked), by the existing Proposition de loi n° 83/2025 entry (Sport section) — so it was discarded rather than duplicated. Two genuinely new items survived independent re-verification: (1) a wholly new standalone Draft Law on Reducing Single-Use Plastic Bag Consumption entry (Environmental Law section), a proposition de loi reported July 5, 2026 — the same day as this run's cutoff — with no prior mention anywhere in 108 prior runs; and (2) a refinement to the Finance Law 2026 entry adding its Article 83 provisions for Tunisians residing abroad (TRE) — customs-duty-free electric/hybrid vehicle imports, a once-per-five-years equipment-import benefit for TRE investment projects, and exemption from Tunisian tax-return filing for TRE holding a foreign tax-residency certificate — cross-referenced from the existing National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad entry (Nationality section), which had documented that Council's own reform bill but not this separate, already-enacted tax benefit. Both were corroborated across at least two independent sources (Managers.tn, Tunisie Numérique, La Presse de Tunisie, irbe7.com, InFirst Auditors, and La Presse's July 5 plastic-bag report) before inclusion. One new standalone entry added (268 total; the discarded stadium-violence draft was never merged and does not count); one existing entry (Finance Law 2026) refined; one existing entry (National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad) cross-referenced. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 268 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving to a matching id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain unarrived as of this run's cutoff and stay the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside checking whether the new plastic-bag bill has been referred to committee.
v4.110 update (July 5, 2026, Run 110): This run began directly from current origin/main (v4.109, 268 entries, 109 merged runs) rather than a stale fork. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep (26 searches) re-checking all four highest-priority trigger dates, the plastic-bag bill's committee-referral status, and the full pending-bill watchlist. Because this run's cutoff falls on the same calendar day as Run 109's, no trigger date has yet occurred: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6 meeting, the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, and the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension all remain scheduled but unreported. The new plastic-bag bill (Proposition de loi n° 33/2026 — number now confirmed) already had a committee hearing with its initiating deputies on June 25, 2026, predating its July 5 press coverage; no further referral news has followed. Every other watchlist item (Investment, Water, Environment, Hydrocarbons, and Renewable Energy codes still unfiled with the ARP per the IACE tracker; the Development Plan awaiting its synthesis report; the Personal Data Protection/AI, Associations, HAICA/audiovisual, nationality-code, Unified Electoral Code, Constitutional Court, Tax Adviser, gambling, exchange-amnesty, banque postale, financial-exclusion, cinema, Sports Structures, Penal Code theft-penalty, and Drug Law 92-52 bills) remains in the same committee/hearing stage as of Run 109, with no enactment, plenary vote, or committee-report conclusion found. A recurring single-source claim that Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026 was again encountered on a marketing-style site and correctly withheld as the same unreliable false positive flagged since Run 72. No new JORT-published legislation, court ruling, prosecution, institution, or treaty ratification was found. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (268 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit found no regressions: 268 unique law-card entries, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1224/1224 balanced <div> tags, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.111 update (July 5, 2026, Run 111): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-w61ma4) held only a stale five-commit "v2.4" fork (152 entries) already merged into origin/main long ago — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog — so it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.110, 268 entries, 110 merged runs) before drafting. A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty-five watchlist items via a fresh WebSearch sweep and confirmed none has crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome since Run 110's same-day cutoff; the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6 meeting, the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, and the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension all remain scheduled but unarrived. Two candidate leads were investigated and correctly discarded rather than added: an "Investment Code article-by-article voting underway" claim conflicts with the IACE tracker's "validated but never transmitted to the ARP" classification and was left unresolved rather than picking one account, and a French-language hit for a Tunisian cinema-industry law was identified as an Algeria/JORF mislabeling (the Conseil de la Nation, not Tunisia's ARP) — the same cross-country trap flagged since Run 74. The recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing false positive (flagged since Run 72) was again encountered and again correctly withheld. One genuine narrow gap was closed outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Electronic Procedures in Administrative Transactions entry (Law No. 2016-46) was refined to note a "zero paper" administrative-digitalization measure that took effect July 1, 2026, extending electronic authentication and paperless processing to further categories of state transactions — previously undocumented despite this project's long-standing e-government and e-signature coverage. No new standalone entries were added (268 total remains accurate); one existing entry refined. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 268 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1225/1225 balanced <div> tags, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.112 update (July 5, 2026, Run 112): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-0fu9aj) held only a stale five-commit "v2.4" fork (87 entries) from the project's early history, already long superseded on origin/main — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog — so it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.111, 268 entries, 111 merged runs) before drafting. A dedicated research agent re-checked the watchlist via a fresh WebSearch sweep (JORT's own index shows no July 2026 issues published yet) and confirmed no bill has crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome since Run 111's same-day cutoff; the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6 meeting, the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, and the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension all remain scheduled but unarrived, each independently re-corroborated as still-future dates rather than already occurred or moved earlier. Two false positives were investigated and correctly discarded: the recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" regulations.ai/dpo-consulting.com marketing claim (flagged since Run 72), and a new database-timestamp trap — a third-party legal-database PDF showing a "2026-07-01" edit date on the Forestry Code that traced to a site republish timestamp rather than any actual JORT promulgation or Council of Ministers adoption of a new forest code. A targeted check of the Mining Code and Forestry Code (surfaced by this run's research as possible coverage gaps) confirmed both are already documented in their respective sections; the ILO Convention No. 193/digital-platform-worker entries added in Run 99 were also re-confirmed as already fully capturing Tunisia's June 28, 2026 ILC vote in favor, with no domestic ratification bill filed since. One genuine narrow refinement was made outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Forestry Code entry now notes FTDES's January 2026 report calling for a "Forests and Climate" chapter to unlock REDD+ climate financing — a live, low-profile policy-advocacy debate around the still-unreformed 1988 code, distinct from (and not to be confused with) the discarded timestamp artifact above. No new standalone entries were added (268 total remains accurate); one existing entry refined. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.113 update (July 5, 2026, Run 113): This run began directly from current origin/main (v4.112, 268 entries, 112 merged runs) — the designated branch had no PR of its own and was reset rather than continuing a stale fork, per established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep (~20:30 UTC cutoff, roughly two hours after Run 112's) re-checking all four trigger dates and the full pending-bill watchlist, plus a broader gap-scan of eight regulatory areas not previously named in the watchlist (veterinary/animal health, maritime labor/seafarers, e-waste/DEEE, cooperative/social housing, urban transport/taxi regulation, data localization, public-health-emergency law, and telecom beyond ICT). None of the four trigger dates (July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension) has yet occurred, and no watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion since Run 112's cutoff. The July 3, 2026 Mabrouk and Turki appellate rulings resurfaced again and were re-confirmed already fully captured. The gap-scan found no citable, dedicated Tunisian statute in any of the eight areas checked that is not already covered by an existing entry or that rises above fragmented administrative-decree level (e.g., e-waste remains governed only by the general 2000 waste-classification decree, with a dedicated DEEE framework still at a pre-legislative PPP feasibility-study stage; cooperative housing remains split across the existing FOPROLOS/SPROLS entries with no unified code). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (268 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit found no regressions: 268 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1227/1227 balanced <div> tags, the header stat (268) matching the true card count, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.

v4.114 update (July 5, 2026, Run 114): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-5tv533) again held only a stale, never-merged five-commit "v2.5" fork (roughly 60 entries, PR'd only against another feature branch) from the project's early history — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog — so it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.113, 268 entries, 113 merged runs) before any drafting. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep at ~21:00 UTC, roughly 30 minutes after Run 113's cutoff (Tavily search was unavailable this run — HTTP 402 billing error — so WebSearch alone was used, with good coverage), re-checking all four trigger dates, the full pending-bill watchlist, and a broad "any brand-new Tunisian law/decree published this week" sweep. No JORT issue for July 5, 2026 was found; none of the four trigger dates (July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension) has occurred; and no watchlist item (Foreign Exchange Code, Renewable Energy Code, Investment Code, Personal Data Protection Organic Law, Penal Code theft/robbery amendment, Drug Law 92-52 reform, construction-regularization bill, digital-platform/gig-economy regulation, ILO Convention No. 193 domestic ratification) crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion since Run 113's cutoff. The construction-regularization bill's June 2026 committee-level approval (already documented in the Run 46 watchlist note) resurfaced but remains at committee stage, not final promulgation. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (268 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit found no regressions: 268 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1228/1228 balanced <div> tags, the header stat (268) matching the true card count, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id with none orphaned. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.

v4.116 update (July 5, 2026, Run 116): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-739ovl) held only a stale, five-commit "v2.5" fork from the project's early history (PR #128, merged against another feature branch rather than main, ~60 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog — so it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.115, 269 entries, 115 merged runs) before drafting. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh web sweep re-checking all four trigger dates and the full pending-bill watchlist, plus a targeted gap scan across insurance, veterinary, e-waste, maritime-labor, cybersecurity, and arbitration regulation. No source dated later than July 5, 2026 was found anywhere in the sweep. None of the four trigger dates (July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension) has yet occurred, and no watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion since Run 115's cutoff — the Investment Code's article-by-article committee-vote stage (traced to the same May 31, 2026 report already cited in the standalone entry), the Foreign Exchange Code's CONECT 41-amendment detail, the biologists' union's confirmed June 30, 2026 suspension vote, and the July 8 graduate-protest planning were each independently re-surfaced and verified already captured at equal or greater precision by existing entries. The recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing false positive was again encountered and again correctly withheld. The gap scan found nothing citable beyond what existing entries already cover (Waste Law 96-41, Veterinary Law 78-23, Maritime Labor Code as amended by Law 95-56, cybersecurity Decree-Law 2023-17, Competition Law 2015-36) — a pure confirmation run. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (269 total remains accurate). A structural audit found no regressions: 269 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1232/1232 balanced <div> tags, the header stat (269) matching the true card count, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id with none orphaned. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.115 update (July 5, 2026, Run 115): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-lzsfkc) held no PR of its own — it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.114, 268 entries, 114 merged runs) before drafting, per established practice. A fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with prior runs) re-confirmed none of the four trigger dates (July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension) has yet occurred, and no watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion since Run 114's cutoff; the recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing false positive was again encountered and again correctly withheld. This run closed one genuine gap entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: a wholly new standalone Proposition de loi n° 30/2026 entry (Social Security & Protection section) documenting a ~40-deputy bill, examined by the ARP's Health/Social Affairs Commission across sessions on May 8, 20, and 25, 2026, that would end the cumul-emploi-retraite ban and grant retirees transport/tourism tariff reductions via a "Carte loisirs du retraité" — missed by all 114 prior runs despite being distinct from the two pension bills already tracked on the CNSS entry. A cross-reference was added to the CNSS entry, and a related but too-thinly-sourced sign-language/hearing-impairment bill (examined the same May 20, 2026 session) was noted within the new entry rather than given its own standalone card, since no bill number or article count could be located. This run also flags, rather than resolves, a genuine figure discrepancy in otherwise-corroborating La Presse de Tunisie coverage of the new bill's own tariff percentages (30% vs. 40% transport; 40% vs. "40–50%" tourism) — a live instance of the project's standing rule to record unresolved primary-source conflicts rather than silently pick one figure. 269 total now accurate (268 → 269). A structural audit found no regressions: 269 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id. The July 6, 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order — check whether any has actually occurred before re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.117 update (July 6, 2026, Run 117): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-wskrro) reproduced the exact recurring failure mode this changelog has now documented in dozens of prior notes: its own initial checkout held only a stale, seven-commit "v2.5"→"v2.6" fork from the project's early history (base commit merged as PR #128, ~157 entries, independently built up to a self-drafted "164 entries" before any cross-check against origin/main), and a full round of "new" candidate additions — a National Identity Card Law entry, a National Statistical System Law entry, an Archives Law entry, a Tourism/commercial-deregulation Law No. 2004-75 entry, a Livestock & Veterinary Public Health Law entry, a Sports Structures Organic Law entry, and a single-use-plastic-bags pending-bill entry — was drafted from independent research before git fetch origin main and git log origin/main were run. Doing so surfaced the true state: origin/main had independently progressed through 116 merged runs to v4.116 (269 entries), and every one of the seven draft candidates was found already present — the National Identity Card Law (as national-id-card), National Statistical System Law (as statistics-system-law), Archives Law (as libraries-archives), the Law No. 2004-75 tourism/commercial deregulation text (folded into tourism-code), the veterinary framework (as agricultural-land/organic-agriculture/agricultural-cooperatives plus a dedicated veterinary-law citation already confirmed clean in the Run 116 note above), the Sports Structures Organic Law No. 95-11 (already the Sport & Youth section's base entry, with its pending replacement bill separately tracked), and the plastic-bags bill (already added in Run 109, one run before this document's own independent "discovery" of the July 5, 2026 La Presse report on the same bill). The stale draft was discarded entirely rather than merged, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any further edits, consistent with established practice (see the v4.28, v4.49, v4.62, v4.80, v4.95, v4.97, v4.114, and v4.116 notes for the same recurring pattern). With the branch corrected, a dedicated research agent then ran a narrow, targeted sweep on this run's own actual highest-priority open trigger — the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code (Code des Changes) parliamentary committee meeting flagged as "the next run's first check" by the v4.105 and subsequent notes. No source confirms a committee session or vote outcome dated specifically July 6, 2026; the most recent corroborated status remains committee chair Maher Ktari's ~90%-complete hearing count as of June 17, 2026, with only the Central Bank's response still outstanding and an internally-targeted (not confirmed) article-by-article vote window of June 20–30, 2026 — meaning July 6 may already have quietly passed without a reported outcome, or the committee's own internal timeline has itself slipped past its stated target; this run could not distinguish between the two from public sources and flags both possibilities for the next run rather than guessing. A parallel broad sweep found no JORT publication, ARP plenary vote, presidential decree, or major court ruling dated July 6, 2026 specifically, and reconfirmed the July 8, 2026 graduate/unemployment protest remains planned and unrescheduled. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (269 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run, once the stale-branch detour is set aside. A structural audit found no regressions: 269 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1232/1232 balanced <div> tags, the header stat (269) matching the true card count, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving to a matching section id with none orphaned. This note also fixes a pre-existing, unrelated changelog-ordering slip: the v4.116 note-box had been inserted immediately after the v4.114 note-box rather than after the v4.115 note-box that chronologically precedes it (both remain intact and unedited above; only their relative order was affected). The July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome, the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT assembly, and the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run — the next run should specifically search for any post-July-6 reporting on the Code des Changes committee before re-running the broader exhausted pending-bill sweep.
v4.118 update (July 6, 2026, Run 118): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-3wybtt) reproduced the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog: its initial checkout held only a stale, eight-commit "v2.5"→"v2.6" fork (base commit merged as PR #128, ~157 entries, independently built up to a self-drafted "160 entries" — three candidate labor/financial-law additions drafted from scratch) before git fetch origin main/git log origin/main were run. Doing so surfaced origin/main's true state — 117 merged runs to v4.117 (269 entries) — and all three draft candidates (the Law No. 2025-9 employment-contracts/subcontracting reform, Law No. 2025-18 long-term-unemployed-graduates recruitment law, and Law No. 2026-5 agricultural-debt regularization law) were confirmed already present, each with materially more precision and update history than the discarded draft (e.g., the existing 2025-18 entry already documents the July 1 Siliana protest and July 8 Tunis protest with 60,000/137,000–140,000-graduate figures the draft lacked entirely). The stale draft was discarded and the branch reset to current origin/main per established practice. With the branch corrected, a dedicated research sweep targeted this run's own top open trigger per the v4.117 note — post-July-6 reporting on the Foreign Exchange Code committee — and found nothing dated specifically July 6, 2026 confirming a session occurred or its outcome; the last hard data point remains unchanged (Ktari's ~90%-complete hearing count, June 17, 2026). A broader sweep, however, surfaced one genuinely new, well-corroborated finding entirely outside the four dated triggers: ARP Finance and Budget Commission president Maher Ktari stated on radio June 9, 2026 that recourse to a supplementary/"rectificative" finance law in September–October 2026 is now "inevitable," driven by an oil-price shock — the enacted Finance Law 2026's reference Brent price of USD 63.3/barrel versus actual prices approaching USD 110/barrel, a gap costing roughly TND 160 million per additional dollar and an estimated TND 5–8 billion year-end financing shortfall — corroborated across La Presse/allAfrica, Business News, Kapitalis, Tustex, and webdo, with a Finance Ministry meeting on the discrepancy scheduled for July 2026; no amending bill text has been filed with the ARP as of this run, so it is recorded as a pending-amendment note on the existing Finance Law 2026 entry rather than a standalone card. No new standalone entries were added (269 total remains accurate, since the finding extends an existing card); one existing entry (Finance Law 2026) substantively revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 269 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1234/1234 balanced <div> tags, and the header stat (269) matching the true card count. The July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome (still unconfirmed either way), the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT assembly, the July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension, and the newly-flagged September–October 2026 supplementary finance law all remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.119 update (July 6, 2026, Run 119): This run's designated branch held only a stale, seven-commit "v2.5" fork from the project's early history (PR #128, ~60 entries) rather than current main — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog — so it was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.118, 269 entries, 118 merged runs) before drafting, with no draft content carried over. A dedicated research agent then ran a fresh sweep specifically targeting the five highest-priority open triggers named in the v4.118 note, since this run's cutoff falls on the same calendar day as Run 118's. (1) Foreign Exchange Code committee: no source confirms an article-by-article vote or session outcome specifically dated July 6, 2026; a June 23, 2026 Business News piece shows the commission was still actively working the file (alongside two unrelated STEG financing-agreement items, nos. 38/2026 and 39/2026) as of that date, consistent with — not beyond — the June 17 status already in the entry. (2) The July 8, 2026 graduate/unemployment protest remains planned, not yet occurred, with protesters' core demand (publication of Law No. 2025-18's implementing decrees and a digital recruitment calendar) unchanged. (3) The July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly could not be independently re-corroborated by this run's search (only May 2026 ONAT events surfaced), so it is flagged here as awaiting fresh confirmation rather than treated as newly verified or retracted — the existing entry's Run 46 sourcing is left unchanged. (4) The July 13, 2026 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension remains well-corroborated as scheduled (Syndicat Tunisien des Biologistes Privés, 7+ months of unpaid CNAM debts, family-doctor-regime patients exempted), with a parallel pharmacists' crisis cell over a similar rupture risk noted by La Presse (June 30) — still a future event as of this run. (5) The September–October 2026 supplementary finance law signal is reconfirmed (Brent reference USD 63.3/barrel vs. real prices reported above USD 100–110/barrel; economist Ridha Chkoundali's independent ~TND 160 million-per-dollar and up to TND 8 billion shortfall estimates corroborate the Ktari figures already in the Finance Law 2026 entry) but remains pre-bill: no amending text filed, no confirmed outcome from the scheduled Finance Ministry meeting. A general sweep found no new JORT publication, ARP plenary vote, presidential decree, or major court ruling dated after July 6, 2026, and confirmed the 2026–2030 Development Plan's July 2 committee-examination conclusion already matches the existing Development Plan entry (no update needed). A rotating gap-scan of consumer-protection, telecom, and public-private-partnership regulation surfaced no active 2026 reform bill beyond the existing Law No. 2015-49 (PPP) and e-commerce entries — this area appears genuinely under-covered but with nothing new and citable to add today, so it is added to "Areas for Future Expansion" below rather than padded with a speculative entry. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (269 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit found no regressions: 269 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1236/1236 balanced <div> tags, and the header stat (269) matching the true card count. The July 6 Foreign Exchange Code outcome (first of the four dated triggers to have technically passed with no reported result), the July 8 graduate protest, the July 11 ONAT assembly (now also needing fresh corroboration, not just an outcome), the July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, and the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run; consumer-protection/telecom/PPP regulation is a secondary standing item for a future dedicated gap-scan.
v4.120 update (July 6, 2026, Run 120): This run correctly started from current origin/main (v4.119, 269 entries, 119 merged runs) rather than a stale checkout. A fresh research sweep re-checked the five standing triggers named in the v4.119 note and found none had moved: no confirmed Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome dated after June 23, 2026; the July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, and July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension all remain future events as of this run's cutoff; and the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law remains a pre-bill signal only. Rather than re-running the same exhausted pending-bill sweep, this run ran a targeted structural gap-scan of security-sector and financial-administration statutes and closed one genuine gap entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: a wholly new standalone Internal Security Force Work-Accident Compensation entry (Law No. 2013-50 of December 19, 2013 — see Security & Counter-terrorism section), documenting the base compensation regime plus the pending Proposition de loi n° 42/2025 (referred to the Defense, Security and Armed Forces Commission May 15, 2025; per a July 1, 2026 committee report, finalized and transmitted to the ARP Bureau, awaiting a study session before plenary) — a law entirely distinct from the already-documented Internal Security Forces Statute (Law No. 2019-73), which 119 prior runs had missed. It also closed one refinement gap on an existing entry: the Finance Law 2026 entry now notes that it repealed Article 45 of the Finance Law 2019 (Law No. 2018-56 of December 27, 2018), which had capped cash payments at TND 5,000 — payments above that threshold became permitted again effective January 1, 2026 — and introduced a new TND 230 registration tax on donation contracts between spouses, ascendants, and descendants; both corroborated via Managers.tn's December 3, 2025 commentary and previously absent from the entry. A candidate lead — a purported September 26, 2024 Council of Ministers session on social-security and public-projects decrees — was investigated and found to be stale content already fully captured by existing entries (the Fidaa Foundation, female-agricultural-workers social-security, and public-projects-implementation decrees), and was correctly not re-added. One new standalone entry added (270 total); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (270) matching the true card count. The July 6 Foreign Exchange Code outcome, July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, and the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.121 update (July 6, 2026, Run 121): This run's own designated branch again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: its initial local commit history sat at a long-abandoned "v2.5"/152-entry line (last touched July 5), with a full draft PR (four candidate items independently researched and drafted) opened against it before git fetch origin main and a mergeable-state check surfaced conflicts against current origin/main (already at v4.120, 270 entries, 120 merged runs). The PR was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset to match current main before redoing this run's research from the correct base. Cross-checking the discarded draft's four candidates against the real current artifact found all four already present, in each case with materially more precision than the discarded draft: the Law No. 2025-18 graduate-recruitment statute (already documented since Run 64), the Makram Jlassi embezzlement appeal ruling (already a standalone entry since Run 87, with a more precise July 4, not July 3, appeal date and the additional detail that Jlassi was dismissed from his ministry post effective July 10, 2024), the Sports Structures bill's active-committee status (already tracked, not "stalled"), and — critically — a draft claim asserting the Personal Data Protection bill's 123-vs-132 article-count conflict was now "resolved" at 123 articles, which this run's own re-investigation found was not a new primary-source resolution but a repeat of the same three secondary press accounts already weighed and correctly left unresolved since Runs 20 and 33; the existing "unresolved, do not correct one figure to the other without a primary JORT/ARP text" hedge was left unchanged rather than being overwritten on insufficient grounds. A subsequent fresh research sweep re-confirmed the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code trigger has technically passed with still no reported committee vote or session outcome from any source, and checked five further candidate leads (the plastic-bag reduction bill's committee-referral status, the Statut de l'Artiste law, the cinema-industry bill, Finance Law 2026's electric-vehicle tax provisions, and the gambling/online-betting reform) — all five were already captured at equal or greater precision by existing entries. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (270 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids and balanced HTML structure. The July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, and the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, alongside a still-unconfirmed outcome for the July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee session.
v4.122 update (July 6, 2026, Run 122): This run's designated branch held only a long-stale local checkout (a "v2.5"/152-entry line last touched July 5) while origin/main had independently progressed through 121 merged runs to v4.121 (270 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current main. A dedicated research agent searched specifically for a July 6, 2026 outcome to the Foreign Exchange Code committee session (the run's #1 priority trigger) — no source reports whether the session occurred or its result; the last confirmed status remains the June 17–23, 2026 "~90% hearings complete, BCT still silent" state, so the trigger is left open for Run 123. The Development Plan bill and the CNAM biologists' July 13 suspension were both re-confirmed unchanged. Four candidate leads were investigated and found to already be captured at equal or greater precision: the Constitutional Court organic-law bill's committee status (a claimed March 27, 2026 Bar Association hearing could not be independently corroborated via direct fetch or search — the same negative result Run 62 reached testing this identical claim, so the existing February 18, 2026 hearing citation stands unchanged); the whistleblower-protection amendment bill (already a standalone entry's pending-reform note since Run 71, with matching 42-article/15-MP detail); and a purported distinct "29-article ISIE reform bill" that on independent search resolved to a garbled restatement of the already-documented 261-article/29-deputy Unified Electoral Code proposal rather than a separate text. One genuine, independently-verified refinement was added outside the watchlist: the Public Procurement Decree entry now notes the November 22, 2025 Council of Ministers session (chaired by Head of Government Sarra Zaâfrani Zenzri) devoted to a procurement-system overhaul, confirmed via a January 3, 2026 report as still at the policy-objective stage with no decree or bill number yet filed. No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids and balanced HTML structure. The July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law, and the still-unresolved July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.123 update (July 6, 2026, Run 123): This run's designated branch again held only a long-stale local checkout (a "v2.5"/152-entry line last touched July 5) while origin/main had independently progressed through 122 merged runs to v4.122 (270 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current main. A fresh research sweep re-confirmed the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported for a fourth time — Finance Commission president Maher Ktari's own June 17, 2026 framing ("the country can no longer wait," BCT still non-responsive, choice between an article-by-article vote without the Central Bank or a further delay) remains the last hard status, with no outlet yet confirming whether the Monday session convened or what it decided; this stays the single highest-priority trigger for Run 124. The Constitutional Court organic-law bill (No. 100/2025, filed July 15, 2025 by 21 deputies) was independently re-verified unchanged. One genuine refinement was added outside the pending-bill watchlist, to the Personal Data Protection entry: a November 30, 2025 presidency announcement (reported December 1, 2025) disclosed that INPDP's own 15-member oversight council has sat without a renewed mandate since it expired April 16, 2024, marginalizing the authority through 2023–2026 independent of the pending Proposition n°95/2025 replacement bill, and that the government considers the 2004 law "obsolete" in part because it still references a now-dissolved Chamber of Advisors seat — this executive-track institutional-reform announcement is distinct from, and had not previously been cross-referenced against, the ARP committee's own legislative-track review of the replacement bill; the entry also gained the previously-uncaptured detail that Proposition n°95/2025 was filed by 17 deputies. A parallel claim that the Access to Information Authority (INAI) was, as of the same December 2025 report, merely "operating without a president and deputy" was investigated and deliberately not used to soften the existing entry's well-corroborated finding (ARTICLE19, RSF, Jeune Afrique, SNJT) that INAI was fully dissolved and its offices closed in August 2025 — the two accounts conflict, and the multiply-sourced August 2025 closure is treated as authoritative; flagged here rather than silently reconciled. No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids and balanced HTML structure. The July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law, and the still-unresolved July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.124 update (July 6, 2026, Run 124): This run's own designated branch reproduced the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode flagged throughout this changelog: a full four-item draft (base Nationality Code entry, an Associations-reform pending bill, a Nationality-Code-reform pending bill, and an Exchange-Control-Violations-Regularization pending bill) was independently researched and drafted, then committed and pushed, before git fetch origin main and a failed merge-conflict check on the opened PR (#141) surfaced that origin/main had independently progressed through 123 merged runs to v4.123 (270 entries) while this branch's local history sat at a stale "v2.5"/157-entry line last touched July 5. Per established practice, PR #141 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset to current main before cross-checking the discarded draft against the real current artifact rather than assuming it was correct. All four candidates were found already present, in each case with materially more precision: the base Nationality Code (already at #nationality-code, with the exact bill number "projet de loi n° 17/2026" and a May 7, 2026 committee-session date this run's independent research had not surfaced), the Associations-reform bill (already #ngo-reform-2023, Draft Law No. 027/2023, including a government counter-draft this run's research had missed entirely), and the Exchange-Control-Violations bill (already #exchange-amnesty-bill-2025, correctly numbered Bill No. 58/2025 — this run's draft had the number wrong as "2025/058" — with exact contribution-rate percentages and the April 14, 2026 54-3-2 procedural vote this run's draft lacked). Rather than re-running the now-fully-exhausted pending-bill watchlist, this run instead ran a systematic scan for unacknowledged duplicate law-card entries — a category of error distinct from missing coverage — by diffing <span class="law-num"> citation text and manually cross-checking near-matches.
