Two genuine, unacknowledged duplicates (full independent entries for the same statute in two sections, with no cross-reference note, unlike the deliberate "cross-referenced from X section" pattern used elsewhere in the document) were found and fixed: Nationality Code (the thin #nationality-civil entry in the Civil Law section duplicated #nationality-code in the Nationality section almost verbatim, but lacked that entry's 2010 amendment detail and 2026 pending-reform note) and Electronic Commerce Law No. 2000-83 (the #e-commerce entry in the Commercial Law section duplicated #e-commerce-law in the Digital Law section near word-for-word). Both thinner duplicates were trimmed to explicit cross-references pointing to their fuller counterparts, matching the established pattern already used for the Organic Law No. 2015-26 counter-terrorism entry; both cards were kept (rather than deleted) for section-level navigability, so the 270 unique law-card id count is unaffected. A fresh check of the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome (the standing #1 priority trigger, unreported for four consecutive runs) turned up no new information — still no outlet confirms whether Monday's session convened or its result. No new standalone entries were added; two existing entries revised to remove redundant content. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids and balanced <div> tags (verified via HTML parser, zero errors). The July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law, and the still-unresolved July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run — alongside a new one: a further pass for additional unacknowledged duplicate entries (this run's diff-based scan covered exact and near-exact law-num matches only, not semantically equivalent entries with differently worded citations, so more may remain undetected).
v4.125 update (July 6, 2026, Run 125): This run's designated branch again held only a long-stale local checkout (a "v2.5"/152-entry line last touched July 5) while origin/main had independently progressed through 124 merged runs to v4.124 (270 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset to current main per established practice (no open PR existed for this branch to close). A structural check found the one remaining id="law-num" collision flagged by a naive grep (two occurrences of "renewables-law") to be a false positive — one is the actual law-card div, the other is a <code>-quoted reference to that same id inside Run 94's own changelog prose, not a second card — so no unacknowledged duplicates remain beyond the two fixed in Run 124. A fresh WebSearch sweep (French- and English-language queries) re-targeted the standing watchlist: the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported for a fifth consecutive run — the newest available source is still the June 23, 2026 status report that the commission "poursuit ses travaux" with the Central Bank hearing outstanding; the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill's committee-to-plenary handoff (concluded July 2, per Run 71) has not yet produced an announced plenary date; and the Constitutional Court organic-law bill, INAI/INPDP institutional-reform tension, and CNAM July 13 tiers-payant suspension were all re-confirmed unchanged. Three candidate leads outside the watchlist were investigated and found to already be captured at equal or greater precision, so none were added: a CPJ "Beyond Decree 54" press-freedom report (already reflected in the #decree-2022-54-press entry's May 2026 RSF/CPJ update), a general Consumer Protection Law gap-check (already covered, per Run 105's confirmation, and re-verified this run), and a Maritime/Ports code gap-check (already covered by three standalone entries — Maritime Commercial Code, Merchant Marine Organization Law, and Maritime Ports Code). No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate); no existing entries altered. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids and balanced <div>/<h3> tags. The July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension, the September–October 2026 supplementary finance law, and the still-unresolved July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.126 update (July 6, 2026, Run 126): This run's designated branch again started from a stale checkout (a "v2.5"/157-entry line) while origin/main had independently progressed through 125 merged runs to v4.125 (270 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and reset to current main before drafting, per established practice. Tavily search again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," so this run relied on WebSearch plus direct WebFetch verification of individual articles. Since today (July 6, 2026) is the date of the Foreign Exchange Code committee's own long-awaited session, this run specifically searched for a same-day outcome report: none exists yet in any source — the newest dated status remains the June 23, 2026 "poursuit ses travaux" report, so the July 6 trigger should be treated as still-pending rather than resolved until a post-July-6 source appears. The 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, Constitutional Court organic-law bill (its Feb 17/18, 2026 committee hearing under chair Faouzi Daâs remains the last confirmed step — the one-day date discrepancy between sources is unresolved and not worth reconciling further), INAI/INPDP institutional tension, and the July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension (both independently re-confirmed via multiple sources as still scheduled and unchanged) were all re-verified unchanged. Two candidate leads were investigated and deliberately withheld as insufficiently corroborated rather than added: a "Projet de loi n° 2024/036 relatif à la cybersécurité," sourced only to a single, undated-status secondary article from December 2024, whose provisions (national cybersecurity authority, mandatory audits, incident notification, data localization) substantially duplicate the already-enacted Decree-Law No. 2023-17 framework — it may be an earlier organic-law draft of the same reform, a distinct parallel track, or a stale/abandoned proposal, and no source establishes which, so future runs encountering this same bill number should treat it as an unresolved open question rather than re-adding it as a fresh pending-bill entry without better sourcing; and a January 30, 2026 procedural article on the Foreign Exchange Code bill's routine referral to the Finance Committee, which predates and is superseded by material already in this artifact. No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit (duplicate-id grep, law-card/section tag balance, header count vs. actual card count) found no regressions. The July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM biologists' suspension are now the two nearest-term triggers and should be the first things the next run checks for an actual outcome, ahead of re-running the broader watchlist sweep.
v4.127 update (July 6, 2026, Run 127): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-r5qjzu) also began from a stale checkout — a self-drafted "v2.5"→"v2.6" line (base commit merged as PR #128, ~157 entries) — and had already been pushed and opened as PR #144 (adding a labor-reform entry and a tax-amnesty note) before a 405 "merge conflicts" error on the merge attempt surfaced that origin/main had independently progressed through 126 merged runs to v4.126 (270 entries), the same late-catch pattern documented in the v4.87/v4.88 notes. PR #144 was closed unmerged (with an explanatory comment) rather than reverting 126 runs of accumulated work, and the branch was reset to current main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline. Cross-checking PR #144's two draft candidates against the real current artifact: the May 2025 anti-subcontracting labor law was already present as the existing, more detailed Law No. 2025-9 entry and was discarded rather than re-added; but the 2026 tax-amnesty/regularization mechanism (Finance Law 2026 Art. 69, operationalized by Tax Administration Circular No. 4/2026 "JIBAYA," with June 30 and September 30, 2026 regularization deadlines for tax debt and unfiled/rectificative declarations respectively) was confirmed genuinely absent via a targeted grep of the current artifact and independently corroborated via Tuniscope reporting before being added to the existing Finance Law 2026 entry. No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate, since this extends an existing card); one existing entry (Finance Law 2026) substantively revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: balanced <div>/<section>/<h3> tags and the header stat (270) matching the true card count. The July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM biologists' tiers-payant suspension remain the two nearest-term triggers for the next run.
v4.128 update (July 6, 2026, Run 128): This run's designated branch started clean, already reset to current origin/main (v4.127, 270 entries, 127 merged runs) with no stale-checkout or PR-conflict issue to resolve — the first run in a while to begin from an accurate baseline without corrective action. A targeted research sweep re-checked the eleven highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Investment Code reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, exchange-violations amnesty bill 58/2025, banque postale bill 82/2025, nationality-code revision, Unified Electoral Code, Tax Adviser Organic Law) and found none had moved since Run 127's cutoff — no committee vote, plenary passage, enactment, or abandonment for any of them, and no new JORT-published legislation in the July 5–6, 2026 window. The Foreign Exchange Code's own July 6 committee-meeting trigger date has now technically arrived a third time (after Runs 121–122's earlier passes) with still no source reporting whether the session occurred or its outcome, beyond a July 3, 2026 IACE commentary reiterating it as one of three reforms Tunisia should promulgate before end-2026 — new framing, not a status change, so it was not added as a separate entry. The July 8 (graduate/unemployed protest) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) triggers were both independently re-confirmed as still scheduled and unchanged; the July 11 ONAT/Bar Association general assembly could not be freshly re-corroborated this run (only May–June 2026 bar-strike history surfaced), consistent with Run 119's identical finding — not a retraction of the existing Run 46 sourcing, but flagged again for confirmation once the date passes. The recurring "Personal Data Protection enforcement began July 11, 2026" marketing false positive and the Algeria/Tunisia mislabeling trap were both checked for and not encountered this run. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (270 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions and synchronized all three version markers (title tag, header meta, footer), which had drifted out of sync again (title read "v4.126" while the header and footer already read "v4.127") — the same recurring version-string bug flagged at v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, v4.59, v4.62, and v4.97. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order.
v4.129 update (July 6, 2026, Run 129): This run's designated branch began directly from current origin/main (v4.128, 270 entries, 128 merged runs) with no stale-checkout correction needed. A dedicated research agent re-checked the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting specifically (today's own trigger date), a general sweep for any JORT-published law, decree-law, or organic law dated after July 3, 2026, movement on the Development Plan/Personal Data Protection/Investment Code bills, and a ten-area structural gap-scan (cinema/audiovisual industry code, national archives law, heritage/antiquities code, waste-management code, cooperatives law, weapons/firearms law, casino/gambling law, veterinary pharmacy law, organ-donation/transplant law, mental-health law) chosen specifically because none had been named in any prior run's gap-scan list. Every one of the ten gap-scan candidates was cross-checked directly against the live artifact via targeted grep (not trusted on the research agent's summary alone) and found already present with an existing standalone entry — the 1960 Film Industry Code, the Archives Law (id libraries-archives), the 1994 Heritage/Patrimoine Code, the 1996 Waste Law, the 1969 Weapons Law, the 1974 gaming/casino Decree-Law, the veterinary-pharmacy statute, the 1991/1995 organ-transplant laws, and the 1992 Mental Health Law all resolved to entries already documented in equal or greater precision than the fresh research surfaced; only "a unified modern cooperatives statute" returned genuinely ambiguous (scattered Code des Obligations et des Contrats/1967-era provisions, no single consolidating text located), and is noted here as an unresolved low-confidence item rather than asserted as a gap. No source dated later than July 3, 2026 was found for any JORT publication, and the Foreign Exchange Code's July 6 committee outcome remains unconfirmed by any outlet as of this run's cutoff (same-day reporting lag, not evidence either way) — the last hard status point remains chair Maher Ktari's ~90%-complete hearing count from June 17, 2026. The Investment Code reform's July 3, 2026 article-by-article committee-voting detail, independently resurfaced by this run's research, was verified already captured verbatim in the existing entry's Run 70 update. This run did, however, close one genuine documentation-quality gap: a broken internal anchor link in the v4.127 changelog note above (#labor-contracts-2025, which resolved to no element) was corrected to the Law No. 2025-9 entry's actual id (labor-2025) — found via a full TOC/anchor-resolution audit rather than the standard pending-bill sweep, a check worth repeating periodically given how many prior runs cross-reference entries by id. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (270 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, the header stat (270) matching the true card count, and — after the fix above — every internal anchor link (both Table of Contents and in-prose changelog cross-references) now resolves to an existing element id. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order.
v4.130 update (July 6, 2026, Run 130): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-jonv44) had no PR history of its own and was stuck at a stale, never-merged v2.5 checkout (~157 entries) from early July while origin/main had independently progressed through 129 merged runs to v4.129 (270 entries) — confirmed via git log/git fetch and an empty GitHub pull-request search for the branch — and was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began, per this project's established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked all four dated watchlist triggers (the July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting — today's own trigger date — plus the July 8 graduate-protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant-suspension dates), swept for JORT-published legislation since July 3, 2026, checked nine pending bills for movement, and ran a ten-area gap-scan chosen to cover ground not named in Run 129's list (space/satellite law, cryptocurrency/blockchain, drone/UAV regulation, genetic-testing/bioethics, surrogacy/ART, franchise law, PPP law, renewable-hydrogen strategy, cybersecurity incident-reporting, and consumer-credit/microcredit regulation). None of the four trigger dates had resolved as of this run's cutoff (July 6's committee outcome remains unreported due to same-day lag; July 8, 11, and 13 have not yet arrived) and no post-July-3 JORT publication was found. Every candidate the agent flagged was independently cross-checked against the live artifact via targeted grep rather than trusted on its summary alone: the Development Plan bill's cited figures (40,748 submitted proposals, 21,100-project portfolio, TND ~102 billion, 4.2% growth target) were found already captured verbatim since the Run 71 update; the Tax Adviser Organic Law's "study day" detail was found already documented since the existing entry's own text; and the agent's claimed "genetic-testing/bioethics gap" and "surrogacy gap" were both found to be false positives — the existing 2015 Bioethics Law entry already covers assisted reproduction, embryo research, and pre-implantation genetic diagnosis in more depth than the fresh research surfaced. Franchise law, PPP law, and consumer-credit regulation were similarly confirmed already covered by existing entries. Two genuine refinement gaps survived independent verification via direct WebFetch of primary reporting and were added: (1) Tunisia's civil drone (UAV) regulatory framework — no dedicated statute exists; the Directorate General of Civil Aviation, coordinating with the Ministry of Defense under Transport Minister Khaled Chili, has completed a draft regulatory text via a dedicated inter-ministerial commission (reported November 12, 2025) now circulated for stakeholder comment, with no decree number or date yet confirmed — added as a pending-regulation note to the Civil Aviation Code entry, cross-referenced against the narrower agricultural carve-out already enacted; (2) that carve-out itself was sharpened with its precise citation — Finance Law 2026 Art. 135, adopted by ARP vote of 60–29–14 on December 4, 2025 — previously described in the Finance Law 2026 entry without an article number or vote tally. A third, lower-confidence finding — the Ministry of Industry's May 2024 "H2Vert.TUN" National Strategy for Green Hydrogen — was added as a clearly-labeled policy-roadmap note (not a law) to the Renewable Energy Law entry, parallel to how the existing AI-strategy note is framed in the Personal Data Protection entry. The space/satellite-law and cybersecurity-incident-reporting gap-scan candidates were investigated and found to be either genuinely non-existent as Tunisian law (space law) or already covered (cybersecurity incident-reporting, via the existing Decree-Law 2023-17 entry) rather than added, consistent with this project's practice of not asserting an entry where no distinct governing text exists. No new standalone entries were added (270 total remains accurate, since all three findings extend existing cards); three existing entries (Civil Aviation Code, Finance Law 2026, Renewable Energy Law) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and the header stat (270) matching the true card count. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, in that order.
v4.131 update (July 6, 2026, Run 131): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-hut13u) had no PR history of its own and was stuck at a stale, never-merged v2.5 checkout (7 commits, ~157 entries, last touched July 5) while origin/main had independently progressed through 130 merged runs to v4.130 (270 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git merge-base --is-ancestor, and an empty GitHub pull-request search for the branch — and was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began, per this project's established practice. This run's own research tooling was significantly degraded: the project's Tavily search integration returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on every call (as in Runs 68, 71, 76, 86, 95, 107), and WebSearch itself intermittently returned "unavailable" on roughly half of all attempts this run (a new, more severe degradation than any prior run's notes describe) — every finding below therefore rests on the subset of queries that succeeded, cross-checked via direct WebFetch of primary sources where possible, and future runs should not assume a "no results" WebSearch response means no news exists. Within that constraint, a targeted sweep re-checked all four dated watchlist triggers: the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a sixth consecutive time (last hard status point still Ktari's ~90%-complete hearing count and June 23, 2026 status report; a June 11, 2026 source re-surfaced during this run predates that and was discarded rather than treated as new); the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly could not be freshly corroborated for a third time (after Runs 119 and 128) — only 2025-dated Ordre des Avocats content and May–June 2026 strike coverage surfaced; and the July 13, 2026 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension remains on track and unchanged. The pharmacists' late-June near-suspension crisis (already documented via the Run 30/43/69 updates) was independently re-confirmed in more granular form — Tunisie Numérique and La Presse de Tunisie report the July 1 threatened suspension was averted specifically because "la Cnam s'est engagée à verser une partie des sommes dues aux pharmaciens" (CNAM committed to pay a portion of the amounts owed) — but this is the same underlying "engagement de paiement" already captured in the CNAM entry, not a new development, so no edit was made to avoid a redundant restatement. A gap-scan check on domestic/household workers ("travailleuses domestiques") found these remain governed only by general Labor Code provisions (verbal contracts permitted, no dedicated statute or minimum-wage schedule) with no bill pending — consistent with this project's practice of not asserting an entry where no distinct governing text exists, this was left undocumented rather than added as a fabricated "law." No JORT publication dated after July 3, 2026 was found, no watchlist item crossed into enactment, plenary vote, or committee-report conclusion, and no new standalone entries were added or existing entries revised (270 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids, 1251/1251 balanced <div> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, the header stat (270) matching the true card count, and every internal anchor link resolving to an existing element id. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run; a secondary priority is simply re-attempting this run's degraded WebSearch queries once the tool stabilizes, since roughly half returned no result through no fault of the underlying research question.
v4.132 update (July 6, 2026, Run 132): This run's designated branch had 130 commits' worth of prior history but had gone stale (last synced at v2.5/#128) while origin/main had independently progressed through 131 merged runs to v4.131 (270 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and a 188-commit git log comparison — and was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with every run since Run 68) covering the Foreign Exchange Code committee, the Development Plan bill, the Personal Data Protection/AI bill, the Investment Code, new JORT publications, ARP plenary activity, and the specialized financial-corruption chamber's docket. None of the tracked watchlist items crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion. The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome — arriving for the second time as a trigger date — remains unreported by any outlet as of this run's cutoff. The July 3, 2026 Mabrouk/Chahed/Ben Gharbia, Turki, and Mekacher rulings the sweep re-surfaced were all independently re-verified already fully captured at equal or greater precision by the existing entries; none were re-added. One genuine refinement was closed: the plastic-bag reduction bill entry (added Run 109) gained newly-reported detail — 25 sponsoring deputies, a 12-month post-JORT-publication phase-out window, 5,000–50,000 TND fines (doubling on repeat offenses, with possible business closure), and 6-months-to-2-years imprisonment for illegal production or import (multiply sourced: La Presse, businessnews.com.tn, Tunisie Numérique, Le Temps, African Manager, WebManagerCenter, all July 5, 2026). One new judicial development was identified but deliberately not turned into a standalone entry, consistent with this project's practice of requiring a verdict or enacted text before creating a card: businessman Maher Chaâbane's trial before the same specialized financial-corruption chamber that produced the Mabrouk/Turki/Mekacher/Haouas convictions was postponed to September 24, 2026 (WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, July 6, 2026) — flagged in the Future Expansion watchlist instead. A single-sourced, internally-conflicting claim of a partial signatory withdrawal from Constitutional Court organic law Proposal No. 100/2025 was investigated and left unresolved for lack of primary-source corroboration, rather than asserted as a status change. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision beyond the plastic-bag refinement (270 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 270 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, the header stat (270) matching the true card count, and all Table of Contents anchors resolving. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term triggers for the next run, alongside watching for post-July-6 Foreign Exchange Code reporting.
v4.133 update (July 6, 2026, Run 133): This run's designated branch was again a stale, never-merged v2.5 checkout (~7 commits, 1991 lines) while origin/main had independently progressed through 132 merged runs to v4.132 (270 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and a stat-diff comparison (565 insertions/2,205 deletions) — and was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began. A dedicated research agent re-checked all eight tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, ONAT assembly, CNAM/biologists suspension, Development Plan bill, the Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes' ARP-transmission status, the Chaâbane trial, Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025, and the rumored rectificative finance law) and found none had changed status since Run 132's same-day cutoff; the July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome specifically remains unreported by any outlet a third consecutive trigger-date pass. A JORT/ARP sweep for any post-July-6 enacted legislation or plenary vote found nothing. A ten-area structural gap-scan (military justice code, public procurement disputes, tourism-real-estate timeshare, whistleblower-protection implementation, fintech/payment licensing, postal code, air-passenger rights, degree-equivalence recognition, public-debt management law, sovereign-wealth/natural-resources fund) found eight already covered or confirmed non-existent as Tunisian law, and closed one genuine gap: a wholly new standalone Shared-Time Tourist Accommodation Law (No. 2008-33 of May 13, 2008) entry (Tourism section), governing Tunisia's "timeshare" sector with its own prior-authorization regime and usufruct-based (not co-ownership) legal characterization of purchased rights — verified independently against JurisiteTunisie's primary-text pages before inclusion, and missed by all 132 prior runs despite the adjacent Tourism Organization Law entry's long-standing presence. Two candidate leads were investigated and correctly discarded: a July 6, 2026 CGA (insurance regulator) "2026–2030 roadmap" press item describing a future "progressive obligation" for agricultural insurance is a policy announcement, not enacted law; and a January–April 2026 ten-deputy sovereign-wealth-fund bill for natural-resources governance remains at the committee-examination stage, not yet enacted, so per this project's standing practice it is flagged here rather than given a standalone card. One new standalone entry added (271 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids and the header stat updated to match. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.134 update (July 6, 2026, Run 134): This run's designated branch had again diverged from a stale early-project checkout (~7 commits, last synced at v2.5/#128, 1991 lines) while origin/main had independently progressed through 133 merged runs to v4.133 (271 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main before any drafting began — and was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent re-checked the seven tracked watchlist items for anything dated July 6–7, 2026: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported an eighth consecutive trigger-date pass; the ONAT general assembly (July 11) and CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension (July 13) remain on schedule with no new corroboration or cancellation signal; the Development Plan bill's July 2 committee conclusion and pending synthesis report were re-confirmed already fully captured by the existing entry; the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was independently re-confirmed as formally decided July 6, 2026 (no earlier hearing occurred); the sovereign natural-resources fund bill remains at committee stage with no new movement; and the Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025 signatory-withdrawal claim remains unresolved (a search surfaced only a different, earlier 2025 Constitutional Court proposal withdrawn April 23, 2025 — not evidence about Proposal 100/2025 itself). A structural gap-scan (water code, urban-planning/zoning code, food-safety law, military justice code, blood-donation law, higher-education law, scientific-research law, condominium/co-ownership law, asylum/refugee law, telecom infrastructure-sharing) found nine of the ten already covered by existing entries or confirmed non-existent as Tunisian law (asylum/refugee: still no enacted statute, only a stalled 2016 draft organic law), and surfaced one genuine, twice-independently-corroborated correction rather than a new gap: the Military Justice Code entry's origin citation ("Law No. 86-79 of September 9, 1986") does not exist in any Tunisian legislative database and its claimed "2019 amendment restricting military-court jurisdiction over civilians" is unsubstantiated and contradicted by multiply-sourced reporting (Amnesty, HRW, ICJ, SNJT) of continuing civilian prosecutions under Kais Saied, including a March 2025 journalist sentencing. The entry now correctly cites the code's true origin (Decree No. 57-9 of January 10, 1957) and its real amendment chain, including a substantive, previously-undocumented 2024 amendment (Loi n° 2024-35 of July 15, 2024, narrowing Article 68's desertion-abroad definition) that had gone unnoticed despite the code being explicitly checked and marked "already covered" in Run 133's own gap-scan. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); one existing entry was substantively corrected. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids and no regressions. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; future runs should also spot-check other older "amended [single year]" citations for the same class of unverified-amendment error this run found in the military justice entry.
v4.135 update (July 6, 2026, Run 135): This run's designated branch was again a stale, never-merged v2.5 checkout (~7 commits, last synced at PR #128) while origin/main had independently progressed through 134 merged runs to v4.134 (271 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and a 192-commit git log comparison — and was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began. A dedicated research agent re-checked all eight tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, ONAT assembly, CNAM/biologists suspension, Maher Chaâbane trial, Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025, sovereign natural-resources fund bill, the Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes' ARP-transmission status, and the Development Plan bill) and found none had changed status since Run 134's same-day cutoff: the July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a ninth consecutive trigger-date pass (last hard data point unchanged since the June 17–23, 2026 status report); the July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM/biologists suspension remain on schedule with no fresh corroboration, negotiation, or cancellation signal (unlike the pharmacists' earlier near-miss, no last-minute CNAM deal has been reported for the biologists); and the Development Plan bill's June 30, 2026 joint committee hearing with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh was confirmed already fully captured by the existing entry, with no plenary date yet set. A ten-area gap-scan (posted-workers/social-security totalization agreements, medical-profession statute, pharmacy-profession statute, e-waste/circular-economy law, data-localization/cloud-sovereignty rules, student-loan/higher-education financing law, VTC/ride-hailing regulation, temporary-work-agency regulation, consumer-arbitration/ombudsman law, and the SMIG/SMAG minimum-wage mechanism) found nine of the ten already covered by existing entries (the temporary-work/subcontracting candidate in particular re-confirmed as the existing, more-detailed Law No. 2025-9 entry) or confirmed to have no distinct governing statute in Tunisia (e-waste, data localization, student loans, VTC, consumer ADR — each addressed only by policy roadmaps, general codes, or informal mechanisms rather than a dedicated law), and surfaced one genuinely new false-positive research trap rather than a legislative gap: a "Conseil de gouvernement"-adopted June 25, 2026 reform of medical-profession Law 08-12, sourced to medias24.com, lavieeco.com, and leseco.ma, is Moroccan legislation (Morocco's weekly cabinet body and its own Ordre National des Médecins statute), not Tunisian — a new country-mislabeling trap parallel to the existing Algeria/Saïd Sayoud party-financing trap, now added to the watchlist note below so future runs recognize it immediately rather than re-verifying it from scratch. The pharmacists' union (CNOPT) elected a new 11-member National Council on July 5, 2026 (Businessnews, La Presse, Tunisie Numérique) — a routine organizational election under the existing, unchanged 1973 Pharmacy Law, not a legislative development, and was correctly left undocumented. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); no existing entry required substantive revision. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids and no regressions. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.136 update (July 6, 2026, Run 136): This run's designated branch was again found to hold only a long-stale, never-merged v2.5-era fork (~7 commits, last synced at PR #128, 157 entries) while origin/main had independently progressed through 135 merged runs to v4.135 (271 entries, 194-commit history) — confirmed via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. Tavily search remains down (HTTP 402, "account disabled" — consistent with the outage first reported around Run 131 and reconfirmed by several runs since); this run relied on the WebSearch tool instead. A same-day re-check of the highest-priority watchlist items found no material change: the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code (Code des Changes, Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a tenth consecutive trigger-date pass, with the last hard status point still Maher Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete report and the BCT's continued non-response; the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement — already documented in the Run 132/134 updates — was independently re-confirmed as formally decided July 6, 2026 by the specialized financial-corruption chamber (Webdo, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter); the July 13, 2026 CNAM/private-biologists tiers-payant suspension and its underlying June 30, 2026 extraordinary general assembly vote remain on track with no last-minute agreement reported, matching the existing CNAM entry's Run 31/43/46 detail precisely; and the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly could not be freshly corroborated with a July-dated source, continuing the standing sourcing gap flagged since Runs 119/128/131. One genuine, dated refinement was closed: a July 6, 2026 Business News fact-check explicitly debunking a circulating rumor that a general Road Traffic Code reform had entered into force July 1, 2026 was added to the existing Road Traffic Code entry as corroboration of its already-correct "pending, not yet enacted" framing of the violence-routière package — a citation addition, not a status change. An eleven-area gap-scan targeting fields not covered by the Run 129/130/135 scans (disability rights, child protection, blood transfusion, tobacco control, food safety, rail transport, postal services, housing/social housing, condominium/co-ownership, civil protection, and refugee/asylum law) found all eleven already governed by existing standalone entries, confirmed via direct keyword search of the current artifact rather than independent re-drafting. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Road Traffic Code) was enriched with a corroborating citation. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1260/1260 balanced <div> tags, and all internal href="#..." anchors resolving to a matching id with none orphaned (verified programmatically). The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains the single highest-priority item to check the moment any post-July-6 reporting surfaces.
v4.137 update (July 6, 2026, Run 137): This run's designated branch again held a long-stale, never-merged v2.5-era fork (~7 commits, last synced at PR #128) while origin/main had independently progressed through 136 merged runs to v4.136 (271 entries, 196-commit history) — confirmed via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. A dedicated research agent re-checked the watchlist on the same calendar day as Run 136's cutoff. The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported an eleventh consecutive trigger-date pass, with the last hard status point unchanged since the June 17-23, 2026 report (Chair Maher Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete status, BCT still not responding); same-day reporting lag in Tunisian outlets makes this expected rather than a sign of stalling. The Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement (already fully documented since the Run 132/134/136 updates) was independently re-confirmed as formally decided today by the specialized financial-corruption chamber (Webdo, WebManagerCenter/Directinfo). The July 13, 2026 CNAM/private-biologists tiers-payant suspension remains on track with no last-minute deal reported. The July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly remains only lightly corroborated — a fresh single-source hit (Réalités Magazine) confirms the Saturday date amid the ongoing Bar/Justice Ministry dispute but adds no detail beyond what the Judicial Statute entry has documented since Run 43/46, so it was not treated as a new fact. The Development Plan bill, Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes' ARP-transmission bottleneck, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025, and the sovereign natural-resources fund bill were all independently re-confirmed unchanged. One genuine, cautiously-worded refinement was added outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Cinema and Cultural Industries entry's June 24, 2026 committee-session citation was enriched with a plausible age-based film-classification detail sourced this run, while a conflicting predecessor-statute-number claim from the same reporting was deliberately withheld rather than allowed to contradict the entry's already-verified Law No. 93-40/CNCI citation — flagged for a future run to resolve against a primary text rather than silently picked one way. An eleven-area gap-scan (cinema/audiovisual reform granularity, whistleblower-protection amendment article count, veterinary law, maritime piracy/security, cooperative banking, habous/waqf administration, national parks/protected areas, cinema classification/censorship, public-health emergency declarations, immigration-detention's statutory basis, and space law) found ten of the eleven already covered by existing entries or confirmed as settled non-gaps, and reconfirmed immigration detention's legal basis (Law No. 68-7, already cited in the Entry and Residence of Foreigners entry) rather than surfacing a new gap. A new research-process note for future runs: web searches for "lanceurs d'alerte" and "Code de l'environnement" heavily surface French domestic legislation that must be filtered out, the same class of cross-country mislabeling trap as the existing Algeria/Saïd Sayoud and Morocco/medical-profession traps documented since Runs 74/135. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Cinema and Cultural Industries) was enriched with a caveated refinement. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all internal href="#..." anchors resolving to a matching id with none orphaned. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains the single highest-priority item to check the moment any post-July-6 reporting finally surfaces.
v4.138 update (July 6, 2026, Run 138): This run's designated branch again held a long-stale, never-merged v2.5-era fork (~7 commits, last synced at PR #128) while origin/main had independently progressed through 137 merged runs to v4.137 (271 entries, 197-commit history) — confirmed via git fetch origin main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. A research sweep roughly an hour after Run 137's cutoff re-checked the highest-priority watchlist items and found none moved: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twelfth consecutive trigger-date pass (Ktari's own June 17-23, 2026 status — ~90% hearings complete, BCT still not responding — remains the last hard data point; one fresh source repeats the same two-option framing, voting article-by-article without the BCT's opinion or extending the consultation, already captured in the standing entry); the July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension remain on track with no new corroboration; and the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement, Development Plan bill, and Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes' ARP-transmission bottleneck were all re-confirmed unchanged. A dedicated check for any JORT publication or ARP plenary action dated after Run 137's cutoff found none. One genuine, dated refinement was closed outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Medical Professions Organization entry was enriched with a July 1, 2026 Ministry of Higher Education response (published on the ARP site July 6, 2026) confirming that Tunisian physicians trained abroad with 5+ years of post-specialty practice are dispensed from the diploma-equivalence exam under an existing October 10, 2023 ministerial order — a real, multiply-sourced (La Presse de Tunisie, Le Temps News) development traced to a genuine ARP written answer rather than the marketing-boilerplate or cross-country-mislabeling patterns flagged in prior runs. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Medical Professions Organization) was enriched. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates and no orphaned internal anchors. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains the single highest-priority item to check the moment any post-July-6 reporting finally surfaces.
v4.139 update (July 6, 2026, Run 139): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-nn1xom) held no prior PR and no substantive commits of its own — just the project's original v2.5-era baseline (7 commits, last synced at PR #128) — while origin/main had independently progressed through 138 merged runs to v4.138 (271 entries, 198-commit history); confirmed via git fetch origin main and list_pull_requests (empty) before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. Tavily search remains down (HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with every run since roughly Run 131); this run relied on WebSearch alone. A same-day research sweep (this run's cutoff is the same July 6, 2026 calendar day as Run 138's) found no watchlist movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a thirteenth consecutive trigger-date pass, with Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status still the last hard data point; no JORT publication or ARP plenary vote dated after Run 138's cutoff was found; and the July 11 ONAT assembly and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension — the two nearest dated triggers — had not yet occurred as of this run's cutoff, so neither could show new movement. A fact-check the search sweep re-surfaced (Business News' July 6, 2026 debunking of the "new Road Traffic Code entered into force July 1, 2026" rumor) was independently confirmed already captured verbatim in the Run 136 update to the Road Traffic Code entry — not re-added. One genuine, independently-verified gap was closed entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Insurance Code entry's existing note on the pending road-accident compensation-cap bill did not mention the underlying Fonds de Garantie des Victimes des Accidents de la Circulation itself (Arts. 172–176, added by Law No. 2005-86 of August 15, 2005) or its subsequent restructuring — confirmed via direct WebFetch of jurisitetunisie.com's Insurance Code and Finance Law 2025 pages — by Article 18 of Finance Law 2025 (Law No. 2024-48), which repealed Arts. 172 and 174–176 effective January 1, 2025 and converted the fund into a special Treasury account (0.2% insurer levy + 2% insured levy on motor-liability premiums, plus subrogation recoveries), a distinct enacted-law detail from the still-pending March 2026 cap-raising bill already documented in the same entry. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Insurance Code) was enriched. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates and no orphaned internal anchors. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; as in Run 138's own note, a run landing within hours of this one should check those two dates directly for an actual outcome before re-running the full same-day sweep.
v4.140 update (July 6, 2026, Run 140): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sqy7ca) held no PR history of its own and was still checked out at a stale v2.5-era baseline (~2,050 lines) while origin/main had independently progressed through 139 merged runs to v4.139 (271 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. A dedicated research agent re-checked the watchlist for anything dated July 4–6, 2026: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a fourteenth consecutive trigger-date pass (Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status is still the last hard data point); the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was independently re-confirmed via same-day (July 6) sources with no new detail; the Development Plan bill, Investment Code reform, nationality-code revision bill, Constitutional Court organic law (Proposal 100/2025), Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, gambling reform, and Tax Adviser Organic Law were all re-confirmed unchanged since Run 139's cutoff; and no JORT issue dated July 2026 could be located in any index. A seventeen-area gap-scan (consumer arbitration/ombudsman, whistleblower protection, cooperatives statute, firearms law, national archives, heritage/antiquities enforcement, casino/gambling, organ donation, mental health, blood donation/transfusion, rail transport/SNCFT, condominium/co-ownership, refugee/asylum, student-loan financing, VTC/ride-hailing, data localization, e-waste) found all seventeen already covered by existing entries or confirmed to have no distinct governing Tunisian statute, substantially overlapping — and reconfirming — the findings of the Run 129/130/133–137 scans. Rather than repeat an eighteenth consecutive "no gap found" pending-bill/gap-scan cycle, this run instead ran a citation-accuracy spot-check (the same method that caught the Run 134 Military Justice Code fabrication) against five entries never individually re-verified since their addition: the National Standardization System Law (2009-38), Public Enterprises & Privatization Law (89-9), Debt-Recovery Companies Law (98-4), and Passports & Travel Documents Law (75-40) were all independently confirmed as exact citation matches against WIPO Lex, ILO NATLEX, and primary Tunisian-government PDF sources. The fifth, the Commercial Register Law entry (added Run 54), turned up a genuine, previously-unnoticed error: it cited Law No. 95-44 of May 2, 1995 as the governing statute — a historically accurate citation, but for a law that direct verification of Article 64 of its successor confirms was entirely repealed by Law No. 2018-52 of October 29, 2018, which created the Registre National des Entreprises (RNE) already discussed later in the same entry's own Run 104 update without ever citing the law that created it. The entry has been corrected to cite Law No. 2018-52 as the operative statute, with 95-44 retained only as repealed historical background — a distinct error pattern from Run 134's fabricated citation (this one was real but superseded, not invented), suggesting future runs should periodically re-verify not just "does this citation exist" but "is this citation still in force," since a project this mature accumulates more supersession-type errors than outright fabrications as time passes. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate, since this corrects rather than extends a card); one existing entry (Commercial Register Law) was substantively corrected. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and no orphaned internal anchor links. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; a further round of citation spot-checks on other early-run (Run 40–70) additions is a standing candidate for future runs once the July 11/13 triggers resolve.
v4.141 update (July 7, 2026, Run 141): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-gt7mtc) again held no PR history and was checked out at a stale v2.5-era baseline while origin/main had independently progressed through 140 merged runs to v4.140 (271 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch, and the branch was reset to current origin/main before drafting, the same recurring pattern as nearly every prior run. Two parallel research agents ran: one re-checked the standing watchlist for movement dated July 6–7, 2026, the other independently re-verified citations on five more early-run entries never individually spot-checked since addition. Watchlist result: no change on the two highest-priority items — the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a fifteenth consecutive pass, and the Personal Data Protection/AI bill's 123-vs-132 article-count conflict remains unresolved at the primary-source level (one new secondary source claimed a "July 11, 2026" entry-into-force date for this bill, but could not be corroborated against any primary ARP/JORT text and — given its suspicious coincidence with the unrelated July 11 ONAT trigger date already on this watchlist — is flagged as likely unreliable and deliberately not added). The Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was independently re-confirmed via a same-day (July 6) court ruling denying release requests, with no new substantive detail beyond what Run 140 already captured. The citation spot-check (Narcotics Law 92-52, Anti-Human Trafficking Law 2016-61, Prison/Penitentiary Law 2001-52, Racial Discrimination Law 2018-50, Private International Law 98-97) found all five accurate and currently in force with no repeals or supersessions — a clean result, unlike Run 140's Commercial Register Law finding — though two (Narcotics, Prison) have pending 2025–2026 reform bills already noted in their entries. Three genuine, dated, previously-undocumented developments were identified and added, all enforcement/implementation actions under already-existing statutes rather than new gaps: (1) the Competition and Prices Law entry was updated with the Conseil de la Concurrence's three December 31, 2025 decisions (reported January 7–8, 2026) fining companies a combined TND 4.2 million across the pharmaceutical (TND 1.2M), smartphone (TND 2.5M), and packaging (TND 0.5M) sectors for anti-competitive practices — distinct from, and additional to, the already-noted pending COMESA-alignment law revision; (2) the Renewable Energy Law entry was updated with the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy's sixth authorization-round call for solar photovoltaic projects (200 MW available, application window April 15 – June 15, 2026, in 1/2/10 MW project-size tiers), following Round 5's 186 preliminary authorizations (~286–288 MW), under the same Law No. 2015-12 authorization regime already documented; (3) the 2026–2030 National Development Plan (Bill) entry — last touched at Run 71 and otherwise untouched for 70 runs — was updated with deputy Issam Chouchane's July 6, 2026 public criticism (Business News) that the plan document lacks strategic vision and leaves gaps on energy transition, infrastructure, and advanced technology/AI, a fresh dated committee-member reaction not previously captured. No new standalone entries were added (271 total remains accurate); three existing entries were enriched. A structural check confirmed 271 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates and no orphaned internal anchors. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run — both are now only days away, so a run landing after either date should check for an actual outcome directly rather than re-running the same-day sweep.
v4.142 update (July 7, 2026, Run 142): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-100kwb) held no prior PR history and was checked out at a stale v2.5-era baseline (157 entries) while origin/main had independently progressed through 141 merged runs to v4.141 (271 entries); a PR (#156) was briefly opened against that stale base and returned a "dirty" mergeable_state on the merge attempt, the same recurring late-catch pattern documented in the v4.87/v4.88/v4.127 notes — it was closed unmerged and the branch reset to current origin/main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline, rather than reverting 141 runs of accumulated work. The stale draft's eight candidate additions (Public Assembly Law, Civil Protection Law, National Statistics Law, Judicial Experts Law, Cinema Sector Decree-Law, Cybersecurity Decree-Law, Standardization Law, and a Handicraft Sector Law) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact via targeted grep rather than blindly re-added: six of the eight were found already present, in every case at greater precision than the discarded draft — Public Assembly Law 69-4, Civil Protection Law 93-121, National Statistics Law, Cinema/CNCI, Cybersecurity Decree-Law 2023-17, and Standardization Law 2009-38 all already exist as standalone entries (the draft's INLUCC and asylum-law "gaps" were also independently confirmed as non-gaps: INLUCC is already documented inside the whistleblower-protection entry, and Tunisia's lack of a dedicated asylum statute is a correctly-documented absence, not a missing entry). Two candidates survived cross-checking as genuinely absent and were independently re-verified via a fresh source check (legislation-securite.tn plus the Agence Nationale de Métrologie's own institutional pages) before inclusion: (1) the Judicial Experts Law (Law No. 93-61 of June 23, 1993, JORT No. 48, as amended by Law No. 2010-33 of June 21, 2010) — governing the registration, qualification, oath, and discipline of court-appointed experts used across civil, commercial, and criminal proceedings, added as a new standalone entry in the Judicial Organization section; (2) the Legal Metrology Law (Law No. 99-40 of May 10, 1999, amended by Law No. 2008-12 of February 11, 2008, which created the Agence Nationale de Métrologie) — distinct from the already-documented Standardization Law and Industrial Property Law, added as a new standalone entry in the Intellectual Property section next to Standardization. A same-day re-check of the two highest-priority watchlist items found no change: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported, and no JORT publication dated after July 6, 2026 was identified. Two new standalone entries were added (273 total, up from 271); no existing entries were altered. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 273 unique law-card ids (the pre-existing renewables-law id "duplicate" flagged by a naive grep is the same Run 94/125-documented false positive — a <code>-quoted reference inside changelog prose, not a second card), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1274/1274 balanced <div> tags, 273/273 balanced <h3> tags, the header stat (273) matching the true card count, and all real internal anchor links resolving to an existing element id. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.143 update (July 7, 2026, Run 143): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-k45fm2) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note: it held eight of its own commits (a self-contained "v2.0–v2.7" line, last touched at PR #158, 162 entries) but zero of the 142 runs merged into origin/main (273 entries) — confirmed only after a full draft was written and a PR (#159) was opened and returned a "dirty" mergeable_state on the merge attempt, the same late-catch pattern flagged at v4.87/v4.88/v4.127/v4.142. PR #159 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset directly to current origin/main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline. The discarded draft's one candidate addition — a "new" Cybersecurity Decree-Law (2023-17) entry — was cross-checked via targeted grep rather than blindly re-added and found already present since v4.2 (Run 3), confirming Run 142's own note on the same point; it was not re-added. A same-day watchlist re-check found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported (Maher Ktari's June 17, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status is still the last hard data point), and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) trigger dates have not yet occurred. A citation spot-check on two entries not previously individually re-verified — the Nationality Code (Décret-loi n° 63-6 of February 28, 1963, ratified by Law No. 63-7 of April 22, 1963) and the Companies Code (Law No. 2000-93 of November 3, 2000, JORT No. 89 of November 7, 2000) — confirmed both citations exact against jurisitetunisie.com, WIPO Lex, and ILO NATLEX, with no corrections needed. No new standalone entries were added (273 total remains accurate); no existing entries required substantive revision. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.144 update (July 7, 2026, Run 144): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-9k7i2v) reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note: it initially held nine commits of its own drafting a self-contained "v2.0–v2.7" line (162 entries, an independent research pass that had already re-derived the Mabrouk/Chahed and Turki entries and the plastics-bill detail, unaware that all of it was already captured in origin/main at far greater precision) rather than the 143 runs actually merged there (273 entries). The discrepancy was caught via git log/git fetch origin main before any PR was opened, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before redoing this run's research from an accurate baseline. A same-day watchlist re-check found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-first consecutive trigger-date pass, and no JORT publication or ARP plenary vote dated July 6–7, 2026 was identified on any topic. One promising lead — an "ARP séance plénière lundi 7 juillet" article surfaced by search — was investigated via direct source dating and found to be a recycled 2025 piece (leconomistemaghrebin.com URLs dated /2025/06/19/ and /2025/07/03/, reporting on a July 7, 2025 session), not a genuine 2026 development; a search-engine AI summary had conflated the two years, reproducing the same same-date-different-year mislabeling risk already flagged for cross-country leads in the Run 74/77 notes — correctly withheld. A citation spot-check on five further entries not yet individually re-verified since addition — the Water Code (75-16/1975), Mining Code (2003-30), Consumer Protection Law (92-117/1992), Arbitration Code (93-42/1993), and Legal Aid Law (2002-52/2002) — confirmed all five citations exact against jurisitetunisie.com, WIPO Lex, ILO NATLEX, FAOLEX, and ONM primary sources, with no repeals or supersessions found. Two candidate refinements the spot-check initially flagged (sharpening the Water Code entry with the July 3, 2026 IACE "validated but never transmitted to the ARP" framing; adding the Legal Aid Law's Law No. 2007-27 amendment extending aid to criminal cassation appeals) were both independently found to already be present in the existing entries (added at Run 70 and Run 44 respectively) — confirming accuracy rather than revealing gaps. No new standalone entries were added (273 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.145 update (July 7, 2026, Run 145): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-8d6xje) held no PR history and was checked out at a stale v2.6-era baseline (August-2026-dated commits topping out at 187 entries) while origin/main had independently progressed through 144 merged runs to v4.144 (273 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and an empty search_pull_requests/list_pull_requests result for the branch, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before drafting, the same recurring pattern documented in nearly every prior run note. Two parallel research agents ran: one re-checked the standing watchlist for movement dated July 6–7, 2026, the other ran a fresh gap-scan (26 legal areas checked: telecom, postal, civil aviation, maritime, road traffic, public health, food safety, veterinary/animal health, forestry, hunting, tourism, franchising, e-commerce, customs, insurance, banking, capital markets, cheques, insolvency, cybercrime, press/broadcasting, political parties, electoral law, local government, administrative courts, judiciary/notaries/bailiffs — all confirmed already covered, with hunting confirmed as a genuine non-gap since it is governed by Title II of the Forest Code rather than a standalone statute) plus a citation spot-check on five entries never individually re-verified since addition. Watchlist result: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-second consecutive trigger-date pass (Finance Commission ~90% through hearings, BCT still non-responsive as of mid-June 2026, a "July" ministerial meeting appears tied to budget execution rather than this bill specifically). The July 13, 2026 CNAM private-biologists' tiers-payant suspension was independently re-confirmed via three dated sources (Business News July 1, L'Economiste Maghrébin July 2, African Manager) as still genuinely scheduled and unresolved — no change to the existing CNAM entry, which already documents this in full. The July 11, 2026 "ONAT general assembly" watchlist item, by contrast, could not be corroborated this run against any primary source for any of the three plausible ONAT bodies (Ordre National des Avocats — last elective GA September 2025; Ordre des Architectes — last GA cycle 2024; Ordre des Experts Comptables — last GA October 2021); it is flagged here as unconfirmed/possibly misattributed pending a primary source, rather than dropped outright, since a prior run's sourcing for it could not be traced. A dpo-consulting.com blog claim of a "July 11, 2026 enforcement deadline" for the Personal Data Protection/AI bill was independently checked and rejected as unreliable — it contradicts every news source (webdo, WMC, Arabesque, all dated Feb 2026 or later) showing the bill still in committee hearings — and was correctly not added. A July 6, 2026 Business News article was also identified debunking a viral rumor that a "new Code de la route" took effect July 1, 2026; no such law exists, and this is noted here only as active misinformation, not as a real legislative development. The citation spot-check found and corrected three genuine, previously-unnoticed errors, all date/detail inaccuracies rather than fabrications: (1) the Insurance Code's enactment date was corrected from "February 9, 1992" to the verified March 9, 1992 (JORT No. 17 of March 17, 1992), confirmed against jurisitetunisie.com's primary text and the CGA's own official PDF; (2) the Forestry Code's enactment date was corrected from "March 13, 1988" to the verified April 13, 1988 (JORT No. 25 of April 15, 1988), confirmed against the primary JORT text and FAOLEX; (3) the Financial Market Law's amendment-year list dropped an unverifiable "2016" entry and replaced it with the confirmed 1999 (Law 99-92), 2005 (Law 2005-96), 2009, and 2019 (Law 2019-47) amendments. No new standalone entries were added (273 total remains accurate, since this run corrects and enriches existing cards rather than extending the count); three existing entries were corrected and one (Forestry Code) was enriched with the hunting-law clarification. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 273 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all internal anchor links resolving. The July 11 (now flagged unconfirmed) and July 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run; a run landing after July 13 should check for the biologists' suspension's actual outcome rather than re-running the same-day sweep.
v4.146 update (July 7, 2026, Run 146): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-755szj) held eight of its own commits (a self-contained "v2.0–v2.6" line, 162 entries, last touched at PR #158) with zero of the 145 runs merged into origin/main (273 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. Tavily search integration returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on every call this run (as in Runs 68, 71, 76, 86, 95, 107, 131); all findings below rest on WebSearch/WebFetch instead. A same-day watchlist re-check found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-third consecutive trigger-date pass, and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM biologists) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Two candidate leads were investigated and correctly rejected as false positives: (1) a WebSearch summary appearing to show a Tunisian Council of Ministers session adopting a "civil-service code of ethics" bill and a "Marrakech Treaty ratification" bill turned out, on direct WebFetch of the cited pm.gov.tn article, to be a stale September 26, 2024 session (Medical City of Aghlabides decree, Fidaa Foundation, agricultural-workers social security) with search results additionally cross-contaminated by unrelated French (not Tunisian) Conseil des ministres pages from elysee.fr/info.gouv.fr — a new variant of the cross-country/stale-date conflation trap flagged in the Runs 74/77/97/122/144 notes; independent verification also confirmed Tunisia acceded to the Marrakesh Treaty in 2016 (first Arab state to do so) with no pending ratification bill, and that a public-servants code of conduct already exists via Decree No. 2014-4030, so neither lead described a real 2026 development; (2) an adams.africa legal-blog claim that Tunisia's ARP "approved" the pending copyright/Marrakesh-Treaty-implementation bill (No. 047/2024, amending Law No. 94-36 on literary and artistic property — already documented in the Copyright entry as "under review," most recently via a June 24, 2026 parliamentary study day) on May 12, 2026 was independently checked against two further sources (businessnews.com.tn's own March 24 and May 12/13, 2026 reporting) and found to conflate this still-pending bill with the unrelated Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions Law (Organic Law No. 55/2023, already captured in this artifact's Tourism & Culture section via the Run 40 note), which is what the ARP actually adopted on May 12, 2026 (76–1–0) — the Copyright entry's existing "not yet enacted" status stands uncorrected and correct. A targeted citation spot-check found and corrected one genuine, previously-unnoticed imprecision: the SICAR Law entry cited only "Law No. 95-87 of October 30, 1995" as if it were the foundational statute, when independent verification against jurisitetunisie.com and the CMF's own official reference PDF shows the actual investment-company framework was created by Law No. 88-92 of August 2, 1988 (repealing Law No. 59-29, creating the SICAV/SICAF regime), with Law No. 95-87 being a later, real but distinct amendment (following an intermediate 1992 amendment) that specifically developed the SICAR venture-capital sub-category — now corrected to cite both. No new standalone entries were added (273 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding enriches an existing card rather than extending the count); one existing entry (SICAR Law) corrected, and two external false-positive leads investigated and rejected rather than added. Also fixed a recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged at v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, v4.59, v4.62, v4.97, and v4.128): the footer had been left at "Version 4.144 (Run 144)" for two full runs while the title/header already read "v4.145 (Run 145)." A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 273 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, all three version markers synchronized, and all internal anchor links resolving. The July 11 and 13, 2026 dates remain the nearest-term watchlist triggers for the next run.
v4.148 update (July 7, 2026, Run 148): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-t0a7s6) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it held eight commits topping out at a v2.6-era baseline (PRs #77–#158, 162 entries) while origin/main had independently progressed through 147 merged runs to v4.147 (274 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline --all before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main (git checkout -B) rather than drafting on the stale base. Note also that no v4.147 changelog entry had actually been added to this section despite the version bump and the inline Run 147 note in the "Areas for Future Expansion" section below — that gap is not backfilled here to avoid rewriting another run's record, but future runs should add their changelog entry to this section, not only to the future-expansion note. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on every call; all research below used WebSearch only. A fresh watchlist sweep found no movement on any tracked item: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-fifth consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026 (Ktari's mid-June ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status is still the last hard data point, corroborated by a July search turning up only the same pre-July-6 reporting); and the July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's July 7, 2026 cutoff. Several candidate leads surfaced by fresh search were traced and rejected as stale-date false positives rather than genuine developments: a "L'ARP adopte la loi sur les conseils locaux et régionaux" headline traced to the organic law's actual March 2025 adoption (already documented), a "signature de la loi sur les recrutements exceptionnels" item traced to the already-documented Law No. 2025-18 of December 22, 2025, and the July 5, 2026 plastic-bag reduction bill reconfirmed already captured under the existing Draft Law on Reducing Single-Use Plastic Bag Consumption entry (added Run 109). The Internal Security Force Work-Accident Compensation Law amendment (Proposition n° 42/2025, see standalone Security & Counter-terrorism entry) was reconfirmed at the same "report completed, transmitted to ARP Bureau" stage captured at Run 120, with no further movement. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction this run (274 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize checking July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 for actual outcomes the moment each date passes, rather than re-running the same exhausted same-day pending-bill sweep.
v4.149 update (July 7, 2026, Run 149): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-3ee7x2) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it held eight commits topping out at the same v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (PRs #77–#158, 162 entries) while origin/main had independently progressed to v4.148 (274 entries, 148 merged runs). A PR (#166) was opened against this stale base before the discrepancy was caught (via git fetch origin main and a diff-stat check showing ~2,400 lines of apparent "deletions") — the PR was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before restarting, per established practice. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on every call; all research below used WebSearch only. Before discarding the stale draft's research entirely, its three candidate findings were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact: the "Conspiracy Against State Security 2" appeal and the "Secret Apparatus" case verdict were both found already documented (with materially more precision — exact co-defendant names, sentence figures, and case provenance) in the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry, and the gambling/games-of-chance reform bill was found already documented as its own standalone pending-reform entry since Run 6 (v4.5). One candidate survived cross-checking as a genuine, previously-undocumented gap: the "Instalingo" case — a fourth, distinct prosecution (41 defendants including Ghannouchi, tied to a September 2021 raid on a Kalâa Kebira digital-media company, with its own independent February 5, 2025 first-instance verdict and January 14, 2026 appeal upholding a 22-year sentence) — absent from all 148 prior runs despite the artifact's otherwise thorough coverage of the overlapping Case 1/Case 2/Secret Apparatus conspiracy prosecutions against the same defendant. Corroborated across Anadolu Agency, Middle East Monitor, and North Africa Post before being added as an "Update (Run 149)" extension to the existing consolidated entry, consistent with this artifact's practice of grouping thematically related developments under one card rather than opening a thin new one. No new standalone entries added (274 total remains accurate, since this run's finding enriches an existing card); no existing entries required correction beyond the enrichment. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 274 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <div> tags, and all version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.149/Run 149. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 8/11/13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
v4.150 update (July 7, 2026, Run 150): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-qt0k8b) again held only a stale, never-merged baseline (eight commits topping out at v2.6, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) with no open or merged PR of its own, while origin/main had independently progressed through 149 merged runs to v4.149 (274 entries) — confirmed via git log/git diff --stat against origin/main and an empty search_pull_requests result for the branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice, avoiding yet another repeat of the stale-draft-then-conflict pattern documented in most prior run notes. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep prioritizing breadth over re-confirming every watchlist item individually: it checked specifically for a reported outcome of the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee session (none found — unreported a twenty-sixth consecutive trigger-date pass), searched jort.tn directly for July 2026 law/decree publications (none beyond routine "Annonces Légales" numbering), and ran broad "nouvelle loi Tunisie juillet 2026"/"JORT juillet 2026" queries that surfaced no genuinely new enacted legislation.
