The correct originating instrument is Decree No. 57-9 of January 10, 1957, subsequently amended by Décret-loi n° 86-5/1986 (ratified by Loi n° 86-101/1986), the post-revolution procedural reform Décret-loi n° 2011-69 of July 29, 2011 (created a military appeals chamber and expanded civil-party rights, but did not remove civilian jurisdiction), and most recently Loi n° 2024-35 of July 15, 2024, which narrowly amended Article 68's definition of desertion abroad in peacetime (unauthorized border crossing or absence from one's unit for 3+ days in peacetime, reduced to 1 day in wartime; also covers failure to return within 3 days after leave, mission, or training abroad ends). Military courts retain jurisdiction over civilians under Article 8 of the code, and Article 91 (criticizing/insulting the armed forces) remains actively used to prosecute journalists and activists: the 2014 Constitution's transitional provisions kept the 1957 code in force pending amendment, a November 2018 reform bill to exclude civilians from military jurisdiction stalled in the ARP and was never adopted, and civilian prosecutions have continued and increased under President Kais Saied — the National Syndicate of Tunisian Journalists (SNJT) recorded 39+ cases against journalists before military courts since May 2023, including journalist Nasreddine Halimi's March 2025 sentencing by the Kef Military Court over Facebook posts. Confirmed by Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch (World Report 2025), the ICJ, and Tunisia's November 2022 UN Universal Periodic Review submission.
SecurityCriminal

Weapons Law — Law No. 69-33 of June 12, 1969

Enacted: June 12, 1969 | Published: JORT N°23, June 13, 1969 | Implementing decree: Decree No. 70-60 of February 21, 1970
Regulates the import, trade, possession, and carrying of weapons and ammunition in Tunisia. Classifies weapons into five categories: military weapons, defensive/sporting weapons, hunting weapons, historical/collectible weapons, and air rifles. No unified "Code des Armes" exists — the framework remains this single 1969 law plus implementing decrees, with no confirmed later amendment identified.
SecurityCriminal

Law No. 93-121 of December 27, 1993National Civil Protection Office (Fire & Rescue Services) (Run 81 addition)

Enacted: December 27, 1993 | Org-chart decree: Decree No. 99-2058 of September 13, 1999 | Administrative/financial-organization decree: Decree No. 94-568 of March 15, 1994
A wholly new standalone entry — despite extensive coverage of the National Police and National Guard under the Internal Security Forces Statute above, Tunisia's separate civil-defense/fire-and-rescue institution had never been documented. Creates the Office National de la Protection Civile as an établissement public à caractère industriel et commercial (EPIC) with legal personality and financial autonomy, headquartered in Tunis and placed under the supervision of the Ministry of Interior, tasked broadly with disaster and catastrophe response (Art. 2). Confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and cross-checked secondary sourcing; the primary JORT/legislation.tn text itself could not be directly reached (HTTP 503), so this entry carries medium-high rather than primary-source-verified confidence. Note: Decree No. 99-2058 only fixes the Office's organizational chart, not general implementation — Decree No. 94-568 (administrative and financial organization) is the more substantive implementing text.
SecurityAdministrative

Law No. 2002-81 of August 3, 2002 (as amended)Private Security, Guarding & Valuables-Transport Activities (Run 159 addition)

Enacted: August 3, 2002 | Amended: Law No. 2008-14 of February 18, 2008
A wholly new standalone entry, missed by all 158 prior runs. Regulates three categories of private security activity: property monitoring/guarding ("gardiennage"), armed transport of funds and precious metals, and physical protection of persons. Companies and their legal representatives must obtain prior authorization from the Minister of Interior (Art. 4), which is personal, non-transferable, and valid for a five-year renewable term. Licensing conditions (Art. 6) require Tunisian nationality held for at least five years, enjoyment of civil and political rights, absence of convictions for theft, fraud, forgery, or terrorism-related offenses, good conduct, commercial registration, no bankruptcy history, and no concurrent professional activity. Employees (Art. 11) must meet similar nationality/conduct standards, be at least 20 years old, hold a ninth-grade education minimum, and pass physical/mental fitness requirements; agents carry a professional card whose specimen and uniform were separately fixed by Interior Ministry orders of June 11, 2003 (professional-card digitalization introduced in 2024). Article 32 criminalizes unauthorized practice or continuing operations after suspension/revocation, punishable by three years' imprisonment and a fine of TND 50,000–100,000. Law No. 2008-14 subsequently amended several provisions, including the criminal-penalty and transitional-requirement articles. (Run 160 update) Article 15 confines licensed agents strictly to their authorized "gardiennage"/protection mission and expressly bars them from crime-prevention or suspect-pursuit acts, and from any act infringing a person's freedom of movement, physical integrity, or private life (including body/bag searches or seizure of personal effects without the owner's express consent); physical force is prohibited except in self-defense. Article 16 further confines agents to their assigned premises, with street-level intervention authorized only exceptionally, against theft or property damage. Read together, these provisions are the legal basis for the position — confirmed by the Interior Ministry's licensing framework — that private-detective and private-investigation activity (surveillance, filature, personal inquiries) is not a licensable profession in Tunisia, notwithstanding the many "private detective" services advertised online to the Tunisian market.
SecurityAdministrative

"Sumood/Global Sumud Flotilla" Money-Laundering & Terrorism-Financing Prosecutions (Run 91 addition)

Arrests: March 6–7, 2026 | Remanded into custody: March 16, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, entirely distinct from the "state security conspiracy" cluster above — at least seven Tunisian activists linked to the Sumood/Global Sumud Flotilla coordination unit (a pro-Palestinian, Gaza-bound aid-flotilla solidarity network), including Nabil Channoufi, Wael Naouar, Sana Msahli, Jawaher Channa, and Mohamed Amin Bannour, were arrested March 6–7, 2026 and remanded into custody March 16, 2026 after prolonged anti-terrorism-unit questioning. Prosecutors invoke Organic Law No. 2015-26 (Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering, see entry above) alongside general money-laundering provisions, charging the group with "forming a money laundering conspiracy" and misuse of funds raised through an international crowdfunding campaign tied to the first Global Sumud Flotilla's September 2025 Gaza-aid mission. Tunisian and international rights groups (Amnesty International, Middle East Monitor) have called for the activists' release, framing the prosecution as a use of counter-terrorism/AML statutes against pro-Palestinian civic activism rather than a genuine financial-crime case; no trial date or verdict has been reported as of this run.
SecurityCriminal

Affaire Bilel Chaouachi — Terrorist-Organization Leadership Conviction (Run 152 addition)

First instance: December 11, 2025 | Confirmed on appeal: July 6, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the state-security-conspiracy cluster and the Secret Apparatus/Instalingo cases above — an ordinary (non-political) prosecution under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Organic Law No. 2015-26, see Countering Terrorism entry above) of a jihadist leader rather than an opposition figure. Bilel (also spelled Bilal) Chaouachi, alias Abou Yahya Zakaria, a leader of Ansar al-Charia and Daech/ISIS, was prosecuted for terrorist acts committed in Syria, planning terrorist operations inside Tunisia, and recruiting and facilitating young Tunisians' departure to conflict zones. He appeared in a video released after the November 24, 2015 bombing of a presidential security bus in central Tunis, threatening further attacks; he has been listed on Tunisia's national list of terrorist persons (with an associated asset freeze) since 2019. The Tunis Court of First Instance's anti-terrorism chamber sentenced him to 55 years' imprisonment on December 11, 2025, and the Tunis Court of Appeal confirmed the full sentence on July 6, 2026 — corroborated across Kapitalis, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, webdo, and La Presse de Tunisie.
SecurityCriminal

Affaire "Créateurs de Contenu" — Mass TikTok/Instagram Prosecution (Run 155 addition)

Justice Ministry announcement: October 27, 2025 | Verdict: July 6, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the state-security-conspiracy, Secret Apparatus, Instalingo, and Chaouachi cases above — an ordinary (non-political) mass prosecution of social-media "content creators." Following an October 27, 2025 Justice Ministry announcement of prosecutions targeting online content "contrary to public morals," the criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance referred 18 defendants to trial and, on July 6, 2026, sentenced them to terms ranging from 1 to 27 years' imprisonment. Charges spanned production, dissemination, publication, or transmission of sexually explicit videos and images tied to TikTok and Instagram activity, alongside human trafficking, money laundering, and drug-trafficking/promotion offenses. Prosecutors relied on three statutes in combination: Organic Law No. 2015-61 of August 3, 2015 (preventing and combating trafficking in persons, see Criminal Law section), Decree-Law No. 2022-54 of September 13, 2022 (combating offenses relating to information and communication systems, see Digital Law section), and Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015 (countering terrorism and money laundering, see entry above) (webdo.tn, July 7, 2026).
SecurityCriminalDigital

🛂 Nationality, Immigration & Asylum

Nationality Code — Law No. 63-6 of February 28, 1963

Enacted: February 28, 1963 | In force: 1963 | Amended: 1975, 2002, 2010
Defines Tunisian nationality by birth, descent, naturalization, and reacquisition. Originally limited transmission of nationality through the paternal line; the 2010 amendment allows Tunisian mothers married to foreign nationals to transmit nationality to their children. Governs loss, renunciation, and deprivation of nationality. Naturalization requires 5 years of lawful residence. Pending reform: a bill filed at the ARP on February 27, 2026 by 36 co-sponsoring deputies (now identifiable as projet de loi n° 17/2026) would tighten naturalization by requiring proof of legal/regular entry into Tunisia plus 7 consecutive years of legal residence (up from 5), while easing rules for stateless persons born in Tunisia and creating an expedited "exceptional interest" naturalization track for high-skill foreign nationals. The ARP Rights and Freedoms Commission examined the bill at a session held May 7, 2026, which press coverage of that session also described as addressing a requirement that a stateless child's parents hold legal Tunisian residency status for the child's citizenship eligibility. Under committee review as of this update; not yet adopted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 6: Nationality by birth to a Tunisian father (jus sanguinis)
  • Art. 12: Naturalization requires 5-year lawful residence
  • 2010 amendment: children of Tunisian mother and foreign father may acquire nationality
  • Art. 30–35: Loss and deprivation of Tunisian nationality
NationalityCivil

Law No. 68-7 of March 8, 1968Entry and Residence of Foreigners

Enacted: March 8, 1968 | In force: 1968 | Amended: 1975, 2004
Governs the entry, stay, and expulsion of foreign nationals in Tunisia. Requires visa for most non-Maghreb nationals (bilateral visa exemptions apply). Establishes residence permit categories and procedures. Governs administrative detention pending deportation. Tunisia has no comprehensive asylum law; refugee status was determined by UNHCR under its mandate, but in June 2024 Tunisian authorities instructed UNHCR to suspend new registration, pre-registration, and refugee-status-determination processing (services to already-registered refugees continued); the government's stated rationale was an intent to establish a national asylum system, which had not been enacted as of Human Rights Watch's 2025 and 2026 World Report chapters on Tunisia. Update (Run 103): an Amnesty International report of July 2, 2026 quantified the suspension's effect for the first time in this artifact — UNHCR-registered refugees in Tunisia fell from 18,362 in June 2024 (when the undisclosed suspension order was given) to 7,515 by April 2026, a roughly 59% decline — and documented a government-organized "voluntary return" program for migrants operating since June 2025, alongside continued reporting of abuse against refugees and asylum-seekers left without a functioning status-determination process. (Added Run 106) See the separate Passports & Travel Documents Law entry below for the distinct 1975 statute whose border-crossing offenses (rather than this law's visa/residence-permit framework) have been used to prosecute refugee-aid humanitarian workers.
NationalityAdministrative

Passports & Travel Documents Law — Law No. 75-40 of May 14, 1975

Enacted: May 14, 1975 | In force: 1975 | Amended: Organic Law No. 2015-46 of November 23, 2015; Organic Law No. 2024-23 of March 11, 2024
Governs issuance of Tunisian passports and travel documents — ordinary passports, diplomatic/service passports, laissez-passer "B," and titres de voyage "C" and "D" for stateless persons and refugees. Articles 38, 39, and 41 criminalize facilitating, arranging, or concealing a person's irregular entry into or exit from Tunisian territory, and participating in an organization or agreement to do so — offenses distinct from the Irregular Migration Law's (2004-6) criminalization of the migrants themselves and from the 2016 Anti-Human Trafficking Law's exploitation-focused offenses. Most recently amended by Organic Law No. 2024-23 of March 11, 2024, the companion statute to that year's biometric national ID card reform (see the National Identity Card Law entry), which established the biometric passport; an earlier amendment, Organic Law No. 2015-46 of November 23, 2015, updated passport-issuance procedures. Genuinely new standalone entry (Run 106): human rights organizations have documented Articles 38/39/41 being used against humanitarian workers rather than smugglers. Tunisian authorities closed the Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR) — a UNHCR-mandated organization providing asylum-application assistance and emergency care since 2016 — in May 2024, arresting founder Mustapha Djemali and project manager Abderrazek Krimi (plus four other staff) on charges under these articles for allegedly facilitating and harboring irregular border crossings through the CTR's refugee-assistance activities. The Tunis Court of First Instance convicted both men on November 24, 2025 (2 years, 6 months suspended; released the same day, having already served roughly 18 months in pretrial detention), and the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber upheld the conviction on May 14, 2026. Human Rights Watch (May 11, 2026) and Amnesty International characterize the prosecution as an abusive misuse of anti-smuggling provisions to criminalize refugee-aid work, consistent with this artifact's broader documentation of civil-society prosecutions (see the Associations and Judicial Organization sections). (Cross-ref, Run 213): a distinct organization prosecuted under the same articles is tracked separately at the "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" entry below.
NationalityCriminal

EU–Tunisia Memorandum of UnderstandingStrategic and Global Partnership

Signed: July 16, 2023
Non-binding political declaration between the EU and Tunisia covering five pillars: macroeconomic stability, trade and investment, green energy, people-to-people contacts, and — most emphasized in implementation — migration and mobility. The EU pledged roughly €105 million for border-control capacity, €150 million in direct budget support, and a conditional €900 million in macro-financial assistance tied to an IMF program Tunisia ultimately declined to conclude. Tunisia committed to combating smuggling networks, conducting search-and-rescue operations in its territorial waters, and accepting returns of third-country nationals. The MOU bypassed parliamentary ratification on both sides. In January 2025 the European Commission overhauled its disbursement mechanism following reports of abuses by EU-funded security forces against migrants; Tunisian officials have since pushed to renegotiate the arrangement toward greater reciprocity. Update (Run 43): marking the MOU's third anniversary, a joint letter dated July 1, 2026 from Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International, the International Commission of Jurists, the Committee to Protect Journalists, EuroMed Rights, and Avocats Sans Frontières was sent to European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen calling for a full human-rights review of the partnership and conditionality on further EU funding, citing continued reports of abuses against migrants in Tunisian territory. Forms the policy backdrop to the domestic 2023–2026 migrant-policy shift above and the EU's 2026 "safe country of origin" designation below.
NationalityAdministrative

EU Designation of Tunisia as a "Safe Country of Origin"EU Pact on Migration and Asylum

European Parliament vote: February 10, 2026 (408–184–60) | Council approval: February 23, 2026 | Rules in force: June 12, 2026
Not Tunisian domestic law, but an EU-law development directly affecting Tunisian nationals abroad: under the EU's Pact on Migration and Asylum, the European Parliament and Council designated Tunisia one of several countries presumed "safe" EU-wide, subjecting Tunisian asylum-seekers in the EU to accelerated, presumptive-rejection border-procedure examination of their claims from June 12, 2026 onward. Distinct from, but connected to, the domestic 2023–2026 migrant-policy shift and UNHCR-registration suspension documented above and the 2023 EU-Tunisia migration MOU above. Flagged as a single-source-class finding from this run pending independent corroboration of the exact vote tallies and in-force date against a European Parliament or Official Journal of the EU primary text.
NationalityUnconfirmed

Irregular Migration Law — Law No. 2004-6 of February 3, 2004

Enacted: February 3, 2004 | In force: 2004
Criminalizes irregular emigration by Tunisian nationals ("harraga") with penalties of up to 3 years imprisonment. Criticized by human rights organizations as criminalizing movement. Used to prosecute individuals attempting to cross to Europe irregularly. Tunisia also receives migrants from sub-Saharan Africa; their status is governed by the 1968 Immigration Law and UNHCR procedure. Note: No domestic asylum law as of 2026; draft asylum law discussed since 2013 but not enacted.
NationalityCriminal

Affaire Abdallah SaïdCriminalization of Migrant-Solidarity Humanitarian Work

Detained: November 12, 2024 | First-instance conviction: April 22, 2026 (Médenine Court of First Instance) | Public-prosecutor appeal: pending
Abdallah Saïd, a physician with over thirty years in public health and founding president of the Médenine-based humanitarian association "Les Enfants de la Lune" (which coordinates a network of local associations providing medical and psychological support, schooling assistance, and economic-integration programs to disabled Tunisian children, migrants, and other vulnerable groups), was placed in pre-trial detention on November 12, 2024 by the national financial-crimes investigation unit. He was initially referred to the anti-terrorism judicial unit, which found no terrorism-related basis for the case. After roughly a year and a half in provisional detention, the Médenine Court of First Instance convicted him on April 22, 2026 and sentenced him to one year's imprisonment; the public prosecutor has appealed the verdict, and Saïd remains detained. Human rights organizations, including the Committee for Justice (CFJ), characterize the case as part of the same pattern documented in the Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry above and in the criminalization of migrant-solidarity work under the Irregular Migration Law framework — using provisional detention and shifting accusations (initially framed as terrorism-adjacent, ultimately prosecuted on other grounds) against humanitarian actors assisting migrants and refugees in southern Tunisia. A July 8, 2026 report by the NGO Intersection pour les droits et les libertés cited Saïd's case among 88 documented civil-society-related judicial and administrative actions recorded between May 2024 and May 2026.
CriminalNationalitySocial

Affaire "Tunisie Terre d'Asile"Criminalization of Migrant-Solidarity Humanitarian Work

Arrested: May 2024 | First-instance conviction: January 5, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance, criminal chamber) | Released: January 5–6, 2026
A distinct case from the Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR) prosecution above, involving a different organization and defendants despite an identical legal theory and near-identical fact pattern. Sherifa Riahi (former director), Yadh Bousselmi, and Mohamed Joo — current or former staff of "Tunisie Terre d'Asile," the Tunisian branch of the French NGO France Terre d'Asile, which assisted asylum-seekers and migrants under a program coordinated with Tunisian authorities, local governments, and UN agencies — were placed in pre-trial detention in May 2024 alongside two former municipal officials, Mohamed Iqbal Khaled and Imen Ouardani. Initial charges of money laundering and abuse of function were dropped during proceedings, leaving prosecution solely under Passports & Travel Documents Law (No. 75-40 of 1975) Articles 38, 39, and 41 (facilitating or sheltering irregular entry, exit, or stay) and the 1968 Law on the Status of Foreigners (Law No. 68-7). After roughly 19 months (580 days) in detention, the Tunis Court of First Instance convicted all five on January 5, 2026 to two years' imprisonment with suspended execution; all were released the same or following day. Eighteen other co-defendants who remained at liberty during the proceedings were acquitted. The International Commission of Jurists, Human Rights Watch, and Amnesty International condemned the conviction as criminalizing legitimate humanitarian work, consistent with this artifact's broader documentation of civil-society and migrant-solidarity prosecutions (see the Administrative Suspensions and Affaire Abdallah Saïd entries). No appeal outcome had been reported as of this run.
CriminalNationalitySocial

National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad — Law No. 2016-68 of August 3, 2016 (reform bill pending, 2024–2026)

Enacted: August 3, 2016 | In force: 2016 | Implementing decree: Government Decree No. 2019-820 of September 10, 2019 | Reform bill: n° 2024-064, under ARP committee review since 2024
Creates the National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad (Conseil National des Tunisiens Résidant à l'Étranger), a consultative public body with legal personality and administrative/financial autonomy, seated in Tunis and attached to the ministry responsible for Tunisians abroad. Represents Tunisia's diaspora (estimated at over 1.5 million people) before public authorities and issues an annual activity report to the President, the ARP Speaker, and the Head of Government. Government Decree No. 2019-820 (September 10, 2019) set the Council's administrative and financial organization. A reform bill (projet de loi n° 2024/064) has been under continued examination by the ARP's Foreign Affairs, International Cooperation, Tunisians Abroad, and Migration Committee since 2024, with a working session held June 4, 2026 on its text, followed by a further joint hearing on June 22, 2026 with the President of the National Council for Tunisians Abroad herself. The bill would restructure the Council's general assembly, introduce individual, direct, voluntary-basis voting (including electronic voting) for diaspora representatives on a gender-parity basis — replacing the current framework's limited effective functioning since 2016 — and reposition the Council as a horizontal coordination body unifying public policy across ministries dealing with Tunisians abroad and mobilizing expatriate expertise for national economic development. Not yet adopted as of this run. (Cross-ref added Run 109): separately from this reform bill, Article 83 of the Finance Law 2026 already enacted new tax and customs advantages specifically for this Council's constituency — see the Finance Law 2026 entry (Tax & Fiscal section).
NationalityAdministrativePending

⚖️ Judicial Organization

Judicial Organization Law — Law No. 2013-13 of April 2, 2013 (as amended 2022)

Enacted: April 2, 2013 | In force: 2013 | Amended: 2022
Organizes the court system of Tunisia: Cantonal Courts (minor matters), Courts of First Instance (24, one per governorate), Courts of Appeal (3 — Tunis, Sousse, Sfax), Court of Cassation (apex civil/criminal court, Tunis), Administrative Court (apex administrative jurisdiction), and the Constitutional Court (established under 2022 Constitution). Specialized jurisdictions include the Terrorism Chamber at TFI Tunis.
Court structure
  • Cantonal Courts: minor civil matters, misdemeanor infractions
  • Courts of First Instance (24): civil, commercial, criminal chambers
  • Courts of Appeal (3 — Tunis, Sousse, Sfax): appellate jurisdiction
  • Court of Cassation (Tunis): reviews points of law only
  • Administrative Court: all public law disputes
  • Terrorism Chamber: specialized jurisdiction at TFI Tunis level
  • Constitutional Court: established under 2022 Constitution (Arts. 116–120)
AdministrativeConstitutional

Judicial Statute — Law No. 67-29 of July 14, 1967 (as amended)

Enacted: July 14, 1967 | In force: 1967 | Amended: 1997, 2013, 2022
Governs the appointment, promotion, transfer, discipline, and removal of judges. Establishes the Superior Council of the Judiciary (Conseil Supérieur de la Magistrature — CSM) as the body responsible for judicial governance. Post-2022, the CSM's composition was changed by presidential decree, significantly reducing judicial independence according to the UN Special Rapporteur on the Independence of Judges and Lawyers. Update (Run 40): tensions over judicial and legal-profession independence escalated in mid-2026. The Tunisian National Bar Association (ONAT) staged an escalating protest campaign — a May 13 Bar Council escalation vote, a symbolic May 18 red-armband action, then rallies at courts in Tunis (Bab Bnet), Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef, and Siliana — culminating in a nationwide lawyers' general strike on June 18, 2026 demanding reform of the profession's legal framework and stronger judicial-independence guarantees; Bâtonnier Boubaker Belthabet called it a success, but no government response had followed as of late June 2026, and on June 9, 2026 forty international legal and human-rights organizations issued a joint statement condemning an escalating campaign against lawyers, judges, and the Bar. Separately, judge Anas Hmaidi — president of the Association of Tunisian Magistrates (AMT), re-elected at its February 2026 congress despite government pressure — was sentenced to one year in prison on April 6, 2026, prompting condemnation from the International Union of Magistrates and the African Commission on Human and Peoples' Rights, both linking the prosecution to the continued absence of a permanent, independent Superior Judicial Council (see the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council entry below). Update (Run 42): the Tunis Court of Appeal's correctional chamber confirmed Hmaidi's one-year sentence on July 1, 2026 in a ruling issued by default (par défaut/in absentia), this time ordering immediate execution of the sentence — a harsher procedural posture than the first-instance judgment. TAP/Diwan FM reporting confirmed the same legal qualification (Art. 136 Penal Code, obstruction of the freedom to work) tied to his conduct at a June 13, 2022 hearing in Monastir; Hmaidi continues to dispute the legitimacy of the Provisional Supreme Council of the Judiciary body that lifted his immunity on September 20, 2022. Update (Run 43): ONAT's annual general assembly was reported scheduled for July 11, 2026, a potential venue for the Bar to set conditions for renewed dialogue with the government following the unresolved June 18, 2026 general strike; no government response to the strike's demands had been reported as of this run. Update (Run 46): confirmed and refined — the July 11, 2026 assembly is not an elective session (Belthabet was already elected bâtonnier for a 2025–2028 term in September 2025); Belthabet has framed its purpose as convening the Bar Council and general assembly to "evaluate the evolution of the dispute [with the authorities] and adopt consequent measures," i.e., a strategy review on whether to escalate or seek renewed dialogue. Separately, the public prosecutor at the Tunis Court of Appeal filed an appeal challenging the quorum/procedural validity of an earlier lawyers' extraordinary general assembly, with a hearing reported set for June 10, 2026 — outcome not independently confirmed. As of this run (July 2, 2026), the government still had not proposed any official or informal mediation, per Belthabet, though ONAT says it remains open to dialogue. Update (Run 60): on July 2, 2026, UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers Margaret Satterthwaite publicly stated that Hmaidi's conviction "should have been overturned," a statement the AMT amplified in its own denunciation of the July 1 appeals ruling as unfair — the first UN Special Procedures reaction specifically naming this case, distinct from the broader CSM-composition criticism already cited above. Update (Run 91): a further, previously undocumented lawyer-harassment case closed a genuine gap — Ahmed Souab, a lawyer, former administrative judge, and prominent human rights defender, was arrested at his home on April 21, 2025 by anti-terrorism officers over public statements he made after representing defendants in the "state security conspiracy" case (see that standalone entry above), and was convicted at first instance to 5 years' imprisonment following a trial Amnesty International said lasted only seven minutes. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal reduced his sentence to 10 months' firm imprisonment plus a 2-year suspended sentence and ordered his immediate release on February 23, 2026 (already served), also lifting the administrative-surveillance measure imposed at first instance — a rare, if partial, judicial correction amid the broader pattern of prosecutions against lawyers and judges documented in this entry. Update (Run 156): the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly's sourcing — flagged as unconfirmable on independent re-checks by Runs 119, 128, 131, 145, and 147 — is now corroborated by a further, previously uncited independent source: a June 30, 2026 Réalités Magazine article ("Avocats: Dans l'attente d'un dialogue") confirms the assembly "devrait avoir lieu samedi 11 juillet et pourrait faire connaître les conditions de reprise du dialogue," matching the strategy-review framing already documented above from Runs 43/46 rather than contradicting it.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Decree-Law No. 2022-11 of February 12, 2022Provisional Supreme Judicial Council

Enacted: February 12, 2022 | Published: JORT No. 2022-016
The specific decree-law referenced above: dissolved the elected Supreme Judicial Council (created under Organic Law No. 2016-34) and replaced it with a "Provisional Supreme Council of the Judiciary," composed predominantly of senior ex officio magistrates plus members appointed by the President from candidates submitted via the Prime Ministry. Retains three specialized sub-councils (judicial, administrative, financial). Remains the operative — still "provisional" — body as of mid-2026, with no permanent council established to replace it.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Decree-Law No. 2022-35 of June 1, 2022Magistrate Dismissal Power (Completing Decree-Law No. 2022-11)

Enacted: June 1, 2022
Grants the President of the Republic power to summarily dismiss any magistrate by simple presidential decree in cases of declared "emergency," threat to public security, or harm to "the country's superior interest," based on a report from unspecified "competent authorities." Dismissal triggers automatic criminal referral; the dismissed magistrate cannot appeal until after a final criminal judgment. Used the same day to dismiss 57 judges and prosecutors, condemned by HRW, Amnesty International, and EuroMed Rights; the Tunisian Association of Magistrates continued to denounce the dismissals as unresolved injustice as of June 2026. Update (Run 263): a professional-reconversion detail, per a July 10, 2026 Business News report: six of the magistrates dismissed under this decree-law have since completed the process of registering as practicing lawyers with the Tunisian Bar, joining the profession rather than pursuing (or after exhausting) the criminal-referral track the decree-law attaches to their dismissal — illustrating one concrete downstream career outcome for the 57 dismissed magistrates nearly four years on, alongside the still-unresolved criminal referrals and appeals tracked elsewhere in this entry.
AdministrativeConstitutionalCriminal

Organic Law No. 2013-53 of December 24, 2013Transitional Justice

Enacted: December 24, 2013 | In force: 2014
Establishes Tunisia's transitional justice framework including the Truth and Dignity Commission (IVD — Instance Vérité et Dignité). Covers: truth-seeking, reparations, institutional reform, and accountability for human rights violations committed between July 1, 1955 and December 31, 2013. IVD operated from 2014–2019; issued final report in 2019 documenting systematic torture, political persecution, and economic crimes.
AdministrativeConstitutionalCriminal

Organic Law No. 2013-43 of October 2013National Authority for the Prevention of Torture (INPT) (Run 66 addition)

Adopted by NCA: October 9, 2013 | Enacted: October 21 or 23, 2013 (sources diverge on the exact day; JORT No. 85/2013) | Members elected by ARP: March 29–30, 2016
A wholly new standalone entry — despite 65 prior research runs and the extradition entry below referencing "torture risk" as a ground for refusal, no dedicated entry existed for Tunisia's National Preventive Mechanism against torture. Implements Tunisia's OPCAT obligations (ratified June 2011) via the Instance Nationale pour la Prévention de la Torture (INPT), the first such mechanism in the MENA region: 16 members elected by Parliament from human-rights associations, magistrates, doctors, and child-protection specialists, holding legal immunity and empowered to conduct unannounced visits to any place of deprivation of liberty; authorities may refuse a visit only for narrow, written, reasoned reasons. The INPT remains active as of mid-2026, distinct from the (separately documented) Truth and Dignity Commission above, which addressed historical rather than ongoing torture: it publicly criticized severe prison overcrowding in November 2024 (population risen from ~23,000 to over 32,000–33,000 against ~17,000 capacity), published a "Guide to Detainees' Rights" in November 2025, and issued a "zero impunity" statement for the UN International Day in Support of Victims of Torture on June 27, 2026. No confirmed reports of INPT defunding or leadership removal were found for 2024–2026.
JudicialCriminalConstitutional

"State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions and Related 2025–2026 Convictions

Arrests from: February 2023 | Instalingo first-instance: February 5, 2025 | Case 1 sentences: April 19, 2025 | Case 1 appeal: November 27–28, 2025 | Instalingo appeal: January 14, 2026 | Case 2 appeal: February 2, 2026 | "Secret Apparatus" case verdict: June 2, 2026 | Bensedrine sentenced: June 26, 2026 | Smara appeal: July 2, 2026 | "Ramadan Evening Gathering" verdict: April 14–15, 2026
A mass prosecution of dozens of opposition politicians, lawyers, and activists on charges of conspiracy against state security, prosecuted under general Penal Code state-security provisions (Arts. 72–85, see Criminal Law section) rather than a dedicated statute — added here as a standalone entry given its significance to Tunisia's post-2021 rule-of-law trajectory, previously undocumented in this artifact despite 39 prior research runs. Case 1 (defendants including Khayam Turki, Ridha Belhaj, Ghazi Chaouachi, Jaouhar Ben Mbarek, Issam Chebbi, and Abdelhamid Jelassi) produced severe sentences on April 19, 2025, described by Human Rights Watch as a "sham trial"; on appeal (Nov 27–28, 2025), 34 of 37 sentences (5–45 years) were upheld and 3 defendants acquitted (Lazher Akremi, Hattab Ben Slama, Noureddine Boutar), with several sentences increased (Chebbi, Ben Mbarek, Chaouachi, and Belhaj raised from 18 to 20 years) while businessman Kamel Ltaief's sentence was reduced from 66 to 45 years, remaining the heaviest in the case. A related but separate Case 2, focused on Ennahda party leadership, produced an appeals ruling on February 2, 2026 sentencing roughly 20 defendants to 3–35 years; Ennahda leader Rached Ghannouchi's sentence was raised from 14 to 20 years (56 years cumulative across his cases), and co-defendants Kamel Guizani, Rafik Abdessalem, Lotfi Zitoun, and Mouadh Ghannouchi received 35 years in absentia — Amnesty International described the ruling as deepening a "rule-of-law crisis." Separately, Sihem Bensedrine — journalist and former president of the Truth and Dignity Commission (IVD, see the Transitional Justice entry above) — was sentenced to 25 years in prison (two consecutive terms of 20 and 5 years across two cases) on June 26, 2026 by the Tunis Court of First Instance, among the most severe individual sentences of a rights defender since 2021; the case concerns "using position to gain unfair advantage," fraud, and forgery allegedly connected to an arbitral reconciliation agreement the IVD concluded with businessman Slim Chiboub during her tenure, with Bensedrine, Mabrouk Korchid, and Chiboub jointly ordered to pay a fine of roughly 1.776 billion dinars. (Run 153 addition): lawyer and fellow former IVD official Khaled Krichi — arrested June 3, 2026 on the same case — was sentenced the same day (June 26, 2026) to a cumulative 10 years (two five-year terms) and named in the same joint fine, for exploiting his position to obtain undue advantages and violating procedural regulations; the Tunisian Human Rights League (LTDH) condemned both sentences as disproportionate (Businessnews, La Presse de Tunisie, Kapitalis, Human Rights Watch, July 7, 2026). Opposition politician Abir Moussi (Free Destourian Party leader, detained since October 3, 2023, when she was arrested attempting to file a legal challenge against presidential decrees on electoral redistricting) faces at least two further parallel cases beyond this conspiracy cluster. (Run 168 update): in the "bureau d'ordre" case, concerning an incident at the Presidency of the Republic, a 12-year first-instance sentence (December 2025) was reduced on appeal to 10 years by the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber on March 13, 2026 — 9 years for "aggression aimed at disrupting the functioning of the state, incitement to violence between citizens, and public disorder," plus 6 months each for unauthorized processing of personal data and obstruction of freedom to work; PDL co-defendant Meriem Sassi received 1 year on the same two lesser counts. Separately, an ISIE-initiated complaint under Decree-Law No. 2022-54 Art. 24 ("spreading false information") produced a first-instance 2-year sentence (August 5, 2024), reduced to 16 months on appeal, then annulled and remanded by the Court of Cassation; a newly-constituted Tunis Court of Appeal panel re-confirmed the 2-year sentence on December 30, 2025 (Amnesty International, Kapitalis, Business News, cross-corroborated), replacing this entry's previously vague "ruling reported around March 2026" placeholder. Amnesty International condemned both prosecutions as unjust. See the Judicial Statute entry above for the related prosecution of AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi, whose one-year sentence was confirmed on appeal (with immediate execution ordered) on July 1, 2026 — Run 42 update. Update (Run 60): a related but distinct terrorism-chamber case opened July 3, 2026: the Tunis Court of First Instance's terrorism chamber rejected pre-trial release requests (June 30, 2026) for Ennahda secretary-general Ajmi Lourimi and co-defendant Mossaâb Gharbi, prosecuted under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Law No. 2015-26, see Criminal Law section) for failing to report a terrorism-related offense; no verdict has been reported as of this run. (Run 155 update): the specialized terrorism chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance delivered its verdict on July 4, 2026 — three-year prison sentences for both defendants, Lourimi for failing to report a terrorism-related offense and Gharbi for harboring, concealing, and facilitating the escape of a person involved in terrorism cases. Ennahdha denounced the case as fabricated (July 4, 2026), and the LTDH condemned the verdicts against Lourimi and Gharbi as disproportionate in a July 4–5, 2026 statement (Businessnews, webdo.tn, Mosaïque FM, Réalités, Kapitalis). (Run 190 correction/update): that same LTDH statement was previously read as naming Elyes Chaouachi a co-defendant in this Lourimi/Gharbi case; direct verification (Businessnews, Kapitalis, July 4–5, 2026) shows Chaouachi's July 4, 2026 sentence was instead handed down the same day in a wholly distinct anti-terrorism-chamber case, arising from a July 2024 arrest alongside a sought Ennahdha activist — the LTDH statement addressed both same-day verdicts together, not a shared docket, and this entry is corrected accordingly. Elyes Chaouachi — a France-based activist and son of Case 1 defendant Ghazi Chaouachi — received 15 years in that separate case, bringing his cumulative total across a long series of predominantly in-absentia convictions to more than 95 years and 8 months (up from over 80 years reported May 13, 2026, and 38 years reported November 25, 2025); he attributes the accumulating prosecutions to his public advocacy for freedoms and democracy and to his father's status as a conspiracy-case defendant, and the LTDH's July 4, 2026 statement separately denounced this sentence as "severe" and raising doubts about fair-trial guarantees and the presumption of innocence. Update (Run 63): a further related but distinct prosecution — former MP Sahbi Smara, charged not with joining the conspiracy but with fabricating one to incriminate political opponents ahead of the October 2024 presidential election — had his 11-year first-instance sentence reduced to approximately nine and a half years on appeal, July 2, 2026 (Kapitalis, Business News, Tunisie Numérique, Gnet News). Smara was separately sentenced in March 2026 to 6 months' imprisonment for assaulting Abir Moussi inside Parliament while both were sitting deputies, illustrating how this cluster of prosecutions has widened to include cases arising from, rather than merely alongside, the original conspiracy proceedings. Update (Run 81): Case 1 defendant Khayam Turki carries two further, entirely distinct convictions that should not be conflated with his conspiracy sentence above: a April 21, 2026 Tunis Court of First Instance ruling of 14 years for fraud, money laundering, and aggravated breach of trust, arising from a UAE company's complaint over a North African land-acquisition project (Kapitalis, Businessnews); and a July 3, 2026 first-instance conviction of 4 years on strictly tax-related offenses tied to his business-portfolio management, distinct from both prior cases (La Presse de Tunisie, Businessnews, allAfrica). Three separate Turki dockets are now on record: the conspiracy case (above), the April 2026 money-laundering case, and the July 2026 tax case. Update (Run 101): a wholly distinct, considerably larger and more severe case — the "affaire de l'appareil secret" ("Secret Apparatus" case) — reached a first-instance verdict June 2, 2026 from the specialized anti-terrorism criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance, and should not be conflated with the Case 1/Case 2 conspiracy prosecutions above: it was opened in 2022 following a complaint by lawyers for the families of assassinated leftist politicians Chokri Belaïd and Mohamed Brahmi (killed 2013), alleging that a clandestine Ennahda security structure — directly supervised, per the prosecution, by Rached Ghannouchi — orchestrated political assassinations, conducted espionage, and infiltrated state institutions; the 35 defendants were charged under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Law No. 2015-26, see Criminal Law section) with constituting a terrorist organization, rather than under the Penal Code Arts. 72–85 state-security-conspiracy provisions underlying Case 1/Case 2. Sentences: Ghannouchi received life imprisonment plus 30 additional years; alleged mastermind Mustapha Khedher received life imprisonment plus 96 additional years (the case's heaviest sentence); former Prime Minister Ali Laarayedh received 42 years; eleven further defendants received life sentences plus additional terms, and thirteen others received 10–48 years. Ennahdha denounced the sentences as "scandaleuses" and politically motivated; rights groups (DAWN) and Ghannouchi's defense contested the verdict's legitimacy (Al Jazeera, Bloomberg, La Presse de Tunisie, businessnews.com.tn, Le Courrier de l'Atlas, Kapitalis, Middle East Eye, Middle East Monitor, all June 1–6, 2026). No appeal outcome has been reported as of this run. See the separate Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial entry (Run 239 addition) for the distinct direct prosecution of former prosecutor Béchir Akremi and Habib Ellouze over the investigative/prosecutorial handling of the Belaïd killing itself — a different docket with no defendant overlap. (Run 256 addition): a third, distinct Belaïd-case docket — the direct prosecution of the assassination's execution/recruitment/planning groups — reached a final appeal verdict on October 14, 2025 (two death sentences upheld, four life terms, lesser terms for the remainder of 24 defendants, Ennahda's leadership not implicated); see the new Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Main Case entry below. Update (Run 149): a fourth, previously undocumented distinct prosecution — the "Instalingo" case — was identified and cross-checked against 148 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Instalingo" in any prior entry). Following a September 10, 2021 raid on Instalingo, a digital-media/content-production company in Kalâa Kebira (Sousse governorate), 41 defendants including Ghannouchi were charged with conspiring against state security, seeking to alter the nature of the state, incitement to violence, and money laundering — distinct from Case 1, Case 2, and the Secret Apparatus case above, both in defendant count and in its own independent sentence track. A Tunis first-instance court sentenced Ghannouchi to 22 years, an 80,000 TND fine, and a 10-year public-office ban on February 5, 2025 (co-defendants: 5–54 years); the Tunis Court of Appeal upheld the 22-year sentence and the bulk of convictions on January 14, 2026, while reducing one female defendant's sentence and ordering her release (Anadolu Agency, Middle East Monitor, North Africa Post, corroborated against Middle East Eye's broader case-cluster reporting). No further appeal outcome has been reported as of this run. Update (Run 172): a fifth, previously undocumented distinct prosecution — the "Ramadan Evening Gathering" case (also reported as the "El-Moussamara"/"discussion circle" affair) — was identified and cross-checked against 171 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Ramadan gathering," "Moussamara," or the named co-defendants in any prior entry). The charges arise from a small political gathering Ghannouchi hosted at his home in April 2023, days before his arrest, at which he discussed the country's political situation; prosecutors relied on recordings of the gathering, which the defense contends were fabricated. On April 14–15, 2026, the criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance sentenced Ghannouchi, Youssef Nouri, and Ahmed Mcharki to 20 years' imprisonment each on charges of "conspiracy to change the form of the state," with five further defendants — including Ghannouchi's son-in-law and former foreign minister Rafik Abdessalem, former MP Maher Zaid, and blogger Mohamed Samati — sentenced in absentia to 20 years; the defense stated Ghannouchi's lawyers were barred from his initial hearing for 48 hours and that counter-terrorism procedural rules were improperly applied to a non-terrorism charge (Jeune Afrique, Espace Manager, Sada News Agency, corroborated against a further wire-service report, all April 14–15, 2026). Tunisian press (Jeune Afrique, Espace Manager) reported Ghannouchi's cumulative fixed-term sentences across all five cases now exceed 60 years, on top of the separate life sentence in the Secret Apparatus case — a figure that should be read as press-reported rather than an official court tally, consistent with this entry's existing practice of flagging cumulative-total figures as approximate.
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire de l'assassinat de Chokri Belaïd — Akremi/Ellouze Direct Prosecution (Run 239 addition)

Assassination: February 6, 2013 | First-instance verdict: March 4, 2026 | Appeal hearings: June 23, then July 6, 2026 (both postponed) | Next appeal hearing: July 13, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 238 prior runs found no dedicated card for this docket, only an indirect mention within the "Secret Apparatus" case update (Run 101), which covers a related but legally distinct prosecution (a clandestine-organization/terrorism charge against Rached Ghannouchi and 34 co-defendants). This entry instead covers the direct criminal prosecution over the February 6, 2013 assassination of opposition leader Chokri Belaïd, tried before the specialized terrorism-chamber criminal court of the Tunis Court of First Instance. On March 4, 2026, that court convicted former Republic Prosecutor Béchir Akremi (revoked from office) to a combined 23 years across two related dossiers, chiefly for concealing documents and evidence determinant to the assassination investigation, and convicted Ennahdha figure and former MP Habib Ellouze — along with the imam of the Bab El Khadra mosque and several other co-accused — to 13 years each; all convicted parties, the Chokri Belaïd family's defense collective, and the public prosecution itself filed appeals. The appeal, before the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism-chamber criminal division, was first calendared for June 23, 2026, postponed to July 6, 2026, and postponed again at that hearing to July 13, 2026 — the same trigger date already tracked in this artifact for the unrelated CNAM/private-biologists tiers-payant suspension; no reasons for either postponement were reported, and no outlet has yet reported the July 13 hearing's outcome (Businessnews, Webdo.tn, Mosaïque FM, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager, all June 23 – July 7, 2026). Distinct from — and should not be conflated with — the "Secret Apparatus" case above, which prosecutes an alleged clandestine Ennahda security structure under Anti-Terrorism Law charges of orchestrating the Belaïd and Mohamed Brahmi assassinations among broader allegations; this entry's docket instead prosecutes the direct investigative/prosecutorial handling of the Belaïd killing itself, with no defendant overlap between the two cases. (Run 256 addition): a third, distinct docket — the direct prosecution of the assassination's execution/recruitment/planning groups (the original gunmen and their handlers, not the investigative complicity prosecuted here) — reached its own final appeal verdict on October 14, 2025, entirely separate from this Akremi/Ellouze docket's still-pending appeal; see the new Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Main Case entry below.
CriminalPending

Affaire de l'assassinat de Chokri Belaïd — Main Case (Execution/Recruitment/Planning Groups) (Run 256 addition)

Assassination: February 6, 2013 | Alleged gunman Kamel Gadhgadhi killed by security forces: February 2014 | First-instance verdict (4 death sentences): March 27, 2024 | Final appeal verdict: October 14, 2025
A wholly new standalone entry closing a gap that had eluded all 255 prior runs: the direct prosecution of the assassination itself — the execution, recruitment, and planning groups — which is a third, distinct docket within the broader Belaïd case file, alongside (but separate from) the Akremi/Ellouze direct prosecution above and the "Secret Apparatus" case's organizational-responsibility prosecution. The terrorism-specialized chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance sentenced four defendants to death at first instance on March 27, 2024 (alleged gunman Kamel Gadhgadhi had already been killed in a February 2014 security operation and was never tried). On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's terrorism chamber delivered what multiple outlets reported as a final verdict on October 14, 2025, covering 24 defendants: it upheld death sentences by hanging for Mohamed Aouadi and Ezzedine Abdellaoui (plus 105 and 10 further years respectively on related counts), handed down life imprisonment to four further defendants, and lesser terms down to four years to the remainder — while explicitly not implicating the Ennahda movement or its leadership in the killing, a finding Ennahda publicly characterized (statement of October 15, 2025) as clearing it and its president "beyond any doubt" after "twelve years of false and malicious accusations." Belaïd's family criticized the ruling as leaving the case's separate organizational-responsibility file — covering the alleged clandestine security apparatus and its leadership, i.e. the docket now covered by the Secret Apparatus case above — unresolved, and questioned who was "blocking" that parallel investigation. Independently corroborated across TAP (Agence Tunis Afrique Presse), Al-Estiklal, and The Arab Weekly, all reporting the same October 14–15, 2025 verdict and sentences. This verdict predates this artifact's v4.0 baseline by several months yet had never been documented in any of the 255 prior runs' otherwise-thorough coverage of the Belaïd case file, apparently because research repeatedly focused on the still-pending Akremi/Ellouze and Secret Apparatus dockets rather than the (wrongly assumed, still-pending) main case, which had in fact already concluded.
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire Marzouki / Daïmi / Kilani — "Paris Press Conference" Case (Run 205 addition)

Verdict: June 20, 2025 (Tunis Court of First Instance, terrorism-specialized chamber)
A wholly new standalone entry, cross-checked against 204 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Marzouki," "Daïmi," or "Kilani" in any prior entry) — a genuine and significant gap, since it is the only tracked prosecution of a former Tunisian head of state. Exiled former president Moncef Marzouki (2011–2014, Tunisia's first president elected after the 2011 revolution) was sentenced in absentia on June 20, 2025 to 22 years' imprisonment on terrorism and state-security charges, arising from a Paris press conference at which he, former presidential adviser Imed Daïmi, and former National Bar Association (ONAT) president Abderrazak Kilani sharply criticized Tunisian state institutions and members of the judiciary; Daïmi and Kilani received identical 22-year in-absentia sentences, with at least two further co-defendants reportedly sentenced alongside them (The New Arab, Arab News, both corroborating the same verdict). All named defendants remain in exile; Marzouki called the ruling "surreal." Distinct from — and should not be conflated with — the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above, which does not name any of these three defendants.
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire Habib Haouas / SOREAL — Organized Money Laundering Conviction (Run 85 addition)

Arrest warrant: November 17, 2023 | Customs reconciliation agreement: June 22, 2026 | Verdict: June 29, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, entirely distinct from the Mabrouk/Chahed/Turki dockets above — this is an ordinary (non-political) organized-crime financial case, not part of the "state security conspiracy" cluster. Businessman Habib Haouas, former director of metal-recycling firm SOREAL Tunisie (founded 1997) and resident abroad since at least 2017, was sentenced on June 29, 2026 by the specialized financial-corruption criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance to 22 years' imprisonment (a co-defendant received 24 years) for organized-group money laundering, abusive exploitation of company mechanisms, and property damage, with customs, exchange-control, and tax-fraud offenses also charged; the underlying investigation dates to a November 17, 2023 arrest warrant from the Economic and Financial Judicial Pole. A June 22, 2026 reconciliation agreement with the Customs Directorate-General (~12 million TND) was presented to the court by defense counsel but did not alter the sentence (Businessnews, Kapitalis, La Presse de Tunisie, L'Economiste Maghrébin, all June 30–July 1, 2026).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Makram Jlassi — Embezzlement Conviction (Run 87 addition)

Dismissed from Justice Ministry post: effective July 10, 2024 (JORT) | First-instance sentence: March 11, 2026 (5 years) | Appeal ruling: July 4, 2026 (raised to 8 years)
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from both the "state security conspiracy" cluster and the Haouas/SOREAL case above — an ordinary embezzlement/breach-of-trust prosecution of a former Justice Ministry official. Makram Jlassi, a former mission officer (chargé de mission) in the Justice Minister's cabinet dismissed from that post effective July 10, 2024, was convicted by the Tunis Court of First Instance's criminal chamber on March 11, 2026 and sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment for embezzlement and breach of trust, following a complaint filed by the Tunisian Association for the Prevention of Road Accidents. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber confirmed the underlying conviction but increased the sentence to 8 years on July 4, 2026 (reported same-day by WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, webdo, African Manager, Réalités, and Kapitalis).
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire Samir Abdelli — Money Laundering & Terrorism-Financing Conviction (Run 181 addition)

Verdict: July 7, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance, terrorism-specialized criminal chamber)
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 180 prior runs found no match for "Abdelli." Samir Abdelli, a lawyer and 2014 presidential candidate previously named in the SwissLeaks and Panama Papers disclosures, was sentenced by the terrorism-specialized criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance to 18 years' imprisonment with no possibility of parole, after two years in pre-trial detention, on charges of money laundering, terrorism-related offenses, and conspiracy against the state's internal and external security — charged under the same general Penal Code state-security provisions and Anti-Terrorism Law (No. 2015-26) framework as the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above, but a distinct docket with no defendant overlap identified. His driver and secretary, tried at liberty in the same case, received 3 years and 2 years respectively. No appeal had been reported as of this run (Business News, Kapitalis, Mosaïque FM, all July 7–8, 2026).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Chawki Tabib — Former INLUCC & Bar Association President, Forgery Conviction (Run 182 addition)

Verdict: May 21, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance)
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 181 prior runs found no match for "Tabib." Chawki Tabib, former president of the National Bar Association (ONAT) and, from 2016–2020, former president of INLUCC (see the Declaration of Assets/INLUCC entry above), was sentenced on May 21, 2026 to a combined 10 years' imprisonment (7 years for forgery plus 3 years for possession/use of forged documents). The prosecution traces to a complaint arising from an INLUCC report issued during Tabib's own tenure that had accused former Prime Minister Elyes Fakhfakh of a conflict of interest. Independently corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie, the IAPL Monitoring Committee on Attacks on Lawyers, and CRLDHT, the latter characterizing the conviction of a former anti-corruption-authority president on forgery charges as marking a further stage in the erosion of the rule of law.
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire Ayachi Hammami — Defense Counsel Prosecuted as a Defendant in His Own Clients' Case (Run 181 addition)

Added to suspect list: May 2023 | Indicted: October 2023 | Appeal conviction: ~November 2025 (5 years) | Incarcerated: December 3, 2025 | Hunger strike: ended January 15, 2026 (43 days) | UN Human Rights Council 62nd session closed: July 8, 2026
A genuine gap despite 180 prior research runs' detailed coverage of the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above: a Run 104 changelog note asserted this was "already substantively captured within the precision of the existing consolidated entries," but a direct full-text check found no prior mention of Hammami's conviction, detention, or hunger strike anywhere in the artifact — only his December 2025 arrest had been noted in passing in that changelog entry itself, not in a law-card. Ayachi Hammami, a prominent human-rights lawyer, represented several Case 1 defendants in the conspiracy prosecution until May 2023, when the investigating judge added him to the suspect list; he was indicted in October 2023 and, on appeal, sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment in the same mega-trial, beginning to serve the sentence at El Mornaguia prison on December 3, 2025. He conducted a 43-day hunger strike protesting his detention and restrictions on visits from his own defense counsel, ending it January 15, 2026; supporters marked 100 days of detention on March 12, 2026 by forming a national support committee. UN special-procedures experts (OHCHR) said they were "shocked" by the conviction, calling it a direct attack on the independence of the legal profession; the UN Human Rights Council's 62nd session (closed July 8, 2026) saw the UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers and UN Human Rights Chief Volker Türk cite Hammami's case alongside those of AMT president Anas Hmaidi (see the Judicial Statute entry above) and Sihem Bensedrine (above) as part of a broader pattern of retaliation against Tunisia's legal and judicial community (OHCHR, Front Line Defenders, FIDH, LDH, Jeune Afrique, Kapitalis, HRW, all cross-corroborated).
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire Taoufik Mekacher — Unsecured Public-Bank Loans Conviction (Run 92 addition)

Related ex-bank-official first-instance sentence: February 1, 2026 (2 years) | Mekacher first-instance verdict: July 3, 2026 (6 years)
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the Haouas/SOREAL and Jlassi cases above — another ordinary (non-political) financial-crime prosecution from the same specialized financial-corruption criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance that has produced several of this cluster's convictions in the same window (see the Run 81 Mabrouk/Chahed and Turki updates on the Anti-Terrorism/AML and State Security Conspiracy entries above). Businessman Taoufik Mekacher — previously convicted in July 2021 to 5 years for uncovered checks and forgery, a separate matter — was sentenced on July 3, 2026 to 6 years' imprisonment for obtaining substantial loans from a public bank without sufficient guarantees, causing the institution financial harm; the court entered a non-lieu (discontinuance) for a co-defendant bank official in this same ruling. The case is part of a broader "affaire des prêts bancaires sans garantie" implicating some 31 suspects including a former minister (Samir Saïed, ordered kept in provisional detention as of a January 2025 ruling), within which a separate ex-bank-official defendant had already been convicted to 2 years on February 1, 2026 for the same underlying loan-approval practice (La Presse de Tunisie, Businessnews/Mkacher variant spelling, allAfrica, all July 3, 2026; Kapitalis and Webdo for the broader case background).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Taïeb Rached — "Cassations Sans Renvoi" Judicial Corruption Case (Run 103 addition)

Unauthorized chambers created: 2019 | First-instance verdict: October 27–28, 2025 | Appeal verdict: May 23, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry — distinct from the Haouas/SOREAL, Jlassi, and Mekacher financial-crime cases above, and the first prosecution of a judge of this rank in Tunisian judicial history. In 2019, as First President of the Court of Cassation, Taïeb Rached created two chambers without legal basis (Nos. 35 and 36) and appointed magistrates Abderrazek Bahouri and Marouane Tlili to sit on them; those chambers issued "cassation sans renvoi" rulings (cassation without remand for retrial) that benefited businessmen Nejib Ismaïl and Fethi Jenayah, shielding them from further prosecution and causing the Tunisian state an estimated one billion TND in losses. The Tunis Court of First Instance's specialized financial-corruption chamber convicted Rached on October 27–28, 2025 to 30 years' imprisonment, with Bahouri and Tlili (both since revoked from the magistracy) sentenced to 20 years each, alongside civil damages ordered against the judges and businessman Fethi Jenayah. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized financial-corruption chamber reduced all three judges' sentences to 10 years each on May 23, 2026, while confirming roughly 935 million TND in civil damages against the three judges and Jenayah; other businessman co-defendants in the case include Adel Jenayah, Kamel Taboubi, and El Wardi Nouisri. Independently corroborated across Kapitalis, L'Économiste Maghrébin, La Presse de Tunisie, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, African Manager, and allAfrica.
CriminalJudicialFinancial

Sworn Interpreters Law — Law No. 94-80 of July 4, 1994(Run 179 addition)

Enacted: July 4, 1994 | Fee schedule: Joint Order of the Ministers of Justice and Finance, December 19, 1996 | Registration-exam rules: Ministerial Order of March 2, 2000 | Procedure manual: Ministerial Order of November 24, 1998
A wholly new standalone entry — a companion legal-profession statute to the Notaries Law (No. 94-60, above) and the Judicial Officers/Bailiffs statute, neither of which had ever been cross-referenced against this one despite both being individually documented. Organizes the profession of interprète assermenté (sworn interpreter/translator), the only category of person legally authorized to produce certified translations for use before Tunisian courts, notaries, and public administration. Establishes a roll (registre) of sworn interpreters maintained by territorial jurisdiction under the supervision of the Ministry of Justice, with admission conditioned on passing a qualifying examination whose regulations and syllabus are fixed by ministerial order (most recently the Order of March 2, 2000). A 1996 joint order of the Ministers of Justice and Finance fixed sworn interpreters' official fee schedule, and a 1998 Ministry of Justice order approved the profession's standard procedure manual. The Ministry of Justice and Tunisian consular authorities abroad (e.g., the French Consulate General in Tunis) publish official lists of registered sworn interpreters by language pair. Confirmed via ILO NATLEX's citation of the law's full title and Tunisia's Ministry of Justice site.
Judicial

Mediation Law — Law No. 2002-82 of August 3, 2002

Enacted: August 3, 2002 | In force: 2002
Establishes the legal framework for civil and commercial mediation as an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanism. Mediators must be registered and trained. A mediation agreement, once signed by parties and approved by the competent court, is enforceable as a court judgment. The Startup Act (2018) encourages mediation for commercial disputes involving labeled startups. Reduces court caseloads and offers faster, confidential dispute resolution.
JudicialCivil

Notaries Law — Law No. 94-60 of May 23, 1994(Run 44 correction)

Enacted: May 23, 1994 | Predecessor: Law No. 88-27 of July 12, 1988
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited this statute as "Law No. 67-39 of June 8, 1967" — no such law could be found in any source searched (NATLEX, jurisitetunisie.com, legislation-securite.tn), and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. The verified currently-operative statute, confirmed directly from jurisitetunisie.com's full-text reproduction and cited in the preamble of the 2011 decree-law reorganizing the lawyer profession, is Law No. 94-60 of May 23, 1994 — 65 articles across 8 chapters (registration on the professional roll; powers; legal status; rights/obligations; discipline; regional chambers and national association; transitional provisions). It separated the notarial function from that of court bailiff (huissier de justice), a split not present in the probable predecessor, Law No. 88-27 of July 12, 1988. Notaries are public officers authenticating deeds, contracts, inheritance declarations, and real property transactions; notarial acts are enforceable without court order, and all real property transfers, mortgage deeds, and company-formation documents must be notarized. Supervised by regional chambers of notaries and a national professional association.
JudicialCivil

Law No. 87-79 of December 29, 1987 (as amended)Lawyers Statute (Barreau de Tunisie)

Enacted: December 29, 1987 | In force: 1988 | Amended: 1999, 2009
Governs the legal profession and bar association in Tunisia. The Bar Association (Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie — ONAT) is the self-regulatory body for all lawyers admitted to the Tunisian bar. Sets qualification requirements (law degree + 3-year pupillage), bar admission conditions, and continuing education obligations. Regulates fee structures, professional conduct, confidentiality (avocat-client privilege), conflict of interest rules, and disciplinary procedures. Guarantees lawyers' immunity from prosecution for oral or written statements made in the exercise of their functions before courts. 2026 ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff (added Run 170): resolves the "July 11 ONAT" watchlist item this artifact had carried unconfirmed since Run 145 — it refers to this Law's own regulatory body (avocat.org.tn), not the architects' or chartered-accountants' orders. ONAT delivered written demands for legal-profession reforms to the Justice Ministry on January 23, 2026, centered on three grievances: prosecutions targeting individual lawyers, the continued absence of a permanent Supreme Judicial Council (see the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council entry above), and the composition/appointment procedure of criminal-court chambers. An exceptional ONAT general assembly held May 1, 2026 was challenged by the Tunis Court of Appeal's prosecutor general over its procedural legality (hearing held June 10, 2026); ONAT's Council responded May 13, 2026 by approving regional strikes, red armbands, and a boycott of improperly-composed criminal chambers, culminating in a national general strike and mobilization at the Tunis Palace of Justice on June 18, 2026. ONAT president Boubaker Ben Thabet maintained the actions were lawful and did not disrupt court operations, while stating openness to dialogue. (Run 249 addition): at the June 10, 2026 hearing, some 580 lawyers filed a formal acte de constitution to defend ONAT's position against the prosecutor general's challenge; the Tunis Court of First Instance postponed examination of the case to October 28, 2026 — a date business commentary (Business News) noted risks mooting the appeal's practical effect, since the actions taken at the contested May 1 assembly (strikes, boycotts) would largely have run their course well before that hearing date. ONAT's routine annual general assembly, held Saturday July 11, 2026, was expected to set out conditions for resuming dialogue with the Justice Ministry if the dispute remained unresolved by that date; no outlet had reported that assembly's outcome as of this run's cutoff — a future run should check for its resolution.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–10: Bar membership; qualification requirements; pupillage (stage) period
  • Art. 11–20: Professional rights — audience before all courts; freedom of speech in court
  • Art. 21–28: Inviolability of lawyer-client communications; prohibition of office searches without ONAT involvement
  • Art. 29–36: Professional conduct rules; prohibition of conflicts of interest
  • Art. 37–44: Fee arrangements; prohibition of contingency fees as primary arrangement
  • Art. 45–52: ONAT governance — National Council, regional bars (Tunis, Sousse, Sfax, etc.)
  • Art. 53–60: Disciplinary council; sanctions ranging from warning to permanent disbarment
JudicialAdministrative

Law No. 2002-52 of June 3, 2002Legal Aid (Aide Judiciaire) (Run 44 correction)

Enacted: June 3, 2002 | Amended: Law No. 2007-27 of May 7, 2007
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited this statute as "Law No. 2002-42 of April 3, 2002" — no such law could be verified in any source searched, and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. The verified statute, confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and jurisitetunisie.com's "bureau d'aide judiciaire" page, is Law No. 2002-52 of June 3, 2002, amended by Law No. 2007-27 of May 7, 2007 (extending eligibility to criminal cassation appeals). Establishes Tunisia's legal aid system for civil and criminal matters: any natural person lacking sufficient income to cover court/enforcement costs without compromising basic necessities may apply, at any stage of proceedings; non-profit legal persons and foreigners (subject to reciprocity) also qualify. Applications are decided by a Legal Aid Bureau attached to each Court of First Instance, chaired by the Republic Prosecutor with a Finance Ministry representative and a lawyer as members; decisions are not subject to appeal, though a new application may be resubmitted. A separate, older track — Decree No. 74-882 of September 26, 1974, replaced by Organic Law No. 2011-2 of January 3, 2011 — governs legal aid specifically before the Administrative Court.
JudicialSocial

Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 (Run 161 correction)Judicial Officers (Huissiers de Justice)

Enacted: January 30, 2018 | Predecessor: Law No. 95-29 of March 13, 1995 (itself replacing a 1958 governing text) | In force: 2018
Run 161 correction: prior runs cited a non-existent "Law No. 66-49 of July 2, 1966" as the governing statute; independent verification (WebSearch plus a direct fetch of the legislation-securite.tn primary-source text) found no such 1966 law and instead confirmed the actual succession: a 1958 governing text was repealed and replaced by Law No. 95-29 of March 13, 1995, which was in turn repealed and replaced by the currently operative Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 — both successor statutes titled identically, "portant organisation de la profession des huissiers de justice." Regulates the profession of judicial officers (huissiers de justice) in Tunisia — public officers responsible for drafting and serving protests, notices, and summonses; executing judicial and administrative decisions; conducting inspections and judicial auctions (ventes aux enchères); and performing voluntary collections. Huissiers are appointed by the Justice Minister following a competitive exam at the Institut Supérieur de la Magistrature (judges and registered lawyers are exempted from the exam). Regulated by the National Chamber of Huissiers (Chambre Nationale des Huissiers de Justice) under a disciplinary council chaired by the national dean. Pending reform: a further modernization bill (digitized archives, revised access conditions to the profession, expanded legal protections for huissiers) is under review, supported by the professional order; a provision within it would additionally allow mutual-consent divorce to be finalized before a huissier-notaire without court involvement, which women's rights groups have criticized as weakening Personal Status Code judicial safeguards for wives. Not yet enacted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 5–9: Status and qualifications — Tunisian nationality (5+ years), legal age, law degree required
  • Art. 7: Judges and registered lawyers exempted from the competitive entry exam
  • Art. 11–12: Oath before the appellate court; registration of signature with the Justice Ministry; liability insurance required within 3 months of appointment
  • Art. 13: Core duties — service of process, execution of judicial/administrative decisions, inspections, judicial auctions, voluntary collections
  • Art. 23–38: Professional conduct — bar on other public office, commerce, or legal practice; mandatory continuing education
  • Art. 29–32: Two numbered registers (general and execution-related), inspected every 6 months, records retained 15 years
  • Art. 43–44: Fee tariff set jointly by the Justice and Finance Ministers, reviewed every 3 years; right to refuse work absent an advance payment
  • Art. 62–75: Professional discipline — warnings, suspension, or permanent removal via disciplinary council
JudicialCivilPending

Law No. 93-61 of June 23, 1993Judicial Experts (Experts Judiciaires) (as amended by Law No. 2010-33 of June 21, 2010)

Enacted: June 23, 1993 | Published: JORT No. 48, June 29, 1993 | Amended: June 21, 2010
Governs court-appointed experts (experts judiciaires) used across civil, commercial, criminal, and administrative proceedings for technical assessments ordered by a judge — registration on an official expert roll organized by specialty, qualification and integrity conditions, oath-taking, professional discipline, and fee-setting for court-ordered expert reports. The 2010 amendment revised the chapters on registration, professional obligations, and discipline. Complements the general expert-evidence provisions of the Code of Civil and Commercial Procedure and the Code of Criminal Procedure, which authorize courts to order expert reports but do not themselves regulate the experts' professional status.
JudicialCivil

Extradition & Mutual Legal Assistance — Code of Criminal Procedure, Book IV, Ch. VIII (Arts. 308–335)

Base code: Law No. 68-23 of July 24, 1968 | Extradition chapter amended by Law No. 93-113 of November 22, 1993
Tunisia has no standalone extradition or mutual-legal-assistance statute — the current cybercrime entry elsewhere in this artifact notes only in passing that the Budapest Convention "complement[s]" extradition mechanisms, without a dedicated entry for the underlying domestic framework.
