Extradition of foreigners is instead governed entirely by Book IV, Chapter VIII of the Code of Criminal Procedure (conditions, Arts. 308–315; procedure, Arts. 316–328, including a chambre d'accusation review and presidential decree under Art. 324; effects, Arts. 329–335), supplemented by bilateral and multilateral treaties. Tunisian nationals cannot be extradited; political offenses and torture risk are recognized grounds for refusal. Update (Run 66): beyond the 1972 France convention, dedicated bilateral extradition treaties are in force with Belgium (1990), Malta (2006), Portugal (1998), Turkey (1983), Syria (2007), and Germany (1969); a new Algeria–Tunisia judicial-cooperation and extradition convention was signed December 15, 2021 and ratified by Tunisia via Presidential Decree No. 2022-312 of April 8, 2022 (Algeria's ratification reported February 2025). No dedicated extradition treaty was confirmed with Italy, Libya, Egypt, Morocco, the UK, or the US. Multilaterally, Tunisia is party to the UN Convention against Transnational Organized Crime/Palermo Convention (ratified June 19, 2003) and its Trafficking Protocol (July 14, 2003), UNCAC (ratified September 23, 2008), and the 1998 Arab Convention on the Suppression of Terrorism (ratified via Law No. 99-10 of February 15, 1999). A 2025–2026 test case saw the Paris Court of Appeal reject Tunisia's extradition request for Halima Ben Ali, daughter of the late president, in April 2026, reportedly over inadequate fair-trial guarantees.
JudicialCriminal

Affaire Mohamed Ben Salem — Default Customs Conviction, Arrest & Release(Run 207 addition)

Default conviction: 2 years (date not independently confirmed) | Arrested: July 8, 2026 | Released after opposition filed: July 9, 2026 | Merits hearing set: July 21, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 206 prior runs found no match for "Ben Salem." Mohamed Ben Salem, a former Minister of Agriculture and Ennahda party leader, was arrested July 8, 2026 in the Cap Bon region on the strength of a prior default (in absentia) conviction to 2 years' imprisonment for customs-law offenses, handed down by the correctional chamber of the Tunis Court of Appeal. Ben Salem filed formal opposition to the default judgment, and the appellate prosecutor ordered his release on July 9, 2026 under the procedure applicable to defaulted convictions contested in this way. A merits hearing — allowing Ben Salem to mount a full defense rather than be tried in absentia — has been set for July 21, 2026 before the Tunis Court of Appeal's summer correctional chamber (Kapitalis, webdo.tn, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Tuniscope, all July 9, 2026). Distinct from the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above — this case rests on customs allegations rather than state-security or speech-related charges, and Ben Salem is not named among that cluster's defendants.
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire ETAP / Champ Gazier de Nawara — Public-Funds Embezzlement Appeal Ruling(Run 257 addition)

Underlying contract: Nawara gas field concession (Tataouine) | First-instance conviction: prior to this update (date not independently confirmed) | Appeal ruling: July 11, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the Mabrouk/Chahed/Turki, Haouas/SOREAL, Jlassi, and Mekacher financial-corruption dockets above — this case involves the state hydrocarbons company. On July 11, 2026, the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized criminal (financial-corruption) chamber ruled on the appeal in an embezzlement-of-public-funds case tied to state oil company ETAP's (Entreprise Tunisienne d'Activités Pétrolières — see the Hydrocarbons Code entry for ETAP's general statutory role) Nawara gas field concession contract in Tataouine. The court upheld the 2-year first-instance conviction of former ETAP CEO Mohamed Ali Khalil, while reducing the sentences of two other former officials, Abdelwaheb Khamassi and Monji Nairi, from 8 years each to 4 years each on appeal; the accompanying fines were maintained unchanged for all three defendants (directinfo.webmanagercenter.com and allAfrica, both July 11, 2026, cross-corroborated).
CriminalFinancialEnergy

Draft Organic Law on Enforcement of Court Rulings ("Ending Administrative Impunity")(Run 272 addition)

Filed with the ARP's General Legislation Committee: reported July 10, 2026 | Status: newly filed, committee review stage
A wholly new standalone entry — a 19-article member's bill filed by 10 deputies that would criminalize non-enforcement of final court rulings by public administration, imposing up to 3 years' imprisonment plus fines on officials who obstruct or fail to execute judicial decisions, and mandating annual administrative-compliance reporting to track enforcement rates. Framed by sponsors as closing a long-standing accountability gap in which Tunisian courts issue judgments against the administration — including reinstatement and compensation orders — that go unenforced for years without consequence for the officials responsible. Reported by La Presse ("Quand la justice retrouve ses clés," July 10, 2026) and reprinted by allAfrica (July 11, 2026); distinct from the general judicial-independence and court-organization framework already documented elsewhere in this section. No committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run.
JudicialAdministrativePending

🌍 International Law & Key Treaties

Key International Conventions Ratified by Tunisia

Various dates
Tunisia is party to numerous international conventions forming part of domestic law upon ratification (monist approach per Art. 20 of the 2022 Constitution).
Major conventions
  • UN Charter (1956) — upon independence
  • ICCPR — ratified March 18, 1969
  • ICESCR — ratified March 18, 1969
  • CEDAW — ratified September 20, 1985 (reservations partially lifted 2011)
  • Convention on the Rights of the Child — ratified January 29, 1992
  • UN Convention Against Torture — ratified September 23, 1988
  • African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights — ratified 1983
  • Arab Charter on Human Rights — ratified 2008
  • New York Convention on Arbitral Awards (1958) — ratified July 22, 1967
  • ILO Core Conventions — 62 conventions ratified
  • TRIPS Agreement — WTO member since March 29, 1995
  • WIPO Copyright Treaty — ratified 2012
  • Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — member since March 1, 2001
  • UNCAC — ratified October 23, 2008
  • Paris Agreement on Climate Change — ratified July 3, 2017
  • UNCLOS — ratified April 24, 1985
  • EU-Tunisia Association Agreement — in force March 1, 1998
Constitutional

Withdrawal of the Article 34(6) DeclarationProtocol to the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights on the Establishment of an African Court

Reported: March 2026
Tunisia reportedly withdrew its declaration under Article 34(6) of the Ouagadougou Protocol establishing the African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights, which had permitted individuals and NGOs to file complaints directly with the Court without first exhausting proceedings before the African Commission. If confirmed, the withdrawal would close that direct-access route for Tunisian complainants going forward, mirroring earlier withdrawals by Rwanda, Tanzania, Benin, and Côte d'Ivoire, and would mark a rollback of one of Tunisia's post-2011 international human-rights-law commitments (Tunisia ratified the underlying African Charter in 1983 and the Protocol establishing the Court separately). Flagged as a single-source finding from this run's research pass, pending stronger corroboration (e.g., an African Court press release or JORT/Ministry of Foreign Affairs text) in a future run — consistent with this artifact's practice of noting unverified leads rather than silently omitting or overstating them.
ConstitutionalUnconfirmed

EU-Tunisia Association Agreement — Law No. 97-37 of July 1, 1997

Signed: July 17, 1995 | Ratified by Tunisia: July 1, 1997 | In force: March 1, 1998
Association agreement between Tunisia and the European Union — the first Euro-Mediterranean Partnership agreement. Established a free trade area for manufactured goods, progressively eliminating tariffs by 2008. Includes provisions on services, capital, intellectual property, and political dialogue. Supplemented by Action Plans under the European Neighbourhood Policy. DCFTA (Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area) negotiations launched 2016, not yet concluded as of 2026.
CommercialConstitutional

Arab Maghreb Union Treaty — 1989 (Marrakesh Treaty)

Signed: February 17, 1989 | Ratified: 1989
Treaty establishing the Arab Maghreb Union (UMA) among Tunisia, Algeria, Morocco, Libya, and Mauritania. Aims for eventual economic integration and free movement. In practice largely dormant due to Algeria-Morocco tensions. Bilateral agreements within the AMU framework remain in force, including free movement protocols for Maghreb nationals within the region.
CommercialConstitutional

Decree No. 23 of 2025Ratification of the North Western Sahara Aquifer System Consultation Mechanism

Underlying agreement signed: April 24, 2024 (Algeria, tripartite agriculture-ministers meeting) | Ratifying decree published: JORT Issue No. 4, January 11, 2025
A two-article decree: Art. 1 ratifies the tripartite agreement establishing a consultation mechanism ("mécanisme de concertation") for shared management of the North Western Sahara Aquifer System (SASS/NWSAS), a transboundary fossil groundwater resource shared by Tunisia, Algeria, and Libya and critical to southern Tunisia's water supply; Art. 2 covers publication. Algeria ratified the same agreement in parallel.
EnvironmentalConstitutional

UNCLOS Ratification — April 24, 1985

Ratified: April 24, 1985 | Convention in force: November 16, 1994
Tunisia ratified the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS). Defines maritime zones: 12-nautical-mile territorial sea, 24 nm contiguous zone, and 200 nm Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ). Bilateral maritime delimitation agreements concluded with Italy (1971, continental shelf) and Libya (boundary established by ICJ ruling in 1982). Tunisia's EEZ in the Mediterranean is significant for fisheries and hydrocarbon exploration rights.
ConstitutionalTransport

WTO Membership and Bilateral Trade Agreements

WTO membership: March 29, 1995 | Ongoing bilateral and regional agreements
Tunisia is a WTO member and party to multiple bilateral and regional trade agreements: Pan-Arab Free Trade Area (PAFTA, 1997); EU Association Agreement (1998); Agadir Agreement with Egypt, Jordan, and Morocco (2007, implementing diagonal cumulation of origin); EFTA-Tunisia Free Trade Agreement (2005). DCFTA talks with the EU and negotiations for African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) integration remain active as of 2026.
CommercialConstitutional

Agadir Agreement — Law No. 2004-20 of February 24, 2004 (ratification) | Operational: 2007

Agreement signed: February 25, 2004 | Ratified by Tunisia: February 24, 2004 | Operational: March 2007
The Agadir Agreement on the establishment of a free trade zone between Tunisia, Morocco, Egypt, and Jordan — four countries with EU Association Agreements. Eliminates tariffs and non-tariff barriers on trade in goods between member states. A key feature is the application of diagonal cumulation of origin with the EU, allowing products manufactured from inputs originating in any member country to benefit from EU preferential tariffs. Managed by the Agadir Technical Unit (ATU) based in Amman. Tunisia's exports to Egypt and Jordan are primarily industrial goods, olive oil, and chemicals. The agreement fits within the Euro-Mediterranean Partnership framework and enhances supply chain integration between North Africa and the Middle East.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–5: Establishment of the free trade zone; objectives
  • Art. 6–10: Tariff elimination schedule for industrial goods (immediate for most)
  • Art. 11–15: Rules of origin: Pan-Euro-Mediterranean cumulation rules apply
  • Art. 16–20: Agricultural goods: partial liberalization with tariff-rate quotas for sensitive items
  • Art. 21–25: Trade facilitation: simplified customs procedures, single document
  • Art. 26–30: Dispute resolution mechanism; role of ATU
  • Art. 31–35: Safeguard clauses; anti-dumping provisions
CommercialConstitutional

AfCFTA Ratification — Law No. 2021-19 of June 7, 2021

Agreement adopted: March 21, 2018 | Tunisia ratified: June 7, 2021 | Trading began: January 1, 2021 (for earlier ratifiers)
Tunisia ratified the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) in June 2021, becoming one of 44 countries to ratify by that date (out of 55 AU member states). The AfCFTA creates the world's largest free trade area by number of participating countries, covering 1.3 billion people and a GDP of approximately $3.4 trillion. Phase I covers trade in goods (tariff reduction) and trade in services. Phase II covers investment, intellectual property, competition, and e-commerce. Tunisia hopes to use the AfCFTA to diversify its export markets beyond Europe (currently ~70% of trade), targeting sub-Saharan Africa's consumer markets, particularly for processed foods, pharmaceuticals, textiles, and ICT services.
Key provisions
  • Tariff reduction: 90% of tariff lines phased to zero over 5–10 years (LDC treatment: 15 years)
  • Sensitive goods: 7% of tariff lines; longer liberalization schedules
  • Excluded goods: 3% (limited exclusions for security or development reasons)
  • Rules of origin: negotiated per sector; generally 25–35% local value addition
  • AfCFTA Secretariat in Accra, Ghana manages dispute resolution and implementation
  • Services schedules: Phase II negotiations ongoing; Tunisia targeting professional services and ICT
CommercialConstitutional

Organic Law (Projet de loi organique n° 07/2026) — Ratified by ARP April 7, 2026Hosting Agreement for the African Centre of Excellence for Inclusive Markets (AIMEC)

Underlying agreement signed: July 14, 2022 (sidelines of the 41st AU Executive Council session, Lusaka) | Ratified by ARP plenary: April 7, 2026 (92 for, 3 against, 3 abstentions) | AIMEC officially launched in Tunis: October 14, 2025
Organic law ratifying the 2022 headquarters agreement between Tunisia and the African Union Commission establishing the seat of the African Centre of Excellence for Inclusive Markets (AIMEC) in Tunis. AIMEC is an AU Commission institution supporting the development of inclusive, integrated markets across Africa — fostering the participation of vulnerable groups in production and consumption, advancing Sustainable Development Goals, and promoting innovation in economic policy. The law establishes the legal framework governing the Centre's activities in Tunisia and guarantees its administrative independence. During ARP committee review, Trade Minister Samir Abid was questioned over a perceived deficit of Tunisian diplomatic representation within the Centre's governance. Following ratification, Youth and Sports Minister Sadok Mourali received AIMEC's executive director, Dr. Youssef Ahmed Kardfani, on April 10, 2026, to discuss deepening cooperation on youth economic inclusion. (Run 77 addition — a genuine gap in the international-treaties watchlist that 76 prior runs had missed entirely, cross-verified across African Manager, WebManagerCenter, Business News, and La Presse de Tunisie.)
ConstitutionalCommercial

ILO Convention C193 — Decent Work in the Digital Platform Economy (Run 99 addition)

Adopted: 114th International Labour Conference, Geneva, June 28, 2026 (406–8–36) | Tunisia's vote: in favor | Status: not yet ratified domestically
Not yet Tunisian domestic law — a newly-adopted ILO convention Tunisia voted to support, distinct from ratification. The 114th International Labour Conference adopted Convention No. 193 extending core labor protections (occupational safety and health, fair compensation, protection from violence and harassment, data-privacy safeguards, algorithmic-transparency/review rights, and social-security access) to digital-platform workers, described by the ILO as a "decisive step" ensuring technological innovation respects worker rights. Tunisia's Minister of Employment and Vocational Training, Issam Lahmar, announced at the same conference that Tunisia is developing a governance-framework study with the ILO covering an estimated 150,000–250,000 platform workers across roughly 60 platforms in 15 sectors — a considerably broader figure than the domestic UGTT study's narrower 60,000-worker estimate for delivery/care/moto-transport platforms specifically (see the companion Labor Law section entry). No domestic ratification instrument, implementing bill, or ARP text has been filed as of this run; watch for a ratification bill and the ILO governance-framework study's completion. (leconomistemaghrebin.com, letemps.news, both June 28–29, 2026.)
LaborConstitutionalPending

🚢 Transport Law

Maritime Commercial Code — Law No. 62-13 of April 24, 1962(as amended) (Run 44 correction)

Enacted: April 24, 1962 | In force: July 1, 1962 | Amended: 1972, 1990, 1998, 2004-3
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited "Law No. 62-36 of March 27, 1962" — no such law could be verified, and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. NATLEX (ILO's legislative database) directly confirms the actual founding instrument as Law No. 62-13 of April 24, 1962, "portant promulgation du Code de commerce maritime," entering into force July 1, 1962; corroborated by droit-afrique.com/FAOLEX and the Ministry of Transport's own consolidated text. Comprehensive statute governing merchant shipping, maritime contracts, and maritime liability: registration/flagging of vessels, maritime liens and mortgages (ranked above ordinary privileges), carriage of goods incorporating the 1924 Brussels Convention (Hague Rules) — carrier liability capped at 400 dinars per package since a 1990 decree (up from 100). Law No. 2004-3 (January 20, 2004) replaced the ship-ownership/registration articles. Maritime ports managed by OMMP (Office de la Marine Marchande et des Ports).
TransportCommercial

Merchant Marine Organization Law — Law No. 95-33 of April 14, 1995

Enacted: April 14, 1995 | In force: 1995
Governs professions in the Tunisian merchant marine: maritime officers, engineers, and seafarers. Establishes training, certification, and competency requirements for seafarers (implementing the STCW Convention — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping). Sets working conditions, wages, and social protection for crew members. Regulates the Tunisian ship register and conditions for flagging vessels. Enforced by the OMMP and the Merchant Navy General Secretariat (created by Decree 2019-144).
TransportLabor

Maritime Ports Code — Law No. 2009-48 of July 8, 2009

Enacted: July 8, 2009 | In force: 2009 | Replaced: Law No. 99-25 (commercial ports) and Law No. 2002-47 (fishing ports)
Unifies the legal regime for Tunisia's commercial and fishing ports into a single code, repealing the previously separate 1999 commercial-ports law and 2002 fishing-ports law. Governs port administration, concessions, safety, and access; administered by OMMP (Office de la Marine Marchande et des Ports), the same authority referenced under the Maritime Commercial Code above, of which this ports-specific code had not previously had its own standalone entry. (Run 158 addition): the Skhira commercial port (Sfax governorate) illustrates a long-running gap in this code's practical application to shared oil-terminal infrastructure: the concession originally granted to the Société de Transport des Hydrocarbures par Pipelines au Sahara (TRAPSA) expired roughly 18 years ago, leaving OMMP (the code's designated port authority) and TRAPSA (the terminal's historical operator) without a current convention governing their respective responsibilities, maritime/land boundary delimitation, or public-domain status. In response to a deputy's (Saber Masmoudi) written parliamentary question, the Ministry of Transport announced on July 6, 2026 urgent measures to finalize the OMMP–TRAPSA operating convention, delimit the port's boundaries, and draft the implementing text on public-domain delimitation, alongside integrating Skhira into the 2040 national ports master plan (due for approval in 2027) — the latest in a string of unresolved regularization attempts (a 2020 memorandum of understanding, a 2021 convention announcement, 2023 development studies, and a 2025 TRAPSA regularization request) (Businessnews, Tunisie Tribune, Le Temps News, African Manager, Managers.tn, La Presse de Tunisie, all July 6, 2026).
TransportAdministrative

Civil Aviation Code — Law No. 99-58 of June 29, 1999

Enacted: June 29, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Implementing law: 98-110 (OACA)
Governs civil aviation in Tunisia: aircraft registration, airworthiness certification, pilot licensing, air navigation rules, airport operations, and air carrier liability. The Office of Civil Aviation and Airports (OACA — Office de l'Aviation Civile et des Aéroports), created by Law No. 98-110 of December 28, 1998, manages 7 international airports (Tunis-Carthage, Djerba-Zarzis, Sfax-Thyna, Monastir-Habib Bourguiba, Tozeur-Nefta, Tabarka, Gafsa-Ksar). Tunisia is an ICAO member. National carrier: Tunisair (state-owned). Domestic aviation partially opened to private carriers. Pending drone (UAV) regulation (added Run 130): civilian drone use remains prohibited outside case-by-case military-authorized exceptions; Transport Minister Khaled Chili confirmed (reported November 12, 2025) that the Directorate General of Civil Aviation, coordinating with the Ministry of Defense, has completed a draft regulatory text — via a dedicated inter-ministerial commission — governing civil and security aspects of drone use, now circulated to stakeholders for comment; no decree number or publication date has been confirmed. A narrower carve-out was separately enacted first: Finance Law 2026 Art. 135 (see that entry) authorizes farmers specifically to import and use agricultural drones, distinct from this still-pending general framework.
Key provisions
  • Aircraft registration: Tunisian nationality mark TS-
  • OACA: national aviation authority; safety, security, airspace management
  • Air carrier liability: aligned with Montreal Convention (ratified)
  • Airport concessions: governed by OACA and Ministry of Transport
  • Private carrier licensing: open skies principles for international routes (EU Horizontal Agreement 2021)
TransportCommercial

Road Traffic Code — Law No. 99-71 of July 26, 1999 (as amended) (Run 50 correction)

Enacted: July 26, 1999 | Adopted by Chamber of Deputies: July 6, 1999 | Published: JORT No. 61, July 30, 1999 | In force: 6 months after publication | Amended: 2001-101, 2003, 2014, 2019
Run 50 correction: prior runs cited an enactment date of "August 23, 1999" — this could not be verified, and two independent sources (jurisitetunisie.com's promulgation-law page and a matching targeted web search) instead confirm July 26, 1999 as the date of Law No. 99-71, adopted by the Chamber of Deputies on July 6, 1999 and published in JORT No. 61 of July 30, 1999; treat July 26 as the correct date absent a primary JORT text showing otherwise. Governs road traffic, vehicle registration, driver licensing, traffic offenses, and penalties in Tunisia. Sets speed limits (50 km/h urban, 90 km/h interurban, 110 km/h highways), blood alcohol limits (0.3‰ — stricter than EU), seat belt and helmet requirements, and mobile phone restrictions. Amended by Law No. 2001-101 of October 22, 2001, among later amendments; the 2014 amendment introduced a points-based driving license system and increased penalties for serious traffic offenses, and the 2019 amendment strengthened sanctions for drunk driving and speeding. Road safety authority under the Ministry of Transport. Pending reform: a "violence routière" (road violence) reform package — automatic driving-license withdrawal for speeding 10 km/h or more over the limit, expanded radar/camera enforcement, and a zero-tolerance regime — was approved as a draft bill by the Council of Ministers on July 28, 2025 (not, as sometimes reported, an already-enacted law of that date) and forwarded to the ARP; as of the most recent confirmed reporting (December 2025) it remained under ARP examination, with no JORT promulgation number identified as of June 2026. Update (Run 136): a July 6, 2026 Business News fact-check explicitly debunked a circulating rumor that a general reform of this Code had entered into force on July 1, 2026, confirming the operative text remains unchanged Law No. 99-71 as amended above — corroborating, rather than altering, this entry's existing "pending, not yet enacted" framing of the violence-routière package. Update (Run 164): driving-instructor licensing and driving-school (auto-école) approval operate at decree level under this Code rather than via a separate statute — Décret gouvernemental n° 2021-510 of June 18, 2021 (JORT No. 2021-059) sets driving-license categories and the required theoretical/practical training curriculum delivered through ATTT-approved establishments, and a September 1, 2025 procedural tightening made a signed training contract between candidate and driving school mandatory before sitting the theoretical exam; confirms this is a decree/administrative-approval matter, not a standalone legislative gap.
Transport

Land Transport Law — Law No. 2004-33 of April 19, 2004

Enacted: April 19, 2004 | In force: 2004
Regulates land transport of passengers and goods in Tunisia. Governs the licensing of transport operators (buses, taxis, trucks, coaches). Establishes the Land Transport Observatory. Sets vehicle technical inspection requirements and roadworthiness standards. Regulates freight transport contracts (lettre de voiture). Governs special transport (hazardous goods, heavy loads, exceptional convoys). National carrier TRANSTU operates urban buses and metro in Tunis; regional bus networks operated by SRTG companies. (Added Run 196, resolved Run 197): taxi, louage (shared intercity taxi), and rural-transport operators called a general strike for July 13, 2026, over the continued non-implementation of provisions tied to this Law and Decree No. 2023-581 of September 7, 2023 (JORT No. 2023-104) — now confirmed via 9anoun.tn (JORT mirror) to concern the "organisation du transport public routier non régulier des personnes" (organization of non-regular/irregular public road transport of persons, i.e. taxis, louages, and rural transport), repealing and replacing the prior Decree No. 2202-2007 (as amended by Decree No. 828-2016) on the same subject; African Manager's July 3, 2026 strike coverage independently corroborates the subject matter, citing operators' demand for "finalisation des textes relatifs à la loi n°33 de 2004 et au décret n°581 de 2023."
TransportCommercial

Railway Law — Law No. 69-31 of May 9, 1969, Law No. 98-74 of August 19, 1998 & Law No. 98-90 of November 2, 1998

Enacted: May 9, 1969; August 19, 1998; November 2, 1998 | In force: 1969 (statute), 1998 (network & operator framework)
Governs Tunisia's national railway network and its state operator, SNCFT (Société Nationale des Chemins de Fer Tunisiens — itself dating to a 1956 nationalization of the colonial-era railway administration), across three successive laws distinct from the general Land Transport Law (No. 2004-33) above, which covers road-based transport (buses, taxis, trucks) rather than rail. Law No. 69-31 approved SNCFT's original corporate/public-establishment statute. Law No. 98-74 ("relative aux chemins de fer") is the general railway-system law, covering the network and its operation, including safety regulation — Article 42 mandates periodic medical, safety-knowledge, and practical-competency exams for train drivers and other safety-critical railway agents. Law No. 98-90 updated SNCFT's own statute to align with the 1998 general railway framework. Supplemented by implementing decrees on SNCFT personnel regulations (Decree No. 2000-1230), public-service-obligation state compensation (Decree No. 2001-2621), organizational structure (Decree No. 2002-422), tax advantages (Decree No. 98-2243), and administrative/financial organization (Decree No. 2004-1056). (Added Run 163) A wholly new standalone entry closing a gap that survived at least two prior rail-transport gap-scans (Runs 136 and 140), both of which had assumed rail was covered by the Land Transport Law without verifying that law's text actually addresses railways — it does not. Verified via SNCFT's own official documents page (sncft.com.tn) and cross-corroborated by Tunisia's official legislation.tn portal, which cites Law No. 98-74 Art. 42 in a 2004 implementing arrêté. JORT issue numbers for the three laws could not be independently confirmed this run and remain unverified pending a future primary-source check.
TransportCommercial

⚡ Energy & Natural Resources

Hydrocarbons Code — Law No. 99-93 of August 17, 1999 (amended 2002, 2004, 2008, 2017)

Enacted: August 17, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Last amended: Law 2017-41 of May 30, 2017
Primary legal framework for oil and gas exploration and production in Tunisia. Governs concession agreements, production sharing contracts, and service contracts. State oil company ETAP (Entreprise Tunisienne d'Activités Pétrolières) holds mandatory minimum participation. The 2017 amendment required parliamentary approval of new hydrocarbon contracts and introduced transparency obligations aligned with EITI standards. Tunisia has been an EITI member since 2016. Pending reform (added Run 70): a restricted ministerial council chaired by then-Head of Government Kamel Maddouri examined a draft law amending the Code on February 7, 2025, recommending the text be finalized (incorporating remarks raised) before submission to the full Council of Ministers, in order to open new investment areas including renewable energy alongside traditional hydrocarbon exploration. A June 26, 2026 report (Webmanagercenter) renewed calls for urgent revision amid declining domestic hydrocarbon production, and a July 3, 2026 IACE report — corroborated across African Manager, La Presse de Tunisie, and Tustex — classifies this reform among five priority codes (with Investment, Water, and Environment) "technically finalized and validated by the Council of Ministers" but "never transmitted to the ARP," meaning no bill had reached parliamentary committee as of this update. The 1999 Code, as amended through 2017, remains in force unchanged.
Key provisions
  • Prospecting permit: maximum 1 year
  • Exploration permit: maximum 5 years (with 2 extensions of 2 years each)
  • Production concession: maximum 30 years (renewable)
  • ETAP mandatory participation: minimum share in all concessions
  • CIT rate: reduced to 50% if ETAP holds ≥40% stake (vs. standard 75% otherwise)
  • Art. 59: Mandatory compliance with environmental, health and safety standards
  • 2017 amendment: parliamentary approval required for contracts; EITI alignment
EnergyCommercial

Mining Code — Law No. 2003-30 of April 28, 2003 (as amended)

Enacted: April 28, 2003 | In force: 2003 | Amended: 2010
Governs exploration and exploitation of non-hydrocarbon mineral resources (phosphates, iron ore, lead, zinc, fluorite, salt, sand, limestone). Key provisions: 3-year exploration licenses; 30-year mining concessions; 1% royalty on production value payable semi-annually; 25% CIT on mining profits with a 5-year tax holiday from production start. Enforced by the National Office of Mines (ONM — Office National des Mines). Tunisia is the world's 5th largest phosphate producer; Compagnie des Phosphates de Gafsa (CPG) is the principal public mining entity. Pending reform: the Ministry of Industry called in July 2025 for accelerated finalization of a mining code revision framed around reinforcing state sovereignty over natural resources; as of mid-2026 no revised code had been adopted.
Key provisions
  • Exploration license: 3 years, renewable
  • Mining concession: 30 years, renewable
  • Royalty: 1% of production value (semi-annual payments)
  • Tax holiday: 5 years from production commencement
  • CIT rate: 25% on mining profits
  • Environmental rehabilitation bond: mandatory for concession holders
  • Artisanal mining rights: recognized in designated areas
EnergyCommercial

Electricity and Gas Law — Law No. 62-8 of April 3, 1962 (and Law No. 2002-28 of March 25, 2002)

STEG created: April 3, 1962 | Market opening: Law 2002-28
Law 62-8 created the Société Tunisienne de l'Electricité et du Gaz (STEG) as the national monopoly utility for electricity generation, transmission, distribution, and natural gas distribution. STEG remains a state monopoly for the transmission grid and distribution network. Law No. 2002-28 (March 25, 2002) partially opened electricity generation to private Independent Power Producers (IPPs) under power purchase agreements (PPAs) with STEG, while maintaining STEG's grid monopoly. Supervised by the Ministry of Industry and Energy.
EnergyCommercial

Renewable Energy Law — Law No. 2015-12 of May 11, 2015 (amended by Law 2019-47)

Enacted: May 11, 2015 | Amended: 2019 (business climate law) | New consolidating Code transmitted to committee: April 1, 2026
Establishes three authorization regimes for electricity production from renewable sources: (1) concession regime for large projects (>30 MW); (2) authorization regime for medium projects (1–30 MW) selling to STEG under feed-in tariff PPA; (3) self-consumption regime for own-use generation. National target: 35% of electricity from renewables by 2030. The 2019 Investment Climate Law expanded self-consumption to allow inter-site wheeling by companies. ANME (National Agency for Energy Conservation) coordinates renewable energy development. Recent application: the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD) approved, on May 13, 2026, five concession-regime agreements (TND ~1.6 billion combined) for utility-scale photovoltaic plants in Sidi Bouzid, Gafsa, and Gabès governorates — the El Khabna plant (198 MW) plus four 100 MW plants — with all output to be injected exclusively into the STEG grid under the law's concession-regime PPA framework. Pending consolidation — new "Code des Énergies Renouvelables" (added Run 94): a Council of Ministers meeting held December 24, 2024 ordered completion ("parachèvement") of a unified Renewable Energy Code intended to gather this law's dispersed provisions into a single coherent framework; deputy Yosri Baoueb confirmed on Express FM (April 1, 2026) that the draft text had just been transmitted to the ARP's competent parliamentary commission, alongside a parallel Hydrocarbons Code project, both framed as part of an "energy sovereignty" push amid rising hydrocarbon-import costs and worsening energy deficits. Its most contested provision reserves 51% of renewable-energy project ownership for Tunisian actors, justified by Baoueb as a defense against foreign consortiums outpacing domestic operators (citing the 600 MW Tunisia–Italy ElMed interconnection as a capacity-absorption risk). A July 3, 2026 IACE report separately names this Renewable Energy Code, alongside the Foreign Exchange Code and Investment Code, as one of three reforms it urges be enacted by end of 2026 — distinct from (and not one of) the four other IACE-flagged codes (Investment, Water, Environment, Hydrocarbons) already noted as "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" elsewhere in this document; no committee vote or report has been confirmed since the April 1, 2026 transmission. Green hydrogen (added Run 130): Tunisia has no dedicated green-hydrogen statute; the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy officially launched the National Strategy for the Development of Green Hydrogen and its Derivatives ("H2Vert.TUN," developed with German GIZ support) in May 2024, targeting a hydrogen/derivatives export and domestic-decarbonization framework through 2050 with first production projects expected from 2030 — a policy-level roadmap, not yet a law or decree, and not integrated into this entry's or the pending Renewable Energy Code's statutory text as of this run. Round 6 solar authorizations (added Run 141): the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy opened a sixth call for projects under this Law's authorization regime for solar photovoltaic electricity production, published April 2026: 200 MW total capacity available across three project-size tiers (1 MW, 2 MW, and 10 MW — the maximum allowed under the authorization regime), with applications (two paper copies plus two digital copies) accepted at the ministry's central registry from April 15 to June 15, 2026 at noon. Selected projects benefit from guaranteed STEG purchase rates set by an October 2024 ministerial order. This follows Round 5, which resulted in 186 preliminary authorizations totaling approximately 286–288 MW — a concrete, dated implementation of the authorization regime distinct from the concession-regime approvals and pending Code consolidation already noted above. ARP origin and controversy of the May 13 CNRD concessions (added Run 169): the five concession-regime agreements the CNRD concurred with on May 13, 2026 were first adopted as five separate ratification bills — Laws n°01/2026 through n°05/2026, covering the El Khobna/El Khabna, Mezzouna (Sidi Bouzid), El Ksar (Gafsa), Segdoud (Gafsa), and Menzel Habib (Gabès) photovoltaic plants (combined ~598 MW, ~TND 1.64 billion investment, purchase tariffs of 98.8–124.4 millimes/kWh, a projected 13.3% cut to Tunisia's gas-import bill) — by the ARP on April 28, 2026, each by a separately recorded plenary vote (ranging from 70 for/30 against/8 abstaining to 75/33/9). Each agreement grants the foreign project operator a 20-year concession (renewable once for 10 years) with a five-year corporate-tax exemption, with all output sold exclusively to STEG. The same-day vote drew UGTT opposition and a deputy (Bilel El Mechri) publicly calling the arrangement "energy colonisation"; President Kais Saied dismissed Industry, Mines & Energy Minister Fatma Thabet Chiboub that same day (April 28, 2026), installing Equipment & Housing Minister Salah Zouari as interim minister with no permanent successor confirmed as of this run (africanews.com, entreprises-magazine.com, Managers.tn, webdo.tn).
EnergyEnvironmental

📊 Public Finance & Budget

Organic Budget Law — Law No. 2019-15 of February 13, 2019

Enacted: February 13, 2019 | In force: 2019 | Replaced: Organic Law No. 67-53 of December 8, 1967 (as amended, including by Law No. 2004-42 of May 13, 2004)
Correction (Run 65): this entry previously cited Law No. 2004-42 of 2004 as the operative organic budget law; that law was in fact only an amendment to the original 1967 organic law (No. 67-53), and both have since been fully superseded by Law No. 2019-15 of February 13, 2019, confirmed against the Finance Ministry's official text. Law No. 2019-15 defines the current constitutional and legal rules for preparation, adoption, execution, and control of Tunisia's state budget, formally introducing performance/program-based budgeting (budgétisation par objectifs) alongside the traditional Title I/Title II expenditure classification, plus multi-year budget programming and expanded rules for execution control, results evaluation, and the budget settlement law (loi de règlement). Organizes the annual Finance Law (loi de finances) process: Cabinet submits draft to parliament by October 15; parliament must adopt by December 31.
Key provisions
  • Finance Law submitted to parliament by October 15 each year
  • Parliamentary vote required by December 31; if not, prior-year budget extended by decree
  • Title I: recurrent expenditures (staff, goods, services, debt service)
  • Title II: development expenditures (public investment)
  • Special accounts (comptes spéciaux du Trésor) regulated separately
  • Court of Accounts certifies final state accounts (loi de règlement)
AdministrativeFinancial

Court of Accounts — Law No. 68-8 of March 8, 1968 (as amended, most recently Law 2019-56)

Enacted: March 8, 1968 | In force: 1968 | Major reform: Law 2019-56
Tunisia's supreme audit institution (Cour des Comptes / ديوان المحاسبات). Audits central government, public enterprises, and local authority accounts. Issues binding rulings (arrêts) imposing financial liability (débet) on public accountants for unauthorized expenditures. Issues annual public report on state budget execution presented to parliament. Audit opinions required before adoption of final budget accounts (loi de règlement). The 2019 reform modernized audit methods and strengthened transparency of findings. Also plays a role in asset declaration review. Update (Run 103): the Court's 32nd annual report, presented to President Kaïs Saïed on January 9, 2026 by First Vice-President Fadhila Gargouri, found roughly TND 1.07 billion in cumulative losses across eleven audited public entities — national carrier Tunisair alone (over TND 316 million) and the Office of Maritime Commerce and Ports (nearly TND 291 million) among the largest — attributing them to salaries, bonuses, and allowances paid without legal basis and weakened internal-control mechanisms; Saied publicly demanded prosecutions and recovery of the funds.
AdministrativeFinancial

Public Debt Management — Law No. 97-42 of July 28, 1997 and annual Finance Laws

Framework law: July 28, 1997 | Debt limits: set annually in Finance Law
Governs the state's borrowing authority and public debt management. The General Treasury of Tunisia manages the debt portfolio. External borrowing requires parliamentary authorization in the annual Finance Law. Domestic instruments: Treasury bills (BTC — 13/26/52 weeks), medium-term Treasury bonds (OAT). Tunisia has issued Eurobonds and Sukuk (Islamic bonds) on international capital markets (2012, 2015, 2020, 2021). IMF extended credit facility (2016–2020, revised 2023). Public debt reached approximately 80% of GDP by 2024.
FinancialAdministrative

Presidential Decree No. 2026-108Afreximbank US$500 Million Financing Facility

BCT–Afreximbank facility signed: June 16, 2026 | Presidential decree published: JORT, June 8, 2026 | BCT board approval: March 18, 2026
Under the Public Debt Management framework (Law No. 97-42) and the external-financing authorization in the Finance Law 2026, the Central Bank of Tunisia (BCT), acting on the state's behalf, concluded a US$500 million (≈TND 1.471 billion) financing facility with the African Export-Import Bank (Afreximbank) to help cover the FY2026 budget's external financing needs, including strategic imports of energy and food products. Signed by President Kais Saied as Presidential Decree No. 2026-108, ratifying the BCT board's March 2026 decision. Part of a broader financing relationship under which Afreximbank has extended roughly TND 5.15 billion to Tunisia over four years.
FinancialAdministrative

Draft Laws n° 38/2026 & n° 39/2026 (pending)State Guarantees for STEG IBRD Loans (~US$430 Million)

Underlying loan agreements signed: November 3, 2025 | Finance & Budget Committee approval: June 28, 2026 | Plenary debate opened: July 10, 2026, then postponed to Tuesday, July 14, 2026 (no vote yet reported)
Two companion government bills authorizing state guarantees for International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD/World Bank) loans to the Société Tunisienne de l'Électricité et du Gaz (STEG). Draft Law n° 38/2026 guarantees a US$400 million loan extended directly to STEG; Draft Law n° 39/2026 guarantees a complementary US$30 million loan channeled through the IBRD in its capacity as implementing entity of the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). Both finance a STEG energy-sector modernization program aimed at improving the utility's operational efficiency, financial performance, and governance, against a backdrop of STEG's reported TND 7.356 billion total indebtedness and over TND 6 billion in unpaid receivables (per a June 27, 2026 La Presse report). The ARP Finance and Budget Committee approved both texts on June 28, 2026; they were transmitted to the ARP plenary for final adoption but had not been put to a plenary vote as of this update. Update (Run 219): the ARP Bureau calendared these bills for the same July 9–10, 2026 plenary window as the Development Plan bill (see that entry), but no outlet reporting on that session's Development Plan vote (La Presse, Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, webdo.tn) mentioned a STEG vote outcome — still awaiting plenary action as of this update. Update (Run 222): the plenary debate on both bills opened July 10, 2026, with the Minister of Equipment addressing deputies (La Presse, "Énergie: l'ARP se penche sur deux prêts de la BIRD en faveur de la STEG," 2026-07-10), but the debate was reported only as "underway" ("entamé") — no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome has been reported as of this update; still the single highest-priority item to resolve for the next run. Distinct from the unrelated "combating financial exclusion" bill (Draft Law n° 023/2024 — see entry below), which some secondary sources conflated with these same bill numbers; the 38/2026 and 39/2026 numbering is independently confirmed for the STEG guarantees via the ARP's official bill registry and Arabic-language press (لجنة المالية، مشروع قانون عدد 38 و39 لسنة 2026). Update (Run 238): the July 10, 2026 plenary session (held Friday at Bardo, with Minister of Equipment and Housing Salah Zouari — also acting head of the Industry, Mines and Energy portfolio — addressing deputies) did not reach a vote before adjourning; African Manager's follow-up headline ("Le Parlement reporte à mardi l'examen de deux prêts destinés à la transition énergétique") and a same-story cross-reference on La Presse's own site independently confirm the ARP will resume examination of both bills on the following Tuesday, rather than the vote outcome remaining merely unreported as prior runs had characterized it — the debate was affirmatively adjourned and rescheduled, a more precise status than "underway." Correction (Run 248): Run 238 resolved "mardi"/"Tuesday" to the calendar date July 15, 2026, but July 15, 2026 is in fact a Wednesday (verified via direct date computation) — the Tuesday immediately following the Friday, July 10, 2026 adjournment is July 14, 2026. La Presse's own republication of this story is dated July 11, 2026 (a Saturday) and still describes the resumption only as "mardi prochain" (next Tuesday) without independently stating a calendar date, so no primary source has actually named "July 15" — that figure appears to have been an arithmetic slip introduced in Run 238 rather than a sourced claim. Corrected the date field above accordingly; still no vote outcome reported as of this update. Update (Run 261): a July 12, 2026 La Presse follow-up ("STEG: un député dénonce des prêts qui favoriseraient les entreprises étrangères") confirms plenary debate continued past the July 10 adjournment rather than pausing entirely until July 14 as the prior "adjourned and rescheduled" characterization implied; deputy Issam Jabri publicly objected that the loan terms favor foreign contractors over domestic firms. Still no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome as of this update. Update (Run 283): a July 13, 2026 businessnews.com.tn report ("Report du vote sur les prêts à la STEG: des députés dénoncent un 'scandale' et accusent Bouderbala de vouloir influer sur le scrutin") adds a genuinely new procedural-controversy detail beyond the plain rescheduling already documented: deputy Bilel El Mechri publicly accused ARP Speaker Ibrahim Bouderbala of deliberately postponing the vote to July 14 after observing that opposing votes outnumbered favorable ones during the July 10 session, calling the move a "scandal," and announcing he will boycott the July 14 sitting, which he characterized as a "masquerade." A same-day directinfo.webmanagercenter.com report independently corroborates only the procedural fact that the ARP resumes examination Tuesday, July 14, without addressing the manipulation accusation. Still no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome as of this update; the Mechri allegation itself remains a claim by one deputy, not an established fact, and is presented here as such.
FinancialEnergyPending

2026–2030 National Development Plan (Bill)

Filed under Constitution Arts. 75 & 77 | ARP standing-committee examination: June 29 – July 2, 2026 (concluded) | Plenary session: July 9–10, 2026 | Adopted by ARP plenary: July 10, 2026 (64 for, 24 against, 15 abstentions) | Not yet promulgated/published in JORT
The economic and social planning framework law for 2026–2030, formally deposited at the ARP as draft law n°42/2026 on June 17, 2026 following its adoption by the Council of Ministers. Structured as a single-article bill — the Assembly votes yes/no/abstain on the plan as a whole rather than amending individual provisions, per committee rapporteur Saber Jlassi — it was examined jointly by all Assembly of People's Representatives standing committees beginning June 29–30, 2026. Committee discussion noted the prior five-year plan's execution rate stood at under 40%, a benchmark cited by critical deputies alongside the new plan's more ambitious targets. Targets raising the average annual growth rate to 4.2% over the period (versus 2.4% in the prior five-year period), reaching 5.1% by 2030, and raising investment from roughly 15.5% to 20% of GDP via a public-investment project portfolio of approximately TND 102 billion. A June 30, 2026 joint hearing with Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh preceded committee deliberations, followed by a second joint ARP audition of the Minister on July 1, 2026 to gather further committee input (Tunisian media noted the plan's limited treatment of the media/audiovisual sector as a specific point raised in that session, and a July 1, 2026 report noted several deputies voicing doubts about the feasibility of the plan's growth and investment targets, with the Finance Committee separately flagging concerns over the underlying economic and financial assumptions). Separately on July 1, 2026, the National Union of Tunisian Journalists (SNJT) publicly criticized the plan by name for omitting a dedicated media-sector strategy, calling for the assembly to formally recognize media as an economic and cultural sector, incorporate press-freedom legislation, and include the sector in the plan's monitoring mechanisms. The plan's headline targets include lowering the poverty rate to under 15%, a portfolio of roughly 21,100 public-investment projects funded at approximately TND 102 billion, and raising the renewable-energy share of electricity production to 35% by 2030; committee coverage also noted that of 40,748 development proposals submitted by local councils, 34,715 were retained in the plan's regional components. Once adopted, it is expected to serve as the legal reference framework underpinning the national AI strategy and the NDC 3.0 climate financing plan already noted elsewhere in this document. Update (Run 71): ARP President Ibrahim Bouderbala announced July 2, 2026 that the joint standing-committee examination had formally concluded — independently reported by both Le Temps News and African Manager (both July 3, 2026) — with a consolidated synthesis report now being drafted for submission to the ARP Bureau, which will schedule the plenary session before the text proceeds to the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts under Art. 77; no plenary date has yet been announced. Bouderbala used the same occasion to publicly call for accelerating adoption of the separately-tracked Foreign Exchange, Water, Hydrocarbons, and Investment codes (see those entries). Not yet enacted — the most active legislative process in Tunisia as of this update. Update (Run 141): Finance Commission member Issam Chouchane publicly criticized the plan on July 6, 2026 (Business News), arguing the document reads as a project list rather than a genuine strategic vision and lacks explicit objectives in several sectors he considers priorities, including food security, agriculture, phosphate, tourism, and new technologies/artificial intelligence; he also questioned the feasibility of the plan's average-growth target given recent economic performance, and reiterated that deputies hold only a consultative, up-or-down role on the single-article bill (echoing an earlier June 26, 2026 critique from the same deputy on the plan's financial feasibility). Update (Run 174): on Monday, July 6, 2026, the ARP Bureau formally referred the bill to a fresh round of joint examination by all permanent standing committees — chaired by the Assembly president or a vice-president — rather than sending it directly to plenary; a consolidated synthesis report from that joint round will go back to the Bureau, which will then schedule the plenary session (RTCI, July 6–7, 2026). No plenary date has yet been announced as of this update. Update (Run 185): the ARP Bureau met the morning of July 7, 2026 and formally scheduled the plenary session on the bill for Thursday, July 9, 2026, with the possibility of extending into Friday, July 10, 2026 if debate runs long (RTCI, July 7, 2026, 14:54, corroborated by a La Presse de Tunisie report of a 10:30 AM Thursday sitting) — the first concrete plenary date announced since the bill's June 17, 2026 filing. No vote outcome had been reported as of this update; still not yet enacted. Update (Run 202): the plenary session opened as scheduled at 10:30 AM on July 9, 2026 and floor debate ran the full day with no vote outcome reported by its close, consistent with the Bureau's built-in contingency to extend proceedings into July 10. Two deputies delivered the most substantive floor criticism of the bill documented to date. Deputy Mohamed Ali argued deputies were being asked to grant "un chèque en blanc pour cinq ans" (a blank check for five years) rather than vote on an ordinary law, since Parliament had not participated in drafting the plan and the single-article, up/down structure leaves no room for genuine amendment; he cited public debt of roughly TND 147 billion (over 80% of GDP) against a still-low national savings rate and a persistently deficient budget, faulted the plan for listing a large number of projects without a clear implementation priority ranking or demonstrated administrative capacity, and closed by calling on deputies to vote against the bill to defend "le droit du peuple à un développement réel, pas un développement sur papier" (Business News, July 9, 2026). Separately, ARP Bureau vice-president Nabil Hamdi — while confirming the Bureau's own procedural decision to send the bill to this plenary — criticized its substance, saying the plan in its current form "reflète davantage la volonté de l'administration que celle du peuple" (reflects the will of the administration more than that of the people), faulting the project portfolio as insufficiently detailed for the standing committees and citing what he called a "systematic exclusion" of Parliament from the plan's drafting phase (Radio Express FM, July 9, 2026). No JORT publication or confirmed floor vote had followed by this run's cutoff; still not yet enacted. Update (Run 218): the ARP plenary concluded debate on July 10, 2026 — Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh replied to deputies' interventions before the vote (Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, same-day) — and adopted the bill in its entirety by a recorded vote of 64 for, 24 against, and 15 abstentions (La Presse de Tunisie, July 10, 2026), resolving the single most-tracked pending item across this changelog's recent runs. This vote outcome was, as of this update, reported only by La Presse among the outlets checked (African Manager and Tunisie Tribune's own July 10 pieces describe the session as still in progress ahead of the vote, likely filed before it concluded); flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with this artifact's established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings. The bill still requires presidential promulgation and JORT publication before entering into force — neither had been identified as of this update. Update (Run 219): the 64-24-15 tally is now independently corroborated by a second outlet, webdo.tn (July 10, 2026, 12:02 PM), reporting identical figures — no longer single-sourced. Presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding as of this update; the companion STEG loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026, see below) remain undecided despite reportedly sharing the same July 9–10 plenary window. Update (Run 232): a procedural detail not previously captured: per Constitution Art. 77's bicameral design, the plan bill does not proceed straight from ARP adoption to presidential promulgation — it must also pass a second-chamber vote at the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD), with disagreements between the two chambers referred to a 10-member joint committee and, if still unresolved, final say reverting to the CNRD (Tunisie Numérique, quoting deputy Jmaï Zouidi). This clarifies why promulgation/JORT publication remain outstanding well past the July 10 ARP vote: a CNRD vote is a still-outstanding procedural step, not merely an administrative delay in signature. No CNRD vote date had been reported as of this update. Update (Run 236): the CNRD's examination has now expanded beyond the first district (Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef) to a second district as well, per an African Manager report of CNRD president Imed Derbali's hearing with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh on regional-disparity priorities — specific figures cited included a 0.51 territorial-development index for Bizerte and a 7.3% poverty rate for Jendouba. Still no CNRD floor vote, presidential promulgation, or JORT publication confirmed as of this update. Update (Run 266): the CNRD, under president Imed Derbali, examined the plan's regional/territorial components on or around July 12, 2026, per La Presse de Tunisie ("La Tunisie acte le virage de la planification par la base," July 12, 2026) and a corroborating African Manager report on the same session calling for reduced territorial disparities — confirming the CNRD stage is actively underway post-ARP-vote and consistent with the sequence documented at Run 232/236 above. No CNRD floor vote, presidential promulgation, or JORT publication had been reported as of this update.
FinancialConstitutional2026

🌾 Agriculture & Fisheries

Fisheries Code — Law No. 94-13 of January 31, 1994 (as amended 2002, 2014)

Enacted: January 31, 1994 | In force: 1994 | Amended: 2002, 2014
Comprehensive statute governing fishing activities in Tunisian territorial waters and the EEZ. Commercial fishing vessels require licenses from the Ministry of Agriculture. Sets fishing quotas, prohibited fishing methods (dynamite, poison, trawling within 3 nm of coast), protected marine species, and seasonal closures. Establishes the National Fisheries Office (ONP — Office National des Pêches) for market organization and port management. Tuna and swordfish fishing regulated under ICCAT commitments. Aquaculture (fish farming) licensing governed by supplementary 2006 decree.
AgricultureEnvironmental

Plant Protection Law — Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992; Veterinary Law — Law No. 2005-35 of May 25, 2005

Plant Protection: August 3, 1992 | Veterinary: May 25, 2005
(v4.178 correction, Run 178): The plant-protection half of this entry previously cited "Law No. 99-24 of February 23, 1999" and an administering "INAP — National Institute for Plant Protection." Neither checks out: Law No. 99-24 is in fact dated March 9, 1999 and governs an unrelated subject — veterinary sanitary control at import and export — not plant health (confirmed via two independent legal-database sources), and no institute named "INAP" could be independently corroborated as Tunisia's plant-protection body (the confirmed body is the Direction Générale de la Santé Végétale et du Contrôle des Intrants Agricoles, under the Ministry of Agriculture). The correct citation for Tunisia's phytosanitary/plant-protection framework is Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992 (recasting prior phytosanitary legislation into a unified regime), independently confirmed via FAOLEX's full text and a second corroborating source. It prohibits import, possession, cultivation, and transit of quarantine pests absent authorization and conditions plant/plant-product imports on a phytosanitary certificate per the International Plant Protection Convention model (implementing detail set by ministerial order, e.g. a February 19, 2016 decree on import requirements and inspection procedures), with narrow exemptions for processed agricultural goods. The separate Law No. 99-24 of March 9, 1999 (veterinary sanitary control at import/export) remains a genuine, distinct Tunisian statute not otherwise documented in this artifact. The Veterinary Law (2005-35) citation is unchanged from prior runs and was not re-verified this pass; it regulates animal health surveillance, veterinary practice licensing, slaughterhouse standards, and control of notifiable animal diseases. Both plant- and animal-health regimes implement Tunisia's WTO Agreement on Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement) obligations for agricultural trade.
AgricultureHealth

Veterinary Pharmacy Law — Law No. 78-23 of March 8, 1978 (as amended by Law No. 2000-40 of April 5, 2000)Réglementation de la Pharmacie Vétérinaire (Run 174 addition)

Enacted: March 8, 1978 | Amended: April 5, 2000 | Implementing decree: Décret n° 79-831 of September 28, 1979
A previously undocumented, narrowly distinct statute from the Veterinary Law entry above: while Law No. 2005-35 governs animal health surveillance and veterinary practice licensing, this earlier 1978 law (as substantially amended in 2000) specifically regulates veterinary medicines — manufacturing-facility authorization, market-authorization (visa) applications and renewals for veterinary pharmaceutical products, and pharmacovigilance, administered by the Ministry of Public Health's Direction de la Pharmacie et du Médicament (DPM). Décret n° 79-831 of September 28, 1979 sets good-manufacturing-practice and quality-control standards, plus packaging/labeling rules, for veterinary-medicine production. This narrow medicines-licensing gap survived at least four prior targeted "veterinary" gap-scans (Runs 82, 90, 113, 116) that confirmed only the broader animal-health/practice statute, without distinguishing this separate pharmaceutical-regulation track — parallel to how Run 147 found the companion-animal-welfare gap after similar prior scans of adjacent terrain.
AgricultureHealth

Agricultural Development Law — Law No. 71-72 of December 31, 1971 and Law No. 2000-30 of March 20, 2000

Development Areas: 1971 | Irrigated Perimeters: March 20, 2000
The 1971 law empowers the Ministry of Agriculture to designate agricultural development areas with state support for irrigation and land improvement. The 2000 law governs management of public irrigated perimeters (périmètres irrigués publics — PIPs): water pricing through GDA user associations (Groupements de Développement Agricole), infrastructure maintenance, and cost recovery. Emergency water restrictions (2023 drought) were issued under the Water Code's provisions with inter-ministerial coordination.
AgricultureLand

Organic Agriculture Law — Law No. 99-30 of April 5, 1999

Enacted: April 5, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Amended: 2009
Establishes the legal framework for organic farming, certification, labeling, and inspection in Tunisia. Organic products must be certified by an accredited certification body and labeled with the national organic mark. Controls use of synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, and GMOs on certified organic land. Administered by the Ministry of Agriculture and the Office for Organic Farming Development (BIO). Tunisia exports significant volumes of certified organic olive oil and dates to EU markets.
AgricultureCommercial

Law No. 67-41 of June 24, 1967 (as amended)Agricultural Cooperatives

Enacted: June 24, 1967 | In force: 1967 | Amended: 1982, 2001, 2020
Establishes the legal framework for agricultural cooperatives (coopératives agricoles) in Tunisia. Agricultural cooperatives are governed by a hybrid regime combining principles from general cooperative law and specific agricultural provisions. Types include: production cooperatives, service cooperatives (input supply, marketing), and credit cooperatives (Caisses de Crédit Agricole Mutuels). Registration with the Ministry of Agriculture and the Office of Agricultural Credit Cooperatives (ODCO) is required. Cooperatives benefit from tax exemptions on profits and reduced registration fees. The Tunisian Cooperative Union (UTAP — Union Tunisienne de l'Agriculture et de la Pêche) represents cooperatives at national level. The 2020 amendment strengthened governance requirements and introduced digital voting for cooperative assemblies.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–10: Definition of agricultural cooperative; democratic governance (one member, one vote)
  • Art. 11–20: Types: production, marketing, supply, credit cooperatives
  • Art. 21–28: Registration requirements; statutes; minimum capital
  • Art. 29–36: Distribution of surplus to members proportional to participation
  • Art. 37–44: Tax advantages: exemption from corporate tax; reduced stamp duties
  • Art. 45–52: Dissolution and liquidation procedures
AgricultureCommercial

Law No. 2001-94 of August 7, 2001Pastoral and Communal Lands Code

Enacted: August 7, 2001 | In force: 2001
Regulates the use and management of collective pastoral lands (terres collectives de parcours) and communal grazing areas in Tunisia, particularly relevant in the arid and semi-arid regions of central and southern Tunisia. These lands — covering approximately 5 million hectares — are traditionally managed by tribal communities. The code establishes a management framework balancing customary pastoral rights with sustainable land management obligations. Creates Pastoral Development Associations (associations de développement pastoral) for each defined rangeland unit. Prohibits unauthorized subdivision, sale, or permanent agricultural conversion of collective pastoral lands without Ministry of Agriculture approval. Provides mechanisms for resolving boundary disputes between pastoral communities.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–8: Definition of collective pastoral lands; state ownership with usufruct rights to communities
  • Art. 9–16: Pastoral Development Associations (PDA) — formation, governance, mandate
  • Art. 17–24: Seasonal grazing circuits; carrying capacity limits per zone
  • Art. 25–30: Prohibition of unauthorized permanent crop installation; penalties
  • Art. 31–36: Water point management in pastoral zones
  • Art. 37–42: Dispute resolution between communities; role of Ministry of Agriculture
AgricultureLand

Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019Food and Animal Feed Safety

Enacted: February 26, 2019 | In force: 2019
(v4.178 correction, Run 178): This entry previously cited "Law No. 2009-11 of February 11, 2009" as the food-safety framework law and "ANCSEP" as the body it created — both incorrect. Law No. 2009-11 (March 2, 2009) is in fact Tunisia's building fire/explosion/panic-safety code, an entirely unrelated statute (confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and citet.nat.tn), and ANCSEP (Agence Nationale de Contrôle Sanitaire et Environnemental des Produits) was created earlier, by Decree No. 99-779 of April 5, 1999, as a general sanitary/environmental product-control coordinator — not a food-safety-specific body created by this law. The actual food-safety framework statute is Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019, relative à la sécurité sanitaire des denrées alimentaires et des aliments pour animaux, which establishes a unified legal regime for foodstuff and animal-feed safety grounded in three principles: risk analysis based on scientific evidence, precaution, and transparency (public consultation and consumer information). It creates the National Authority for Food Safety (Instance Nationale de la Sécurité Sanitaire des Produits Alimentaires — INSSPA), which brings together representatives from the health, agriculture, and commerce ministries and functions as Tunisia's sole risk-management and risk-notification authority for food products — distinct from and complementing ANCSEP's broader coordination role. Mandates food-chain traceability from producer to consumer. Confirmed via FAOLEX's full text of the law and INSSPA/ANCSEP institutional-comparison sources; specific article numbers are not asserted here pending direct verification against the official JORT text, consistent with this project's practice of omitting unverifiable citations.
AgricultureHealthCommercial

Law No. 2026-5 of March 11, 2026Regularization of Distressed Agricultural Debts

Adopted by ARP: March 10, 2026 | Promulgated: March 11, 2026 | Provisions apply through: December 31, 2026
Requires public and private banks to reschedule the full principal and initial contractual interest of classified/distressed agricultural debts (BCT class 4 and above, held before September 30, 2025) owed by farmers and agricultural companies, over up to 7 years including a 1-year grace period. Mandates automatic removal of the resulting negative banking classification once a rescheduling agreement is approved, restoring debtors' access to new financing. The Central Bank of Tunisia (BCT) was required to issue implementing conditions within 15 days of publication. Excludes debts connected to unresolved corruption or money-laundering proceedings. Enacted to address accumulated financial distress in the agricultural sector that had left many farmers and agri-businesses unable to settle liabilities or obtain new credit.
AgricultureFinancial

Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 (pending)Animal Protection and Domestic-Carnivore Regulation

Filed: July 23, 2024 (Deputy Chokri Bahri) | Examined: ARP Agriculture, Food Security & Maritime Fishing Commission, May 5, 2026 | Status: not yet enacted as of July 2026
A member's bill that would modernize Tunisia's animal-protection framework — described by its authors as dating to 19th-century legislative instruments never adapted to current social, economic, and environmental conditions — and specifically regulate the detention and circulation of domestic carnivores (dogs and cats) amid a persistent stray-dog and rabies-control problem in Tunisian cities. Proposes shifting municipal stray-animal management away from mass culling campaigns toward prevention, registration, and sterilization programs, while strengthening penalties for cruelty and negligence. At the May 5, 2026 Agriculture Commission session, deputies broadly welcomed the initiative as aligning Tunisia with international animal-welfare standards but raised concerns about the technical and financial capacity of municipalities to run effective sterilization programs at scale. Remains at the committee-examination stage; no committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run. (Added Run 147 — a wholly new standalone entry outside the pending-bill watchlist, closing a gap in the Agriculture & Fisheries section that prior runs' "veterinary/animal health" gap-scans had not distinguished from this separate animal-welfare/companion-animal track.) Update (Run 176): Review of the bill accelerated after a video showing a performance-troupe member dancing on the belly of a horse lying on the ground during a wedding ceremony in Sahline (Monastir governorate) went viral around July 6–7, 2026; the animal-protection association Arrahma announced it was pursuing a formal criminal complaint against the identified troupe (a second, initially accused troupe was cleared after verification) and separately called on Parliament to expedite the reform. Business News (July 7, 2026) reported the ARP was accelerating examination of the animal-welfare framework, and press coverage detailed proposed tiered penalties not previously captured here: 15 days'–1 month imprisonment plus a TND 500 fine for general mistreatment, rising to 1–10 years plus a TND 2,000 fine where abuse causes permanent disability, and 5 years where it causes death, with enhanced sentences for repeat offenders. Corroborated across Business News, Univers News, and allAfrica (citing Arrahma's president Jamila Ammar); no committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run. This is the same Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 tracked above, not a separate bill, despite one headline's reference to "deux propositions de loi." Correction & update (Run 203): that Run 176 dismissal appears to have been premature. A separate Business News article with the same "deux propositions de loi" headline (July 7, 2026, sourcing deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher's same-day Diwan FM interview) was independently re-checked and, across three separately-worded searches, consistently identified Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 — also filed July 23, 2024 "by a group of deputies," also before the Agriculture Commission, which "has already heard the project initiators and several sector stakeholders" — as the bill carrying the reinforced financial/criminal sanctions for mistreatment, capture, or killing of an animal (i.e., the tiered-penalty provisions Run 176 attributed to n° 74/2024 above may in fact belong to this companion bill), while n° 74/2024 is described in this account as the complementary prevention/awareness text providing for animal-welfare and environmental-protection content in school curricula. This conflicts with the July 26, 2024 Kapitalis filing announcement (sourced directly via WebFetch), which names only Deputy Chokri Bahri and a single unnumbered bill combining both sanction-strengthening and "culture of respect" language — the article that appears to underlie this artifact's existing n° 74/2024 attribution above. Given this unresolved conflict between a 2024 primary filing source (one bill, one sponsor, mixed content) and 2026 committee-status coverage (two numbered bills, split by content) — and a failed direct WebFetch of the July 7, 2026 Business News article itself (HTTP 503) that prevented full-text confirmation — this update stops short of re-splitting the entry or reattributing content, and instead flags Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 as a probable second, separately-numbered animal-protection bill before the same Agriculture Commission, to be confirmed or resolved by a future run with direct access to the ARP's bill registry or the source article's full text. Update (Run 204): a direct WebFetch of the same July 7, 2026 Business News article again failed (HTTP 503, matching Run 203's failure and confirming this is a persistent access issue rather than a transient one), so full-text confirmation still could not be obtained; however, two independently-worded WebSearch queries run this pass both returned AI-generated summaries of that article converging on the same account: Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 (filed July 23, 2024 "by a group of deputies") is the bill carrying the reinforced sanctions — a summary this run renders as 1–3 years' imprisonment plus a fine of up to TND 2,000 where abuse causes permanent injury, rising to 5 years where it causes death (qualified as a "crime" rather than a lesser offense) — while n° 74/2024 remains the complementary prevention/awareness bill (school-curriculum content on animal welfare and environmental protection). The same summaries quote deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher explaining the delay since 2024: government observations prompted reworking of the text's legal definitions and implementation modalities, and the Agriculture Commission has already heard both the bills' initiators and sector stakeholders. Note the sanction figures in this account (1–3 years/TND 2,000/permanent injury; 5 years/death) do not exactly match the tiered penalties Run 176 sourced to the same underlying reporting (15 days'–1 month/TND 500/general mistreatment; 1–10 years/TND 2,000/permanent disability; 5 years/death) — since both derive from search-engine summarization rather than a directly fetched primary text, this discrepancy is flagged rather than silently resolved in either direction. On balance this run treats the bill-number split (n° 66/2024 = sanctions, n° 74/2024 = prevention) as now reasonably well-corroborated across three independent search passes (Runs 203 and 204), even though the exact sanction figures remain unconfirmed pending a successful direct fetch or ARP registry check.
AgriculturePending

🏛️ Tourism & Cultural Heritage

Tourism Organization Law — Law No. 2012-49 of July 2, 2012 (Code du Tourisme)

Enacted: July 2, 2012 | In force: 2012 | Replaced: Decree-Laws No. 73-3 & 73-4 of October 3, 1973 (ratified by Laws No. 73-58 & 73-59 of November 19, 1973) and their implementing classification Decree No. 73-510 of October 30, 1973
Governs all tourism activities in Tunisia: classification and licensing of hotels (1–5 stars), travel agencies, tour operators, holiday villages, and tourist guides. Hotels classified by the Ministry of Tourism with annual inspection. The National Tourism Office (ONTT — Office National du Tourisme Tunisien) promotes tourism internationally. Investment incentives for hotel renovation under the Investment Law 2016. Tourism zone master plans govern coastal development. Replaced the sector's original 1973 framework — Decree-Law 73-3 (ratified by Law 73-58) controlled tourist-establishment management/operation, Decree-Law 73-4 (ratified by Law 73-59) controlled their construction, and Decree No. 73-510 set hotel classification standards (itself amended in 1983 and 1986, later updated by Decree No. 2007-457 of March 6, 2007, prior to this 2012 Code's full recodification). 2025: Draft Law No. 93/2025 proposed 30% social tourism quota, generating hotel federation opposition. (Run 174 addition): a second, previously undocumented reform bill was examined alongside the social-tourism quota at the same ARP Tourism Committee session on March 11, 2026 — a proposal to replace the tourist-guide licensing regime, unchanged since the 1970s, with stricter qualification requirements (specialized training, Tunisian history/culture knowledge, foreign-language proficiency); as of that session, the committee had not yet decided whether to amend the existing framework or draft an entirely new one, and said it would consult further stakeholders before finalizing the text (La Presse, Managers.tn, both March 2026). No later committee action or vote has been found as of this run.
TourismCommercial

Law No. 2008-33 of May 13, 2008Shared-Time Tourist Accommodation (Hébergement Touristique à Temps Partagé)

Enacted: May 13, 2008 | Published: JORT No. 40, May 16, 2008 | Implementing texts: Decree No. 2009-1935 of June 15, 2009; Ministerial Order of June 16, 2009
Regulates Tunisia's "timeshare" tourist-accommodation sector, distinct from the general Tourism Organization Law above. Amends an earlier 1997 framework and requires operators to obtain prior authorization before offering shared-time accommodation rights. Characterizes the right acquired by a purchaser as a droit de jouissance (usufruct-type real right) rather than co-ownership (multipropriété) as sometimes marketed — a deliberate divergence from the ownership model used in some other jurisdictions.
