  • (Confirmed Run 139) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 11 and 13, 2026 triggers themselves had still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff, so no update to either is expected until a run lands after those dates. This run's genuine contribution was outside the pending-bill watchlist entirely: the Insurance Code entry's Fonds de Garantie des Victimes des Accidents de la Circulation — the traffic-accident-victim compensation fund underlying the still-pending cap-raising bill already tracked there — had never been documented itself, nor its January 1, 2025 restructuring into a special Treasury account under Finance Law 2025 Art. 18 (repealing Insurance Code Arts. 172/174–176). Future runs should not re-flag this fund as a gap; it is now fully cited. The next run landing before July 11 should likewise expect a same-day, no-movement watchlist result and prioritize a fresh structural gap-scan (in an area not yet covered by the dozen-plus prior scans) over re-running the identical pending-bill sweep.
  • (Confirmed Run 140) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a fourteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; July 11 and 13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. With pending-bill sweeps and structural gap-scans now showing near-zero marginal yield across roughly twenty combined passes, this run instead spot-checked citation accuracy on five older entries never individually re-verified since addition — four (National Standardization System Law 2009-38, Public Enterprises & Privatization Law 89-9, Debt-Recovery Companies Law 98-4, Passports & Travel Documents Law 75-40) confirmed exact matches, but the Commercial Register Law entry was found to cite a since-repealed statute (Law No. 95-44 of 1995) rather than its actual current successor (Law No. 2018-52 of October 29, 2018, creating the RNE) — now corrected. Future runs should prioritize (1) checking July 11 and July 13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes, over re-running the exhausted same-day sweep, and (2) continuing a rolling citation spot-check of early-run (Run 1–70) additions, since this "real but superseded citation" error class appears distinct from — and likely more common than — the outright-fabrication class Run 134 found, and no systematic pass for it has been run project-wide.
  • (Confirmed Run 141) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a fifteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; July 11 and 13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff (only days away now). A second five-entry citation spot-check (Narcotics Law 92-52, Anti-Human Trafficking Law 2016-61, Prison/Penitentiary Law 2001-52, Racial Discrimination Law 2018-50, Private International Law 98-97) found all five accurate and currently in force — no repeat of Run 140's superseded-citation finding this round. This run's genuine yield came from three enforcement/implementation-level developments under already-documented statutes: the Competition and Prices Law entry gained the Conseil de la Concurrence's December 31, 2025 fines (TND 4.2M combined, reported January 2026); the Renewable Energy Law entry gained the Round 6 solar-authorization call (200 MW, April 15 – June 15, 2026 application window); and the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry — untouched since Run 71 — gained deputy Issam Chouchane's July 6, 2026 public criticism. A secondary source's claim that the Personal Data Protection/AI bill enters into force July 11, 2026 could not be corroborated against any primary text and was deliberately withheld pending confirmation. Future runs should continue the rolling early-run citation spot-check (roughly 15 of ~120 Run 1–70 additions now individually re-verified across Runs 140–141) and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 146) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-third consecutive trigger-date pass, and July 11/13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Rather than re-running the exhausted same-day sweep, this run traced two external leads to ground: a search-engine summary conflating a stale 2024 Tunisian Council of Ministers session with unrelated French (elysee.fr/info.gouv.fr) government content (a new variant of the stale-date/cross-country conflation trap), and an adams.africa legal blog's inaccurate claim that the pending copyright/Marrakesh-Treaty bill (No. 047/2024) was "approved" May 12, 2026 — actually the unrelated Status of the Artist Law's adoption date. Both were correctly withheld. A citation spot-check corrected the SICAR Law entry, which had cited only its 1995 amendment as if it were the foundational statute rather than the true base law (88-92 of 1988). Future runs should continue the rolling citation spot-check of early-run additions and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 147) This run's designated branch held no PR history and was checked out at a stale v2.6-era baseline (July 2026-dated commits topping out at 6 entries/v2.6) while origin/main had independently progressed through 146 merged runs to v4.146 (273 entries); confirmed via git log origin/main and an empty search_pull_requests result for the branch, and reset directly to current origin/main before drafting — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A fresh sweep re-confirmed the standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-fourth consecutive trigger-date pass (Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status as of mid-June 2026 is still the last hard data point); the Personal Data Protection/AI bill's "enforcement began July 11, 2026" claim resurfaced from the same dpo-consulting.com/regulations.ai marketing sources and was again independently rejected as uncorroborated (primary Tunisian outlets — businessnews.com.tn, WMC, africanmanager — still show the bill in ARP Rights & Freedoms Commission hearings as of May 2026, with no plenary vote); and the July 11, 2026 "ONAT general assembly" item's Run 145 sourcing concern (unconfirmable against any of the three plausible ONAT bodies) could not be resolved either way this run and remains flagged as an open sourcing question. July 11 and 13, 2026 had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine contribution was a new standalone entry entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 (see the new Animal Protection and Domestic-Carnivore Regulation entry, Agriculture & Fisheries section) — filed by Deputy Chokri Bahri on July 23, 2024 and examined by the ARP Agriculture, Food Security & Maritime Fishing Commission on May 5, 2026, independently corroborated via Kapitalis (July 26, 2024 filing report) and two businessnews.com.tn articles (both May 6, 2026, on the committee session). Prior runs' "veterinary/animal health" gap-scans (Runs 82, 113, 116) had confirmed only the general Veterinary Law (No. 2005-35) as covering animal health, without distinguishing this separate companion-animal-welfare/stray-dog legislative track — a genuine gap that survived at least three prior targeted scans of adjacent terrain. One new standalone entry added (274 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 274 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and the header stat, footer version string, and page title all synchronized to v4.147/274. Future runs should continue the rolling citation spot-check of early-run additions and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes — and the ONAT sourcing question — the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 148) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-fifth consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026; the July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff — all three are now imminent and should be the first check for the next run landing after any of them. Three fresh-search candidate leads (a "conseils locaux et régionaux" law-adoption headline, a public-sector "recrutements exceptionnels" signature headline, and the July 5 plastic-bag bill) were each traced to already-documented material rather than new developments — see the v4.148 changelog note above for the specific stale-date/already-covered resolution of each. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 149) This run again hit the stale-branch failure mode (see the v4.149 changelog note above) and, after resetting to origin/main, closed one genuine gap missed by all 148 prior runs: the "Instalingo" case, a fourth distinct conspiracy-cluster prosecution (41 defendants, Feb. 5, 2025 first-instance verdict, Jan. 14, 2026 appeal upholding Ghannouchi's 22-year sentence), added as a Run 149 extension to the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain imminent and are the first check for the next run landing after any of them.
  • (Confirmed Run 150) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-sixth consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026 — no outlet has confirmed whether that session occurred or its result; Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status of mid-June 2026 is still the last hard data point. A direct check of jort.tn found no July 2026 law/decree publications, only routine "Annonces Légales" numbering (issues 076–078). The July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates were all reconfirmed as still scheduled but not yet arrived as of this same-day cutoff — these remain the first check for the next run landing after any of them. Two already-flagged false-positive traps resurfaced and were again correctly withheld: the "conseils locaux et régionaux" law headline (still its actual Feb. 27, 2025 vote/March 13, 2025 JORT publication, a database-crawl-timestamp trap) and the dpo-consulting.com "Personal Data Protection enforcement begins July 11, 2026" marketing claim (still uncorroborated by any primary source). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 151) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-seventh consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates were all reconfirmed as still scheduled but not yet arrived as of this same-day cutoff. A fresh sweep also surfaced a discrepancy worth flagging for reconciliation rather than silent resolution: the Investment Code's ARP committee-track status ("article-by-article voting phase," per the standalone entry) and the Run 70 IACE finding that the same reform is "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" continue to read as describing two distinct tracks (executive-drafted vs. MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024) rather than a contradiction — no primary source yet reconciles them, so both are left standing as previously documented. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Added Run 152) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-eighth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff (roughly one hour after Run 151's). This run's genuine contribution was outside the pending-bill watchlist: a new standalone Affaire Bilel Chaouachi entry (Security & Counter-terrorism section) documenting a jihadist leader's 55-year terrorism sentence, confirmed on appeal July 6, 2026 — a fourth distinct Anti-Terrorism Law (Organic Law 2015-26) prosecution track alongside the state-security-conspiracy, Secret Apparatus, and Instalingo cases already documented. One new standalone entry added (275 total).
  • (Confirmed Run 153) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-ninth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run enriched two existing rights-defender entries (Bensedrine/Krichi in the State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry; Mosbah and five Mnemty co-defendants in the Racial Discrimination Law entry) via a July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report — see the v4.153 changelog note above for full detail. New false-positive trap for future runs: a "July 8" mass Ennahda/opposition sentencing (21 of 24 defendants, 12–35 years) surfaced by a fresh search is the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 2 first-instance verdict with its year dropped by a search-engine summary, not a new 2026 event — treat any bare "July 8" reference to this case as this same trap rather than re-investigating. No new standalone entries were added (275 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 154) This run again hit the stale-branch failure mode (an initial drafting pass was mistakenly started against the v2.6-era fork before being caught and discarded — see the v4.154 changelog note above). After resetting to origin/main, the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome was reconfirmed unreported a thirtieth consecutive trigger-date pass — the only July 6, 2026 hit found was a deputy's floor remarks urging faster passage, not a reported committee vote. No new JORT publication, watchlist-item movement, or court ruling newer than Run 153's July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report was found. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (275 total remains accurate). The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
  • (Added Run 155) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-first consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine contributions: the previously-pending Ajmi Lourimi/Mossaâb Gharbi terrorism-chamber case (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry) was updated with its July 4, 2026 verdict (3 years each), and a new standalone Affaire "Créateurs de Contenu" entry (Security & Counter-terrorism section) documents an 18-defendant, 1–27-year mass sentencing of TikTok/Instagram content creators on July 6, 2026. One new standalone entry added (276 total).
  • (Confirmed Run 156) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-second consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run closed a standing sourcing question rather than a substantive gap: the July 11 ONAT general assembly — independently unconfirmable to Runs 119, 128, 131, 145, and 147 — is now corroborated by a June 30, 2026 Réalités Magazine article (see the Judicial Statute entry's Run 156 update). A "graduate-recruitment bill approved" headline and a "zero paper" digitalization lead were both traced to already-documented material (Law No. 2025-18 and the Electronic Procedures entry's Run 111 update, respectively). No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 158) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported (Run 157 did not re-check this item; this run's fresh search found no post-July-6, 2026 committee session outcome either, extending the no-movement streak Run 156 last confirmed at its thirty-second consecutive pass); the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run closed a narrow implementation-level gap instead: the Maritime Ports Code entry gained the Skhira commercial port's 18-year-old OMMP/TRAPSA concession impasse and the Transport Ministry's July 6, 2026 regularization measures, previously undocumented across 157 prior runs. No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate).
  • (Added Run 159) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff, and a dedicated research pass found no watchlist-item status change beyond what Run 158 already captured. This run's genuine yield was two structural gaps entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist, both verified against primary/near-primary sources: the Private Security, Guarding & Valuables-Transport Activities Law (No. 2002-81/2002) and the Telemedicine framework (Presidential Decree No. 2022-318/2022, supplemented by June 2024 ministerial orders) — both areas of everyday regulated activity this project had never documented despite 158 prior runs' gap-scans. A candidate French decree (2024-164) was correctly excluded after direct-source verification revealed it governs French, not Tunisian, teleconsultation companies. Future runs should continue prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes, and consider further "everyday regulated activity" gap-scans (e.g., private detective/investigation licensing, funeral/burial services regulation, taxi-meter/fare regulation) as a productive vein alongside the pending-bill watchlist, since both new entries this run came from that category rather than from politics/legislation news.
  • (Confirmed/enriched Run 160) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Of Run 159's three suggested next-vein leads, two (funeral-services regulation, taxi-meter tariffs) surfaced no citable dedicated Tunisian statute — both are administrative/decree-level, not standalone codes — but the third, private-detective/investigation licensing, resolved into a genuine enrichment rather than a dead end: Article 15 (and the related Article 16) of the Private Security Law (No. 2002-81, added Run 159) was fetched directly from legislation-securite.tn and added to that entry as the specific legal basis, previously undocumented, for Tunisia's prohibition on private-investigation activity. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Private Security Law) enriched. Future runs should continue prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes.
  • (Corrected Run 161) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. A thirteen-topic "everyday regulated activity" gap-scan closed no new standalone gaps — twelve of thirteen topics checked were already covered by existing entries or exist only at decree/administrative level (credit-rating agencies confirmed as a genuine legislative void, not a citable gap; elderly care homes traced to the already-existing Elderly Persons — Rights and Protection entry, Law No. 1994-2). This run's genuine yield came from a citation spot-check instead: the Judicial Officers (Huissiers de Justice) entry's long-standing "Law No. 66-49 of 1966" citation was found, via direct primary-source verification, to reference a law that does not exist — corrected to the real governing statute, Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 (successor to Law No. 95-29 of 1995). Future runs should treat "Law No. 66-49" as a closed, resolved error rather than re-investigating, and should continue both the rolling early-run citation spot-check and prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 162) The July 8, 2026 trigger date has now arrived, but same-day indexing lag left the graduate-recruitment protest's outcome (and whether implementing decrees for Law No. 2025-18 were published) unconfirmed — the next run should check this first. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A ten-topic economic/regulatory gap-scan (Environmental Code, Water Code, Data Protection Law, Competition Law, Telecommunications Code, e-invoicing, Investment Code, renewable-energy self-production, Hydrocarbons Code, Constitutional Court organic law) found all ten already covered by existing entries or correctly excluded as still-unenacted drafts — no genuine gaps. A viral false claim of a new Road Traffic Code entering into force July 1, 2026 was investigated and correctly excluded. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize same-day confirmation of the July 8 decree-publication status, then July 11 and July 13.
  • (Added Run 163) The July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest/Law No. 2025-18 decree-publication outcome remains unconfirmed by same-day reporting; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine yield came from naming specific "everyday regulated activity" statutes rather than assuming coverage by a broader entry — a technique that closed two gaps missed by prior scans: a new standalone Sales Techniques and Commercial Advertising Law (No. 98-40/1998) entry (Commercial Law) and a new standalone Railway Law entry (Transport) covering SNCFT's three founding/governing statutes (Laws No. 69-31/1969, 98-74/1998, 98-90/1998) — the latter specifically survived the Run 136/140 "rail transport" gap-scans, which had apparently assumed the general Land Transport Law (2004-33) covers rail without checking that its text is road-only. Note for future runs: the railway-law entry's underlying JORT issue numbers could not be independently confirmed this run (only titles/dates, via SNCFT's own site and legislation.tn) — a citation spot-check target for a future run. Two new standalone entries added (280 total). Future runs should prioritize the July 8 decree-publication status, then July 11 and July 13, and continue naming specific narrow statutes (e.g., driving-school licensing decree-vs-statute status, cinema/audiovisual production incentives, port-worker/docker labor statute) rather than re-running broad thematic gap-scans, which have shown near-zero marginal yield across 30+ combined passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 167) The July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest was confirmed as having actually occurred today near Place de la Kasbah in Tunis (webdo.tn), with Law No. 2025-18's implementing decrees and digital application platform still unpublished — now reflected directly in that entry's own text. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported (a June 11, 2026 article suggesting movement toward a plenary session was checked and found superseded by the already-documented June 17, 2026 status). July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain unchanged and not yet arrived. Two narrow leads (insurance-broker/actuary licensing, halal-certification authority) were confirmed already adequately covered by the existing Insurance Code entry and the National Standardization System Law entry, respectively — not standalone gaps. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize July 11 and July 13 the moment each passes, and continue naming specific narrow statutes (e.g., driving-school licensing, cinema/audiovisual production incentives, port-worker/docker labor statute) as a gap-finding technique.
  • (Added Run 169) The Renewable Energy Law entry's May 13, 2026 CNRD note gained its missing ARP origin: the five photovoltaic concession bills (Laws n°01/2026–05/2026) were first adopted by the ARP on April 28, 2026 with individually recorded votes, the same day President Saied dismissed Energy Minister Fatma Thabet Chiboub amid UGTT/opposition "energy colonisation" criticism of the 20(+10)-year foreign-operator concessions — cross-verified via direct WebFetch of africanews.com and entreprises-magazine.com rather than trusted from a WebSearch synthesis alone. A related WebSearch synthesis claiming a "July 6, 2026" vote on the Local/Regional/District Councils organic law was checked via direct source fetch and found to be a date artifact of search summarization, not a real event — the underlying Organic Law No. 2025-4 (March 12, 2025) was already accurately documented since Run 13; future runs encountering this "July 6" date for that specific law should treat it as an already-debunked search-synthesis artifact. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the nearest-term triggers, still not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff.
  • (Resolved Run 170) The "July 11 ONAT" trigger, unconfirmed since Run 145 flagged it as possibly misattributed among three candidate professional orders, is now resolved: ONAT is the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (Bar Association), confirmed via avocat.org.tn. The Lawyers Statute entry — previously silent on any 2026 development — was enriched with the underlying ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff (January 23, 2026 reform demands; the disputed May 1, 2026 exceptional general assembly; the June 18, 2026 national general strike; the July 11, 2026 annual assembly expected to set dialogue terms). The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11's assembly outcome and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the nearest-term triggers, still not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A five-topic check (midwifery, customs brokers, driving-school licensing, hunting, Development Plan bill plenary status) found no new standalone gaps — all are either already covered or exist only at ministerial-order/administrative level. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed/enriched Run 171) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-h8259v) again hit the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it held a self-contained v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's very first commit, 249 commits behind origin/main's independent 170-run v4.170 history. Two candidate "gaps" drafted before catching this (Law No. 2025-9 labor-subcontracting reform; Decree-Law 2023-17/ANCS cybersecurity) were discarded unmerged upon discovering both were already fully documented in main (see the Employment Reform Law and National Cybersecurity Framework entries) — a useful confirmation that this changelog's "near-zero marginal yield" warning extends even to gaps that look real from a stale baseline. After resetting to origin/main, the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment/Law 2025-18 decree-publication status was rechecked and remains unpublished as of the latest identified reporting (Le Temps News, June 22, 2026) — no change from Run 170. July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine yield was a narrow enrichment: the Cinema and Cultural Industries entry gained a distinct, previously undocumented fiscal measure — Proposition de loi n° 103, an ARP floor vote on December 3, 2025 (57–34–8) setting a 7% tax rate on cinema/audiovisual production equipment and a full customs-duty exemption on its import — corroborated via two independent sources (La Presse, WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, both Dec. 3, 2025); its actual in-force/JORT-publication status is flagged as unresolved per the Finance Minister's own floor-debate characterization of it as separate regulatory text. A driving-school/auto-école lead and a docker/port-worker-labor-statute lead were both checked and found either already covered (Run 164) or not resolving into a citable standalone statute. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize July 11/13 outcomes the moment each passes, and should verify their designated branch against origin/main (not local commit history alone) before drafting any changes.
  • (Added/corrected Run 172) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-1xaalz) again hit the stale-checkout failure mode: it held a self-contained v1.0–v2.10 lineage (13 commits, its own changelog topping out at "170" entries) forked from the project's first commit, entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 171-run v4.171 history (280 entries) until git fetch origin main/git ls-remote --heads origin surfaced it — a fresh commit had already been pushed against the stale base before the discrepancy was caught; the branch was reset to origin/main and that commit's candidate findings were re-verified from scratch rather than reused. Of six candidates drafted against the stale base, four were confirmed already present in main with equal or greater precision and discarded: the Sports Structures Law (already Organic Law No. 95-11, corrected in Run 44), the CNAM health-insurance law (already the extensively-maintained CNAM entry, actively tracking the July 13 tiers-payant trigger), the Agadir Agreement (already the detailed Agadir Agreement entry), and GAFTA (already covered as "PAFTA, 1997" within the WTO Membership and Bilateral Trade Agreements entry — same agreement, different common acronym). One candidate — an initial search result appearing to show a Tunisian court verdict dated exactly July 8, 2026 — was investigated and found to be the search tool conflating the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 1 first-instance verdict with the June 2026 Secret Apparatus sentencing; discarded as a false positive, consistent with this changelog's established practice of rejecting date-conflation artifacts. That same investigation surfaced a genuine sixth Ghannouchi prosecution — the "Ramadan Evening Gathering" case (April 14–15, 2026, 20 years, five further defendants in absentia) — added as a Run 172 update within the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry after cross-checking against all 171 prior runs' coverage (no match found). Two further genuine items were closed: a new standalone Tourism Investment Code entry (Law No. 90-21 of 1990), a sector-specific statute distinct from both the general Investment Law and the Tourism Organization Law and never previously documented; and a correction to the Deposit Guarantee Fund entry, which had cited the fund's superseded 2006 predecessor (Law No. 2006-19, "FGTD," TND 20,000 cap) — independent verification against the fund's own official site (fgdb.gov.tn) confirmed the scheme was re-created under Art. 149 of the 2016 Banking Law as the FGDB, with a TND 60,000 cap and 20-working-day payout. One new standalone entry added (281 total); two existing entries substantially revised (one corrected, one extended). July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff.
  • (Enriched Run 173) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-b0aab2) again held only a stale v2.0–v2.10 lineage (13 commits) while origin/main had independently progressed through 172 merged runs to v4.172 (281 entries) — the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any research began, avoiding the recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A research pass rechecked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code committee status, the July 11 ONAT and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant triggers) and found no change: none of the three dates had arrived or produced a reported outcome as of this run's cutoff, and the Code des Changes remains at Ktari's mid-June ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with no confirmed committee vote or plenary date. Cross-checking CPJ's May 2026 "Beyond Decree 54" report (already cited in the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry) against its full source text surfaced a genuine, previously undocumented gap: the escalation of the civil-society crackdown from mere administrative suspension (already tracked in the Administrative Suspensions entry) to actual judicial dissolution proceedings. Al Khatt — the association publishing the investigative outlet Inkyfada (an ICIJ partner) since 2014 — was suspended for 30 days in October 2025, lost its court challenge to that suspension, and was then targeted by a Presidency-filed dissolution request (announced April 21–23, 2026); the Tunis Court of First Instance opened joint dissolution proceedings against Al Khatt and the anti-racism association Mnemty (whose president Saadia Mosbah is separately being prosecuted on unrelated charges, see the Racial Discrimination Law entry) on May 11, 2026, postponing the session first to June 1 and then to July 6, 2026 — corroborated across RSF, ICIJ, businessnews.com.tn, webdo.tn, and Inkyfada's own reporting, with no outcome confirmed by any source as of this run's cutoff. Both the Administrative Suspensions entry (retitled to reflect the dissolution escalation) and the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry were cross-referenced accordingly. No new standalone entry was added (281 total remains accurate, since this enriches two existing cards); two existing entries substantially revised. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 281 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.173/Run 173. July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside the still-unresolved LTDH appeal (postponed to July 15) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution case's still-pending July 6 outcome.
  • (v4.174 update, Run 174) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-8pzix2) again held only a stale, self-contained v1.0–v2.10 lineage entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 173-run history (281 entries, v4.173) — reset directly to current origin/main before any research began, per this changelog's established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a structured gap-scan across twelve regulated-activity/profession candidates (money-changers, public weighbridges, beekeeping, veterinary pharmacy, dockworker labor, private tutoring, car rental, tour-guide licensing, hunting/wildlife, dam safety, PPP implementing decrees, data-center/cloud licensing, consumer arbitration) plus a fresh July 6–8, 2026 news sweep. Ten of the twelve candidates were confirmed already covered at equal or greater precision by existing entries, decree/circular-level (not a legislative gap), or a genuine regulatory absence not yet reduced to enacted text (consumer ADR) — none re-added. Two survived independent verification: (1) a new standalone Veterinary Pharmacy Law entry (Law No. 78-23 of 1978, as amended by Law No. 2000-40 of 2000) — a narrow but genuine gap distinct from the existing Veterinary Law (2005-35) entry, since 78-23 governs veterinary-medicine manufacturing/market-authorization rather than animal-health surveillance or practice licensing, and had survived four prior "veterinary" gap-scans (Runs 82, 90, 113, 116); (2) a pending tour-guide-licensing reform bill examined by the ARP Tourism Committee on March 11, 2026 alongside the already-tracked social-tourism-quota bill, added as an enrichment to the existing Tourism Organization Law entry rather than a new card. A third finding — the ARP Bureau's Monday, July 6, 2026 referral of the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill to a fresh joint-committee examination round rather than directly to plenary — was added as an enrichment to that entry (confirmed via direct fetch of the primary RTCI report). A claimed "Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025" gap and a claimed "Personal Data Protection full enforcement July 11, 2026" claim were both investigated and found already accurately captured (the former in the existing Constitutional Court entry with more precision than the new lead; the latter re-confirmed as the same recurring marketing-blog false positive rejected in the Run 147 note above). The July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome remains unreported by any source as of this run's cutoff — a genuine "not yet indexed" result rather than a resolved item, consistent with the two-days-since-hearing timing. A recurring "July 8" date-conflation trap (the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 2 verdict, entry 220) was re-encountered and correctly not re-added. One new standalone entry added (282 total); three existing entries enriched (Tourism Organization Law, 2026–2030 National Development Plan, and — via the research agent's confirmation — no change needed to the Constitutional Court entry). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.174/Run 174. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 175) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-kx88s2) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.10-era fork (unaware of origin/main's 174-run history, 282 entries, v4.174) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the standing highest-priority watchlist — the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome, the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee status, the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, the Personal Data Protection & AI organic bill, the Associations Decree reform, the July 11 ONAT assembly, the July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, and the July 15 LTDH appeal — and found no change to any item: the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution outcome remains unreported by any source, and the three forward trigger dates (July 11, 13, 15) had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Four candidate leads were investigated and confirmed already fully captured rather than gaps: Saadia Mosbah/Mnemty co-defendants' June 23, 2026 appeal ruling (already documented in detail since Run 153, Racial Discrimination Law entry); the July 1, 2026 CNAM–pharmacist "engagement de paiement" resolution (already documented since Run 30, CNAM entry); the July 11 ONAT trigger (already resolved at Run 170 as the Bar Association, Lawyers Statute entry); and a recirculating rumor of a new Road Traffic Code entering into force July 1, 2026 (already investigated and debunked at Run 162). One newly-encountered false-positive trap was identified and correctly rejected rather than added: a search-engine synthesis suggesting a "July 8, 2026" mass sentencing of 21 defendants including Rached Ghannouchi did not match the text of the HRW source article it was purportedly drawn from on direct fetch — an apparent hallucination/conflation with an earlier (2025) proceeding, distinct from the already-documented "State Security Conspiracy" prosecutions entry; flagged here so a future run encountering the same claim does not re-investigate it as if new. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (282 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.175/Run 175. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Enriched Run 176) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sk21m2) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (13 commits, its own changelog topping out at "171" entries) forked from the project's very first commit, entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 175-run history (282 entries, v4.175); a fresh commit had already been drafted and pushed against the stale base (adding an LTDH-suspension note and a standalone animal-welfare pending-reform card) before git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main surfaced the divergence. Both candidates were checked against the real current artifact rather than reused: the LTDH suspension was found already covered in far greater depth by the existing Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry (which additionally tracks ASF Tunisia, ATFD, FTDES, Al Khatt, and Mnemty), and the animal-welfare bill was found already documented as the existing Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 entry — both discarded as duplicates rather than re-added, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before restarting. A re-check of the standing watchlist (Al Khatt/Mnemty July 6 hearing outcome, Foreign Exchange Code committee status) found no movement on either since Run 175. This run's genuine yield was a same-day news update to the animal-welfare entry: a video showing a performance-troupe member dancing on the belly of a horse lying on the ground during a wedding ceremony in Sahline (Monastir governorate) went viral around July 6–7, 2026, prompting the animal-protection association Arrahma to pursue a formal criminal complaint against the identified troupe and Parliament to accelerate review of the pending bill; Business News (July 7, 2026) reported the acceleration, and press coverage (Business News, Univers News, allAfrica) supplied the bill's previously-uncaptured tiered penalty structure (15 days–1 month/TND 500 for general mistreatment, 1–10 years/TND 2,000 for abuse causing permanent disability, 5 years for abuse causing death). No new standalone entry was added (282 total remains accurate, since this enriches one existing card); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.176/Run 176. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 177) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ssaj54) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.10 lineage (12 commits, its own changelog topping out at a "v2.10" label with no matching pull request found via list_pull_requests), entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 176-run history (282 entries, v4.176) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" (consistent with every run since roughly Run 68), so this run relied on WebSearch alone. A fresh sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist — the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee vote, the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, the Personal Data Protection & AI organic bill, the Associations Decree reform, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, the July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, the July 15 LTDH appeal, and the still-unreported July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome — and found no movement on any item; a direct check for new July 2026 JORT publications likewise surfaced nothing beyond what prior runs already captured. This run also re-encountered the recurring "July 8" mass Ennahda/opposition sentencing false-positive trap (21 of 24 defendants, 12–35 years, purportedly including Rached Ghannouchi) first flagged at Run 153 and re-confirmed at Run 175; rather than relying on search-summary text alone, this run fetched the actual source articles directly (HRW's July 7, 2026 "Tunisia: Harsh Sentences for Rights Defenders" and July 8, 2026 "Sounding the Alarm Over Tunisia's Crackdown") and confirmed neither mentions Ghannouchi or any 21-defendant ruling — both instead discuss the already-documented Bensedrine/Krichi/Mosbah rights-defender sentencings — reinforcing with primary-source verification that this remains the same year-dropped conflation with the July 8, 2025 Case 2 verdict, not a new 2026 event. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (282 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.177/Run 177. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.178 update, Run 178): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-vlq0nj) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (its own changelog topping out at a "v2.11" label, freshly drafted and opened as PR #201 before a merge-conflict error on submission caught the divergence from origin/main's independent 177-run history, 282 entries, v4.177) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any further edits, per established practice. The stale draft's own candidate findings (a food-safety entry, a plant-protection entry, and an Abir Moussi case entry) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than reused: the Abir Moussi case was found already covered, at greater precision, within the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry (Run 168's detailed "bureau d'ordre" and ISIE-case update). The food-safety and plant-protection leads, however, led to a genuine discovery: both topics already had standalone entries (Food Safety, Plant Health/Veterinary), but cross-checking the stale draft's independently-sourced law numbers against theirs surfaced two real, previously-unnoticed citation errors. (1) The Food Safety entry's citation of "Law No. 2009-11 of February 11, 2009" and "ANCSEP" as its creating authority was wrong on both counts: Law No. 2009-11 (actually dated March 2, 2009) is Tunisia's building fire/explosion/panic-safety code, unrelated to food safety (confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and citet.nat.tn), and ANCSEP was created separately by Decree No. 99-779 of 1999 as a general product-safety coordinator. The entry is corrected to cite Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019, which creates the actual food-safety-specific authority, INSSPA, confirmed via FAOLEX's full text and independent INSSPA/ANCSEP institutional-comparison sources. (2) The Plant Health entry's citation of "Law No. 99-24 of February 23, 1999" for plant protection was also wrong: Law No. 99-24 is in fact dated March 9, 1999 and governs veterinary sanitary control at import/export, an unrelated subject, corroborated across two independent legal databases; the entry is corrected to cite Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992, Tunisia's actual phytosanitary/plant-protection recast statute (FAOLEX-confirmed), and its unverifiable "INAP" institutional claim is replaced with the independently-confirmed Direction Générale de la Santé Végétale et du Contrôle des Intrants Agricoles. Both corrections retain the artifact's practice of not asserting article-level citations that cannot be independently verified. A fresh check of the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, July 11 ONAT, July 13 CNAM/biologists, July 15 LTDH appeal, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome) found no movement on any item, and this run's own attempt at a broader gap-scan (tobacco, gambling, whistleblower, urban-transport candidates) found each already covered by prior runs. No new standalone entries were added (282 total remains accurate, since both genuine findings are corrections to existing cards); two existing entries (Food Safety, Plant Health) corrected for citation accuracy. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.178/Run 178. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.179 update, Run 179): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-olmm1d) again held no commits of its own beyond a stale fork, and was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.178, 282 entries, 178 merged runs) before drafting, per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the full standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff; and the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported by any outlet checked (businessnews, webdo, Kapitalis, Inkyfada, RSF, ICIJ, Amnesty) — a real information gap rather than a resolved item, flagged for the next run to re-check rather than assumed to be a further postponement. The 2026–2030 Development Plan bill's July 2–3 committee-conclusion status was reconfirmed as already accurately captured by the existing entry (including its Run 174 "referred to fresh joint-examination round" update), so no change was made there despite an initial research lead suggesting a discrepancy. A ten-topic "everyday regulated activity" gap-scan (consumer protection, firearms, prison administration, an ombudsman-equivalent institution, forestry, mining, civil aviation, postal services, notaries, and sworn translators/interpreters) found eight of ten already covered by existing entries at the correct citation (cross-checked directly against the live document rather than trusting the agent's summary — e.g., a candidate "Consumer Protection Law" citation matched the existing entry verbatim, and a candidate "Notaries Law" citation matched the existing entry, both already correct since Run 44). Two survived as genuine gaps, independently verified via legislation-securite.tn, AOMF, and ILO NATLEX before inclusion: (1) a new standalone Administrative Mediator (Médiateur Administratif) entry (Decree No. 92-2143 of 1992; Law No. 93-51 of May 3, 1993) — Tunisia's ombudsman-equivalent institution for administrative grievances, never previously documented despite the closely related Administrative Court entry existing since early runs; (2) a new standalone Sworn Interpreters Law entry (Law No. 94-80 of July 4, 1994) — a companion legal-profession statute to the Notaries and Bailiffs statutes, both already documented, that had never itself been added. Two new standalone entries added (284 total). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 284 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 284/284 balanced <h3> tags, 1346/1346 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.179/Run 179. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 180): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-tij8kc) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (14 commits, its own changelog topping out at a "v2.12" label freshly drafted — adding a graduate-recruitment-law entry and a Postal Bank bill entry — before git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main revealed origin/main's independent 179-run history, 284 entries, v4.179); consistent with this changelog's established practice, the branch was reset directly to origin/main and the stale draft's two candidate findings were cross-checked rather than reused — both were found already present in main (the Law No. 2025-18 graduate-recruitment statute, added at Run 64 and since enriched through Run 167 — including the exact July 8, 2026 Kasbah protest this run's own research had independently resurfaced — and the Postal Bank bill, already tracked in Pending Reforms) and discarded as duplicates. A fresh research pass re-checked the full standing watchlist — the Foreign Exchange Code committee status, the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates, and the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome — and found no movement on any item: none of the three forward dates had arrived as of this run's cutoff (July 8, 2026, ~21:00 UTC), the Exchange Code remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with a July meeting with the Finance Minister still only "envisaged," and the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remains genuinely unreported by any outlet checked. Two HRW pieces dated July 7 and July 8, 2026 ("Tunisia: Harsh Sentences for Rights Defenders" and "Sounding the Alarm Over Tunisia's Crackdown") were fetched directly and confirmed to recap only the already-documented June 2026 Bensedrine and Mosbah/Mnemty sentences, with no new July 2026 legal development and no Ghannouchi mass-sentencing event — reinforcing the same year-dropped July 8, 2025-vs-2026 conflation trap flagged at Runs 153/175/177. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (284 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 284 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 284/284 balanced <h3> tags, 1346/1346 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.180/Run 180. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.182 update, Run 182): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-94lzz6) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage forked from the project's first commit (fourteen commits, its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label) while origin/main had independently progressed through 181 merged runs to v4.181 (286 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main ^HEAD (269 commits ahead) after a first commit had already been pushed against the stale base; that commit was not reused, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. The stale draft's three candidate case entries (Samir Abdelli, Chawki Tabib, Rached Khiari) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than blindly re-added: Abdelli was found already present, at equal precision, as the existing Affaire Samir Abdelli entry (added Run 181) — not re-added. Khiari's case was found genuinely under-documented: a Run 155 changelog note had asserted it was "already fully captured," but a direct full-text search for "Khiari" found no such content anywhere in the artifact body, only in that changelog note itself — the existing Telecommunications Code entry documented only the Zied El Heni Article 86 prosecution; added as a new Run 182 update to that same entry (his 8-month first-instance sentence reduced to 6 months on appeal, April 10, 2026, plus CFJ's health concerns), rather than a new standalone card, matching the entry's existing convention of grouping Article 86 prosecutions together. One candidate survived as a genuine, previously-undocumented standalone gap: the Affaire Chawki Tabib case — the former ONAT (Bar Association) president and, notably, former 2016–2020 president of INLUCC (the anti-corruption body already documented in the Declaration of Assets/INLUCC entry), sentenced May 21, 2026 to 10 years for forgery in a case tied to an INLUCC-era report against former PM Elyes Fakhfakh — a full-text search of 181 prior runs found no match for "Tabib"; corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie, the IAPL Monitoring Committee on Attacks on Lawyers, and CRLDHT, and cross-referenced both directions with the existing INLUCC entry. One new standalone entry added (287 total, up from 286); one existing entry (Telecommunications Code) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 287 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.182/Run 182. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
  • (v4.181 update, Run 181): This run's designated branch again held no commits of its own beyond a stale, never-merged v2.11-era fork built without first checking origin/main — the branch was reset directly to origin/main (v4.180, 284 entries, 180 merged runs) before drafting, per established practice; the stale draft's own candidate findings (three new case entries and a plastics-bill/land-release/Family-Code note) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than reused. Two survived as genuine gaps; the rest did not. Cross-checking found the stale draft's Makram Jlassi and single-use-plastics-bill candidates already present in main at equal or greater precision (Jlassi since Run 87; the plastics bill since Runs 109/132, already citing the same 25-deputy sponsorship, bag-material standards, and imprisonment tier the stale draft had independently re-derived), and its proposed "Family Code/joint-custody" pending-reform entry was found to duplicate, without adding anything the existing 2018 Presidential Commission Report/Inheritance Equality entry doesn't already cover more precisely — none of the three were re-added. Two genuine, independently-verified gaps closed: (1) a wholly new standalone Affaire Samir Abdelli entry (Judicial Organization section) — a lawyer and 2014 presidential candidate previously named in the SwissLeaks/Panama Papers disclosures, sentenced July 7, 2026 by the Tunis terrorism-specialized criminal chamber to 18 years for money laundering, terrorism financing, and state-security conspiracy (driver and secretary separately sentenced to 3 and 2 years), a case with zero prior mentions across 180 runs — corroborated across Business News, Kapitalis, and Mosaïque FM; (2) a wholly new standalone Affaire Ayachi Hammami entry, a genuine gap despite a Run 104 changelog note asserting his case was "already substantively captured" — direct full-text verification found no prior law-card documented his conviction, El Mornaguia incarceration (December 3, 2025), 43-day hunger strike (ended January 15, 2026), or the UN Human Rights Council's 62nd-session citation (closed July 8, 2026) of his case by the UN Special Rapporteur on judicial independence and High Commissioner Volker Türk — only the fact of his 2025 arrest had been name-dropped in prior changelog prose, never in a card; corroborated across OHCHR, Front Line Defenders, FIDH, LDH, and Jeune Afrique, and cross-referenced from the existing State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry, since Hammami was defense counsel to several of that case's defendants before being added as a defendant himself. One further refinement outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Sociétés Communautaires (Decree-Law 2022-15/2025-3) entry gained a July 6, 2026 concrete-application note — the state's release of roughly 17,000 hectares of state-owned agricultural land to newly-formed community enterprises under this framework's existing land-leasing mechanism (La Presse, single-sourced pending further corroboration). No change was made to the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, July 11 ONAT, July 13 CNAM/biologists, July 15 LTDH appeal, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome), all reconfirmed unchanged as of this run's cutoff. Two new standalone entries added (286 total, up from 284); one existing entry (Sociétés Communautaires) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 286 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, all internal anchor links resolving, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.181/Run 181.
  • (v4.183 update, Run 183): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ad0npp) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's first commit (its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label documenting a Penal Code Art. 96/98 note and a new animal-welfare pending-reform card) while origin/main had independently progressed through 182 merged runs to v4.182 (287 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline e0a880f..origin/main (271 commits ahead) after a first commit had already been pushed against the stale base; that commit was not reused, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. Both of the stale draft's candidate findings were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than blindly re-added, and both were found already present at equal or greater precision: the Penal Code amendment as the existing Law No. 2025-14 of July 28, 2025 standalone entry, and the animal-welfare bill as the existing Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 entry — neither was re-added. A fresh research pass then re-checked the full standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6, 2026 session outcome remains unconfirmed by any outlet as of this run's cutoff, though a June 11, 2026 Tuniscope/Tunisie Numérique report (not previously cited) added texture to the already-documented committee posture — Finance Committee president Maher Ktari was quoted arguing it is "not possible to block a proposed law due to the absence of interaction from the Central Bank," with article-by-article voting then targeted for June 20–30, 2026 — but no source confirms that vote actually occurred, so the entry's existing "not yet enacted" status stands unchanged. The Al Khatt/Mnemty judicial dissolution hearing, postponed to July 6, 2026 per the existing entry, likewise has no reported outcome from any source checked (Amnesty, ICIJ, RSF, Inkyfada, businessnews.com.tn, webdo.tn, directinfo.webmanagercenter.com) — still genuinely unreported, not a research gap. The ONAT general assembly (July 11) and CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension (July 13) trigger dates had not yet arrived as of this run's July 9, 2026 cutoff. No newly enacted JORT law, floor vote, or court ruling dated to July 8–9, 2026 was identified in a targeted sweep (JORT publications, Council of Ministers decisions, ARP committee activity). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision — a pure confirmation run (287 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 287 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <div>/<section>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.183/Run 183. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
  • (v4.184 update, Run 184): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-2rsat5) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's first commit (its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label documenting Abdallah Saïd-unrelated Hammami/Abdelli entries and a local-councils false-positive check) while origin/main had independently progressed through 183 merged runs to v4.183 (287 entries); a first commit and PR (#207) had already been opened against the stale base before git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main surfaced the divergence — the PR was closed without merging (it would have regressed main from 287 entries to 172) and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. Both of the stale draft's candidate case entries (Samir Abdelli, Ayachi Hammami) were cross-checked against the real current artifact and found already present, at equal or greater precision, as the existing Affaire Samir Abdelli and Affaire Ayachi Hammami entries (both added Run 181) — neither was re-added. A fresh research pass re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution outcome, ONAT July 11, CNAM/biologists July 13) and found no movement on any item, consistent with Run 183's same-day findings. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and closed via a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis report on an Intersection pour les droits et les libertés civil-society survey: a new standalone Affaire Abdallah Saïd entry — founding president of the Médenine humanitarian association "Les Enfants de la Lune," detained November 12, 2024 by the financial-crimes investigation unit (after the anti-terrorism unit itself found no terrorism basis), convicted April 22, 2026 to one year's imprisonment for activities supporting migrant, refugee, and disabled children, with the public prosecutor's appeal still pending — corroborated across Committee for Justice, Business News, Kapitalis, and Réalités Magazine, and cross-referenced from the existing Administrative Suspensions and Irregular Migration Law entries; a full-text search of 183 prior runs found no match for "Saïd" or "Enfants de la Lune." The same survey's other named organizations (ATFD, Mourakiboun, I Watch) were checked and found already covered by the existing civil-society-suspensions entry since Run 39. One new standalone entry added (288 total, up from 287). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates (one false-positive duplicate flagged by a naive id-grep was traced to changelog prose referencing an id inside a <code> tag, not a real duplicate element), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.184/Run 184. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
  • (v4.185 update, Run 185): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-pinz66) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.11 lineage (14 commits topping out at a "v2.11" label, 170 entries) unaware of origin/main's independent 184-run history (288 entries, v4.184) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main per established practice; no stale content was reused. Eight showed no change; one genuine, dated enrichment was found and verified before inclusion: the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry's Run 174 text explicitly stated "no plenary date has yet been announced" — a fresh RTCI report (July 7, 2026, 14:54), corroborated by a La Presse de Tunisie report of a 10:30 AM sitting, shows the ARP Bureau met the morning of July 7 and formally scheduled the plenary session for Thursday, July 9, 2026 (today), possibly extending to Friday, July 10 if debate runs long — the first concrete plenary date since the bill's June 17, 2026 filing; no vote outcome had been reported as of this update. The already-flagged Personal Data Protection "full enforcement July 11, 2026" marketing-site claim resurfaced and was again correctly withheld as uncorroborated by any primary source (consistent with Runs 72/76/174). No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate); one existing entry was enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.185/Run 185. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome, today's Development Plan plenary vote result, and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.

Cross-checking the stale draft's four candidate findings against the real current artifact found all four already present, in every case with materially more precision: the civil-society suspension wave (LTDH, ASF Tunisia, ATFD, FTDES, Aswat Nissa, OMCT-Tunisia, I Watch, Nawaat) as the existing Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry, which additionally tracks the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution case the stale draft had missed entirely; the Bilel Chaouachi terrorism case as the existing standalone entry added at Run 152; the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary-session detail (bill n°42/2026, July 9 10:30am opening, possible extension to July 10) as the existing entry's Run 185 update, worded almost identically; and the Tunisian Council for Refugees/Terre d'Asile humanitarian-worker prosecutions (Djemali, Krimi) as the existing Passports & Travel Documents Law entry added at Run 106 — none were re-added. A fresh research pass then re-checked the standing watchlist and found no movement: no Foreign Exchange Code floor vote or JORT publication, no Development Plan vote outcome (plenary session opened as scheduled but remained in deputies'-interventions stage as of this run's cutoff, per a fresh RTCI/La Presse check), and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had not yet arrived. One genuine, previously-uncaptured enrichment survived: the Intersection association for rights and freedoms' civil-society monitoring report (published via Kapitalis, July 8, 2026 — the same underlying source as Run 184's Abdallah Saïd addition, but whose aggregate statistics had not themselves been extracted into the artifact) supplies the first documented overall scale figures for the pattern — 88 cases over its May 2024–May 2026 window, 47 organizational restriction/prosecution situations, 41 individual judicial proceedings, 8 pre-trial detentions, 34 releases, 7 first-instance convictions, 22 one-month suspension orders, and four named recurring mechanisms — added as an update to the existing Administrative Suspensions entry rather than a new card. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.186/Run 186. A fresh research pass re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan plenary vote, Personal Data Protection organic law, ONAT/CNAM/LTDH trigger dates) via WebSearch and found no movement: the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary session opened as scheduled at 10:30 AM on July 9, 2026 (bill n°42/2026) with no vote outcome yet reported (RTCI, Business News, La Presse de Tunisie, all July 8–9, 2026, consistent with the existing entry's Run 185/186 text), and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had not yet arrived. A candidate lead surfaced by search — a "July 8" report of a Tunis court sentencing 21 Ennahda-linked defendants including a "14-year" in-absentia sentence for Rached Ghannouchi — was investigated and rejected as stale-date/search-index conflation: the underlying event is the already-documented July 8, 2025 first-instance "Conspiracy 2" verdict, whose sentence was subsequently raised to 20 years on the February 2, 2026 appeal already captured in the State Security Conspiracy entry — not a new 2026 ruling, and correctly not added, consistent with the cross-country/stale-date conflation trap flagged in the Runs 74/77/97/122/144/146 notes. One genuine, dated enrichment survived verification: the Constitutional Court entry's Run 76 text ended at a February 18, 2026 hearing with "consultations would continue"; a fresh search found the General Legislation Committee relaunched the file on March 27, 2026 with a hearing of the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (ONAT), whose delegation objected that the bill's all-magistrate composition would isolate the Court from outside legal expertise, while the bâtonnier linked the Court's creation to the still-unformed Conseil Supérieur de la Magistrature — corroborated across two independent WebSearch passes returning matching detail from businessnews.com.tn, though a fully distinct second outlet's independent report of this specific hearing could not be located this run and the entry flags that limitation explicitly. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding enriches an existing card rather than extending the count); one existing entry (Constitutional Court) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.187/Run 187. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (288 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.189/Run 189. A dedicated research agent re-checked the full standing watchlist via WebSearch (Tavily's extract tool again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with prior runs) and found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with no confirmed floor vote (africanmanager.com, lapresse.tn, businessnews.com.tn, June 17–23, 2026); the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary (opened July 9, 10:30 AM, per Run 187/189) still had no reported vote outcome as of this run's cutoff; the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had not yet arrived; and the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported (a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis civil-society report still lists the case as "ongoing"). One genuine finding, both a correction and an enrichment, was verified via direct WebFetch/search cross-checking of businessnews.com.tn and kapitalis.com (July 4–6, 2026): the existing State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry's Run 155 text had described Elyes Chaouachi as a "separately-named co-defendant" condemned by the LTDH alongside Lourimi and Gharbi in their July 4, 2026 terrorism-chamber verdict — but primary-source verification shows Chaouachi's 15-year sentence was handed down the same day in a wholly distinct case (arising from a July 2024 arrest alongside a sought Ennahdha activist, no shared docket with Lourimi/Gharbi), with the LTDH's July 4–5 statement simply addressing both same-day verdicts together. The entry is corrected to reflect two separate cases, and enriched with previously-uncaptured detail: Chaouachi, a France-based activist and son of Case 1 defendant Ghazi Chaouachi, has accumulated a long series of predominantly in-absentia convictions now totaling more than 95 years and 8 months (up from over 80 years as of May 13, 2026, and 38 years as of November 25, 2025) — a serial-conviction pattern this artifact had never previously tracked as a running total despite 189 prior runs' coverage of the broader conspiracy-case cluster. No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding corrects and enriches an existing card); one existing entry (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) revised for accuracy and currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.190/Run 190. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome, the Development Plan plenary vote result, and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.

One genuine, previously-undocumented enrichment survived verification across two independent sources (La Presse de Tunisie and Tustex, both July 7–8, 2026): the Central Bank Law entry, whose monetary-policy detail had not been updated since Run 40, gained the BCT's newly signed US$312 million (76.7 billion Arab accounting dinars) balance-of-payments support facility with the Arab Monetary Fund, disbursed in three tranches over a seven-year maturity — a genuinely new financing arrangement distinct from anything previously tracked in this entry or the existing STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills. No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Central Bank Law) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.191/Run 191.
