A dedicated research agent re-checked all four standing watchlist items via WebSearch and found no resolution on any of them, with the caveat that search indices had not yet caught up to same-day (July 9) reporting: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete status with chair Maher Ktari's floated option of a vote without BCT input still not exercised, per the latest indexed coverage (June 17–23, 2026, businessnews.com.tn, La Presse de Tunisie); the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary (bill n°42/2026), confirmed opened July 9, 10:30 AM as scheduled (Business News, July 8), had no reported vote outcome in any indexed source as of this run's cutoff; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported, with a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis report still describing both associations as under "ongoing" dissolution procedures; and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates were reconfirmed as not yet arrived and unchanged. A broader sweep for new JORT publications or ARP-adopted laws from July 7–9, 2026 across finance/tax, foreign exchange, labor, digital/AI, environment, and health found nothing beyond already-known items. Two candidate leads were checked and found already captured rather than gaps: Mnemty president Saâdia Mosbah's June 23, 2026 Tunis Court of Appeal sentence confirmation (8 years) is the same criminal-case development already documented in the Run 192 changelog note as distinct from the dissolution proceeding itself; and the BCT's US$312 million Arab Monetary Fund balance-of-payments facility (announced July 8) matched the existing enrichment already added to the Central Bank Law entry at Run 191. No new standalone entry was added and no existing entry required revision (288 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run, consistent with Runs 191–193's own recent findings. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.194/Run 194. A general JORT/ARP sweep for new July 7–9, 2026 enactments found nothing beyond already-known items. One candidate lead was investigated and correctly rejected rather than added: a viral claim that a "new Road Code" took effect July 1, 2026 was independently debunked as false by businessnews.com.tn (July 6, 2026) — a rumor, not a legal development, so it was not added as an entry. A dedicated research agent re-checked all four standing watchlist items via WebSearch and found no resolution: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete status with no floor vote or BCT response since; the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary (bill n°42/2026), confirmed opened July 9, 10:30 AM as scheduled, was newly reported as explicitly slated by the ARP Bureau to possibly extend into July 10 for full deputy debate and the Economy Minister's responses (rtci.tn, leconomistemaghrebin.com), with no vote outcome reported by any source as of this run's cutoff; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported (distinct from Mnemty president Saâdia Mosbah's separate, already-documented June 23, 2026 criminal-appeal confirmation); and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates were reconfirmed as not yet arrived. A newly-surfaced related item was added to the watchlist rather than the confirmed record: a general strike of taxis, louages, and rural-transport operators is also set for July 13, 2026 (webmanagercenter.com, lapresse.tn, tunisienumerique.com), over unimplemented provisions tied to the Land Transport Law and Decree No. 2023-581 — landing the same day as the CNAM suspension. Two genuine enrichments survived independent verification: (1) the Sociétés Communautaires entry's Run 181 finding (a single-sourced, ~17,000-hectare state land allocation to community enterprises, flagged then as "pending further corroboration") was independently corroborated by a second outlet (businessnews.com.tn, July 6, 2026), which supplied precise figures — 38 state farms totaling 17,014 hectares — under a November 4, 2025 joint ministerial order, resolving the prior caveat; (2) the Land Transport Law entry, previously undocumented beyond its 2004 base text, gained a note on the July 13, 2026 transport strike referencing Decree No. 2023-581, whose specific subject matter could not be independently confirmed from available reporting and is flagged as an implementation-gap watch item rather than described further. The viral "new Road Traffic Code effective July 1, 2026" rumor, already debunked and documented at Run 190, was reconfirmed false and not re-added. No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since both genuine findings enrich existing cards); two existing entries (Sociétés Communautaires, Land Transport Law) enriched. One genuine enrichment closed a Run 196 open item: the Land Transport Law entry's flagged-but-unresolved Decree No. 2023-581 is now confirmed, via 9anoun.tn's JORT mirror (Decree of September 7, 2023, JORT No. 2023-104) independently cross-checked against African Manager's July 3, 2026 strike coverage, to concern the "organisation du transport public routier non régulier des personnes" — the licensing framework for taxis, louages, and rural transport, repealing the prior Decree No. 2202-2007 (as amended by Decree No. 828-2016). A candidate lead — a Le Desk (Morocco) report framing the ONAT lawyers' dispute as having "become regional mobilization" — was investigated and correctly excluded as insufficiently corroborated (a single non-Tunisian source, unmatched by any Tunisian outlet's framing). No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since the genuine finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Land Transport Law) enriched. A fresh independent WebSearch/WebFetch sweep converged exactly with Run 198's findings: the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary (bill n°42/2026) was confirmed, via same-day Business News and Tunisie Numérique reporting, to be in session starting 10:30am on July 9, 2026 itself, with no vote outcome yet reported; the Foreign Exchange Code reform remains at its previously-documented committee stage; and no JORT-published law, decree-law, or organic law dated July 8–9, 2026 was identified. Two genuine, independently-sourced developments survived cross-checking against the live document and were added: (1) a July 9, 2026 update to the Political Parties entry documenting deputy Fatma Mseddi's newly-filed 6-article proposition de loi seeking to classify Ennahdha as a terrorist organization — a fresh filing distinct from this decree-law's own party-dissolution mechanism, with no prior mention across 200 runs (L'Économiste Maghrébin, Business News); (2) a July 9, 2026 update to the Law No. 2025-18 graduate-recruitment entry noting 52 ARP deputies' formal petition to the Head of Government demanding the still-unpublished implementing decrees (La Presse). A WebSearch/WebFetch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist: the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary opened as scheduled at 10:30 AM on July 9, 2026 and ran the full day with no adoption/rejection vote reported by this run's cutoff, consistent with the Bureau's built-in contingency to extend into July 10; the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remains at its BCT-non-responsive committee status with no floor vote or JORT publication; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' outcome remains unreported since the July 6 hearing; and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had still not arrived. A village-justice.com opinion piece on the pending associations-reform organic law and a businessnews.com.tn fact-check debunking a false "new Highway Code entered into force July 1, 2026" rumor were both independently checked against the live document and found to add nothing new: the associations reform is already tracked in depth at Decree-Law No. 2011-88 and the pending organic-law entry, and no Highway Code claim exists anywhere in this artifact to correct. One genuine, dated development survived cross-checking and was added: a Run 202 update to the Development Plan 2026-2030 entry documenting the two most substantive floor criticisms of the bill to date — deputy Mohamed Ali's "chèque en blanc pour cinq ans" (blank-check) speech citing TND 147 billion in public debt (over 80% of GDP) and calling on deputies to vote against the bill, and ARP Bureau vice-president Nabil Hamdi's floor criticism that the plan "reflète davantage la volonté de l'administration que celle du peuple" and systematically excluded Parliament from its drafting (Business News and Radio Express FM, both July 9, 2026) — neither previously captured in the entry itself (Run 201's changelog note had referenced these deputies' criticism only in passing, without the entry being updated). No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since the finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.202/Run 202. One genuine finding survived cross-checking and was added, with appropriate hedging given a source conflict: a July 7, 2026 Business News article ("deux propositions de loi"), independently re-confirmed across three separately-worded searches, names Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 — filed the same day (July 23, 2024) as the already-tracked n° 74/2024 and before the same Agriculture Commission — as a probable second, separately-numbered animal-protection bill carrying the reinforced sanctions provisions, contradicting Run 176's dismissal of that headline as referring to a single bill. This conflicts with a July 26, 2024 Kapitalis filing announcement (fetched directly) naming only Deputy Chokri Bahri and one unnumbered bill with mixed sanction/awareness content, and a direct WebFetch of the July 7, 2026 article itself failed (HTTP 503), so the animal-welfare entry was updated to flag the probable second bill and the unresolved sourcing conflict rather than re-splitting the card or reattributing its existing content. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since the finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.203/Run 203. Tavily extract access was tested directly on the Run 203-flagged Business News animal-welfare URL and confirmed still disabled (HTTP 402, account-disabled error), and a direct WebFetch of the same URL again returned HTTP 503 — the third consecutive run unable to obtain the primary text directly. In its place, two independently-worded WebSearch queries this run both returned AI-summarized excerpts of that article converging on Run 203's "probable" attribution — Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 as the sanctions bill, n° 74/2024 as the prevention/awareness bill — with added detail (a specific 1–3-year/5-year sanction tier and deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher's quoted explanation for the delay), which was added to the entry with an explicit flag that the sanction figures conflict with Run 176's differently-sourced tiers and remain unconfirmed by primary text. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since the one genuine finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry (animal-welfare-bill-2024) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.204/Run 204. The Development Plan plenary vote result, the still-unconfirmed n° 66/2024 sanction figures, and the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.205 update, Run 205): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-v7xrfl) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (170 entries, a self-contained changelog running to "v2.12") unaware of origin/main's independent 204-run history (288 entries, v4.204) — a first draft round had already been committed on the stale fork (adding the same Marzouki/Daïmi/Kilani finding below, plus a Constitutional Court enrichment) before the mismatch was caught via git ls-remote origin/git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main, consistent with the identical failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note; the branch was reset directly to origin/main and the stale commit discarded rather than merged. Per established practice, both stale-draft candidates were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact before re-adding anything: the proposed Constitutional Court enrichment (a March 27, 2026 General Legislation Commission hearing of the National Bar Association, plus the bill's 61-article/9-member structure) was found already fully documented, in materially greater depth, at the Constitutional Court entry since Run 187 — correctly discarded as a duplicate. The Marzouki/Daïmi/Kilani finding survived cross-checking (no match for any of the three names anywhere in origin/main) and was independently re-verified via two further sources (The New Arab, Arab News) before being added as a wholly new standalone entry in the Judicial Organization section: a June 20, 2025 in-absentia terrorism conviction of exiled former president Moncef Marzouki, alongside former presidential adviser Imed Daïmi and former ONAT president Abderrazak Kilani (22 years each), arising from a critical Paris press conference — a genuine gap given it is the only tracked prosecution of a former Tunisian head of state, despite falling squarely within the artifact's 2022–2026 research window. A brief follow-up WebSearch sweep for JORT publications and ARP floor votes dated July 8–9, 2026 found nothing beyond the already-documented Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary session (still without a reported vote outcome) and Finance Law 2026; the Foreign Exchange Code, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution, and July 11/13/15, 2026 trigger dates remain unchanged from Run 204. One new standalone entry added (289 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 289 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 289/289 balanced <h3> tags, 1366/1366 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.205/Run 205. The Development Plan plenary vote result and the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 206): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-udx7lm) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (fourteen commits topping out at a "v2.11" label) with no pull request of its own — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and mcp__github__list_pull_requests (empty) before any drafting began — and was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.205, 289 entries, 205 merged runs) per established practice; no stale content existed to cross-check beyond the original fork point. A dedicated research sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary (opened July 9, 10:30am, per Run 187/189/190) was independently reconfirmed still in session with deputies raising financing-source and monitoring-mechanism concerns (businessnews.com.tn, africanmanager.com, rtci.tn, all July 8–9) — no vote tally or adoption outcome found by any outlet as of this run's cutoff; a distinct July 10, 2026 plenary item (bill n°39/2026, IBRD/STEG clean-energy loan-guarantee conventions) was identified and correctly not conflated with the Plan vote. The Foreign Exchange Code committee (~90% hearings complete, BCT still non-responsive per chair Maher Ktari) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome (postponed to July 6, still genuinely unreported by any outlet checked) both remain unchanged since Run 205. The July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates were each independently reconfirmed still on track and had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A single-sourced Kapitalis claim of a "July 5, 2026" Lourimi (3 years)/Chaouachi (15 years) verdict was checked and found to be the same verdict already documented in the State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry (Run 155/190 updates) at July 4, 2026 — a one-day source variance, not a new event; discarded as a duplicate. The Personal Data Protection entry's existing text (committee sessions through June 25, 2026, the 132-vs-123-article resolution) was reconfirmed already current and was not altered. A ten-area structural gap-scan (veterinary profession, architects, private security/gardiennage, Mining Code, Civil Aviation Code, maritime/ports, Insurance Code, cybersecurity agency ANCS, midwifery, real-estate agents/brokers) found eight already covered by existing entries at correct citation. Midwifery was re-examined consistent with Run 170's finding that it exists only at general paramedical-framework level (Law No. 92-74 of August 3, 1992) with no dedicated professional statute or Order — still not a standalone gap, though a live 2026 advocacy campaign for one was noted (lapresse.tn, May 2026). A candidate Real Estate Agents/Brokers citation ("Law 81-55"/"Law 2001-66") could not be corroborated on jurisitetunisie.com or any primary database and was correctly withheld — flagging a new false-positive trap for future runs: generic francophone "agent immobilier"-style professional-licensing searches are prone to pulling French/Belgian/Gabonese law by shared terminology, and should be anchored with "Tunisie"/"JORT" and cross-checked against jurisitetunisie.com or legislation.tn rather than generic legal-SEO sites. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (289 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 289 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 289/289 balanced <h3> tags, 1366/1366 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.206/Run 206. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.

Only one candidate from the stale draft survived cross-checking as a genuine, previously-undocumented gap: the Mohamed Ben Salem customs-conviction, arrest, and release (arrested July 8, released July 9, merits hearing set July 21, 2026) — a full-text search of all 206 prior runs found no match for "Ben Salem" — independently re-verified against four sources (Kapitalis, webdo.tn, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Tuniscope, all July 9, 2026) before being added as a new standalone Judicial Organization entry. Two further candidates from the stale draft were investigated and correctly declined: (1) two STEG/World Bank (IBRD) loan-guarantee ratification bills (n°38/2026, n°39/2026) headed to the July 10, 2026 plenary session — already documented in this run's own predecessor note (Run 206) and judged, consistent with that note, too routine for a standalone entry; (2) a trendsnafrica.com claim that the Personal Data Protection reform reaches "full enforcement beginning July 11, 2026" — re-investigated and re-confirmed unreliable per the Run 97 finding, contradicted by every primary source, which places the bill still in ARP Rights and Freedoms Committee review with no vote held. One new standalone entry added (290 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, 1370/1370 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.207/Run 207. Two dedicated research passes re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill n°42/2026, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Consumer Credit bill, Sports Structures bill, Animal Protection bill, theft-penalty bill, Games of Chance reform, construction-regularization bill, STEG/IBRD guarantee bills, exchange-violations amnesty bill) plus a scan for new JORT-published legislation and rule-of-law developments in the July 8–10, 2026 window. The Development Plan plenary (opened July 9, with a built-in contingency to extend into July 10) remained without a confirmed vote outcome as of this run's cutoff despite a dedicated verification pass — reconfirmed still the single highest-priority open item. The recent Mosbah, Bensedrine, Hmaidi, and Lourimi/Gharbi rulings, the Fatma Mseddi Ennahdha "terrorist organization" filing, and the reconstituted Criminal Reconciliation Commission (Decree No. 131/2026) were all independently re-surfaced and confirmed already fully documented at their respective entries (Runs 40/91/155/190/201/46). One genuine, independently-verified enrichment survived cross-checking: La Presse de Tunisie (June 19, 2026) reports the General Legislation Committee, chaired by MP Faouzi Daâs, found the pending theft/aggravated-robbery Penal Code amendment (Arts. 261, 262 & 264) so substantially altered during hearings that it asked the bill's initiators to file a new proposal rather than continue amending this one — effectively restarting that bill's legislative process; added to the existing Harsher Theft & Aggravated-Robbery Penalties entry. No new standalone entries were added (290 total remains accurate, since this finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.208/Run 208. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.

A dedicated research sweep re-checked the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary first: multiple French- and Arabic-language sources (webmanagercenter.com, businessnews.com.tn, La Presse, African Manager, RTCI, Tunisie Numérique, Kapitalis, Mosaïque FM, Le Maghreb) confirm the session opened July 9, 10:30am under Speaker Ibrahim Bouderbala and remained sharply contested (pro-plan deputies calling it a foundational roadmap; critics objecting parliament received a "closed text" with no drafting role) — but no outlet reports a vote tally, pass, or rejection as of this run's cutoff, so it remains unresolved a second consecutive run. The related STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee ratification bills (n°38/39, referred to the same July 10 plenary by the ARP Bureau) likewise show no reported vote outcome. No new JORT-published legislation was found in the July 9–10, 2026 window. A cross-check of judicial matters surfaced during this sweep (Khayam Turki's July 3 tax conviction, the Lourimi/Gharbi July 3 verdict, Bilel Chaouachi's July 6 appeal, Samir Abdelli's July 7 money-laundering/terrorism conviction, and the July 7 mass sentencing of eighteen TikTok/Instagram content creators) confirmed all five already fully documented at their respective entries — none added as new. One false positive was caught and discarded: an elbalad.news "development plan" article referenced Egypt's 2026/2027 plan, not Tunisia's. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.209/Run 209. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary and the related STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39 of 2026 — independently re-confirmed as a $400M direct BIRD guarantee plus a $30M Clean Technology Fund co-guarantee, both stemming from a November 3, 2025 financing agreement) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across six outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, RTCI, Tunisie Numérique, allAfrica). The Foreign Exchange Code was reconfirmed at its ~90%-hearings-complete status (African Manager, Tuniscope), with chair Maher Ktari's floated option of a committee vote without the Banque Centrale still not exercised as of this run's cutoff. The Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source four days on. Two candidate leads were investigated and correctly excluded rather than added: (1) a cluster of foreign crypto-industry blog posts (lightspark.com, coinfomania.com) claiming an ARP committee is "considering a draft bill to decriminalize cryptocurrency possession" — no primary Tunisian source (JORT, ARP registry, or mainstream Tunisian press) corroborates any such bill, consistent with this artifact's established practice of declining single-source, non-primary claims; Tunisia's underlying prohibition (BCT foreign-exchange circular, criminalizing unauthorized crypto transactions, up to 5 years' imprisonment) remains accurately reflected in the existing Digital Law & ICT section with no pending-reform claim added. (2) A July 6, 2026 Business News fact-check ("Non, aucun nouveau Code de la route n'est entré en vigueur en Tunisie le 1er juillet 2026") debunking a social-media rumor of a new Traffic Code taking effect July 1 was independently re-surfaced and confirmed to describe a false claim this artifact never carried — no action needed. A re-derivation check of the Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions Law (independently re-confirmed as Law No. 2026-8 of May 13, 2026, adopted 76–1–0) found it already captured at the existing entry since Run 40, with greater precision than this run's independent search. No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 9–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.211/Run 211. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary (bill n°42/2026, in session since 10:30 AM July 9 under its built-in contingency to extend into July 10) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across the outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique). (2) A July 9, 2026 webdo.tn article on the JORT publication of the labor-contracts/anti-subcontracting law was independently re-surfaced and confirmed to describe the same May 2025 reform already comprehensively documented at the existing Law No. 2025-9 entry — no new provision or later JORT publication was identified. A re-derivation check of deputy Fatma Mseddi's Ennahdha "terrorist organization" bill (formally filed July 9, 2026) and the Ghannouchi "Secret Apparatus"/Instalingo case sentencing detail (including co-defendant Mustapha Khedher's life-plus-96-years sentence) both independently re-confirmed already captured at the Political Parties and State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entries respectively, in each case with equal or greater precision than this run's own research. No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 9–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.212/Run 212. Tavily search access remained disabled (HTTP 402, "account currently disabled") for this run as well, so research relied on WebSearch/WebFetch. A dedicated sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary and the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across the outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique, Radio Express FM); the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings (July 6 hearing outcome still unreported) both remain unchanged. The BCT's US$312 million Arab Monetary Fund balance-of-payments facility (signed July 7, announced July 8) was re-surfaced and reconfirmed already captured at the Central Bank Law entry since Run 191. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and closed: the "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" case — five defendants (Sherifa Riahi, Yadh Bousselmi, Mohamed Joo, Mohamed Iqbal Khaled, Imen Ouardani) convicted January 5, 2026 by the Tunis Court of First Instance to two years' suspended imprisonment under Passports & Travel Documents Law Articles 38/39/41 and the 1968 Foreigners Law, after roughly 19 months' pre-trial detention, with 18 co-defendants acquitted. Prior runs (see the Run 186 note) had conflated this case with the already-documented Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR)/Djemali prosecution at the Passports & Travel Documents Law entry; independent verification (FIDH, ICJ, Amnesty International, Business News, Kapitalis, L'Economiste Maghrébin) confirmed "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" (the Tunisian branch of France Terre d'Asile) is a distinct organization from CTR, with different defendants and a different verdict date (January 5, 2026 vs. November 24, 2025) — a genuine gap, not a duplicate. Added as a new standalone entry cross-referenced from the CTR entry, the Administrative Suspensions entry, and the Affaire Abdallah Saïd entry. One new standalone entry added (291 total, up from 290). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates (the one apparent "renewables-law" id collision found by a naive grep, as in Run 208, traces to changelog prose referencing the id inside a <code> tag, not a real duplicate element), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.213/Run 213. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.

Tavily search was unavailable this run (account disabled, HTTP 402), so research relied entirely on the built-in WebSearch/WebFetch tools. A sixth consecutive sweep of the standing watchlist found no resolution: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary, continuing under its built-in July 9→10 contingency, still had no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across every outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, African Manager, webdo.tn) as of this run's cutoff; the companion STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n° 38/2026 & 39/2026) were likewise confirmed still scheduled for the same July 10 plenary with no vote outcome yet reported; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source found, now eight days on. A candidate lead — a "Tunisie: Le parlement adopte son nouveau règlement intérieur" headline resurfacing in current search results — was investigated via direct WebFetch and confirmed to be a mis-dated republication of an April 29, 2023 article (121-2-1 vote adopting the ARP's original 171-article rules of procedure), not a 2026 event; it was discarded rather than added. Two further candidate items — the Law No. 2025-18 graduate-recruitment statute's July 9, 2026 deputies' petition (52 signatories) and Foreign Exchange Code bill n° 115/2025's CONECT 41-amendment submission — were each confirmed already present in the existing Law No. 2025-18 and Foreign Exchange Code entries with equal or greater precision, and were left unchanged. No new JORT-published legislation and no new standalone entries were found (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.215/Run 215. The Development Plan and STEG loan-guarantee plenary vote outcomes remain the highest-priority items to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
  • (v4.216 update, Run 216): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-dz381z) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (13 commits, last touched July 8) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.215 (291 entries, 215 merged runs) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git merge-base origin/main HEAD (resolving to the original v1.0 fork point), and mcp__github__search_pull_requests (zero PRs, open or merged, against this branch) before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Tavily search remained unavailable (HTTP 402, "account currently disabled"), so research again relied entirely on WebSearch/WebFetch. A dedicated sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary (bill n°42/2026) and the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026), both convened for the July 10, 2026 plenary, showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across the outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, African Manager, allAfrica) as of this run's cutoff; the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained at the committee stage per the most recent reporting (La Presse, June 17, 2026), with no plenary session yet scheduled; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source found, now four days on. Six candidate leads were investigated via direct WebFetch/cross-source verification and each confirmed already fully captured rather than added as new: (1) a "L'ARP adopte la loi sur les conseils locaux et régionaux" headline (109–2–4 vote, 9 of 10 articles) — direct WebFetch of the source confirmed its actual publication date as February 27, 2025, not the 2026 date implied by the search index, matching this artifact's existing Local Government Code entry's Organic Law No. 2025-4 detail exactly; (2) a "21 Ennahda leaders sentenced 12–35 years, Ghannouchi 14 years" headline — traced via cross-source verification to the July 8, 2025 first-instance "Case 2" verdict, already documented at the State Security Conspiracy entry together with its February 2, 2026 appeal outcome (Ghannouchi raised to 20 years); (3) the recurring "Personal Data Protection full enforcement begins July 11, 2026" marketing claim was again correctly withheld — the organic bill remains under Rights & Freedoms Committee review with no plenary vote, making an "enforcement date" for still-pending legislation internally contradictory; (4) the July 8, 2026 Intersection pour les droits et les libertés civil-society report figures (88 documented cases, 47 association-level restrictions, 41 individual proceedings, 8 pre-trial detentions, 22 one-month suspensions) were independently re-derived via direct Kapitalis fetch and matched the existing Administrative Suspensions entry's Run 186 figures exactly; (5) the Mohamed Ben Salem customs-case arrest (July 8) and appellate-prosecutor-ordered release (July 9) were matched to the existing entry verbatim, including the July 21 merits-hearing date; (6) the Business News July 6, 2026 fact-check debunking a rumored new Road Traffic Code entering force July 1, 2026 matched the existing Road Traffic Code entry's Run 136 note exactly. No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 8–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.216/Run 216. The Development Plan and STEG loan-guarantee plenary vote outcomes remain the highest-priority items to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.

No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 8–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run.   • (v4.218 update, Run 218): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-hynsgk) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11/v2.12 lineage (15 commits, last touched July 8, topping out at a "v2.12" draft with 171 entries built against the stale base without checking origin/main first) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.217 (291 entries, 217 merged runs) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice; the stale draft's two candidate findings (the anti-subcontracting Law No. 2025-9 and the Development Plan bill's existence) were both confirmed already captured, in each case with materially greater precision, at the existing Law No. 2025-9 and Development Plan entries — no duplication resulted. A dedicated WebSearch sweep resolved the single item every run since Run 71 has flagged as highest-priority: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary concluded and the ARP adopted the bill in its entirety on July 10, 2026 by a recorded vote of 64 for, 24 against, and 15 abstentions (La Presse de Tunisie), following the minister's replies to deputies that Tunisie Tribune and African Manager had reported earlier the same day as still in progress. This vote tally was, as of this update, reported only by La Presse among the outlets checked — flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings; the bill still requires presidential promulgation and JORT publication before entering into force. The companion STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) were re-checked but no vote outcome had been reported as of this update. A candidate lead — a July 2025-dated wave of Article 230 LGBTQ arrests in Djerba/Tunis (Damj, 14 arrested, 6 already sentenced) surfaced by a search using a 2026 query — was verified via direct WebFetch of the source to actually be dated July 17, 2025, over a year stale, and correctly excluded rather than added as a new development. No new standalone entries were added; one existing entry (Development Plan) was updated with its concluding vote outcome (291 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.218/Run 218. The STEG loan-guarantee vote outcome and next-run corroboration of the Development Plan tally remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
  • (v4.219 update, Run 219): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-papbt1) held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (13 commits, last touched July 8) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.218 (291 entries, 218 merged runs) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and reset directly to current origin/main per established practice before any drafting began, avoiding a repeat of the stale-draft pattern documented in the v4.218 note above. A dedicated WebSearch sweep resolved the two highest-priority items Run 218 left open: (1) the 2026–2030 Development Plan vote tally (64 for, 24 against, 15 abstentions) is now independently corroborated by a second outlet, webdo.tn (July 10, 2026, 12:02 PM), reporting identical figures via direct WebFetch verification — no longer single-sourced, though presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding; (2) the companion STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) were confirmed calendared for the same July 9–10, 2026 plenary window as the Development Plan, but no outlet (La Presse, Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, webdo.tn) reported a STEG vote outcome — genuinely still undecided rather than merely unreported, so both entries were updated to reflect this. A broader sweep of six further pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Tax Adviser Organic Law, gambling/online-betting reform) and a dedicated JORT search for July 8–10, 2026 found no genuine new developments — a claimed July 29 public-sector recruitment law vote and a French (not Tunisian) Journal Officiel entry were both identified as stale/mislabeled and correctly excluded; a direct fetch of iort.gov.tn returned HTTP 503 and could not independently corroborate the JORT-silence finding. The Investment Code reform entry's existing two-track framing (government track pre-transmission vs. MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024 in committee) was independently re-verified against the same July 3, 2026 IACE/African Manager reporting and found already accurate — no correction needed. No new standalone entries were added; two existing entries (Development Plan, STEG loan guarantees) were updated with genuinely new detail (291 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.219/Run 219. The July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, plus the still-unreported STEG vote outcome and Al Khatt/Mnemty case, remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.220 update, Run 220): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sttts7) initially held a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (14 commits, last touched July 8, plus a fresh draft this session — a "v2.12" with 174 entries, PR #234 — built and pushed before checking origin/main) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.219 (291 entries, 219 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. Unlike most prior recurrences, the discrepancy was only caught after PR #234 had already been opened and its merge attempt failed with a "merge conflicts" error; all four of the draft's candidate additions (Law No. 2025-9 anti-subcontracting labor reform, Law No. 2026-5 agricultural-debt regularization, Decree-Law No. 2022-13 penal reconciliation, and deputy Fatma Mseddi's July 9, 2026 Ennahdha-terrorist-designation bill) were individually cross-checked via grep against the real current artifact and found already present — in every case with equal or greater precision (see Law No. 2025-9, #agri-debt-regularization, Decree-Law 2022-13, and the Political Parties entry's existing Run 201 update). PR #234 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment rather than force-merged or reused, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before restarting, consistent with how PR #32 (Run 28) and other prior stale-PR incidents were handled. A fresh WebSearch sweep then re-checked the full standing watchlist: the 2026–2030 Development Plan vote tally (64–24–15) was independently re-corroborated by a third and fourth outlet (L'Économiste Maghrébin, Tunisie Tribune, both July 10, 2026) but presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained without a reported plenary vote outcome across every outlet checked; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported, now four days on; and the Constitutional Court bill (n°100/2025) remained at its already-documented committee stage with no session dated after February 17, 2026 found. One candidate lead — an aa.com.tr article on a bill to strip the Administrative Court of electoral-dispute jurisdiction — was verified via direct WebFetch to be dated September 22, 2024, nearly two years stale, and correctly excluded, consistent with the established stale-news discipline flagged at multiple prior runs (e.g. the original artifact's own v2.8-era note on the same story). No new JORT-published legislation, floor vote, or court verdict dated specifically to July 9–10, 2026 was identified beyond what Run 219 already captured. The Development Plan's 64–24–15 adoption tally was independently re-confirmed by three further outlets dated July 10, 2026 (La Presse, Réalités, L'Économiste Maghrébin), but presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding as of this update. Two independent research passes ran this session: (1) a full re-check of the standing highest-priority watchlist — the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "debate underway, no tally reported" across every outlet checked (direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article confirms it explicitly describes only the examination stage, not a vote); the Development Plan's 64–24–15 tally was re-confirmed by four concordant outlets (La Presse, L'Économiste Maghrébin, African Manager, Réalités) but no presidential promulgation or JORT publication was found (JORT's next scheduled edition falls Tuesday, July 14, 2026); the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any outlet found; and the July 11 (ONAT) trigger date had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff (still July 10, 2026), with no pre-emptive agenda or outcome reporting located. (2) A deliberately different-angle gap hunt, avoiding the already-exhausted pending-bill watchlist, checked seven areas for entirely new enacted legislation: direct JORT/iort.gov.tn browsing (technically inaccessible — dynamic search interface, no static listing, a known limitation rather than evidence of silence); local/municipal governance reform (none since the already-documented Organic Law No. 2025-4 of March 12, 2025, which repealed the 2018 Code des Collectivités Locales's region/district provisions — see the Local Government entry); education law; Code of Personal Status/inheritance; consumer protection (still Law No. 92-117 of December 7, 1992, unamended); asylum/migration law (Tunisia still has no domestic asylum statute; the only 2026 movement is the EU's unrelated February 2026 "safe country of origin" designation, outside this artifact's domestic-law scope); and telecom/5G regulation (no new text since the January 2025 licensing decree). None of the seven angles surfaced a genuine, sourced, dated new enactment. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run testing fresh research angles rather than repeating prior runs' exact queries. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.223/Run 223. The STEG loan-guarantee vote outcome, Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates.

  • (v4.230 update, Run 230): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.229, 291 entries, 229 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A dedicated research agent re-checked the five standing highest-priority watchlist items several hours after Run 229's same-day cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly was independently re-confirmed as scheduled/held Saturday, July 11, 2026, but no outlet had yet reported its outcome or any resumed-dialogue conditions by this run's cutoff either; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported," matching the existing entry's Run 222 detail verbatim, including the same July 10 La Presse "Énergie: l'ARP se penche..." source; the Development Plan's 64–24–15 adoption tally was reconfirmed with presidential promulgation and JORT publication still outstanding; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found, now the sixth consecutive run to find nothing; and the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete committee stage. Four candidate leads surfaced by the sweep were individually cross-checked against the live artifact and each confirmed already fully captured rather than genuinely new: (1) Khayam Turki's July 3, 2026 four-year tax-offense conviction, already documented at the State Security Conspiracy entry's Run 81 update with identical detail; (2) Mnemty president Saadia Mosbah's June 23, 2026 appellate confirmation (8 years) and the five co-defendants' sentences, already documented at the Racial Discrimination Law entry's Run 153 update with materially greater precision (fine amounts, individual co-defendant breakdown, July 7, 2026 HRW corroboration) than this run's own research produced; (3) the STEG loan-guarantee bills' committee/plenary detail, already matching the existing entry verbatim; (4) a Jeune Afrique headline suggesting a new July 2026 Constitutional Court (Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025) development could not be corroborated by any second source or dated beyond the already-documented March 27, 2026 ONAT-hearing update (Run 187) — treated as an unconfirmed lead rather than added, consistent with this project's single-source discipline. One genuinely new but insufficiently anchored lead was investigated and deliberately withheld rather than forced into a standalone entry: a corruption/money-laundering trial of businessman Maher Chaâbane and more than ten co-defendants, previously undocumented, was reported postponed from July 6 to September 24, 2026 (Tunisie Tribune, single-sourced) — since this is a bare postponement with no first-instance verdict yet, unlike the Mabrouk/Chahed, Haouas/SOREAL, Mekacher, and Turki entries which were each added only upon an actual conviction, it is flagged here as a lead for a future run to revisit once (and if) a verdict is reported. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.230/Run 230. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.

  • (v4.232 update, Run 232): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-oiv763) held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (15 commits, root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.12," last touched July 10), unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.231 (291 entries, 231 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; caught via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline --all, and an empty list_pull_requests result for this branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the six standing highest-priority watchlist items a few hours after Run 231's cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly (held Saturday, July 11) still had no reported outcome across every outlet checked, likely same-day search-index lag rather than a null result; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported" — a direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article confirmed it explicitly does not report a vote count; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found (including a direct fetch of Inkyfada's own homepage), now the eighth consecutive run to find nothing; the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete stage; and the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was reconfirmed across five outlets, with no verdict expected before then. One genuine, if modest, enrichment was found for the Development Plan 2026–2030 entry: a procedural detail — sourced from Tunisie Numérique quoting deputy Jmaï Zouidi — that the bill's outstanding promulgation is not merely awaiting presidential signature but requires a second-chamber vote at the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD) under Constitution Art. 77's bicameral design first, with unresolved inter-chamber disagreements referred to a 10-member joint committee and, failing that, final say reverting to the CNRD; added to the entry to explain why promulgation/JORT publication remain outstanding well past the July 10 ARP floor vote. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision beyond that one enrichment (291 total remains accurate) — essentially a confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.232/Run 232. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.

  • (v4.236 update, Run 236): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.235, 291 entries, 235 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A dedicated research agent re-checked the six standing highest-priority watchlist items on the same calendar day as Run 235's cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly (held earlier today, Saturday, July 11) still had no reported outcome across every outlet checked (Business News, African Manager, Réalités, Mosaïque FM, Kapitalis, Webdo, Tunisie Numérique) — expected, since same-day/Saturday reporting typically lags into the following week; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported," confirmed via a direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found, now the eleventh consecutive run to find nothing (a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis civil-society report still lists the case only as "ongoing," suggesting the silence reflects a genuine reporting gap rather than a missed source); the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete stage, BCT still non-responsive; the CNAM/private biologists' tiers-payant suspension was reconfirmed still on track for July 13, 2026 (two days out), distinct from the already-resolved private pharmacists' July 1 dispute; and the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was reconfirmed, with the added detail that the same July 6 hearing also denied the defense's release requests. One genuine, if modest, enrichment was found for the Development Plan 2026–2030 entry: the CNRD's second-chamber examination has expanded beyond the first district (Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef) to a second district, per an African Manager report of CNRD president Imed Derbali's hearing with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh, citing a 0.51 territorial-development index for Bizerte and a 7.3% poverty rate for Jendouba — still no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication confirmed. Three candidate leads from a fresh gap-scan were each cross-checked and found already fully captured rather than genuinely new: (1) the mass "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram prosecution's July 6, 2026 verdict (1–27 years, 18 defendants), already documented at that standalone entry since Run 155 with identical figures and sourcing; (2) the Makram Jlassi embezzlement case's July 4, 2026 appellate sentence increase (5 to 8 years), already documented at that standalone entry since Run 87 with identical figures; (3) AMT president Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appellate sentence confirmation, already documented at the Judicial Statute entry since Run 42. A fourth lead — a purported July 9, 2026 Council of Ministers meeting adopting several decree-laws (Fidaa foundation amendment, agricultural-workers' social-security regime, major-public-project procedures) — was traced via direct WebFetch of pm.gov.tn to a mis-dated search-snippet artifact actually describing a September 26, 2024 meeting, and correctly discarded rather than added, consistent with the database/search-snippet mis-dating trap flagged in earlier runs. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision beyond the one Development Plan enrichment (291 total remains accurate) — essentially a confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.236/Run 236.   • (v4.237 update, Run 237): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.236, 291 entries, 236 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A fresh WebSearch sweep, a few hours after Run 236's same-day cutoff, re-checked the same six standing watchlist items and found no status change on any of them: the ONAT assembly's outcome remained unreported by every outlet checked, now the seventh consecutive run since the Saturday, July 11 assembly to find nothing; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills' vote tally remained unreported from the July 10 plenary session; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported, now the twelfth consecutive run to find nothing; the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its committee-hearing stage (June 1 and May 11, 2026 CONECT/entrepreneur hearings reconfirmed as the most recent activity, BCT still non-responsive); the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remained unconfirmed; and the CNAM/biologists' July 13 suspension remained on track, two days out. A fresh gap-scan across five areas (national AI-regulation strategy, administrative-procedure simplification, cyber-harassment/online violence against women, Council of Ministers decree-law activity, and a repeat check of the Foreign Exchange Code via English-language sources) found nothing not already captured: the AI-strategy and Finance-Law-2026 administrative-simplification leads matched existing entries verbatim, and a Council of Ministers "adoption of several decree-laws" lead again resolved via direct WebFetch of pm.gov.tn to the same mis-dated September 26, 2024 meeting snippet Run 236 had already identified and discarded — reconfirming that trap rather than surfacing anything new. One WebSearch-summary claim was tested and refuted by direct source fetch rather than accepted: a search result stated Tunisia's Law No. 2017-58 "does not criminalize digital violence," but a direct WebFetch of the underlying Le Temps article showed the opposite — the law already extends to "violence perpetrated through social networks or other electronic means," with no bill or amendment proposed — so no entry was added or changed, and this specific search-summary inaccuracy is flagged for future runs alongside the project's existing cross-country-mislabeling and stale-date traps as a third distinct false-positive pattern: summarization errors that invert an article's actual finding. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.237/Run 237. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.

A fresh WebSearch/WebFetch sweep then re-checked the standing watchlist: the ONAT assembly's outcome remained unreported by every outlet checked; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported; and the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remained unconfirmed. One genuine, if modest, update was found for the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills entry: the July 10, 2026 plenary session (Bardo, Minister Salah Zouari addressing deputies) adjourned without a vote, and — a more precise status than prior runs' "debate underway, no tally reported" — African Manager's follow-up headline ("Le Parlement reporte à mardi l'examen de deux prêts destinés à la transition énergétique"), cross-referenced on La Presse's own site, confirms the ARP will resume examination Tuesday, July 15, 2026. No new standalone entries were added and no other existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — one existing entry (STEG loan guarantees) updated with the confirmed adjournment/rescheduling detail.   • (v4.239 update, Run 239): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-i6thhq) again initially held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.12") — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main against origin/main's independent progress to v4.238 (291 entries, 238 merged runs); the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any drafting, per established practice, and the stale lineage's draft "Chokri Belaïd" entry was independently re-verified from primary sources rather than reused verbatim. A WebSearch/WebFetch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly, held today (Saturday, July 11, 2026), had no reported outcome from any outlet as of this run's cutoff — expected, since the assembly's own scheduled date has only just arrived; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source, now the tenth consecutive run to find nothing; the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remain unconfirmed; and the STEG loan-guarantee bills' rescheduled July 15, 2026 vote has not yet occurred. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and added as a new standalone entry: the direct criminal prosecution over the February 6, 2013 assassination of Chokri Belaïd — distinct from the already-documented "Secret Apparatus" case, which covers a related but legally separate clandestine-organization prosecution with no defendant overlap. Independently verified via direct WebFetch of webdo.tn and directinfo.webmanagercenter.com: former Republic Prosecutor Béchir Akremi was convicted March 4, 2026 to a combined 23 years (evidence-concealment charges) and Ennahdha figure Habib Ellouze to 13 years, with the appeal — before the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism chamber — postponed twice (June 23 then July 6, 2026) to a new July 13, 2026 hearing, the same trigger date already tracked for the unrelated CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension. The new entry was cross-referenced from the "Secret Apparatus" case entry. One new standalone entry added (292 total, up from 291); one existing entry (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) lightly enriched with the cross-reference. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.239/Run 239. The July 11 (ONAT outcome), July 13 (Chokri Belaïd appeal and CNAM/biologists), July 15 (STEG vote), Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.240 update, Run 240): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-javjm1) again initially held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.13 lineage (root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.13," including a full drafting pass and a pushed commit against that stale base before the discrepancy was caught) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main against origin/main's independent progress to v4.239 (292 entries, 239 merged runs); the stale commit was discarded in full (no content merged forward) and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any further work, consistent with established practice. Before discovering the stale base, this session's own independent research (three parallel WebSearch sweeps covering newly-enacted legislation, the pending-bill watchlist, and the political-prosecution docket) had already surfaced two candidates; cross-checking both against the real origin/main found the Development Plan's July 10, 2026 plenary adoption (64–24–15) already fully captured (including the CNRD bicameral-review detail added at Run 232 and its expanding-district examination added at Run 236) and correctly discarded as a duplicate. The second candidate survived cross-checking as a genuine, previously-undocumented gap: journalist Zied El Heni faces a second, legally distinct prosecution beyond his already-documented Telecommunications Code Article 86 conviction (Run 63) — the "affaire Ounissi," before the specialized anti-terrorism chamber under the Anti-Terrorism Law rather than an ordinary correctional court, originating from his journalistic verification of information that led to his being summoned by the anti-terrorism unit as early as March 2024 (Kapitalis). A hearing was held July 10, 2026 without El Heni, his family, or his defense counsel being informed and without his transfer to court — independently verified via a WebSearch cross-check of businessnews.com.tn's July 10, 2026 report (a direct WebFetch returned HTTP 503, consistent with the intermittent 503s several prior runs have logged against Tunisian outlets) corroborated by Réalités Magazine and Turess/Business News republications of the same reporting; added as an extension to the Telecommunications Code entry, which already hosts El Heni's Article 86 case. A follow-up WebSearch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist a few hours after Run 239's cutoff: the ONAT assembly's outcome remained unreported by every outlet checked, still expected given same-day/weekend reporting lag; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported, now the eleventh consecutive run to find nothing; and the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remained unconfirmed. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate, since this run's finding extends an existing card); one existing entry (Telecommunications Code) enriched. A structural audit corrected a stat-counter bug inherited from prior runs: the header's "Laws Documented" stat had been left at 291 for at least one full run while the true card count was already 292 — corrected here, alongside synchronizing all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) at v4.240/Run 240. The July 11 (ONAT outcome), July 13 (Chokri Belaïd appeal and CNAM/biologists), July 15 (STEG vote), Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside watching for a verdict in El Heni's newly-documented anti-terrorism-chamber case.

One genuine, previously-undocumented development was found and added as an enrichment: a first-ever operation to repatriate Tunisian women and children (reportedly 200+ children, 60+ women, fathers/husbands affiliated with Daesh/ISIS) from Syrian camps and prisons was reported underway July 10, 2026, per OTDH president Mostafa Abdelkebir (Businessnews.com.tn, Kapitalis, both July 10, 2026) — corroborated across two independent outlets but resting on a single named source (the OTDH president), with no official Tunisian government confirmation of the operation, its scale, or any implementing legal framework found in this run; added to the Countering Terrorism Law entry with this single-source caveat explicit, rather than as a new standalone entry, since no dedicated statute or decree was identified to anchor one. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering) enriched. The sweep additionally re-surfaced three prosecutions (Elyes Chaouachi's July 3, 2026 sentence bringing his cumulative total past 95 years; AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appeal confirmation with immediate execution; and the Ajmi Lourimi/Mossaâb Gharbi terrorism-chamber verdicts) as apparent candidates, but a targeted grep cross-check found all three already folded into the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry with materially more precision (exact dates, sentence breakdowns, and distinguishing docket details) than the fresh research alone provided — confirming, rather than extending, existing coverage. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (292 total remains accurate) — a second consecutive pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids, 1378/1378 balanced <div> tags, 292/292 balanced <h3> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.244/Run 244. Every item from the discarded draft was then independently re-checked against the actual current artifact via targeted search: Law No. 2026-8 of May 13, 2026 (Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions) was already present; the Development Plan 2026–2030's ARP adoption was already documented; the retiree work-and-benefits bill was already covered under the Social Security section; and the Fatma Mseddi Ennahdha-designation bill (filed July 9, 2026) was already cross-referenced from the relevant Administrative-law decree entry. No genuine new-content gap was found this run — a WebSearch sweep for July 11, 2026 developments (ONAT general assembly outcome, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution ruling, Chokri Belaïd/CNAM/STEG watchlist items, and a CNAM "E-CNAM" digital-platform migration ruled out as an IT rollout with no decree basis, hence out of scope) surfaced nothing beyond what Run 244 already recorded. This run's one substantive contribution was a link-integrity audit: a full regex sweep for broken internal anchors and duplicate ids found two genuinely broken cross-references inside historical changelog prose — href="#ennahda-conspiracy-2" and href="#cnam", both pointing to ids that no longer exist after entries were later renamed/consolidated (to conspiracy-cases-2023-26 and cnam-health respectively) — and corrected both; a previously-reported "duplicate renewables-law id" was confirmed a false positive (two of the three regex matches are inside <code> prose describing the id, not live elements). Post-fix audit: 292 unique law-card ids, 0 broken internal anchors (down from 2), 1378/1378 balanced <div>, 292/292 <h3>, 30/30 <section>, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.245/Run 245. Total documented items unchanged at 292. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate); one existing entry enriched. One genuine, well-corroborated correction was found and applied to the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills entry: the "Tuesday, July 15, 2026" reschedule date carried since Run 238 and repeated in Runs 244–247 is a date/weekday mismatch — direct date computation confirms July 15, 2026 falls on a Wednesday, not a Tuesday, while July 14, 2026 is the actual Tuesday following the Friday, July 10, 2026 adjournment. A July 11, 2026 La Presse republication of the African Manager story was fetched directly via WebFetch and confirmed it still only says "mardi prochain" (next Tuesday) without independently naming a calendar date, meaning "July 15" was never actually sourced — it appears to have been introduced as an arithmetic slip in Run 238 and then silently propagated by four subsequent runs' watchlist summaries without independent verification, a new failure-mode variant (compounding an unverified derived date across runs) worth flagging alongside the already-documented cross-country-mislabeling and database-timestamp traps. Corrected the entry's date field to July 14, 2026 and added a Run 248 correction note; the STEG vote's actual outcome remains unreported as of this update. No new standalone entries were added and no other existing entry required revision (292 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids, all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.248/Run 248. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the July 13 (Chokri Belaïd appeal, CNAM/biologists) and now-corrected July 14 (STEG vote) trigger dates, and the Development Plan's CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication remain the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside continued monitoring of the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling.
  • (v4.249 update, Run 249): This session's designated branch again initially held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (root-commit fork, topping out at a self-labeled "v2.12") unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.248 (292 entries, 248 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; caught via git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was hard-reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A WebSearch sweep of the standing watchlist found the ONAT assembly outcome, Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling, and July 13/14 trigger-date items (Chokri Belaïd appeal, CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, STEG vote) still unresolved as of this run's cutoff — each date either had not yet arrived or remained unreported by any outlet checked, consistent with prior runs. One genuine, previously-undocumented development was found and added as an enrichment to the existing Lawyers Statute (ONAT) entry rather than a new standalone card: at the June 10, 2026 hearing on the prosecutor general's procedural challenge to ONAT's May 1, 2026 extraordinary general assembly, some 580 lawyers filed a formal acte de constitution defending the Order's position, and the Tunis Court of First Instance postponed examination of the case to October 28, 2026 — a date Business News noted risks mooting the appeal's practical effect, since the contested assembly's strike/boycott actions will largely have run their course before that hearing (independently corroborated via Business News, July 10, 2026). Two other candidate leads were investigated and confirmed already fully captured: an "Elyes Chaouachi sentenced to 95 years" CRLDHT statement (July 7, 2026) restates the same cumulative 95y8m figure already documented in the State Security Conspiracy entry since the Run 190 correction, sourced there via LTDH rather than CRLDHT; and a "Sports Violence Law" (Proposition de loi n° 83/2025) lead traced to the existing entry added in Run 18. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 1378/1378 balanced <div>, 292/292 <h3>, 30/30 <section>, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.249/Run 249. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision — a pure confirmation run (292 total remains accurate).
